metformin.mechanism: Decreases hepatic glucose production, increases insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues, reduces intestinal absorption of glucose
contraindications: Acute heart failure requiring pharmacologic intervention

dosing.adult: Start 500 mg OD/BD with meals → titrate to 1000 mg BD (max 2550 mg/day)
dosing.pediatric: ≥10 years: 500 mg OD → max 2000 mg/day in divided doses
dosing.renal: eGFR 30-45: max 1000 mg/day, do not initiate; eGFR <30: contraindicated
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment (lactic acidosis risk)

adverse_effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain) — up to 30%
metformin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe. Used in gestational DM in some guidelines. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First-line for T2DM
Weight neutral/slight loss
Low hypoglycemia risk
Hold before contrast

atorvastatin.mechanism: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis. Upregulates hepatic LDL receptors.
contraindications: Active liver disease or unexplained persistent transaminase elevation
Concomitant use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors at high statin doses

dosing.adult: 10-80 mg OD, any time of day. Start 10-20 mg; high-intensity: 40-80 mg
dosing.pediatric: 10-17 years (hetFH): 10-20 mg OD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease

monitoring: Lipid panel at baseline, 4-12 weeks, then annually
LFTs at baseline and as clinically indicated
HbA1c in those at risk for diabetes
atorvastatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Can be taken any time of day
No renal adjustment needed
CYP3A4 substrate
High-intensity: 40-80 mg

amlodipine.mechanism: Inhibits calcium ion influx through L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle and myocardium, causing vasodilation and reduced peripheral resistance.

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg OD → max 10 mg OD
dosing.pediatric: 6-17 years: 2.5-5 mg OD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Start 2.5 mg OD in hepatic impairment

amlodipine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Limited data on breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Long half-life (30-50h)
No renal adjustment
Ankle edema common
Once daily dosing

losartan.mechanism: Selectively blocks angiotensin II AT1 receptors, preventing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. Does not inhibit ACE or affect bradykinin.
contraindications: Concomitant use with aliskiren in diabetic patients

dosing.adult: Start 50 mg OD → max 100 mg OD. Start 25 mg if volume-depleted.
dosing.pediatric: 6-16 years: 0.7 mg/kg OD (max 50 mg)
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed; caution if eGFR <30
dosing.hepatic: Start 25 mg OD in hepatic impairment

monitoring: Serum potassium at baseline, 1 week, then periodically
losartan.pregnancy_lactation: Category D (2nd/3rd trimester). Contraindicated. Fetotoxic — oligohydramnios, renal failure, skull hypoplasia.
quick_flags: No dry cough (unlike ACEi)
Uricosuric effect
Watch for hyperkalemia
Teratogenic

aspirin.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits COX-1, preventing thromboxane A2 synthesis and platelet aggregation. At higher doses, also inhibits COX-2 (anti-inflammatory).
contraindications: Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer
Children <16 years with viral illness (Reye syndrome)

dosing.adult: Antiplatelet: 75-150 mg OD. ACS loading: 300-325 mg. Analgesic: 325-650 mg q4-6h (max 4g/day)
dosing.pediatric: Kawasaki: 80-100 mg/kg/day div q6h (acute), then 3-5 mg/kg/day
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <10. Use with caution in CKD.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Bleeding time not useful (use clinical assessment)
aspirin.pregnancy_lactation: Low-dose (75-150 mg) considered safe in 1st/2nd trimester. Avoid in 3rd trimester (premature ductus closure). Compatible with breastfeeding at low doses.
quick_flags: Irreversible platelet inhibition (7-10 days)
Low-dose = antiplatelet
GI protection with PPI if risk factors
Hold 7 days before surgery

clopidogrel.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits ADP binding to P2Y12 receptor on platelets, preventing GPIIb/IIIa activation and platelet aggregation. Prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation.

dosing.adult: Loading: 300-600 mg. Maintenance: 75 mg OD. DAPT duration: 6-12 months post-stent.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data. Not routinely recommended.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in moderate impairment; avoid in severe

monitoring: Consider CYP2C19 genotyping in PCI patients
Platelet function testing if poor response suspected
clopidogrel.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Limited human data. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Prodrug — needs CYP2C19
Poor metabolizers: consider prasugrel/ticagrelor
Hold 5 days before surgery
DAPT with aspirin post-stent

warfarin.mechanism: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), preventing gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C and S.

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg OD (2-3 mg in elderly/low weight). Adjust by INR. Target INR 2-3 (most indications), 2.5-3.5 (mechanical valves).
dosing.pediatric: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg OD. Adjust by INR.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; increased bleeding risk in CKD
dosing.hepatic: Increased sensitivity; use lower doses

monitoring: INR regularly — weekly initially, then monthly when stable
warfarin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X (1st trimester). Crosses placenta — warfarin embryopathy. May be used in 2nd trimester for mechanical valves. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Narrow therapeutic index
Many drug-drug and drug-food interactions
INR monitoring essential
Reversal: Vitamin K, FFP, PCC

pantoprazole.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion.

dosing.adult: GERD: 40 mg OD × 4-8 weeks. Maintenance: 20 mg OD. Zollinger-Ellison: 40-80 mg BD. IV: 40-80 mg q12h.
dosing.pediatric: ≥5 years: 20-40 mg OD based on weight
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Max 20 mg/day in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Consider H. pylori testing before long-term use
pantoprazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take 30 min before breakfast
Avoid long-term use without indication
Drug interactions: clopidogrel (prefer pantoprazole over omeprazole)
Review need every 6-12 months

amoxicillin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs). Bactericidal. Broader gram-negative coverage than penicillin.
indications: Upper and lower respiratory tract infections

dosing.adult: 250-500 mg TDS or 500-875 mg BD. High-dose: 1g TDS (pneumonia).
dosing.pediatric: 25-50 mg/kg/day div TDS. High-dose otitis: 80-90 mg/kg/day div BD.
dosing.renal: eGFR 10-30: dose q12h; eGFR <10: dose q24h
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment. Monitor for cholestatic jaundice.

amoxicillin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First-line for many community infections
Give with clavulanate for beta-lactamase producers
Mononucleosis → rash risk
Good oral bioavailability

azithromycin.mechanism: Binds to 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic (bactericidal at high concentrations). Excellent tissue penetration.
contraindications: History of cholestatic jaundice with azithromycin
Concurrent use with pimozide or ergot alkaloids

dosing.adult: 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg OD days 2-5. Or 500 mg OD × 3 days. STI: 1g single dose.
dosing.pediatric: 10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2-5. Otitis: 30 mg/kg single dose.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic disease

monitoring: ECG if cardiac risk factors (QT prolongation)
azithromycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally safe. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Long tissue half-life (68h)
QT prolongation risk
Few drug interactions vs other macrolides
3-day or 5-day course

ciprofloxacin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication and transcription. Bactericidal.
contraindications: History of tendon disorders with fluoroquinolones

dosing.pediatric: 10-20 mg/kg/day div BD (max 750 mg/dose). Use only when no alternatives.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment unless combined hepatorenal dysfunction

ciprofloxacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in pregnancy (cartilage damage in animal studies). Avoid breastfeeding or pump and dump.
quick_flags: FDA black box: tendinitis, neuropathy, CNS effects
Avoid in elderly on corticosteroids
Chelation with Ca/Mg/Fe/Al (take 2h apart)
Reserve for appropriate indications

metronidazole.mechanism: Reduced intracellularly to cytotoxic intermediates that damage DNA. Active against anaerobes and protozoa. Bactericidal.

dosing.adult: 400 mg TDS × 7-10 days (anaerobes). Amoebiasis: 800 mg TDS × 5-10 days. C. diff: 500 mg TDS × 10-14 days. IV: 500 mg q8h.
dosing.pediatric: 7.5 mg/kg TDS (max 400 mg/dose)
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate. Hemodialysis: supplement after dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50% in severe hepatic impairment

metronidazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category B (but avoid 1st trimester if possible). Single-dose for trichomoniasis acceptable. Pump and discard for 12-24h after dose.
quick_flags: NO alcohol (disulfiram reaction)
Cover anaerobes
Peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use
Interacts with warfarin (↑INR)

prednisolone.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription. Anti-inflammatory: suppresses NF-κB, reduces cytokines, inhibits phospholipase A2. Immunosuppressive at higher doses.

dosing.adult: Varies widely by condition. Asthma exacerbation: 40-60 mg/day × 5-7 days. Tapering needed if >2 weeks use.
dosing.pediatric: Asthma: 1-2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) × 3-5 days. Nephrotic: 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Prednisolone preferred over prednisone (doesn't require hepatic activation)

prednisolone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Cross-placental transfer is minimal (vs dexamethasone). Compatible with breastfeeding at low doses.
quick_flags: Taper if >2 weeks use
Morning dosing mimics physiologic cortisol
PPI/calcium/vitamin D for prolonged use
Adrenal crisis risk if abrupt withdrawal

salbutamol.mechanism: Selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle by increasing intracellular cyclic AMP, leading to rapid bronchodilation.

dosing.adult: MDI: 1-2 puffs every 4-6 hours PRN; Nebulisation: 2.5-5 mg every 20 min x3 for acute attack, then every 1-4 hours; Oral: 2-4 mg TDS
dosing.pediatric: MDI with spacer: 2 puffs every 4-6 hours PRN; Nebulisation: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) every 20 min x3; Syrup: 0.1 mg/kg TDS
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

salbutamol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Inhaled salbutamol is considered safe in pregnancy for asthma management. Can be used as tocolytic in preterm labor (IV). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First-line rescue inhaler for acute asthma — every patient with asthma should have one
GINA 2023: SABA-only treatment no longer recommended as preferred; use ICS-formoterol as needed where possible
Frequent SABA use (>2 canisters/year) indicates poor asthma control
Nebulised salbutamol useful as adjunct for acute hyperkalemia

insulin_glargine.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin analogue with two extra arginine residues at B-chain C-terminus and asparagine replaced by glycine at A21. Forms microprecipitates at physiological pH after SC injection, providing slow, peakless absorption over approximately 24 hours.
indications: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (basal component of basal-bolus regimen)
Type 2 diabetes mellitus (when oral agents fail to achieve glycaemic targets)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to insulin glargine or excipients

dosing.adult: T2DM: Start 10 units (or 0.1-0.2 units/kg) OD at bedtime; titrate by 2 units every 3 days to fasting glucose target; T1DM: individualized as part of basal-bolus regimen (typically 40-50% of total daily dose as basal)
dosing.pediatric: T1DM >=6 years: individualized; T2DM >=10 years: as per adult guidelines
dosing.renal: Insulin clearance reduced — may need dose reduction; increased hypoglycaemia risk
dosing.hepatic: Gluconeogenesis reduced and insulin clearance impaired — reduce dose; monitor closely

monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months (target <7% for most adults)
insulin_glargine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C, but insulin is the preferred treatment for diabetes in pregnancy. NPH insulin has more historical data, but glargine is increasingly used. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Must NOT be mixed with other insulins in the same syringe
Inject at the same time every day — usually bedtime or morning
Basalog (biosimilar) is significantly cheaper than Lantus in India
Peakless profile reduces nocturnal hypoglycaemia compared to NPH
Rotate injection sites to prevent lipodystrophy

furosemide.mechanism: Inhibits Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter (NKCC2) in the thick ascending limb of loop of Henle. Produces potent diuresis with sodium, potassium, and chloride wasting.
indications: Heart failure (acute and chronic — fluid overload)
Acute kidney injury (to maintain urine output)

dosing.adult: Oral: 20-80 mg OD-BD. IV: 20-40 mg bolus, may double q2h. Heart failure: up to 600 mg/day. IV infusion: 5-40 mg/hr.
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 mg/kg/dose q6-12h (max 6 mg/kg/day)
dosing.renal: Higher doses often needed in CKD. Avoid in anuria.
dosing.hepatic: Use with spironolactone in cirrhotic ascites (start low)

furosemide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. May suppress lactation.
quick_flags: Potent diuretic — monitor electrolytes
IV:PO conversion = 1:2 (IV is 2× more potent)
Ototoxic at high IV doses
Give morning dose to avoid nocturia

spironolactone.mechanism: Competitive antagonist of aldosterone at mineralocorticoid receptors in distal convoluted tubule. Promotes sodium excretion while retaining potassium. Also has anti-androgenic effects.
indications: Heart failure with reduced EF (HFrEF) — mortality benefit

dosing.adult: Heart failure: 12.5-25 mg OD (max 50 mg). Ascites: 100-400 mg/day with furosemide. HTN: 25-50 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: 1-3 mg/kg/day in 1-2 divided doses
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30. Use lower doses if eGFR 30-50.
dosing.hepatic: Start low in cirrhosis; commonly used for ascites

monitoring: Serum potassium — baseline, 1 week, 1 month, then q3 months
spironolactone.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Anti-androgenic effects — risk of feminization of male fetus. Avoid in pregnancy.
quick_flags: RALES trial: 30% mortality reduction in HFrEF
Hyperkalemia risk — especially with ACEi/ARB
Gynecomastia — consider eplerenone
Takes 2-3 days for full diuretic effect

paracetamol.mechanism: Inhibits COX in CNS (central analgesic/antipyretic). Weak peripheral anti-inflammatory activity. Also modulates endocannabinoid and serotonergic pathways.

dosing.adult: 500-1000 mg q4-6h (max 4 g/day). Elderly/low weight: max 2-3 g/day.
dosing.pediatric: 10-15 mg/kg q4-6h (max 60 mg/kg/day or 2 g/day whichever is less)
dosing.renal: Extend interval to q6-8h if eGFR <30
dosing.hepatic: Max 2 g/day in chronic liver disease. Avoid if severe.

monitoring: Total daily dose across all formulations (combination products!)
paracetamol.pregnancy_lactation: Generally considered safest analgesic/antipyretic in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: OTC — watch for accidental overdose from combination products
N-acetylcysteine is antidote for overdose
No anti-inflammatory effect
Safe in most patients when dosed correctly

ibuprofen.mechanism: Non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor. Reduces prostaglandin synthesis → anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects.
contraindications: Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer

dosing.adult: 200-400 mg q6-8h (max 1200 mg/day OTC; 3200 mg/day Rx). Take with food.
dosing.pediatric: 5-10 mg/kg q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day). ≥6 months old.
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30. Use lowest dose for shortest duration if eGFR 30-60.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

ibuprofen.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (1st/2nd trimester), D (3rd trimester). Avoid after 30 weeks (premature ductus closure). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with food
Shortest duration at lowest effective dose
CV risk — all NSAIDs
Avoid with anticoagulants if possible

omeprazole.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+-ATPase proton pump in gastric parietal cells.

dosing.adult: 20-40 mg OD before breakfast. H. pylori: 20 mg BD with antibiotics × 14 days.
dosing.pediatric: 1-16 years: 0.7-3.3 mg/kg/day (max 20 mg)
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Max 20 mg/day in severe hepatic impairment

omeprazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Generally considered safe. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: CYP2C19 substrate — interacts with clopidogrel
Prefer pantoprazole if on clopidogrel
Take 30 min before meals
Deprescribe when possible

lisinopril.mechanism: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme, preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Reduces aldosterone, decreases peripheral resistance, and increases bradykinin.

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 5-10 mg OD, max 40 mg. HF: Start 2.5-5 mg OD, target 20-40 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: ≥6 years: 0.07 mg/kg OD (max 5 mg), titrate to max 0.6 mg/kg or 40 mg
dosing.renal: eGFR 10-30: start 2.5-5 mg; eGFR <10: start 2.5 mg
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Potassium and creatinine at baseline, 1-2 weeks, then periodically
lisinopril.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated — fetotoxic (oligohydramnios, renal failure, skull defects).
quick_flags: Dry cough → switch to ARB
Angioedema risk — higher in Black patients
Don't combine with ARB routinely
Teratogenic

carvedilol.drug_class: Non-Selective Beta-Blocker with Alpha-1 Blocking Activity
carvedilol.mechanism: Non-selective beta-adrenergic blocker (β1 and β2) with alpha-1 adrenergic blocking activity. Reduces heart rate, contractility, and peripheral resistance. Antioxidant properties.
indications: Heart failure with reduced EF (HFrEF) — mortality benefit
contraindications: Decompensated heart failure requiring IV inotropes

dosing.adult: HF: Start 3.125 mg BD with food → double every 2 weeks → target 25 mg BD. HTN: Start 6.25 mg BD → max 25 mg BD.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data. 0.1 mg/kg BD → max 0.5 mg/kg BD.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

carvedilol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Beta-blockers may cause fetal bradycardia and hypoglycemia.
quick_flags: Take with food (slows absorption, reduces orthostasis)
Start low, go slow in HF
Mortality benefit in HFrEF
Masks hypoglycemia symptoms

ondansetron.mechanism: Selectively blocks serotonin 5-HT3 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) and vagal afferents in the GI tract, preventing nausea and vomiting.

dosing.adult: CINV: 8 mg IV before chemo, then 8 mg PO BID for 1-2 days. PONV: 4 mg IV at induction. Gastroenteritis: 4-8 mg PO TID PRN.
dosing.pediatric: CINV: 0.15 mg/kg IV (max 16 mg). PONV: 0.1 mg/kg IV. Gastroenteritis: 2-4 years: 2 mg; 4-12 years: 4 mg.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Severe hepatic impairment: max 8 mg/day.

monitoring: ECG in patients with QT risk or receiving high doses (>16 mg)
Serotonin syndrome signs if co-prescribed with SSRIs/SNRIs
ondansetron.pregnancy_lactation: Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Widely used for hyperemesis gravidarum. Some data suggest small risk of cleft palate with first-trimester use. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Max single IV dose 16 mg (higher doses increase QT prolongation risk)
Constipation is very common — anticipate bowel care in chemo patients
ODT formulation useful when patient is actively vomiting

doxycycline.mechanism: Binds to 30S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic. Also has anti-inflammatory and anti-matrix metalloproteinase properties.

dosing.adult: 100 mg BD day 1, then 100 mg OD-BD. Severe infections: 100 mg BD throughout.
dosing.pediatric: ≥8 years: 2-4 mg/kg/day div BD (max 200 mg/day). Single dose for tick bite Lyme prophylaxis in ≥8 years.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment (not renally excreted)
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic disease

adverse_effects: GI upset (take with food, not dairy)
monitoring: CBC and LFTs if prolonged use
doxycycline.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated in 2nd/3rd trimester (tooth/bone effects). Avoid in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with water, remain upright 30 min
Photosensitivity — sunscreen essential
No renal adjustment needed
Avoid dairy/antacids within 2h

levofloxacin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV. Bactericidal. L-isomer of ofloxacin with enhanced activity.
contraindications: History of tendon disorders with fluoroquinolones

dosing.adult: 250-750 mg OD. CAP: 500 mg OD × 7-14 days or 750 mg OD × 5 days.
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely recommended in <18 years
dosing.renal: eGFR 20-49: 250-500 mg initially, then 250 mg q24-48h; eGFR <20: 250-500 mg initially, then 250 mg q48h
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment

levofloxacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in pregnancy. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: FDA black box warnings
QT prolongation risk
Renal dose adjustment needed
Reserve for appropriate indications

ceftriaxone.mechanism: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting cell wall synthesis. Bactericidal. Broad gram-negative coverage plus some gram-positive activity.

dosing.adult: 1-2 g IV/IM OD. Meningitis: 2 g q12h. Gonorrhea: 500 mg IM single dose.
dosing.pediatric: 50-100 mg/kg/day (max 4 g). Meningitis: 100 mg/kg/day div q12h.
dosing.renal: No adjustment unless combined hepatorenal dysfunction
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose if combined hepatorenal failure

ceftriaxone.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Once-daily dosing (long half-life)
Do NOT mix with calcium in neonates
Biliary sludging — reversible on stopping
IM with lidocaine to reduce pain

enoxaparin.mechanism: Binds to antithrombin III, potentiating its inhibition of Factor Xa (primary) and thrombin (Factor IIa). Anti-Xa to anti-IIa ratio ~3.8:1.
contraindications: Epidural catheter in situ (risk of spinal hematoma)

dosing.adult: VTE prophylaxis: 40 mg SC OD. DVT/PE treatment: 1 mg/kg SC BD or 1.5 mg/kg SC OD. ACS: 1 mg/kg SC BD.
dosing.pediatric: Treatment: 1 mg/kg SC BD (<2 months: 1.5 mg/kg BD)
dosing.renal: eGFR <30: reduce to 1 mg/kg OD (treatment) or 20 mg OD (prophylaxis)
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Anti-Xa levels in renal impairment, obesity, pregnancy
Platelet count (baseline and day 4 for HIT)
enoxaparin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Does not cross placenta — preferred anticoagulant in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: SC injection only — do not give IV (except in ACS)
Cannot be reversed with protamine fully (60-80%)
Dose-adjust in renal impairment
No routine monitoring needed (vs UFH)

levothyroxine.mechanism: Synthetic T4 (thyroxine). Converted to active T3 in peripheral tissues. Restores normal thyroid hormone levels and metabolic function.

dosing.adult: Start 1.6 mcg/kg/day (full replacement). Elderly/cardiac: start 12.5-25 mcg OD, increase by 12.5-25 mcg every 4-6 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Neonates: 10-15 mcg/kg/day. Children: 2-5 mcg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment

adverse_effects: Symptoms of hyperthyroidism if over-replaced (palpitations, tremor, weight loss, insomnia)
monitoring: TSH every 6-8 weeks until stable, then every 6-12 months
levothyroxine.pregnancy_lactation: Safe and essential in pregnancy. Dose often needs 25-50% increase. Monitor TSH every 4 weeks in 1st half. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take on empty stomach 30-60 min before breakfast
Separate from calcium, iron, PPIs by 4 hours
Narrow therapeutic index
TSH takes 6-8 weeks to reflect dose change

metoprolol.mechanism: Selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker. Reduces heart rate, contractility, and AV conduction. At higher doses, selectivity is lost.

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 25-50 mg BD (tartrate) or 25-50 mg OD (succinate XL). Max 200-400 mg/day. HF: succinate XL 12.5-25 mg OD → target 200 mg.
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses (max 6 mg/kg/day)
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (extensive first-pass metabolism)

metoprolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May be used in pregnancy for specific indications. Risk of fetal bradycardia/IUGR. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Tartrate = BD dosing; Succinate XL = OD dosing
Only succinate XL has HF mortality data
Do not stop abruptly — taper over 1-2 weeks
Cardioselective at low doses

telmisartan.mechanism: Selectively blocks angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptors, preventing angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. Also has partial PPAR-gamma agonist activity (unique among ARBs).
indications: Cardiovascular risk reduction in high-risk patients (>=55 years)
contraindications: Concurrent use with aliskiren in diabetic patients

dosing.adult: Hypertension: Start 20-40 mg once daily. May increase to 80 mg once daily. CV risk reduction: 80 mg once daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended for children <18 years.
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate impairment. Not removed by dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: Do not exceed 40 mg/day. Severe: Contraindicated.

telmisartan.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in all trimesters of pregnancy (Category D). Discontinue immediately if pregnancy detected. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Longest half-life among ARBs (~24 hours) — true 24-hour BP control
Unique partial PPAR-gamma agonism may provide metabolic benefits (insulin sensitivity)
ONTARGET trial: equivalent to ramipril for CV protection with better tolerability
Can be taken with or without food

hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Inhibits Na+/Cl- cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule. Reduces intravascular volume and peripheral resistance.

dosing.adult: 12.5-25 mg OD (max antihypertensive benefit at 25 mg; higher doses add side effects without much BP benefit)
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 mg/kg/day in 1-2 doses
dosing.renal: Generally ineffective if eGFR <30. Use loop diuretic instead.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution — electrolyte changes may precipitate hepatic encephalopathy

hydrochlorothiazide.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally avoided in pregnancy (volume depletion). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Low-dose (12.5-25 mg) is optimal for HTN
Often combined with ACEi/ARB
Ineffective when eGFR <30
Causes hypercalcemia (unlike loop diuretics)

ramipril.mechanism: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), reducing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. This decreases vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activity. Also inhibits bradykinin degradation (contributing to vasodilation and cough).
indications: Cardiovascular risk reduction in high-risk patients
contraindications: History of angioedema with ACE inhibitor
Concurrent use with sacubitril/valsartan (36-hour washout needed)

dosing.adult: Hypertension: Start 2.5 mg once daily; titrate to 10 mg/day. Heart failure: Start 1.25-2.5 mg once daily; target 10 mg/day. CV risk reduction: 10 mg once daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely recommended; limited pediatric data.
dosing.renal: CrCl <40 mL/min: Start 1.25 mg/day; max 5 mg/day.
dosing.hepatic: Start at lower dose. Max 2.5 mg/day in severe impairment.

monitoring: Renal function (creatinine at baseline, 1-2 weeks, then periodically)
WBC count if on concurrent immunosuppressants
ramipril.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (Category D — fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios). Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: HOPE trial landmark: 22% reduction in composite CV endpoint in high-risk patients
Dry cough (bradykinin-mediated) — switch to ARB if intolerable
Monitor potassium especially when combined with K-sparing diuretics or in CKD
Withhold before surgery/anesthesia to avoid intraoperative hypotension

rosuvastatin.mechanism: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase. Most potent statin — greatest LDL reduction per mg. Minimal CYP3A4 metabolism.

dosing.adult: Start 5-10 mg OD. High-intensity: 20-40 mg. Asian patients: start 5 mg.
dosing.pediatric: 8-17 years (hetFH): 5-20 mg OD
dosing.renal: eGFR <30: start 5 mg, max 10 mg
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease

quick_flags: Most potent statin
Minimal CYP3A4 — fewer drug interactions
Renal adjustment needed
Asian patients: lower starting dose

glimepiride.mechanism: Stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells by binding to SUR1 subunit of K-ATP channels. Also has mild extrapancreatic insulin-sensitizing effects.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (as monotherapy or in combination with metformin, pioglitazone, or insulin)

dosing.adult: Start 1 mg OD with breakfast; titrate by 1 mg every 1-2 weeks; usual maintenance 1-4 mg OD; max 8 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Start 1 mg OD; avoid if GFR <30 ml/min due to hypoglycaemia risk
dosing.hepatic: Start with lowest dose; avoid in severe hepatic impairment

glimepiride.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in pregnancy — switch to insulin. Excreted in breast milk — not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Preferred sulfonylurea due to lower hypoglycaemia risk compared to glibenclamide
Always take with breakfast — skipping meals increases hypoglycaemia risk
Very commonly combined with metformin in India (Amaryl M, Glimisave M)
Educate patients to carry glucose tablets or sugar for hypoglycaemia episodes

digoxin.mechanism: Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase, increasing intracellular calcium → positive inotrope. Vagotonic effects slow AV conduction. Neurohormonal modulation reduces sympathetic tone.
indications: Heart failure (HFrEF — symptomatic benefit, no mortality benefit)

dosing.adult: 0.125-0.25 mg OD. Target level: 0.5-0.9 ng/mL. Elderly/renal impairment: 0.0625-0.125 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: Highly weight-based. Consult pediatric dosing tables.
dosing.renal: Reduce dose significantly — primarily renally excreted. eGFR <50: 0.125 mg OD or alternate days.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

adverse_effects: Arrhythmias (any type — classic: bidirectional VT, PAT with block)
digoxin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Can be used in pregnancy for maternal indications. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Narrow therapeutic index
Toxicity potentiated by hypokalemia
Many drug interactions
Digibind for severe toxicity

empagliflozin.mechanism: Selectively inhibits SGLT2 in the proximal renal tubule, reducing glucose reabsorption and promoting urinary glucose excretion. Also causes osmotic diuresis and natriuresis.
indications: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) — with or without diabetes
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <20 ml/min for glycaemic indication)
History of serious hypersensitivity to empagliflozin

dosing.adult: Diabetes: Start 10 mg OD, may increase to 25 mg OD; Heart failure/CKD: 10 mg OD
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: eGFR 20-45: 10 mg OD (primarily for cardiorenal benefit, limited glycaemic effect); eGFR <20: do not initiate; may continue if already on treatment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

adverse_effects: Acute kidney injury (due to volume depletion)
monitoring: Renal function (eGFR at baseline and periodically)
Signs of ketoacidosis (even with normal glucose)
empagliflozin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Not recommended during second and third trimesters (fetal renal development concern). Not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: EMPA-REG OUTCOME: 38% reduction in cardiovascular death in T2DM with established CVD
EMPEROR trials: proven benefit in heart failure regardless of diabetes status
Counsel patients about euglycaemic DKA — can present with normal blood sugars
Hold before major surgery and during acute illness to prevent DKA
Causes genital infections — advise good perineal hygiene

sitagliptin.mechanism: Inhibits DPP-4 enzyme, preventing degradation of incretin hormones (GLP-1 and GIP). This enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppresses glucagon release.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (monotherapy or combination with metformin, sulfonylureas, pioglitazone, or insulin)

dosing.adult: 100 mg OD irrespective of meals
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years
dosing.renal: GFR 30-45: 50 mg OD; GFR <30 or dialysis: 25 mg OD
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for mild-moderate; no data for severe — use with caution

adverse_effects: Hypoglycaemia (when combined with SU or insulin)
sitagliptin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. No adequate human studies. Use only if clearly needed. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Weight-neutral antidiabetic — does not cause weight gain
Very low risk of hypoglycaemia when used as monotherapy
Dose must be adjusted for renal impairment — commonly missed
Janumet (sitagliptin + metformin) is a very popular fixed-dose combination in India

gabapentin.mechanism: Binds to alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. Does not bind to GABA receptors despite the name.

dosing.adult: Neuropathic pain: Start 300 mg day 1 → 300 mg BD day 2 → 300 mg TDS day 3 → max 3600 mg/day. Epilepsy: 300-600 mg TDS.
dosing.pediatric: ≥3 years epilepsy: 10-15 mg/kg/day, titrate to 25-35 mg/kg/day div TDS
dosing.renal: eGFR 30-59: 200-700 mg BD; eGFR 15-29: 100-300 mg OD; eGFR <15: 100-300 mg alternate days
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (not hepatically metabolized)

monitoring: Suicidal ideation (FDA warning for all anticonvulsants)
Respiratory status if co-prescribed with opioids
gabapentin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: Renal dosing is critical
Taper to discontinue — do not stop abruptly
CNS depression with opioids (FDA warning)
Not a GABA agonist despite the name

clonazepam.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity by binding to benzodiazepine site, increasing chloride channel opening frequency. Anticonvulsant, anxiolytic, muscle relaxant.

dosing.adult: Panic: Start 0.25 mg BD → max 4 mg/day. Seizures: Start 0.5 mg TDS → max 20 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: 0.01-0.03 mg/kg/day div BD-TDS (max 0.05 mg/kg/day)
dosing.renal: Use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; avoid in severe

clonazepam.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Risk of neonatal withdrawal, floppy infant syndrome. Avoid if possible. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Schedule IV controlled substance
Dependence risk — shortest effective duration
Never stop abruptly — taper slowly
Avoid with opioids/alcohol

montelukast.mechanism: Selective antagonist of cysteinyl leukotriene receptor CysLT1, blocking leukotriene D4-mediated bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion, vascular permeability, and eosinophil recruitment.

dosing.adult: 10 mg OD at bedtime
dosing.pediatric: 6 months-5 years: 4 mg OD; 6-14 years: 5 mg OD; >=15 years: 10 mg OD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for mild-moderate impairment; no data for severe

adverse_effects: Neuropsychiatric events (agitation, depression, suicidal ideation — boxed warning)
monitoring: Neuropsychiatric symptoms (especially in children and adolescents)
montelukast.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data but animal studies show no harm. May be continued during pregnancy if asthma control requires it. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: US FDA boxed warning for neuropsychiatric events — counsel patients about mood changes, sleep disturbances, suicidal thoughts
Not a replacement for inhaled corticosteroids
Particularly effective in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)
Very commonly prescribed in India, often in combination with levocetirizine for allergic rhinitis

cetirizine.mechanism: Selective peripheral H1 histamine receptor antagonist. Active carboxylic acid metabolite of hydroxyzine. Inhibits histamine-mediated early-phase allergic reaction with minimal CNS penetration compared to first-generation antihistamines.
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <10 mL/min) without dose adjustment
Hypersensitivity to cetirizine, hydroxyzine, or piperazine derivatives

dosing.adult: 10 mg once daily. May split as 5 mg twice daily.
dosing.pediatric: 6 months-2 years: 2.5 mg once daily. 2-5 years: 2.5-5 mg once daily. >=6 years: 5-10 mg once daily.
dosing.renal: CrCl 11-31: 5 mg once daily. CrCl <11: Avoid. Hemodialysis: 5 mg once daily.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for hepatic impairment alone.

cetirizine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk; use with caution.
quick_flags: Slightly more sedating than fexofenadine and levocetirizine — may affect driving
Can be taken with or without food
Levocetirizine (R-enantiomer) is more potent at half the dose
Very commonly prescribed OTC antihistamine in India

ranitidine.mechanism: Competitively blocks histamine H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells

dosing.adult: 150 mg twice daily or 300 mg at bedtime
dosing.pediatric: 2-4 mg/kg/day in divided doses
dosing.renal: CrCl <50: 150 mg once daily
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment

quick_flags: NDMA contamination led to global recalls in 2020
Many markets have withdrawn ranitidine

forms: Tramadol 37.5 mg + Paracetamol 325 mg combination tablet
tramadol.mechanism: Weak mu-opioid receptor agonist; also inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin, providing dual analgesic mechanism.
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use (within 14 days)
Acute intoxication with alcohol, opioids, or psychotropic drugs

dosing.adult: 50-100 mg every 4-6 hours (max 400 mg/day); SR: 100-200 mg BD
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended <12 years; adolescents >12 years: 1-2 mg/kg every 4-6 hours (max 400 mg/day)
dosing.renal: CrCl <30 ml/min: increase dosing interval to 12 hours; max 200 mg/day
dosing.hepatic: Cirrhosis: 50 mg every 12 hours; avoid in severe hepatic failure

tramadol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Prolonged use in pregnancy may cause neonatal withdrawal syndrome. Excreted in breast milk — not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Lowers seizure threshold — avoid in epilepsy patients
Risk of serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs, SNRIs, or triptans
Schedule H drug in India; prescribe cautiously
Ultracet (tramadol + paracetamol) is one of the most prescribed combinations in India

phenytoin.mechanism: Stabilizes neuronal membranes by blocking voltage-gated sodium channels. Reduces repetitive neuronal firing. Zero-order kinetics at therapeutic doses.
contraindications: Sinus bradycardia, SA block, 2nd/3rd degree AV block

dosing.adult: Loading: 15-20 mg/kg IV (max rate 50 mg/min). Maintenance: 300 mg/day (4-7 mg/kg/day) OD or divided.
dosing.pediatric: Loading: 15-20 mg/kg IV. Maintenance: 5-8 mg/kg/day div BD.
dosing.renal: Free fraction increases — monitor free phenytoin levels
dosing.hepatic: Reduced metabolism — monitor levels closely

phenytoin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Fetal hydantoin syndrome. Supplement with folate. Use only if no alternative.
quick_flags: Zero-order kinetics — small dose changes cause big level changes
HLA-B*1502 screening in Southeast Asian patients
Many drug interactions (CYP inducer)
Gingival hyperplasia — dental hygiene important

amoxicillin_clavulanate.mechanism: Amoxicillin inhibits cell wall synthesis. Clavulanate irreversibly inhibits beta-lactamases, extending spectrum to beta-lactamase-producing organisms.
contraindications: History of cholestatic jaundice with amoxicillin-clavulanate

dosing.adult: 625 mg TDS or 1000 mg BD with meals. Severe: 1000 mg TDS.
dosing.pediatric: 25-45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin component) div BD-TDS. High-dose: 90 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: eGFR 10-30: 625 mg BD; eGFR <10: 625 mg OD
dosing.hepatic: Monitor LFTs; avoid if prior hepatic reaction

adverse_effects: Diarrhea (more than amoxicillin alone due to clavulanate)
amoxicillin_clavulanate.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with food to reduce GI side effects
Diarrhea is from clavulanate (dose-dependent)
Don't substitute two 375mg for one 625mg (different clavulanate ratios)
Good for bite wounds

insulin_regular.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin. Binds to insulin receptors, promoting glucose uptake into muscle/fat, inhibiting hepatic glucose production.

dosing.adult: SC: 0.1-0.2 U/kg pre-meal. DKA: 0.1 U/kg/hr IV infusion. Hyperkalemia: 10 units with 25g dextrose IV.
dosing.pediatric: DKA: 0.1 U/kg/hr IV. SC: individualized.
dosing.renal: Reduce dose — prolonged action in renal failure
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose

insulin_regular.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy. Insulin is preferred treatment for DM in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Only insulin that can be given IV
Onset: 30 min SC, immediate IV
Monitor K+ with IV insulin
Clear solution (vs NPH which is cloudy)

heparin.mechanism: Binds to antithrombin III, potentiating inhibition of thrombin (IIa), Factor Xa, and other clotting factors. Immediate onset when given IV.

dosing.adult: VTE treatment: 80 U/kg bolus → 18 U/kg/hr infusion (adjust by aPTT). Prophylaxis: 5000 U SC q8-12h.
dosing.pediatric: 75 U/kg bolus → 20 U/kg/hr (neonates: 28 U/kg/hr)
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; monitor aPTT closely
dosing.hepatic: Increased sensitivity; monitor closely

monitoring: Platelet count at baseline and q2-3 days (for HIT)
heparin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Does not cross placenta — safe in pregnancy. Does not enter breast milk.
quick_flags: Short half-life (60-90 min) — reversible with protamine
HIT risk — monitor platelets
Weight-based dosing
Only anticoagulant fully reversible

diclofenac.mechanism: Non-selective COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitor; reduces prostaglandin synthesis leading to anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic effects.
contraindications: Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer

dosing.adult: 50 mg TDS or 75 mg BD orally; SR: 100 mg OD; IM: 75 mg once daily (max 2 days)
dosing.pediatric: 1-3 mg/kg/day in divided doses (children >1 year)
dosing.renal: Avoid if GFR <30 ml/min; use lowest effective dose if GFR 30-60 ml/min
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; avoid in severe hepatic impairment

diclofenac.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (first and second trimester), Category D (third trimester). Avoid in third trimester due to risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts — use with caution.
quick_flags: IM injection should not exceed 2 days
Co-prescribe PPI for GI protection in high-risk patients
Topical formulation preferred for localized musculoskeletal pain to minimize systemic side effects

domperidone.mechanism: Peripheral dopamine D2 receptor antagonist at the CTZ and upper GI tract. Enhances gastric motility and increases LES pressure. Does not cross BBB significantly.

dosing.adult: 10 mg TDS before meals (max 30 mg/day). Limit to <7 days if possible.
dosing.pediatric: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg TDS (max 30 mg/day in >35 kg)
dosing.renal: Reduce frequency to BD in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in moderate-severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: ECG in patients >60 years or cardiac risk
domperidone.pregnancy_lactation: Limited data. Use only if clearly needed. Enters breast milk — used as galactagogue off-label.
quick_flags: Max 30 mg/day (cardiac safety)
EMA restricted to <7 days in some regions
Does not cause extrapyramidal effects (unlike metoclopramide)
QT risk at higher doses

esomeprazole.mechanism: S-isomer of omeprazole. Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+-ATPase proton pump. More predictable pharmacokinetics than omeprazole.

dosing.adult: 20-40 mg OD. Erosive esophagitis: 40 mg OD × 4-8 weeks. H. pylori: 40 mg BD with antibiotics.
dosing.pediatric: 1-11 years: weight-based (10-20 mg OD); ≥12 years: 20-40 mg OD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Max 20 mg/day in severe hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Long-term: hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, fracture risk
esomeprazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category B for first trimester. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: S-isomer of omeprazole
Take before breakfast
Less CYP2C19 variability than omeprazole
Deprescribe when possible

cefixime.mechanism: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis. 3rd gen cephalosporin with enhanced gram-negative coverage. Stable against many beta-lactamases. Poor activity against Staphylococcus and Pseudomonas.

dosing.adult: 400 mg OD or 200 mg BD × 5-14 days. Gonorrhea: 400 mg single dose (if susceptible).
dosing.pediatric: 8 mg/kg/day OD or in 2 divided doses (max 400 mg/day). 6 months-12 years: use suspension.
dosing.renal: CrCl 21-60: 75% of normal dose; CrCl ≤20 or hemodialysis: 50% of normal dose
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

cefixime.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts — compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Most prescribed oral cephalosporin in India
Poor against Staphylococci and Pseudomonas
Renal dose adjustment needed
Good for outpatient UTI and ENT infections

iron_sucrose.mechanism: Iron sucrose complex releases iron slowly after IV administration. Iron is incorporated into hemoglobin, myoglobin, and iron-containing enzymes.
indications: Iron deficiency anemia (when oral iron fails/not tolerated)
Inflammatory bowel disease with iron deficiency

dosing.adult: 200 mg IV over 30 min, repeat 2-3 times/week. Total dose = weight(kg) × (target Hb - actual Hb) × 2.4 + 500 mg.
dosing.pediatric: 0.5 mg/kg IV (limited data)
dosing.renal: Standard dosing in CKD. Given during hemodialysis is common.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution

monitoring: BP during and 30 min after infusion
iron_sucrose.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Used in 2nd/3rd trimester when oral iron fails. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: IV only — never IM
Test dose not required (unlike iron dextran)
Monitor BP during infusion
Check ferritin/TSAT before giving

methylprednisolone.mechanism: Synthetic glucocorticoid with potent anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive activity. 5× more potent than hydrocortisone. Minimal mineralocorticoid effect.

dosing.adult: Oral: 4-48 mg/day. IV pulse: 500 mg-1g/day × 3-5 days (MS relapse, lupus nephritis). Taper as appropriate.
dosing.pediatric: 0.5-1.7 mg/kg/day divided q6-12h
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Methylprednisolone preferred — doesn't need hepatic activation

methylprednisolone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use when benefits outweigh risks. Compatible with breastfeeding at usual doses.
quick_flags: IV pulse therapy for severe autoimmune conditions
No mineralocorticoid effect
Taper if >2 weeks use
5× potency of hydrocortisone

enalapril.mechanism: Prodrug converted to enalaprilat, which inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Reduces aldosterone secretion, increases bradykinin levels.
contraindications: Concomitant use with aliskiren in diabetics

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg OD → titrate to max 40 mg/day in 1-2 divided doses. HF: start 2.5 mg BD → target 10-20 mg BD.
dosing.pediatric: 1 month-16 years: 0.08 mg/kg OD → max 0.6 mg/kg/day (up to 40 mg)
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-30: start 2.5 mg OD; eGFR <30: reduce dose and monitor closely; hemodialysis: 2.5 mg on dialysis days
dosing.hepatic: Active metabolite (enalaprilat) — hepatic impairment may delay conversion but usually no dose adjustment needed

monitoring: Serum potassium at baseline, 1 week, then periodically
enalapril.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (1st trimester), Category D (2nd/3rd trimester). Contraindicated in pregnancy — fetotoxic (oligohydramnios, renal failure, skull defects). Excreted in breast milk — not recommended.
quick_flags: Prodrug (converted to enalaprilat)
Dry cough — switch to ARB if intolerant
Reduce dose in renal impairment
Monitor K+ and creatinine

nifedipine.mechanism: Blocks L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle and myometrium, causing vasodilation and uterine relaxation (tocolysis). Does not significantly affect cardiac conduction.
indications: Preterm labour (tocolytic — off-label but widely used)
contraindications: Concurrent use of IV magnesium sulfate (severe hypotension risk)

dosing.adult: Tocolysis (preterm labour): 20 mg oral initially, then 10-20 mg every 4-6 hours for 48 hours (max 160 mg/day); Hypertension: 10 mg TDS (IR) or 20-30 mg OD-BD (SR); Acute hypertension in pregnancy: 10 mg oral, repeat after 30 min if needed
dosing.pediatric: Hypertension: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/dose TDS; not used as tocolytic in children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required; not significantly removed by dialysis
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment — increased bioavailability; start with lower doses

monitoring: Blood pressure (closely during tocolysis and acute hypertension)
Signs of pulmonary oedema (especially if combined with MgSO4)
nifedipine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Widely used in pregnancy for both tocolysis and hypertension with good safety profile. Compatible with breastfeeding — excreted in breast milk in small amounts.
quick_flags: Do NOT use sublingual nifedipine for hypertensive emergencies — risk of precipitous BP drop and stroke
Avoid concurrent IV magnesium sulfate and nifedipine — severe hypotension and neuromuscular blockade
Preferred tocolytic agent in many guidelines (RCOG, NICE) over beta-agonists
RCOG recommends oral nifedipine as first-line antihypertensive in acute severe hypertension of pregnancy

cilnidipine.mechanism: Blocks both L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle (causing vasodilation) and N-type calcium channels in sympathetic nerve terminals (suppressing norepinephrine release), producing antihypertensive effect with less reflex tachycardia.

dosing.adult: Start 5-10 mg once daily. May increase to 20 mg once daily. Take after breakfast.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established.
dosing.renal: Use with caution; no specific dose adjustment available. Provides renoprotective effects by reducing proteinuria.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment; start with lower dose.

monitoring: Renal function and proteinuria if applicable
cilnidipine.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy. Avoid during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Dual L/N-type blockade causes less reflex tachycardia and less pedal edema than amlodipine
Renoprotective — reduces proteinuria via N-type channel blockade in renal sympathetic nerves
Very popular in Indian antihypertensive practice
Once-daily dosing improves compliance

atenolol.mechanism: Selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker. Reduces heart rate, myocardial contractility, and cardiac output. Less lipophilic than metoprolol — minimal CNS penetration.

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 25-50 mg OD → max 100 mg OD. Angina: 50-100 mg OD. Post-MI: 50-100 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 mg/kg/day OD (max 2 mg/kg/day)
dosing.renal: CrCl 15-35: max 50 mg/day; CrCl <15: max 25 mg/day; hemodialysis: 25-50 mg after each session
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (renally eliminated)

atenolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Associated with fetal growth restriction. Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Less lipophilic — fewer CNS side effects
Renal elimination — adjust in CKD
Do NOT stop abruptly (rebound tachycardia)
Less preferred for HF compared to carvedilol/bisoprolol

bisoprolol.mechanism: Highly selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker with no intrinsic sympathomimetic activity. Most cardioselective beta-blocker available. Reduces heart rate, contractility, and renin release.
indications: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF)
contraindications: Second or third-degree AV block (without pacemaker)

dosing.adult: HTN: 5-10 mg OD (max 20 mg). HF: start 1.25 mg OD → double every 2-4 weeks → target 10 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not well established; limited data
dosing.renal: CrCl <20: max 10 mg/day. Generally well tolerated in CKD.
dosing.hepatic: Severe impairment: max 10 mg/day; start low

monitoring: Signs of HF decompensation during uptitration
bisoprolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. May cause fetal bradycardia. Limited data on breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Most cardioselective beta-blocker
CIBIS-II trial: mortality benefit in HFrEF
Uptitrate slowly in HF
Do NOT stop abruptly

nebivolol.drug_class: Beta-1 Selective Adrenergic Blocker with Nitric Oxide-mediated Vasodilation
nebivolol.mechanism: Highly selective beta-1 blocker at low doses. Additionally stimulates endothelial nitric oxide (NO) synthase, causing vasodilation. d-nebivolol is the beta-blocker; l-nebivolol releases NO.
indications: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (off-label in some regions)
Hypertension in patients with metabolic syndrome (favorable metabolic profile)

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 5 mg OD → max 40 mg OD. HF (off-label): start 1.25 mg OD → uptitrate slowly.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established in children
dosing.renal: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <30): start 2.5 mg OD; titrate cautiously
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment: start 2.5 mg OD; contraindicated in severe impairment

monitoring: CYP2D6 status (extensive vs. poor metabolizers affect drug levels)
nebivolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. May cause fetal bradycardia. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Vasodilating beta-blocker (via NO release)
Fewer metabolic side effects (no worsening lipids/glucose)
Less sexual dysfunction than other BBs
CYP2D6 polymorphism affects levels

prazosin.mechanism: Selectively blocks alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation and reducing peripheral vascular resistance.
contraindications: Concurrent use with PDE-5 inhibitors (risk of severe hypotension)

dosing.adult: Hypertension: Start 0.5 mg at bedtime, titrate to 2-20 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses. BPH: 0.5-2 mg twice daily. PTSD nightmares: 1-15 mg at bedtime.
dosing.pediatric: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses; max 0.5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment required; use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment; start at lower doses.

prazosin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts; use with caution during lactation.
quick_flags: Give first dose at bedtime to minimize first-dose syncope
Warn patients about orthostatic hypotension — rise slowly from sitting/lying
Off-label use for PTSD nightmares is well-supported by evidence

clonidine.mechanism: Stimulates central alpha-2 adrenergic receptors in the brainstem (nucleus tractus solitarii), reducing sympathetic outflow from the CNS. Decreases heart rate, peripheral vascular resistance, and renin release.
contraindications: Concurrent use of tricyclic antidepressants (reduces efficacy)

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 0.1 mg BD → titrate by 0.1 mg/week → max 0.6 mg/day. Hypertensive urgency: 0.1-0.2 mg oral, repeat hourly (max 0.6 mg). Patch: apply weekly.
dosing.pediatric: ADHD (≥6 years): start 0.1 mg at bedtime → max 0.4 mg/day. 5-10 mcg/kg/day in divided doses for HTN.
dosing.renal: Start lower doses; titrate cautiously. Not significantly dialyzed.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; hepatic metabolism

monitoring: Blood pressure (especially when stopping — taper over 1 week!)
clonidine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Crosses placenta. Excreted in breast milk — caution advised.
quick_flags: NEVER stop abruptly — rebound HTN crisis!
Taper over 2-4 days minimum
Useful for opioid withdrawal
Dry mouth is most common side effect

chlorthalidone.mechanism: Inhibits sodium-chloride cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule. Longer-acting and more potent than hydrochlorothiazide. Also acts on the proximal tubule (carbonic anhydrase inhibition), contributing to its greater efficacy.

dosing.adult: HTN: 12.5-25 mg OD (max 50 mg/day, but 25 mg usually sufficient). Start 6.25 mg in elderly.
dosing.pediatric: 2 mg/kg 3 times per week or 1 mg/kg OD
dosing.renal: Less effective if eGFR <30; avoid if eGFR <10. Not removed by dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution — electrolyte disturbances may precipitate hepatic encephalopathy

monitoring: Serum electrolytes (K+, Na+, Ca2+) at baseline, 2-4 weeks, then periodically
chlorthalidone.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally avoided in pregnancy (can reduce placental perfusion). Excreted in breast milk — may suppress lactation.
quick_flags: More potent and longer-acting than HCTZ
ALLHAT trial: excellent CV outcomes
Monitor potassium — hypokalemia common
Can worsen gout

torsemide.mechanism: Inhibits Na+/K+/2Cl- cotransporter (NKCC2) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle. Longer acting and more predictable bioavailability (80-100%) than furosemide (50%). Also has mild anti-aldosterone activity.

dosing.adult: HF: 10-20 mg OD → max 200 mg OD. HTN: 5-10 mg OD. Cirrhosis: 5-10 mg OD (with spironolactone). Conversion: torsemide 20 mg ≈ furosemide 40 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/dose
dosing.renal: Higher doses may be needed in CKD (up to 200 mg). No dose adjustment per se but monitor response.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in cirrhosis — risk of hepatic encephalopathy. Always combine with spironolactone.

torsemide.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Better bioavailability than furosemide (80-100% vs 50%)
Longer acting — less rebound sodium retention
Torsemide 20 mg ≈ Furosemide 40 mg
Mild anti-aldosterone effect

rivaroxaban.mechanism: Direct, selective, reversible inhibitor of Factor Xa (both free and clot-bound). Inhibits prothrombinase activity and thrombin generation. Does not require antithrombin for activity.
indications: CAD/PAD (2.5 mg BD with aspirin — COMPASS trial)
contraindications: Concomitant strong CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors/inducers

dosing.adult: AF: 20 mg OD with dinner (must take with food for 15/20 mg). DVT/PE: 15 mg BD × 21 days → 20 mg OD with food. VTE prophylaxis: 10 mg OD. CAD/PAD: 2.5 mg BD + aspirin.
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based dosing available for VTE (limited pediatric data)
dosing.renal: AF: CrCl 15-50: 15 mg OD with dinner; CrCl <15: avoid. DVT/PE: avoid if CrCl <30.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in Child-Pugh B/C or coagulopathy

monitoring: Renal function (at least annually, more often if CrCl 30-50)
rivaroxaban.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in pregnancy (animal teratogenicity). Unknown if excreted in breast milk — avoid.
quick_flags: MUST take 15/20 mg doses WITH FOOD (bioavailability issue)
No routine INR monitoring needed
Andexanet alfa for reversal (limited availability)
COMPASS trial: 2.5 mg BD + ASA for stable CAD/PAD

apixaban.mechanism: Direct, selective, reversible inhibitor of Factor Xa (both free and clot-bound). Inhibits prothrombinase activity and thrombin generation without requiring antithrombin as cofactor.
contraindications: Concomitant strong dual CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir)

dosing.adult: AF: 5 mg BD. Reduce to 2.5 mg BD if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL. DVT/PE: 10 mg BD × 7 days → 5 mg BD × 6 months → 2.5 mg BD (extended). VTE prophylaxis: 2.5 mg BD.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; weight-based dosing in ongoing trials
dosing.renal: AF: 2.5 mg BD if Cr ≥1.5 (with age ≥80 or weight ≤60). No dose adjustment for CrCl >25. CrCl <25 or dialysis: limited data, use cautiously.
dosing.hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B: no adjustment; Child-Pugh C: avoid

monitoring: Renal function annually (more frequently if CrCl <60)
apixaban.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data — avoid in pregnancy. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Safest DOAC in elderly and CKD (ARISTOTLE trial)
BD dosing (unlike rivaroxaban OD)
Dose reduce if ≥2 of: age≥80, wt≤60, Cr≥1.5
Can be used in ESRD on dialysis (limited data)

dabigatran.mechanism: Direct, competitive, reversible inhibitor of thrombin (Factor IIa) — both free and clot-bound. Prevents fibrinogen-to-fibrin conversion, Factor V/VIII/XI/XIII activation, and thrombin-induced platelet aggregation. Given as prodrug dabigatran etexilate.
contraindications: Concomitant P-gp inhibitors in severe renal impairment

dosing.adult: AF: 150 mg BD (110 mg BD if age >80, or high bleeding risk, or on verapamil). DVT/PE: 150 mg BD after ≥5 days of parenteral anticoagulation. VTE prophylaxis: 110 mg × 1 then 220 mg OD.
dosing.pediatric: Not well established in children for most indications
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: consider 110 mg BD; CrCl <30: contraindicated. Not dialyzable effectively.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate; no data for severe

monitoring: Renal function at baseline and at least annually (q6m if CrCl 30-50)
Thrombin time (TT) for qualitative assessment if needed
dabigatran.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in pregnancy based on animal data. Excreted in breast milk — avoid.
quick_flags: Must NOT open or crush capsules (bioavailability increases 75%)
Specific reversal agent: Idarucizumab (Praxbind)
RE-LY trial landmark study
Dyspepsia common — take with food

fondaparinux.mechanism: Synthetic pentasaccharide that selectively binds to antithrombin III (AT-III), potentiating (about 300-fold) the innate inhibition of Factor Xa. Does not inhibit thrombin directly. Does not interact with platelet factor 4 (no HIT risk).
contraindications: Thrombocytopenia with positive test for anti-fondaparinux antibodies

dosing.adult: VTE prophylaxis: 2.5 mg SC OD (start 6-8h post-surgery). Treatment: <50 kg: 5 mg; 50-100 kg: 7.5 mg; >100 kg: 10 mg SC OD. NSTEMI: 2.5 mg SC OD (OASIS-5).
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 0.1 mg/kg SC OD in children
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: use prophylactic dose with caution; CrCl <30: contraindicated
dosing.hepatic: Severe impairment: use with caution (bleeding risk)

fondaparinux.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Limited human data. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: NO risk of HIT (does not bind PF4)
Cannot be reversed with protamine
OASIS-5: preferred over enoxaparin in NSTEMI
SC only — no IV bolus for VTE

streptokinase.mechanism: Derived from beta-hemolytic Streptococcus. Forms a complex with plasminogen, converting it to plasmin, which degrades fibrin clots. Non-fibrin-specific — causes systemic fibrinolysis and fibrinogen depletion.
indications: STEMI (within 12 hours, when PCI not available)

dosing.adult: STEMI: 1.5 million IU IV over 60 minutes. PE: 250000 IU IV loading over 30 min → 100000 IU/hour × 24 hours. Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV premedication to reduce allergic reactions.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 3500-4000 IU/kg loading → 1000-1500 IU/kg/hour
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with extreme caution — coagulopathy increases bleeding risk

monitoring: Reperfusion markers (ST-segment resolution, chest pain relief)
streptokinase.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only in life-threatening situations. High bleeding risk. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Cheapest thrombolytic — widely used in India
Antigenic — CANNOT repeat within 6 months
Premedicate with hydrocortisone
Non-fibrin-specific — more systemic bleeding

tenecteplase.mechanism: Genetically engineered variant of alteplase (tPA) with amino acid substitutions providing: longer half-life (20-24 min vs 4-5 min for alteplase), higher fibrin specificity, and greater resistance to PAI-1 inhibition. Converts plasminogen to plasmin at the fibrin clot surface.
indications: STEMI (within 12 hours, when PCI not available)
Acute ischemic stroke (ongoing trials — EXTEND-IA TNK)

dosing.adult: STEMI: Single IV bolus over 5 seconds — <60 kg: 30 mg; 60-69 kg: 35 mg; 70-79 kg: 40 mg; 80-89 kg: 45 mg; ≥90 kg: 50 mg. Stroke (investigational): 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg).
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe impairment

tenecteplase.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only in life-threatening situations. Limited data.
quick_flags: Single IV bolus — most convenient thrombolytic
Non-antigenic — can be repeated
More fibrin-specific than streptokinase
Weight-based single bolus dosing

alteplase.mechanism: Recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rt-PA). Binds to fibrin in a thrombus, converting entrapped plasminogen to plasmin locally. Fibrin-specific — preferentially activates fibrin-bound plasminogen rather than circulating plasminogen.

dosing.adult: Stroke: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) — 10% as IV bolus, remainder over 60 min. STEMI: 15 mg bolus → 0.75 mg/kg over 30 min (max 50 mg) → 0.5 mg/kg over 60 min (max 35 mg), total max 100 mg. PE: 100 mg IV over 2 hours. Line clearance: 2 mg in 2 mL, dwell 30-120 min.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 0.1-0.6 mg/kg/hour for DVT. Not standard for pediatric stroke.
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with extreme caution if coagulopathy

alteplase.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only in life-threatening situations. Limited data. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Gold standard for acute ischemic stroke
Door-to-needle time target: <60 minutes for stroke
Non-antigenic — can repeat
Angioedema risk with concurrent ACEi

moxifloxacin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, transcription, repair, and recombination. 4th gen FQ with enhanced gram-positive and anaerobic coverage compared to ciprofloxacin/levofloxacin.
contraindications: History of tendon disorders with FQ use
Known QT prolongation or concurrent QT-prolonging drugs

dosing.adult: 400 mg OD (oral or IV) × 5-14 days depending on infection. TB (MDR): 400 mg OD as part of regimen.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years (cartilage toxicity risk). Ophthalmic: 1 drop TDS.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required (hepatic metabolism predominates)
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic insufficiency; no specific dose adjustment

monitoring: ECG if risk factors for QT prolongation
moxifloxacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in pregnancy (cartilage damage in animal studies). Excreted in breast milk — avoid.
quick_flags: No renal dose adjustment needed
Significant QT prolongation — avoid with other QT drugs
Better anaerobic coverage than other FQs
USFDA black box: tendon rupture, neuropathy, CNS effects

cefpodoxime.mechanism: Binds PBPs, inhibits cell wall synthesis

dosing.adult: 100-200 mg twice daily
dosing.pediatric: 10 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: extend dosing interval to once daily
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed

quick_flags: Very popular oral cephalosporin in India
Monocef-O brand widely prescribed

cefuroxime.mechanism: Binds PBPs, inhibits cell wall synthesis. Stable to many beta-lactamases.

dosing.adult: Oral: 250-500 mg twice daily; IV: 750 mg-1.5 g every 8 hours
dosing.pediatric: 20-30 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses (oral)
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-20: standard dose q12h; CrCl <10: standard dose q24h
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed

quick_flags: Ceftum very common in Indian practice
Good for step-down from IV to oral

piperacillin_tazobactam.mechanism: Piperacillin: extended-spectrum penicillin that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to PBPs. Tazobactam: beta-lactamase inhibitor that protects piperacillin from degradation by many beta-lactamases (including ESBLs partially). Covers gram-positives, gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), and anaerobes.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to penicillins or beta-lactamase inhibitors
History of severe allergic reaction to cephalosporins (use with caution)

dosing.adult: 4.5 g IV q6-8h. Severe/Pseudomonal infections: 4.5 g IV q6h. Extended infusion: 4.5 g over 4 hours q8h (improved pharmacodynamics). Febrile neutropenia: 4.5 g q6h.
dosing.pediatric: 2-9 months: 80 mg/kg (piperacillin component) q8h. ≥9 months and ≤40 kg: 100 mg/kg (piperacillin) q8h. Appendicitis/peritonitis: 3.375 g q8h (>40 kg).
dosing.renal: CrCl 20-40: 2.25 g q6h; CrCl <20: 2.25 g q8h. Hemodialysis: 2.25 g q8h + 0.75 g after dialysis. CRRT: 4.5 g q8h.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment; monitor hepatic function

monitoring: CBC with differential (especially platelets if >7 days)
piperacillin_tazobactam.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk — compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: ICU workhorse for nosocomial infections
Covers Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Extended infusion (over 4h) improves outcomes
Renal dose adjustment essential

meropenem.mechanism: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs 2, 3, 4), inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis. Broadest spectrum beta-lactam — covers gram-positives, gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas and ESBLs), and anaerobes. Resistant to most beta-lactamases. Does NOT cover MRSA or Enterococcus faecium.
contraindications: History of anaphylaxis to beta-lactams (use with extreme caution — ~1% cross-reactivity)
Valproic acid co-administration (reduces valproate levels significantly)

dosing.adult: Standard: 1 g IV q8h. Meningitis: 2 g IV q8h. Mild-moderate infections: 500 mg q8h. Extended infusion: 1-2 g over 3 hours q8h (improved pharmacodynamics for resistant organisms).
dosing.pediatric: ≥3 months: 10-40 mg/kg q8h (max 2 g q8h). Meningitis: 40 mg/kg q8h.
dosing.renal: CrCl 26-50: 1 g q12h; CrCl 10-25: 500 mg q12h; CrCl <10: 500 mg q24h. Hemodialysis: give dose after dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment; monitor hepatic function

monitoring: Signs of seizures (especially in CNS infections)
meropenem.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Last resort for MDR gram-negatives
Lower seizure risk than imipenem (safe for meningitis)
Reduces valproic acid levels significantly!
Extended infusion improves PK/PD

imipenem_cilastatin.mechanism: Imipenem: carbapenem that binds to PBPs, inhibiting cell wall synthesis. Broadest spectrum — covers gram-positives, gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), and anaerobes. Cilastatin: inhibits renal dehydropeptidase-I, preventing renal metabolism of imipenem and reducing nephrotoxicity.
contraindications: CNS infections (higher seizure risk — use meropenem instead)

dosing.adult: 500 mg IV q6h (max 4 g/day or 50 mg/kg/day, whichever is lower). Mild-moderate: 250-500 mg q6-8h. Must not exceed 500 mg/dose if giving q6h.
dosing.pediatric: ≥3 months: 15-25 mg/kg q6h (max 4 g/day for children ≥40 kg)
dosing.renal: CrCl 41-70: 500 mg q8h; CrCl 21-40: 250 mg q6-8h; CrCl 6-20: 250 mg q12h. Hemodialysis: dose after dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment

imipenem_cilastatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excreted in breast milk — caution advised.
quick_flags: Cilastatin prevents renal degradation of imipenem
Higher seizure risk than meropenem — avoid in meningitis
Reduce dose significantly in renal impairment
Do NOT exceed 500 mg/dose to minimize seizure risk

vancomycin.mechanism: Binds to D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, inhibiting cell wall synthesis. Bactericidal against most gram-positive organisms. Oral vancomycin is NOT absorbed — works locally in the GI tract for C. difficile. IV vancomycin targets systemic MRSA and other resistant gram-positive infections.
contraindications: Oral vancomycin has no systemic indications (not absorbed)

dosing.adult: IV: 15-20 mg/kg q8-12h (based on actual body weight). Loading dose: 25-30 mg/kg for serious infections. Target trough: 15-20 mcg/mL for serious MRSA (AUC/MIC ≥400 preferred). Oral (C. diff): 125 mg QID × 10-14 days; severe: 500 mg QID.
dosing.pediatric: IV: 15 mg/kg q6h (neonates: 10-15 mg/kg q8-12h based on age). Oral C. diff: 10 mg/kg QID (max 125 mg QID).
dosing.renal: Critically important to adjust! CrCl 30-50: 15 mg/kg q12-24h; CrCl 10-30: 15 mg/kg q24-48h; CrCl <10/HD: 15 mg/kg then redose based on levels. Therapeutic drug monitoring essential.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment

adverse_effects: Red man syndrome (histamine release — infuse over ≥60 min)
monitoring: Trough levels (target 15-20 mcg/mL for serious MRSA) or AUC-guided dosing (AUC 400-600)
vancomycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use if clearly needed — concern for ototoxicity in fetus. Poorly excreted in breast milk (oral not absorbed).
quick_flags: Infuse over ≥60 min to avoid red man syndrome
Oral vancomycin for C. diff ONLY (not absorbed systemically)
Therapeutic drug monitoring essential for IV
Nephrotoxic — avoid with aminoglycosides if possible

teicoplanin.mechanism: Lipoglycopeptide that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors. Similar mechanism to vancomycin but with longer half-life (up to 100 hours), allowing OD dosing. Can be given IM (unlike vancomycin).

dosing.adult: Loading: 400 mg (6 mg/kg) IV/IM q12h × 3 doses (day 1-2). Maintenance: 400 mg (6 mg/kg) OD. Severe infections (endocarditis, osteomyelitis): loading 12 mg/kg q12h × 3-5 doses then 12 mg/kg OD.
dosing.pediatric: Neonates: 16 mg/kg on day 1 then 8 mg/kg OD. >2 months: 10 mg/kg q12h × 3 doses then 6-10 mg/kg OD.
dosing.renal: Day 1-3: normal loading doses. From day 4: CrCl 30-80: half normal dose or q48h; CrCl <30/HD: one-third dose or q72h. Monitor levels.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

monitoring: Trough levels (target 15-30 mcg/mL for serious infections, 20-60 for endocarditis)
teicoplanin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B (limited data). Use if clearly needed. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: No red man syndrome (unlike vancomycin)
Can give IM (unlike vancomycin)
Less nephrotoxic than vancomycin
Loading doses essential for rapid therapeutic levels

linezolid.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 23S ribosomal RNA of the 50S subunit, preventing formation of the 70S initiation complex. Bacteriostatic against Enterococci and Staphylococci, bactericidal against Streptococci. 100% oral bioavailability (IV-to-oral switch seamless).
contraindications: Concurrent use of MAO inhibitors or within 2 weeks
Concurrent serotonergic agents (SSRIs, SNRIs, triptans — serotonin syndrome)
Patients on tyramine-rich foods should exercise caution

dosing.adult: 600 mg BD (oral or IV) × 10-28 days. Max duration: 28 days (thrombocytopenia risk increases beyond). TB: 600 mg OD (to reduce toxicity in prolonged use).
dosing.pediatric: Birth-11 years: 10 mg/kg q8h. ≥12 years: 600 mg BD.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment. Metabolites accumulate — clinical significance uncertain. Consider monitoring in severe renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate impairment

monitoring: Visual acuity and color vision (if >28 days)
linezolid.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Excreted in breast milk in animal studies — caution.
quick_flags: 100% oral bioavailability — oral = IV
Max 28 days due to thrombocytopenia risk
Weak MAO inhibitor — serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs
Monitor CBC weekly

colistin.mechanism: Cationic polypeptide antibiotic that binds to lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in gram-negative bacterial outer membrane, disrupting membrane integrity and causing cell death. Bactericidal with concentration-dependent killing. Given as inactive prodrug colistimethate sodium (CMS).

dosing.adult: Loading dose: 9 million IU (300 mg CBA) IV over 30 min. Maintenance: 4.5 million IU (150 mg CBA) IV q12h. Start maintenance 12h after loading. Nebulized: 1-2 million IU q8-12h (VAP adjunct).
dosing.pediatric: 75000-150000 IU/kg/day IV in 2-3 divided doses
dosing.renal: Critical! CrCl 50-79: 3.5 million IU q12h; CrCl 30-49: 2.5 million IU q12h; CrCl 10-29: 1.5-2 million IU q12h; HD: 2 million IU q12h (supplemental dose after HD). Use ideal body weight.
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment

monitoring: Renal function (creatinine, BUN — daily in ICU)
Drug levels if available (target Css 2-4 mcg/mL)
colistin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only in life-threatening situations. Potential for fetal nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Last resort for XDR gram-negatives
Loading dose is critical for efficacy
Nephrotoxicity very common — monitor daily creatinine
Renal dosing is critical

nitrofurantoin.mechanism: Reduced by bacterial flavoproteins to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, RNA, proteins, and cell wall synthesis. Bactericidal at urinary concentrations. Concentrated in urine — NOT effective for systemic infections. Covers most common UTI pathogens including E. coli and Enterococcus. NOT active against Proteus, Pseudomonas, or Klebsiella.
contraindications: CrCl <30 mL/min (drug not concentrated in urine — ineffective and toxic)
History of nitrofurantoin-associated hepatotoxicity or pulmonary reaction

dosing.adult: Treatment: 100 mg BD × 5-7 days (macrocrystals) or 100 mg MR BD × 5 days. Prophylaxis: 50-100 mg at bedtime. Take with food.
dosing.pediatric: ≥1 month: 5-7 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses (max 400 mg/day). Prophylaxis: 1-2 mg/kg/day at bedtime.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: can use with caution; CrCl <30: CONTRAINDICATED (ineffective and toxic)
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; hepatotoxicity reported

monitoring: Signs of pulmonary toxicity (chronic use — cough, dyspnea)
nitrofurantoin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B (avoid at term — hemolytic anemia in newborn). Safe in 1st and 2nd trimester for UTI. Avoid in G6PD-deficient neonates. Compatible with breastfeeding (avoid if infant has G6PD deficiency).
quick_flags: Only for LOWER UTI (not systemic infections)
AVOID if CrCl <30 (ineffective)
Pulmonary fibrosis with long-term use
Take with food to reduce GI upset and improve absorption

fosfomycin.mechanism: Inhibits the first step of bacterial cell wall synthesis by inactivating MurA (UDP-N-acetylglucosamine enolpyruvyl transferase), which catalyzes peptidoglycan precursor formation. Unique mechanism — no cross-resistance with other antibiotics. Covers E. coli (including ESBL), Enterococcus faecalis, Klebsiella, and Proteus.
contraindications: Severe renal impairment for IV form (CrCl <10)

dosing.adult: Uncomplicated UTI: 3 g oral single dose (dissolve in water, take on empty stomach). IV: 4 g q6-8h for serious infections (up to 16-24 g/day in some protocols for MDR infections).
dosing.pediatric: Oral: >12 years: 3 g single dose. IV: 100-200 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses.
dosing.renal: Oral single dose: no adjustment needed. IV: CrCl 10-30: reduce dose or extend interval; CrCl <10: avoid IV.
