dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

monitoring: Clinical response (symptoms should improve in 2-3 days)
Repeat urine culture if symptoms persist
fosfomycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Single oral dose considered safe in pregnancy for uncomplicated UTI. Limited data on IV use. Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Single oral dose for uncomplicated UTI — excellent compliance
Effective against ESBL E. coli
No cross-resistance with other antibiotics
Dissolve sachet in water — take on empty stomach

fluconazole.mechanism: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450 enzyme 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol. Depletes ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane, increasing membrane permeability and inhibiting fungal growth. Also a potent inhibitor of human CYP2C9 and CYP3A4.
indications: Cryptococcal meningitis (treatment and secondary prophylaxis in HIV)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to fluconazole or other azoles
Co-administration with cisapride, pimozide, or quinidine (QT prolongation risk)
Pregnancy (Category D — skeletal malformations reported with prolonged high-dose use)

dosing.adult: Vulvovaginal candidiasis: 150 mg single oral dose. Oropharyngeal candidiasis: 200 mg on Day 1, then 100 mg OD for 7-14 days. Esophageal candidiasis: 200 mg on Day 1, then 100-200 mg OD for 14-21 days. Systemic candidiasis: 400 mg on Day 1, then 200-400 mg OD. Cryptococcal meningitis (HIV): Loading 800 mg on Day 1, then 400 mg OD for at least 8 weeks (induction), then 200 mg OD (maintenance/secondary prophylaxis).
dosing.pediatric: 3-12 mg/kg/day depending on indication. Neonates: adjust interval due to prolonged half-life.
dosing.renal: CrCl >50 mL/min: no adjustment. CrCl 11-50 mL/min: 50% of recommended dose. Dialysis: full dose after each session.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; monitor LFTs. Discontinue if clinical signs of liver disease develop.

adverse_effects: Elevated transaminases (hepatotoxicity — rare fulminant liver failure)
QT prolongation (especially with other QT-prolonging drugs)
monitoring: Liver function tests (baseline and periodic, especially with prolonged use)
ECG if co-prescribed with QT-prolonging agents
Drug interaction review (CYP2C9/3A4 inhibitor — increases levels of warfarin, phenytoin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, sulfonylureas, statins)
fluconazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Prolonged high-dose use (400-800 mg/day) associated with craniofacial, skeletal, and cardiac defects. Single 150 mg dose for vulvovaginal candidiasis generally considered low risk but alternatives preferred. Excreted in breast milk at concentrations similar to plasma.
quick_flags: CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitor — check warfarin, phenytoin, cyclosporine interactions
Renal dose adjustment: 50% dose if CrCl <50
Category D — avoid in pregnancy (especially chronic high-dose)
QT prolongation risk with concomitant drugs
Good CSF penetration — drug of choice for crypto meningitis maintenance

itraconazole.mechanism: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol, disrupting fungal cell membrane integrity.
contraindications: Concurrent CYP3A4 substrates with QT risk (cisapride, pimozide)

dosing.adult: Onychomycosis: pulse therapy 200 mg BID for 1 week per month (2 pulses for fingernails, 3 for toenails). Systemic mycosis: 200-400 mg/day. Capsules taken with food; solution on empty stomach.
dosing.pediatric: 5 mg/kg/day. Safety data limited in young children.
dosing.renal: Capsules: no adjustment. Oral solution: avoid if CrCl <30 (hydroxypropyl-beta-cyclodextrin accumulation).
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Monitor LFTs. Avoid in severe hepatic impairment.

monitoring: LFTs at baseline and monthly if treatment >1 month
itraconazole.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic in animals). Women of childbearing potential should use contraception during and for 2 months after treatment. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Capsules need ACIDIC gastric pH for absorption — avoid PPIs, take with cola if needed
Oral solution has 60% better bioavailability than capsules
Check for heart failure before prescribing — negative inotropic effect

voriconazole.mechanism: Inhibits fungal CYP51 (14-alpha-demethylase), blocking ergosterol synthesis. Broader spectrum than fluconazole and itraconazole — active against Aspergillus, Fusarium, Scedosporium, and fluconazole-resistant Candida species. Metabolized by CYP2C19 (major), CYP2C9, and CYP3A4.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to voriconazole or other azoles
Co-administration with rifampin, carbamazepine, long-acting barbiturates, sirolimus, ergot alkaloids, pimozide, quinidine, St. John's wort
Co-administration with efavirenz (standard doses) or ritonavir (high doses)
IV formulation: avoid if CrCl <50 mL/min (cyclodextrin vehicle accumulation)

dosing.adult: IV Loading: 6 mg/kg q12h for 2 doses on Day 1, then 4 mg/kg q12h maintenance. Oral: Loading 400 mg q12h for 2 doses (Day 1), then 200 mg q12h maintenance. Patients <40 kg: 100 mg q12h maintenance oral. Take oral doses 1 hour before or after meals.
dosing.pediatric: Children 2-12 years and 12-14 years (<50 kg): IV 9 mg/kg q12h loading then 8 mg/kg q12h. Oral: 9 mg/kg q12h (max 350 mg q12h).
dosing.renal: Oral: no adjustment needed. IV: avoid if CrCl <50 mL/min (cyclodextrin accumulates). Hemodialysis does not clear voriconazole effectively.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate (Child-Pugh A/B): use standard loading dose, halve maintenance dose. Severe (Child-Pugh C): use only if benefit outweighs risk.

adverse_effects: Visual disturbances (photopsia, blurred vision, color vision changes — ~30%, usually transient)
Squamous cell carcinoma and melanoma with long-term use (phototoxicity-related)
Periostitis and fluorosis (with prolonged use >6 months)
monitoring: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) — trough levels essential: target 1-5.5 mcg/mL
LFTs (baseline and weekly for first month, then monthly)
Visual acuity and visual field testing (if prolonged use)
CYP2C19 genotype if available (poor metabolizers have 4x higher exposure)
Dermatologic exam every 6-12 months for skin cancer (with prolonged use)
Fluoride levels if >6 months of therapy
voriconazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic in animals (cleft palate, hydronephrosis). Contraindicated in pregnancy. Effective contraception required. Breastfeeding not recommended.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for invasive aspergillosis
TDM recommended — target trough 1-5.5 mcg/mL
CYP2C19 polymorphisms significantly affect levels (poor metabolizers get toxic levels)
Visual disturbances in ~30% (usually transient, dose-related)
Phototoxicity with long-term use — increased skin cancer risk (use sunscreen)
Category D — contraindicated in pregnancy

amphotericin_b.mechanism: Binds to ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane, forming pores that increase membrane permeability. Leads to leakage of intracellular ions (K+, Mg2+) and cell death. Fungicidal. Broadest spectrum of any antifungal — active against most yeasts and molds including Mucorales.
indications: Mucormycosis (drug of choice — liposomal formulation preferred)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (relative — use liposomal if possible)
Concurrent use with other nephrotoxic agents (relative — monitor closely)

dosing.adult: Conventional (deoxycholate): test dose 1 mg in 20 mL D5W over 20-30 min, then 0.5-1 mg/kg/day IV over 4-6 hours. Liposomal (AmBisome): 3-5 mg/kg/day IV over 1-2 hours (no test dose needed). Mucormycosis: liposomal 5-10 mg/kg/day. Visceral leishmaniasis: liposomal 10 mg/kg total dose. Pre-medication: paracetamol 650 mg + diphenhydramine 25-50 mg + hydrocortisone 25-50 mg IV before each infusion.
dosing.pediatric: Same weight-based dosing as adults. Liposomal preferred in children.
dosing.renal: Conventional: hold if creatinine doubles or exceeds 3 mg/dL, consider switching to liposomal. Liposomal: much less nephrotoxic, preferred in renal impairment. No specific dose adjustment, but monitor closely.
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment. Monitor LFTs.

adverse_effects: Infusion-related reactions (fever, rigors, chills, nausea, vomiting, headache — common with conventional)
monitoring: Serum creatinine and BUN (daily during initiation, then 2-3x/week)
Serum potassium and magnesium (daily during initiation — aggressive replacement often needed)
Vital signs during infusion (for infusion reactions)
Pre-infusion normal saline bolus (500-1000 mL) to reduce nephrotoxicity
amphotericin_b.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data but decades of clinical use without clear teratogenicity. Use when benefit outweighs risk — often the only option for life-threatening fungal infections in pregnancy. Breastfeeding data limited.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for mucormycosis (use liposomal 5-10 mg/kg/day)
Liposomal formulation MUCH less nephrotoxic than conventional
Pre-medicate: paracetamol + diphenhydramine +/- hydrocortisone
Monitor K+, Mg2+, creatinine aggressively — replace electrolytes proactively
NS bolus before infusion reduces nephrotoxicity
Category B — acceptable in pregnancy for life-threatening infections

clindamycin.mechanism: Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit (23S rRNA), inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis by blocking peptide bond formation. Bacteriostatic (may be bactericidal at high concentrations). Excellent coverage against Gram-positive cocci and anaerobes. Also inhibits bacterial toxin production (useful in toxin-mediated diseases).
indications: Skin and soft tissue infections (MRSA — community-acquired)
Bone and joint infections (osteomyelitis — excellent bone penetration)

dosing.adult: Oral: 150-450 mg every 6 hours. IV/IM: 600-900 mg every 8 hours (severe infections up to 2.7 g/day in 2-4 divided doses). Bacterial vaginosis: 300 mg BD x 7 days oral or intravaginal cream. Topical acne: apply 1% gel/lotion BD.
dosing.pediatric: Oral: 8-25 mg/kg/day in 3-4 divided doses. IV/IM: 20-40 mg/kg/day in 3-4 divided doses.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment needed (hepatic metabolism).
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment. Monitor LFTs.

adverse_effects: Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea and pseudomembranous colitis (BLACK BOX WARNING)
Esophageal ulceration (take with full glass of water)
monitoring: Signs and symptoms of C. difficile colitis (diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever)
clindamycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Crosses placenta but no evidence of teratogenicity. Used for GBS prophylaxis in penicillin-allergic patients. Enters breast milk — AAP considers compatible but monitor infant for GI effects.
quick_flags: BLACK BOX: C. difficile colitis risk — counsel patients about diarrhea
Excellent bone penetration — preferred for osteomyelitis
Good anaerobic coverage
No renal dose adjustment needed
Inhibits toxin production — useful as adjunct in necrotizing fasciitis/toxic shock
Category B in pregnancy

cotrimoxazole.drug_class: Folate Antagonist Combination (Dihydrofolate Reductase Inhibitor + Sulfonamide)
forms: Oral suspension (Trimethoprim 40 mg + Sulfamethoxazole 200 mg per 5 mL)
cotrimoxazole.mechanism: Dual sequential blockade of folate synthesis. Sulfamethoxazole inhibits dihydropteroate synthetase (competes with PABA); Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase. Synergistic bactericidal combination. The 1:5 ratio (TMP:SMX) achieves optimal 1:20 ratio in vivo.
indications: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP/PCP) — treatment and prophylaxis
MRSA skin and soft tissue infections
Prophylaxis in immunocompromised patients (HIV with CD4 <200)
contraindications: Megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency

dosing.adult: UTI/general infections: 1 DS tablet (TMP 160/SMX 800) BD for 3-14 days depending on indication. PJP treatment: TMP 15-20 mg/kg/day (divided q6-8h) for 21 days. PJP prophylaxis: 1 SS or 1 DS tablet OD. Nocardiosis: TMP 15 mg/kg/day for months.
dosing.pediatric: TMP 8-10 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses. PJP treatment: TMP 15-20 mg/kg/day in 3-4 divided doses. PJP prophylaxis: TMP 5 mg/kg/day OD (max 160 mg TMP/day).
dosing.renal: CrCl 15-30 mL/min: 50% of standard dose. CrCl <15 mL/min: AVOID. Hemodialysis: dose after dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment. Use with caution in mild-moderate.

adverse_effects: Rash (common), Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis (rare but serious)
Hyperkalemia (trimethoprim blocks ENaC in collecting duct)
Bone marrow suppression (megaloblastic anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia)
monitoring: CBC with differential (baseline and periodic — especially prolonged use)
Renal function and electrolytes (especially potassium — hyperkalemia risk)
Serum creatinine (TMP elevates Cr without true GFR decline — false elevation)
G6PD status before initiating if unknown
Rash monitoring — discontinue at first sign of serious skin reaction
cotrimoxazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Folate antagonist — increased risk of neural tube defects if used in first trimester. Third trimester: displaces bilirubin from albumin — risk of neonatal kernicterus. Supplemental folate recommended if use is unavoidable. Excreted in breast milk — avoid in infants with jaundice, G6PD deficiency, or premature infants.
quick_flags: PJP treatment dose: TMP 15-20 mg/kg/day (much higher than standard dose)
PJP prophylaxis: 1 DS or SS OD
Avoid in G6PD deficiency (hemolysis)
Causes hyperkalemia (trimethoprim blocks ENaC)
Category D — avoid in 1st and 3rd trimesters
Renal: reduce dose if CrCl 15-30, AVOID if <15
TMP falsely elevates serum creatinine

gentamicin.mechanism: Binds irreversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit (specifically 16S rRNA), causing misreading of mRNA and production of defective proteins. Bactericidal with concentration-dependent killing. Post-antibiotic effect (PAE) — continues killing even after serum levels fall below MIC. Active primarily against aerobic Gram-negative bacteria.
indications: Serious Gram-negative infections (Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, E. coli, Enterobacter)
Synergistic combination with beta-lactams for endocarditis (enterococcal, streptococcal)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to gentamicin or other aminoglycosides
Concurrent use with other nephrotoxic or ototoxic agents (relative)

dosing.adult: Traditional dosing: 1-1.7 mg/kg IV/IM every 8 hours. Extended-interval (once-daily/Hartford nomogram): 5-7 mg/kg IV q24h (preferred — equally effective, potentially less nephrotoxic). Endocarditis synergy: 1 mg/kg q8h or 3 mg/kg q24h. Dose based on ideal body weight (IBW); use adjusted body weight if obese.
dosing.pediatric: 2-2.5 mg/kg q8h or 5-7.5 mg/kg q24h (extended-interval). Neonates: 4-5 mg/kg q24-48h depending on gestational age.
dosing.renal: Traditional dosing: extend interval based on CrCl. CrCl 40-60: q12h, CrCl 20-40: q24h, CrCl <20: q48h or re-dose based on levels. Extended-interval: use Hartford nomogram. Dialysis: re-dose after HD (50-75% cleared).
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed.

adverse_effects: Nephrotoxicity (usually reversible — accumulates in proximal tubular cells)
Ototoxicity — vestibular (dizziness, vertigo) and cochlear (hearing loss — often irreversible)
monitoring: Peak and trough levels: Traditional dosing — Trough <2 mcg/mL, Peak 5-10 mcg/mL (general); Peak 3-5 mcg/mL (synergy). Extended-interval: random level at 6-14h, plot on nomogram.
Audiometry (if prolonged course >5 days or baseline hearing concerns)
gentamicin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Aminoglycosides cross the placenta — risk of fetal ototoxicity (8th cranial nerve damage). Avoid unless life-threatening infection with no safer alternative. Small amounts in breast milk — poorly absorbed orally by infant.
quick_flags: Extended-interval dosing (5-7 mg/kg q24h) preferred over traditional
Peak/trough monitoring essential for traditional dosing
Nephrotoxic AND ototoxic — monitor creatinine and hearing
Dose on ideal body weight (use adjusted BW in obesity)
Concentration-dependent killing + post-antibiotic effect
Category D — avoid in pregnancy if possible

amikacin.mechanism: Binds irreversibly to 30S ribosomal subunit (16S rRNA), causing mRNA misreading and defective protein synthesis. Same mechanism as gentamicin but structurally resistant to many aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes, giving it broader coverage against gentamicin-resistant Gram-negative organisms. Bactericidal, concentration-dependent killing with post-antibiotic effect.
indications: Serious Gram-negative infections resistant to gentamicin
Empiric therapy for suspected MDR Gram-negative sepsis
Mycobacterial infections (M. tuberculosis — second-line, M. avium complex)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to amikacin or other aminoglycosides

dosing.adult: Traditional dosing: 5 mg/kg q8h or 7.5 mg/kg q12h. Extended-interval: 15 mg/kg IV q24h (preferred). Maximum: 15 mg/kg/day (traditional) or 1.5 g/day. MDR-TB: 15 mg/kg/day IM/IV (max 1 g) in intensive phase. Dose based on ideal body weight; use adjusted body weight in obese patients.
dosing.pediatric: 15-22.5 mg/kg/day in divided doses q8h. Neonates: 7.5 mg/kg q12h (adjust for gestational age).
dosing.renal: Extend interval based on CrCl. Traditional: CrCl 40-60: q12h, CrCl 20-40: q24h, CrCl <20: q48h or re-dose by levels. Hemodialysis: re-dose after dialysis. TDM-guided dosing recommended.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed.

adverse_effects: Nephrotoxicity (tubular necrosis — usually reversible if caught early)
monitoring: Trough levels: <5 mcg/mL (traditional dosing). Peak: 20-35 mcg/mL.
Extended-interval: random level at 8h, plot on nomogram
Audiometry (baseline and weekly for courses >5 days)
amikacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Crosses placenta — risk of irreversible bilateral congenital deafness reported with aminoglycosides. Avoid unless life-threatening infection. Small amounts excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Broader spectrum than gentamicin — covers some gentamicin-resistant strains
Trough <5, Peak 20-35 mcg/mL (traditional dosing targets)
Extended-interval dosing (15 mg/kg q24h) preferred
Same nephro/ototoxicity concerns as all aminoglycosides
Used as second-line for MDR-TB
Category D — avoid in pregnancy

rifaximin.mechanism: Binds to the beta-subunit of bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase, inhibiting RNA synthesis. Acts locally in the GI tract with minimal systemic absorption (<0.4%).
contraindications: Diarrhea with fever or bloody stools (suggests invasive pathogen — use systemic antibiotics)

dosing.adult: Hepatic encephalopathy: 550 mg twice daily (with lactulose). Traveler's diarrhea: 200 mg three times daily for 3 days. IBS-D: 550 mg three times daily for 14 days. SIBO: 550 mg three times daily for 14 days.
dosing.pediatric: >=12 years: Same as adult for traveler's diarrhea. Not established for other indications in children.
dosing.renal: No adjustment required (minimal systemic absorption).
dosing.hepatic: Systemic exposure increases in severe hepatic impairment; use with caution but still first-line for hepatic encephalopathy.

monitoring: Hepatic encephalopathy symptoms (number connection test, asterixis)
rifaximin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Minimal systemic absorption limits fetal exposure. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Non-absorbed — acts locally in gut; safe even in liver failure
Standard of care (with lactulose) for preventing recurrent hepatic encephalopathy
Does NOT cause significant bacterial resistance due to local action
Do NOT use for invasive/bloody diarrhea — need systemic antibiotics

terbinafine.mechanism: Inhibits squalene epoxidase, a key enzyme in fungal ergosterol biosynthesis. Squalene accumulation and ergosterol depletion disrupt fungal cell membrane integrity. Fungicidal against dermatophytes.
indications: Dermatophyte infections (tinea corporis, tinea cruris, tinea pedis)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <50 ml/min) for oral use

dosing.adult: Oral: 250 mg OD; Tinea corporis/cruris: 2-4 weeks; Tinea pedis: 2-6 weeks; Onychomycosis: fingernails 6 weeks, toenails 12 weeks; Topical: apply OD-BD for 1-2 weeks
dosing.pediatric: <20 kg: 62.5 mg OD; 20-40 kg: 125 mg OD; >40 kg: 250 mg OD; Tinea capitis: 4-8 weeks
dosing.renal: CrCl <50 ml/min: reduce dose by 50% or avoid oral use
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease; monitor LFTs in all patients on oral therapy

adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity (rare but serious — including hepatic failure)
monitoring: LFTs before starting and periodically (every 4-6 weeks) during oral therapy
Signs of hepatotoxicity (nausea, fatigue, dark urine, jaundice)
terbinafine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Avoid oral use during pregnancy unless essential. Topical use acceptable. Excreted in breast milk — oral use not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Oral terbinafine is the most effective treatment for dermatophyte onychomycosis
Dermatophytosis epidemic in India — widespread resistance concerns with topical azoles making oral terbinafine crucial
Warn patients about taste disturbance — usually reversible but can last weeks
LFT monitoring is mandatory for oral courses — hepatic failure cases reported
Topical steroids-antifungal combinations (misused OTC in India) should be replaced by terbinafine monotherapy

dexamethasone.mechanism: Binds to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. Inhibits phospholipase A2, NF-kB, and multiple cytokine pathways.
indications: Oncologic emergencies (spinal cord compression, raised ICP)

dosing.adult: Anti-inflammatory: 0.5-9 mg/day depending on condition. Cerebral oedema: 10 mg IV loading, then 4 mg IV/IM q6h. CINV: 12 mg IV Day 1, then 8 mg PO Days 2-4. COVID-19: 6 mg daily for 10 days.
dosing.pediatric: Croup: 0.15-0.6 mg/kg single dose. Bacterial meningitis: 0.15 mg/kg q6h for 4 days (start before or with first antibiotic). Anti-inflammatory: 0.08-0.3 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may need reduced doses in severe impairment.

monitoring: Adrenal function before tapering long courses
dexamethasone.pregnancy_lactation: Used when benefit outweighs risk (e.g., fetal lung maturity). Preferred over betamethasone for maternal indications. Compatible with breastfeeding at low doses.
quick_flags: 30x more potent than hydrocortisone with minimal mineralocorticoid activity
RECOVERY trial: 6 mg/day reduced mortality in hypoxic COVID-19
Long duration of action (36-72 h) — suitable for once-daily dosing

hydrocortisone.mechanism: Identical to endogenous cortisol. Binds glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid receptors. Provides both anti-inflammatory/immunosuppressive effects (glucocorticoid) and sodium retention/potassium excretion (mineralocorticoid). Reference corticosteroid — all potency comparisons are made against hydrocortisone. Shortest-acting systemic corticosteroid (biological half-life 8-12 hours).
indications: Adrenal insufficiency (primary and secondary — physiologic replacement)

dosing.adult: Physiologic replacement: 10-15 mg/m2/day (typically 15-25 mg/day) divided as 10 mg morning + 5 mg afternoon (or 10+5+5 TDS to mimic circadian rhythm). Stress dose: 100 mg IV bolus, then 50 mg IV q6-8h (taper as clinical status improves). Adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV STAT then 50 mg q6-8h. Septic shock: 50 mg IV q6h (ADRENAL trial) or 200 mg/day continuous infusion.
dosing.pediatric: Physiologic replacement: 8-10 mg/m2/day in 3 divided doses. Stress dose: 50-100 mg/m2/day IV divided q6-8h. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia: 10-15 mg/m2/day in 3 divided doses.
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment.
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment, but active drug (unlike prednisone which needs hepatic activation).

adverse_effects: Sodium and water retention, edema (mineralocorticoid effect)
monitoring: Signs of adrenal insufficiency when tapering
Growth velocity in children (on replacement doses)
Morning cortisol levels (for dose adequacy in replacement)
hydrocortisone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Identical to endogenous cortisol. Extensively metabolized by placental 11-beta-HSD2 (80-90%), so less fetal exposure than dexamethasone. Safe for physiologic replacement in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Reference corticosteroid — 1:1 gluco:mineralo activity
Shortest-acting (8-12h) — needs divided dosing for replacement
Adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV STAT (do not delay for diagnosis)
Stress dosing: 100 mg IV then 50 mg q6-8h
Physiologic replacement mimics circadian: larger AM dose
Equal mineralocorticoid and glucocorticoid effect
Does NOT need hepatic activation (unlike prednisone)

deflazacort.mechanism: Prodrug — rapidly converted to active metabolite 21-desacetyl-deflazacort. Binds glucocorticoid receptors and modulates anti-inflammatory gene transcription similar to other corticosteroids. Purported to have a bone-sparing effect relative to prednisolone (less impact on calcium metabolism and osteoblast function). Minimal mineralocorticoid effect.
indications: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (FDA-approved — slows motor decline)

dosing.adult: Anti-inflammatory: 6-48 mg/day depending on condition severity. Typical maintenance: 6-18 mg/day. Potency equivalence: 6 mg deflazacort = 5 mg prednisolone = 20 mg hydrocortisone.
dosing.pediatric: Duchenne muscular dystrophy: 0.9 mg/kg/day OD (FDA-approved). General anti-inflammatory: 0.25-1.5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment. Use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment. Prodrug requires hepatic activation but this is usually preserved.

adverse_effects: Cushingoid features (less weight gain than prednisolone)
Osteoporosis (reportedly less than equivalent prednisolone doses)
Cataracts (significant with long-term use in DMD patients)
Increased appetite, weight gain (less than prednisolone)
monitoring: Bone density (DEXA scan — especially long-term use)
Ophthalmologic exam (cataracts — annually for chronic use)
deflazacort.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited human data. Animal studies show teratogenicity at high doses. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Breastfeeding data limited — use with caution.
quick_flags: 6 mg deflazacort = 5 mg prednisolone (dose equivalence)
Less bone loss and weight gain than prednisolone (claimed advantage)
FDA-approved for DMD: 0.9 mg/kg/day
Very popular in India for chronic steroid use
Prodrug — converted to active metabolite hepatically
Category C — limited pregnancy data

budesonide_inhaled.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors in airway epithelial and inflammatory cells. Reduces airway inflammation by suppressing cytokine production, reducing eosinophil infiltration, decreasing mucus secretion, and inhibiting inflammatory mediator release. High topical potency with high first-pass hepatic metabolism (~90%) — minimal systemic effects at recommended doses.
indications: Persistent asthma — first-line controller therapy (all severity levels)
COPD (in combination with LABA, or history of frequent exacerbations)
Croup (nebulized — adjunct to dexamethasone in moderate-severe)
contraindications: Primary treatment of acute bronchospasm/status asthmaticus (not a rescue inhaler)

dosing.adult: Asthma (DPI): Low dose: 200-400 mcg/day, Medium dose: 400-800 mcg/day, High dose: >800-1600 mcg/day. Usually given BD. Nebulization: 0.5-1 mg BD.
dosing.pediatric: Ages 6-11 years (DPI): Low 100-200 mcg/day, Medium 200-400 mcg/day, High >400 mcg/day. Nebulization (ages 1-8): 0.25-0.5 mg BD (maintenance); up to 1 mg BD for acute exacerbation. Croup (nebulized): 2 mg as single dose.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment needed.
dosing.hepatic: Plasma levels may increase in significant hepatic impairment (reduced first-pass metabolism). Monitor for systemic effects.

adverse_effects: Oral candidiasis (thrush — prevent by rinsing mouth after inhalation)
Adrenal suppression (high doses, especially in children — rare at standard doses)
Growth retardation in children (1-2 cm reduction in first year, effect diminishes — final adult height minimally affected)
monitoring: Inhaler technique (critical for efficacy — reassess at every visit)
Peak expiratory flow / spirometry (assess asthma control)
HPA axis (if high dose — morning cortisol if concerned)
Step-down therapy when asthma well-controlled for 3+ months
budesonide_inhaled.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Preferred inhaled corticosteroid in pregnancy (most safety data of any ICS). NAEPP guidelines recommend continuing ICS during pregnancy. Minimal systemic absorption. Compatible with breastfeeding — negligible amounts in breast milk.
quick_flags: RINSE MOUTH after every use (prevents oral candidiasis)
First-line controller for persistent asthma (GINA Step 2+)
90% first-pass metabolism — minimal systemic effects
Category B — preferred ICS in pregnancy
NOT a rescue inhaler — do not use for acute bronchospasm
Low: 200-400, Medium: 400-800, High: >800 mcg/day (adult dosing tiers)
Growth monitoring essential in children

valproic_acid.generic_name: Valproic Acid / Sodium Valproate / Divalproex Sodium
valproic_acid.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: (1) Enhances GABA-ergic transmission by inhibiting GABA transaminase and increasing GABA synthesis, (2) Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (use-dependent), (3) Reduces T-type calcium currents (relevant for absence seizures), (4) Modulates intracellular signaling via inositol depletion and GSK-3 inhibition (mood stabilization). Broad-spectrum — effective for generalized and focal seizures.
indications: Generalized epilepsy (first-line — absence, myoclonic, tonic-clonic, JME)
contraindications: Pregnancy — CATEGORY X (neural tube defects 1-2%, other major malformations, neurodevelopmental impairment including reduced IQ)
Women of childbearing potential without effective contraception
Hepatic disease or significant hepatic dysfunction
Known urea cycle disorders (risk of hyperammonemic encephalopathy)
Known mitochondrial disorders caused by POLG mutations (e.g., Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome)

dosing.adult: Epilepsy: Start 500-750 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Increase by 250 mg every 3-7 days. Maintenance: 1000-2000 mg/day (max 60 mg/kg/day). Therapeutic level: 50-100 mcg/mL. IV: same daily dose, infuse at 20 mg/min (or 3-6 mg/kg/min for status). Bipolar mania: loading 20-30 mg/kg/day, or start 750 mg/day in divided doses. Migraine prophylaxis: 500-1000 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Initial: 10-15 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Increase by 5-10 mg/kg/week. Max: 60 mg/kg/day. Same therapeutic level: 50-100 mcg/mL. CAUTION in children <2 years — highest risk of fatal hepatotoxicity.
dosing.renal: Free (unbound) fraction increases in renal impairment — total levels may be misleading. Monitor free valproic acid levels. Consider dose reduction.
dosing.hepatic: CONTRAINDICATED in significant hepatic disease. Hepatic metabolism — clearance reduced, toxicity risk increases.

adverse_effects: Weight gain (significant — up to 20% of patients)
GI upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea — reduced with CR formulations)
Hepatotoxicity (fatal in rare cases — especially <2 years, polytherapy, metabolic disorders)
Pancreatitis (rare but serious — can be fatal)
Hyperammonemia (can occur without hepatic failure — consider in confusion/lethargy)
Teratogenicity (neural tube defects, craniofacial anomalies, reduced IQ — CATEGORY X)
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS — with chronic use in women)
monitoring: Serum valproic acid level: therapeutic 50-100 mcg/mL (trough, just before next dose)
Free valproic acid level if renal impairment, hypoalbuminemia, or on highly protein-bound drugs
LFTs (baseline, then frequently during first 6 months — especially in children <2 years)
CBC with platelets (baseline and periodic — thrombocytopenia is dose-dependent)
Ammonia level if unexplained lethargy, confusion, or vomiting
Pregnancy test before starting in women of childbearing age
valproic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: CATEGORY X. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy if alternatives exist. Neural tube defects (spina bifida) in 1-2% of exposed pregnancies (10x background rate). Other malformations: cleft lip, cardiac defects, hypospadias. Neurodevelopmental effects: mean IQ reduction of 8-9 points, increased autism risk. If unavoidable (no alternative for seizure control), use lowest effective dose, divide doses, and supplement with high-dose folic acid (5 mg/day). Excreted in breast milk (1-10% of maternal level) — generally compatible with breastfeeding as infant levels are low.
quick_flags: CATEGORY X — absolutely avoid in pregnancy (NTDs, reduced IQ)
Therapeutic level: 50-100 mcg/mL
Hepatotoxicity risk highest in children <2 years (can be fatal)
Check ammonia if unexplained confusion/lethargy
Inhibits CYP2C9 and glucuronidation — DOUBLES lamotrigine levels
Weight gain, hair loss, tremor — common triad
Thrombocytopenia at high levels (>100 mcg/mL)

carbamazepine.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (use-dependent/frequency-dependent), stabilizing the inactivated state and reducing repetitive neuronal firing. Also modulates L-type calcium channels. Effective for focal (partial) seizures and generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Can WORSEN absence, myoclonic, and atonic seizures (avoid in generalized epilepsies). Also effective for trigeminal neuralgia (first-line).
indications: Focal (partial) epilepsy — with or without secondary generalization
Bipolar disorder (acute mania, maintenance — alternative to lithium/valproate)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to carbamazepine or tricyclic antidepressants (structural similarity)
Concurrent or recent MAOI use (within 14 days)
HLA-B*1502 positive (mandatory screening in Asian populations — SJS/TEN risk)
HLA-A*3101 positive (associated with hypersensitivity in European populations)

dosing.adult: Epilepsy: Start 100-200 mg BD. Increase by 200 mg/day weekly. Maintenance: 600-1200 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses (CR: BD). Max: 1600 mg/day. Therapeutic level: 4-12 mcg/mL. Trigeminal neuralgia: Start 100-200 mg BD, increase by 200 mg/day q12h as needed. Maintenance: 400-800 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Initial: 5 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Increase weekly. Maintenance: 10-20 mg/kg/day. Max: 35 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment needed (hepatic metabolism). Active metabolite (10,11-epoxide) may accumulate in renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Reduce dose in hepatic impairment. Monitor levels and LFTs closely.

adverse_effects: Dizziness, ataxia, drowsiness, diplopia (dose-related CNS effects — common initially)
Hyponatremia/SIADH (especially in elderly — can be clinically significant)
Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (HLA-B*1502 — Asians)
DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms)
Aplastic anemia (rare but serious — 5-8x background rate)
Autoinduction of metabolism (levels drop over 2-4 weeks — need dose re-titration)
monitoring: HLA-B*1502 genotype BEFORE starting (mandatory in Asian populations)
Serum carbamazepine level: therapeutic 4-12 mcg/mL (re-check 2-4 weeks after initiation due to autoinduction)
CBC with differential (baseline, then monthly for first 2 months, then periodically)
Drug interaction review (potent CYP3A4 inducer — reduces levels of many drugs)
Pregnancy test in women of childbearing age
carbamazepine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic — neural tube defects (0.5-1%), craniofacial defects, fingernail hypoplasia, fetal carbamazepine syndrome. Lower risk than valproate but still significant. Supplement folic acid 5 mg/day. Levels drop during pregnancy (increased clearance) — monitor and adjust. Excreted in breast milk (concentration ~40% of maternal plasma) — generally compatible with breastfeeding but monitor infant for sedation, poor feeding.
quick_flags: HLA-B*1502 screening MANDATORY before starting in Asians (SJS/TEN risk)
Autoinduction: levels drop at 2-4 weeks — re-check levels and adjust dose
Potent CYP3A4 inducer — reduces OCP, warfarin, phenytoin, etc.
Hyponatremia/SIADH — monitor sodium especially in elderly
Can WORSEN absence, myoclonic, atonic seizures — avoid in generalized epilepsy (except GTCS)
Therapeutic level: 4-12 mcg/mL
Category D — teratogenic but less so than valproate

levetiracetam.mechanism: Binds to synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A) on presynaptic vesicles, modulating neurotransmitter release. Does not affect sodium channels, GABA, or glutamate directly. Reduces hypersynchronized neuronal firing. Broad-spectrum activity — effective for focal and generalized seizures. No hepatic CYP enzyme interactions — primarily renal excretion (66% unchanged).
indications: Focal (partial) epilepsy — with or without secondary generalization (monotherapy and adjunctive)
Seizure prophylaxis post-neurosurgery/TBI (off-label but widely used)

dosing.adult: Start 250-500 mg BD. Increase by 500 mg/day every 1-2 weeks. Maintenance: 1000-3000 mg/day in 2 divided doses. IV: same dose as oral (1:1 conversion), infuse over 15 minutes.
dosing.pediatric: 1 month to <6 months: 7 mg/kg BD, increase to 21 mg/kg BD. 6 months to <4 years: 10 mg/kg BD, increase to 25 mg/kg BD. 4-16 years: 10 mg/kg BD, increase to 30 mg/kg BD (max 3000 mg/day). Same titration schedule: increase every 2 weeks.
dosing.renal: CrCl >80: no adjustment. CrCl 50-80: 500-1000 mg BD. CrCl 30-50: 250-750 mg BD. CrCl <30: 250-500 mg BD. Post-hemodialysis supplemental dose: 250-500 mg.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed (renally eliminated). Severe hepatic impairment: 50% dose reduction recommended due to possible impaired renal function.

adverse_effects: Behavioral side effects — irritability, aggression, agitation ('Keppra rage' — ~13%)
Suicidal ideation (class warning for all AEDs — FDA black box)
monitoring: Behavioral and psychiatric status (irritability, aggression, depression — especially early treatment)
Renal function (for dose adjustment — CrCl-based dosing)
No routine drug level monitoring needed (no established therapeutic range, though >12 mcg/mL associated with toxicity)
levetiracetam.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited teratogenicity data — appears relatively safe compared to other AEDs. Registry data suggest low malformation rate. Among the safer AEDs in pregnancy (along with lamotrigine). Excreted in breast milk. Generally compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: NO CYP interactions — safest AED for drug interaction profiles
Renal dose adjustment required (CrCl-based dosing)
Behavioral side effects ('Keppra rage') — warn patients/families
No therapeutic drug monitoring routinely needed
1:1 IV-to-oral conversion
Broad-spectrum: focal + generalized seizures
Category C — relatively safe in pregnancy

oxcarbazepine.mechanism: Prodrug — rapidly converted to active metabolite 10-monohydroxy derivative (MHD/eslicarbazepine). MHD blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (similar to carbamazepine) and modulates high-voltage-activated calcium channels. Better tolerated than carbamazepine. Less autoinduction. Milder CYP enzyme induction than carbamazepine.
indications: Focal (partial) epilepsy — with or without secondary generalization (monotherapy and adjunctive)
contraindications: Cross-reactivity with carbamazepine allergy (25-30% — use with caution or avoid)

dosing.adult: Start 300 mg BD (600 mg/day). Increase by 300-600 mg/day at weekly intervals. Maintenance: 1200-2400 mg/day in 2 divided doses. Max: 2400 mg/day. Monotherapy conversion: start oxcarbazepine while simultaneously reducing other AED over 3-6 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Ages 2-16: Start 8-10 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses (max starting dose 600 mg/day). Increase over 2 weeks to target. Maintenance: 20-46 years: 900 mg/day; 25-30 kg: 900-1200 mg/day; 30-40 kg: 900-1500 mg/day; >40 kg: 1200-2400 mg/day.
dosing.renal: CrCl <30 mL/min: start at 50% of usual dose, titrate slowly. MHD is cleared renally.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment needed. Severe: limited data — use with caution.

adverse_effects: Hyponatremia (clinically significant in 2-3% — more common than with carbamazepine)
SJS/TEN (rare — HLA-B*1502 association less established than for CBZ but still screen)
monitoring: Serum sodium levels (baseline, at 2-4 weeks, then periodic — especially elderly, on diuretics, or on SIADH-causing drugs)
MHD levels if needed (reference range 12-35 mcg/mL — not routinely monitored)
Signs of hyponatremia (confusion, seizure worsening, lethargy)
Consider HLA-B*1502 screening in Asian populations (SJS/TEN risk)
Drug interactions (milder inducer of CYP3A4 than carbamazepine — but still reduces OCP efficacy)
oxcarbazepine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Less teratogenicity data than carbamazepine. Registry data suggest possible increased risk of malformations (similar to carbamazepine). Not as well-studied as lamotrigine or levetiracetam in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk. Supplement folic acid.
quick_flags: Hyponatremia risk — monitor sodium (especially elderly and with diuretics)
25-30% cross-reactivity with carbamazepine allergy
Less autoinduction than carbamazepine
Milder enzyme induction than CBZ (but still reduces OCP levels)
Active metabolite MHD is renally cleared — adjust if CrCl <30
Category C — less pregnancy data than CBZ/lamotrigine/levetiracetam

lamotrigine.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (presynaptic), stabilizing neuronal membranes and inhibiting release of excitatory neurotransmitters (primarily glutamate and aspartate). Also modulates high-voltage-activated calcium channels and HCN channels. Broad-spectrum anticonvulsant with mood-stabilizing properties (especially for bipolar depression).
indications: Bipolar disorder — maintenance (especially prevention of depressive episodes)
Myoclonic seizures (may worsen in some patients — use with caution)

dosing.adult: SLOW TITRATION REQUIRED (SJS/TEN risk). WITHOUT valproate: Weeks 1-2: 25 mg OD. Weeks 3-4: 50 mg OD. Week 5+: increase by 50 mg/day every 1-2 weeks. Maintenance: 100-200 mg/day (monotherapy) or 100-400 mg/day (with enzyme inducers). WITH VALPROATE: HALVE all doses — Weeks 1-2: 25 mg every OTHER day. Weeks 3-4: 25 mg OD. Maintenance: 100-200 mg/day. With enzyme INDUCERS (CBZ, phenytoin, phenobarbital) without valproate: DOUBLE the titration doses. Bipolar: same titration, target 200 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Ages 2-12: Without valproate: 0.3 mg/kg/day (wks 1-2), 0.6 mg/kg/day (wks 3-4), then increase q1-2wks to 4.5-7.5 mg/kg/day. With valproate: HALVE all doses. Max: 200 mg/day.
dosing.renal: Moderate-severe impairment: reduce maintenance dose. ESRD on dialysis: further reduce.
dosing.hepatic: Moderate (Child-Pugh B): reduce by 25%. Severe (Child-Pugh C): reduce by 50%.

adverse_effects: Rash (up to 10% — most common reason for discontinuation)
Stevens-Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis (0.08-0.8% — risk increases with rapid titration, high starting dose, or concomitant valproate)
Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH — very rare, can be fatal)
monitoring: Rash assessment at every visit (especially first 8 weeks — discontinue at first sign of any serious rash)
Drug level if desired: reference range 3-14 mcg/mL (not routinely required but useful for adherence, pregnancy, toxicity)
Adjust dose when adding or removing valproate (valproate DOUBLES lamotrigine levels) or enzyme inducers (HALVE lamotrigine levels)
Dose adjustment in pregnancy (levels drop significantly — monitor monthly)
Signs of aseptic meningitis (headache, fever, neck stiffness)
lamotrigine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Considered the SAFEST AED for epilepsy in pregnancy (relative to alternatives). North American AED Pregnancy Registry shows 2-3% major malformation rate (close to background). However, levels DROP significantly during pregnancy (increased glucuronidation) — monthly level monitoring and dose increases often needed. Post-partum: rapid level increase — dose reduction needed within days of delivery. Excreted in breast milk (significant levels — ~50% of maternal plasma). Generally compatible with breastfeeding — monitor infant.
quick_flags: SLOW TITRATION mandatory (SJS/TEN risk with rapid escalation)
Valproate DOUBLES lamotrigine levels — HALVE all doses when co-prescribed
Enzyme inducers HALVE lamotrigine levels — DOUBLE doses
Safest AED for epilepsy in pregnancy (Category C — relatively)
Levels drop in pregnancy — monthly monitoring needed
Rash is most common reason for discontinuation
Effective for bipolar depression prevention (unique among mood stabilizers)

topiramate.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: (1) Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels (state-dependent), (2) Enhances GABA activity at GABA-A receptors (novel binding site), (3) Antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, (4) Inhibits carbonic anhydrase (isoenzymes II and IV), (5) Reduces high-voltage calcium currents. Broad-spectrum AED. The carbonic anhydrase inhibition accounts for many of its side effects.
contraindications: Women of childbearing potential not using effective contraception
Metabolic acidosis + concurrent metformin (increased lactic acidosis risk)

dosing.adult: Epilepsy: Start 25 mg OD (or at bedtime). Increase by 25-50 mg/day weekly. Maintenance: 100-200 mg BD (monotherapy: 200-400 mg/day). Max: 400 mg/day (monotherapy) or 800 mg/day (adjunctive). Migraine prophylaxis: Start 25 mg OD. Increase by 25 mg/week. Target: 50 mg BD (100 mg/day). Titrate slowly — reduces cognitive and other side effects.
dosing.pediatric: Ages 2-16: Start 1-3 mg/kg/day (or 25 mg) at bedtime. Increase by 1-3 mg/kg/day q1-2 weeks. Maintenance: 5-9 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses.
dosing.renal: CrCl <70 mL/min: 50% of usual dose. Hemodialysis: supplemental dose after dialysis (topiramate is cleared by HD).
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Clearance may be reduced in hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Cognitive dysfunction — word-finding difficulty, concentration problems, psychomotor slowing ('Dopamax')
Weight loss (5-10% — unique among AEDs; useful in obese patients)
Nephrolithiasis/kidney stones (calcium phosphate — carbonic anhydrase inhibition, ~1.5%)
Acute angle-closure glaucoma and acute myopia (rare — idiosyncratic, occurs within first month)
monitoring: Serum bicarbonate (baseline and periodic — metabolic acidosis risk)
Renal function and urinary calcium (kidney stone prevention — adequate hydration essential)
Intraocular pressure if visual complaints (acute glaucoma risk)
Body temperature in children (oligohidrosis/hyperthermia — especially in hot environments)
Pregnancy test before initiation in women of childbearing age
Drug level if needed: reference range 5-20 mcg/mL (not routinely monitored)
topiramate.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic — increased risk of cleft lip and cleft palate (prevalence 1.4% vs 0.38-0.55% background). Also associated with small for gestational age infants. Effective contraception mandatory. Supplement folic acid. Excreted in breast milk. Limited data on breastfeeding safety.
quick_flags: WEIGHT LOSS effect — useful in obese epilepsy/migraine patients
Cognitive 'fogging' ('Dopamax') — word-finding difficulty common
Kidney stones (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) — ensure adequate hydration
Metabolic acidosis — monitor bicarbonate
Teratogenic: cleft lip/palate — Category D, effective contraception required
Migraine prophylaxis: target 50 mg BD (titrate slowly)
Acute glaucoma risk — emergent if sudden visual changes/eye pain

clobazam.mechanism: Unlike conventional 1,4-benzodiazepines, clobazam is a 1,5-benzodiazepine. Binds to GABA-A receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride conductance. Less sedating and less tolerance-prone than 1,4-benzodiazepines (clonazepam, diazepam). Active metabolite N-desmethylclobazam (norclobazam) also has anticonvulsant activity and is metabolized by CYP2C19.
indications: Adjunctive therapy for refractory epilepsy (focal and generalized)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clobazam or other benzodiazepines

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg OD or BD. Increase by 5 mg/day weekly based on response and tolerability. Maintenance: 10-20 mg/day in 1-2 divided doses. Max: 40 mg/day. Doses >5 mg: give in 2 divided doses. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers: start 5 mg OD, max 20 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Weight <=30 kg: Start 5 mg OD. Increase to 5 mg BD (week 2), then 10 mg BD if needed (max 20 mg/day). Weight >30 kg: Start 5 mg BD. Increase to 10 mg BD (week 2), then 20 mg BD if needed (max 40 mg/day). CYP2C19 poor metabolizers: halve the maximum dose.
dosing.renal: Mild-moderate: no adjustment. Severe: limited data — use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate (Child-Pugh A/B): Start 5 mg OD, titrate slowly, max 20 mg/day (moderate) or 40 mg/day (mild). Severe: CONTRAINDICATED.

adverse_effects: Aggression, irritability (paradoxical — more common in children)
Tolerance (less than classic benzodiazepines but still possible)
Respiratory depression (especially with opioids or other CNS depressants)
monitoring: CYP2C19 genotype if available (poor metabolizers have 5x higher norclobazam levels — dose reduction needed)
Respiratory status (especially with concurrent opioids/CNS depressants)
Signs of tolerance (increasing seizure frequency after initial control)
Do NOT abruptly discontinue — taper over minimum 1 week (withdrawal seizure risk)
clobazam.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Benzodiazepines as a class cross the placenta. Potential risks include neonatal sedation, withdrawal symptoms (floppy infant syndrome), and respiratory depression. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excreted in breast milk — monitor infant for sedation and poor feeding.
quick_flags: 1,5-benzodiazepine — LESS sedating than clonazepam
CYP2C19 poor metabolizers get 5x higher levels — genotype if possible, halve max dose
FDA-approved adjunctive for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome
Taper slowly on discontinuation (withdrawal seizure risk)
Less tolerance development than classic benzodiazepines
Category C — neonatal sedation/withdrawal risk

olanzapine.mechanism: Antagonist at multiple receptors including dopamine D2, serotonin 5-HT2A/2C, histamine H1, muscarinic M1-M5, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors. Broad receptor binding profile contributes to efficacy in psychosis and mania but also to metabolic and sedative side effects.
indications: Maintenance treatment of bipolar I disorder
Agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar mania (IM)
contraindications: IM form: do not co-administer with parenteral benzodiazepines (risk of excessive sedation, cardiorespiratory depression)

dosing.adult: Schizophrenia: start 5-10 mg once daily, target 10-20 mg/day. Bipolar mania: 10-15 mg once daily, max 20 mg/day. IM acute agitation: 10 mg IM, may repeat 5-10 mg after 2 hours (max 30 mg/day).
dosing.pediatric: Adolescents ≥13 years (schizophrenia/bipolar): start 2.5-5 mg once daily, target 10 mg/day (max 20 mg/day). Not recommended in children <13 years.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Start at lower dose (5 mg) and titrate cautiously in hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Significant weight gain (most among atypicals with clozapine)
monitoring: Fasting blood glucose and HbA1c at baseline, 12 weeks, then annually
Fasting lipid profile at baseline, 12 weeks, then annually
Body weight and BMI at each visit for first 6 months
Waist circumference at baseline and annually
olanzapine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Neonatal complications (extrapyramidal symptoms, withdrawal) reported with third-trimester exposure. Excreted in breast milk — weigh risks vs benefits of breastfeeding.
quick_flags: High metabolic risk — weight gain, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia
Very sedating — useful in agitated patients
IM form: never co-administer with IM benzodiazepines
Monitor fasting glucose and lipids regularly
Among the most weight-causing antipsychotics

forms: Risperdal Consta 25 mg IM depot (q2 weeks)
Risperdal Consta 37.5 mg IM depot (q2 weeks)
Risperdal Consta 50 mg IM depot (q2 weeks)
risperidone.mechanism: Potent antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors. Also blocks alpha-1 adrenergic and histamine H1 receptors. High D2 affinity at higher doses leads to more extrapyramidal effects. Strong prolactin elevation due to D2 blockade in tuberoinfundibular pathway.
indications: Short-term treatment of acute agitation in schizophrenia

dosing.adult: Schizophrenia: start 1 mg BD, increase by 1-2 mg/day at intervals ≥24 hours, target 2-6 mg/day (max 8 mg/day). Bipolar mania: start 2-3 mg once daily, adjust by 1 mg/day (range 1-6 mg/day). Risperdal Consta: 25 mg IM q2 weeks, may increase to 37.5-50 mg q2 weeks (continue oral for first 3 weeks after initial injection).
dosing.pediatric: Autism (≥5 years, 15-20 kg): start 0.25 mg/day; (>20 kg): start 0.5 mg/day. Titrate cautiously. Schizophrenia (≥13 years): start 0.5 mg once daily, target 1-3 mg/day.
dosing.renal: CrCl <30 mL/min: start 0.5 mg BD, titrate slowly (max 1.5 mg BD). Reduce depot dose accordingly.
dosing.hepatic: Start 0.5 mg BD, titrate slowly. Increased free fraction due to reduced albumin.

adverse_effects: Hyperprolactinemia (highest among atypicals — galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction)
Extrapyramidal symptoms at higher doses (>6 mg/day)
monitoring: Prolactin levels if symptomatic (menstrual irregularity, galactorrhea)
Fasting glucose and lipids at baseline and periodically
ECG if risk factors for QT prolongation
risperidone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Third-trimester exposure may cause neonatal extrapyramidal symptoms and withdrawal. Excreted in breast milk — risperidone and metabolite paliperidone both present.
quick_flags: Highest prolactin elevation among antipsychotics
EPS increases at doses >6 mg/day
Less metabolic effects than olanzapine but still significant
Depot (Consta) requires 3 weeks oral overlap after first injection
Active metabolite is paliperidone

haloperidol.mechanism: Potent dopamine D2 receptor antagonist with high binding affinity. Minimal activity at muscarinic, histamine H1, or alpha-1 adrenergic receptors compared to low-potency typicals. High D2 blockade leads to significant extrapyramidal effects and prolactin elevation.
contraindications: QT prolongation or history of cardiac arrhythmias (especially for IV use)
Dementia-related psychosis in elderly (FDA black box)

dosing.adult: Acute psychosis/agitation: 2-5 mg IM q4-8h (max 20 mg/day). Oral: start 0.5-5 mg BD-TDS, target 5-20 mg/day. Delirium (ICU): 0.5-2 mg IV, repeat q20-30 min PRN. Decanoate depot: 10-20× daily oral dose IM q4 weeks (max 450 mg/month).
dosing.pediatric: 3-12 years (>15 kg): 0.025-0.05 mg/kg/day divided BD-TDS. Tourette: 0.05-0.075 mg/kg/day. Not recommended under 3 years.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use lower doses and titrate cautiously.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment; extensively hepatically metabolized.

adverse_effects: High risk of extrapyramidal symptoms — acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism
QT prolongation (especially IV route — torsades de pointes)
Minimal sedation and weight gain (high-potency typical)
monitoring: ECG before and during IV use (QTc monitoring)
Extrapyramidal symptom assessment at each visit
Involuntary movement scales (AIMS) for tardive dyskinesia (q6 months)
Temperature and CK if NMS suspected
haloperidol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limb defects reported in some case reports (not confirmed in larger studies). Use lowest effective dose. Third-trimester: risk of neonatal EPS. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: HIGH EPS risk — always have benztropine/trihexyphenidyl available
IV use: QT prolongation — requires continuous ECG monitoring
NMS risk: fever + rigidity + AMS + elevated CK = medical emergency
Decanoate depot is different preparation from IV/IM — NEVER give decanoate IV
Drug of choice for acute agitation in many Indian ICU protocols

quetiapine.mechanism: Antagonist at serotonin 5-HT2A, dopamine D2, histamine H1, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors. Active metabolite norquetiapine also inhibits norepinephrine reuptake and is a partial 5-HT1A agonist (contributes to antidepressant effect). Low D2 occupancy — less EPS and prolactin elevation.
indications: Bipolar disorder — acute mania and depression
Insomnia (off-label, low dose — very commonly used in India)

dosing.adult: Schizophrenia: titrate from 25 mg BD on day 1 to 300-800 mg/day by day 4-7 (divided BD-TDS). Bipolar depression: day 1: 50 mg OD at bedtime, day 2: 100 mg, day 3: 200 mg, day 4: 300 mg. Insomnia (off-label): 25-100 mg at bedtime. XR: once daily dosing.
dosing.pediatric: Schizophrenia (≥13 years): titrate to 400-800 mg/day. Bipolar mania (≥10 years): titrate to 400-600 mg/day. Lower starting doses, slower titration.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Start 25 mg/day, titrate by 25-50 mg/day increments. Clearance reduced by 30% in hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Weight gain and metabolic effects (less than olanzapine but significant)
Cataract risk (seen in animal studies — lens exam recommended)
monitoring: Fasting glucose and lipid profile at baseline and periodically
Eye exam for cataracts at baseline and every 6 months (per some guidelines)
quetiapine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited human data. Neonatal withdrawal/EPS reported with third-trimester use. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Very sedating — H1 blockade dominant at low doses
Off-label insomnia use very common but controversial
Must titrate to avoid orthostatic hypotension
Less EPS and prolactin elevation than risperidone
Monitor thyroid function — can decrease free T4

aripiprazole.drug_class: Atypical Antipsychotic (Partial Dopamine D2 Agonist / Third-Generation Antipsychotic)
forms: 400 mg IM depot injection (Abilify Maintena — monthly)
aripiprazole.mechanism: Unique partial agonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT1A receptors, and antagonist at 5-HT2A receptors. Stabilizes dopamine activity — reduces excess dopamine in mesolimbic pathway (antipsychotic effect) while preserving dopamine function in other pathways (fewer EPS, less prolactin elevation, better cognition).
indications: Bipolar I disorder — acute mania and maintenance
Adjunct to antidepressants in major depressive disorder

dosing.adult: Schizophrenia: 10-15 mg once daily, target 10-30 mg/day. Bipolar mania: 15-30 mg once daily. MDD adjunct: start 2-5 mg/day, target 5-15 mg/day. IM depot: 400 mg monthly (continue oral 14 days after first injection). CYP2D6 poor metabolizers or with strong CYP2D6/3A4 inhibitors: reduce dose by 50%.
dosing.pediatric: Schizophrenia (≥13 years): start 2 mg/day, target 10 mg/day (max 30 mg). Bipolar (≥10 years): start 2 mg/day, target 10 mg/day. Autism irritability (6-17 years): start 2 mg/day, target 5-15 mg/day.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dosage adjustment required.

adverse_effects: Akathisia (most common and bothersome side effect)
Weight neutral (least weight gain among atypicals)
Minimal prolactin elevation (may even decrease prolactin)
Compulsive behaviors (pathological gambling, binge eating — rare but FDA-warned)
monitoring: Fasting glucose and lipid profile (though lowest metabolic risk)
aripiprazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Third-trimester exposure may cause neonatal EPS. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Unique partial D2 agonist — 'dopamine stabilizer'
Least metabolic effects among atypical antipsychotics
Akathisia is the most common side effect — manage with dose reduction or propranolol
Weight-neutral — preferred when metabolic risk is a concern
Can cause initial agitation/insomnia — start low
FDA warning: impulse control disorders (gambling, binge eating)

lithium.mechanism: Exact mechanism unclear. Modulates multiple intracellular signaling pathways: inhibits inositol monophosphatase (depletes IP3/DAG signaling), inhibits glycogen synthase kinase-3 beta (GSK-3β), modulates CREB and BDNF expression, affects sodium transport in neurons. Net effect: mood stabilization, neuroprotection, anti-suicidal properties.
indications: Bipolar disorder — maintenance (gold standard for relapse prevention)
contraindications: First trimester pregnancy if avoidable (Ebstein anomaly)

dosing.adult: Start 300 mg BD-TDS, titrate to achieve target serum level. Acute mania target: 0.8-1.2 mEq/L. Maintenance target: 0.6-0.8 mEq/L. Usual dose range: 900-1800 mg/day in divided doses. Levels drawn 12 hours post-dose (trough). Steady state in 5-7 days.
dosing.pediatric: ≥12 years: 600-1800 mg/day in divided doses, guided by serum levels. <12 years: 15-60 mg/kg/day divided TDS (limited data).
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-50 mL/min: 50-75% of normal dose. CrCl <10 mL/min: 25-50% of dose. Avoid if CrCl <30 (relative contraindication). Lithium is dialyzable.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment needed (not hepatically metabolized — entirely renally excreted).

adverse_effects: Polyuria and polydipsia (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — up to 40%)
GI effects (nausea, diarrhea — especially non-CR forms)
Chronic nephropathy (interstitial nephritis with long-term use)
Toxicity: coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, vomiting, seizures, coma
monitoring: Serum lithium levels: weekly during titration, then every 1-3 months (trough, 12h post-dose)
Renal function (BUN, creatinine) at baseline, every 2-3 months initially, then every 6 months
Thyroid function (TSH, free T4) at baseline, every 6 months
ECG at baseline in patients >40 years or with cardiac history
lithium.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. First trimester: risk of Ebstein anomaly (tricuspid valve malformation, risk ~0.1% vs 0.005% baseline — lower than historically thought but still elevated). If used in pregnancy, fetal echocardiography at 16-20 weeks. Levels fluctuate with physiological changes in pregnancy — requires close monitoring. Excreted in breast milk (30-50% of maternal serum) — generally not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: NARROW therapeutic index — toxicity starts at >1.5 mEq/L, lethal >2.5 mEq/L
DRUG INTERACTIONS: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazide diuretics all INCREASE lithium levels
Dehydration/sodium depletion → lithium toxicity (warn patients)
Causes hypothyroidism in 10-20% — check TSH regularly
Causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (polyuria/polydipsia)
Teratogenic: Ebstein anomaly (Category D)
ONLY psychiatric drug with proven anti-suicidal effect
Not hepatically metabolized — 100% renal excretion

chlorpromazine.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors (lower affinity than haloperidol). Also blocks histamine H1 (sedation), muscarinic M1 (anticholinergic), alpha-1 adrenergic (hypotension), and serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. Low potency typical — more sedation and autonomic effects, fewer EPS compared to haloperidol.

dosing.adult: Psychosis: start 25-75 mg/day in divided doses, increase to 200-800 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Usual: 25-200 mg TDS. Hiccups: 25-50 mg TDS-QDS. IM: 25-50 mg, may repeat in 1-4 hours (max 400 mg/day IM).
dosing.pediatric: ≥6 months: 0.5-1 mg/kg q4-6h. Older children: 1-2 mg/kg/day in divided doses. Not commonly used first-line in children.
dosing.renal: Use with caution; start with lower doses.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic disease. Metabolized hepatically — reduce dose in impairment.

adverse_effects: Orthostatic hypotension (alpha-1 blockade — can be severe)
Anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision)
Blue-grey skin and corneal discoloration (long-term high-dose use)
EPS (less than haloperidol but still present)
monitoring: Eye examination with prolonged use (corneal/lens deposits)
Involuntary movement assessment for tardive dyskinesia
chlorpromazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta. Neonatal jaundice and extrapyramidal effects reported. Use only if necessary. Excreted in breast milk — may cause sedation in nursing infant.
quick_flags: Low-potency typical: more sedation + hypotension, less EPS
Classic drug for intractable hiccups
Photosensitivity — advise sunscreen and sun avoidance
Blue-grey skin discoloration with chronic high-dose use
Orthostatic hypotension can be severe — titrate slowly
IM injection: NEVER give IV (severe hypotension)

clozapine.mechanism: Unique receptor binding profile: relatively low D2 affinity but strong 5-HT2A, D4, muscarinic M1/M4, histamine H1, and alpha-1/alpha-2 adrenergic blockade. High D4:D2 selectivity ratio. This unique profile explains efficacy in treatment-resistant schizophrenia and near absence of EPS and tardive dyskinesia.
indications: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia (ONLY approved indication — after failure of ≥2 adequate antipsychotic trials)
Reduction of suicidal behavior in schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder
Psychosis in Parkinson disease (off-label, low dose)
contraindications: History of clozapine-induced agranulocytosis or severe granulocytopenia
Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) below threshold per monitoring protocol

dosing.adult: Start 12.5 mg once or twice daily, increase by 25-50 mg/day every 1-2 days. Target: 300-450 mg/day in divided doses. Max: 900 mg/day. Therapeutic level: generally >350 ng/mL. Titrate slowly to reduce seizure and hypotension risk. If discontinued >48 hours, must retitrate from 12.5 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data. Adolescents with treatment-resistant schizophrenia: same titration as adults but slower, lower target doses (200-400 mg/day).
dosing.renal: Use with caution in renal impairment. Dose reduction may be needed.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment. Reduce dose and monitor closely in moderate impairment.

adverse_effects: Agranulocytosis (1-2%) — potentially fatal, requires mandatory ANC monitoring
Seizures (dose-dependent: 3% at <300 mg, 5% at 300-600 mg, 10% at >600 mg)
Myocarditis and cardiomyopathy (especially in first 2 months)
Severe metabolic syndrome (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia — highest metabolic risk with olanzapine)
Severe constipation (can progress to ileus — potentially fatal)
monitoring: MANDATORY ANC monitoring: weekly for first 6 months → biweekly for next 6 months → monthly thereafter
If ANC <1500/μL (general population) or <1000/μL (benign ethnic neutropenia): hold and follow protocol
Fasting glucose, HbA1c, and lipids at baseline, 3 months, then annually
ECG and troponin if myocarditis suspected (fever, tachycardia, chest pain in first 2 months)
Bowel function monitoring (constipation can be fatal)
clozapine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data but no clear teratogenicity. Neonatal complications (sedation, agitation) possible. Excreted in breast milk. Use only if treatment-resistant schizophrenia requires continuation.
quick_flags: ONLY for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failed ≥2 antipsychotics)
MANDATORY blood monitoring for agranulocytosis (ANC protocol)
Weekly CBC × 6 months → biweekly × 6 months → monthly lifelong
Myocarditis risk — monitor for fever + tachycardia in first 2 months
Constipation can be FATAL (bowel obstruction/ileus)
Seizure risk dose-dependent — consider prophylactic valproate at high doses
Must retitrate if missed >48 hours
Near-zero risk of tardive dyskinesia

sertraline.mechanism: Selectively inhibits presynaptic serotonin (5-HT) reuptake transporter (SERT), increasing serotonin in the synaptic cleft. Also has mild dopamine reuptake inhibition at higher doses. Minimal effect on norepinephrine, muscarinic, histaminic, or adrenergic receptors.
contraindications: Concurrent MAOI use (or within 14 days — risk of serotonin syndrome)
Concurrent use with disulfiram (oral concentrate contains alcohol)

dosing.adult: Depression/OCD: start 50 mg once daily, increase by 50 mg at weekly intervals, max 200 mg/day. Panic/PTSD/Social anxiety: start 25 mg once daily, increase to 50 mg after 1 week, then titrate (max 200 mg/day). PMDD: 50-150 mg daily or during luteal phase only.
dosing.pediatric: OCD (6-12 years): start 25 mg once daily, max 200 mg. OCD (13-17 years): start 50 mg once daily, max 200 mg. Depression: not FDA-approved in children but used off-label.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50% in hepatic impairment. Avoid in severe hepatic disease.

adverse_effects: GI effects (nausea, diarrhea, dyspepsia — most common initially)
Sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, anorgasmia, delayed ejaculation)
Suicidality risk in young adults <25 (FDA black box)
monitoring: Symptom response (PHQ-9 or equivalent) at 2, 4, 6, 8 weeks
Suicidality assessment especially in first weeks and in patients <25 years
Sodium levels in elderly or those on diuretics
Bleeding events if co-prescribed with NSAIDs/anticoagulants
sertraline.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. SSRIs in third trimester: risk of persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn (PPHN) and neonatal adaptation syndrome. Sertraline is considered SAFEST SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding (lowest milk-to-plasma ratio among SSRIs). Often first-line SSRI when treatment is needed during pregnancy.
quick_flags: Safest SSRI in cardiac patients and pregnancy/breastfeeding
Fewer CYP drug interactions than fluoxetine or paroxetine
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) common initially — usually resolve in 1-2 weeks
Mild dopamine reuptake inhibition may provide activating effect
FDA black box: suicidality risk in patients <25 years

fluoxetine.mechanism: Potent and selective inhibitor of serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT). Active metabolite norfluoxetine is also a potent SERT inhibitor with extremely long half-life. Strong CYP2D6 inhibitor — significant drug interaction potential.
indications: Bipolar depression (in combination with olanzapine — Symbyax)
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAOI use (14 days before; 5 weeks washout after stopping fluoxetine due to long half-life)

dosing.adult: Depression: start 20 mg once daily in the morning, may increase after several weeks, max 80 mg/day. OCD: 20-60 mg/day (may need higher doses). Bulimia: 60 mg/day (target dose). Panic: start 10 mg/day, target 20-60 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Depression (≥8 years): start 10 mg/day, may increase to 20 mg/day after 1 week. OCD (≥7 years): start 10 mg/day, target 20-60 mg/day.
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate. Reduce dose or frequency in severe renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50%. Lower dose or less frequent dosing (e.g., alternate days) in cirrhosis.

adverse_effects: Weight loss initially (may gain weight with long-term use)
Suicidality risk in young adults <25 (FDA black box)
fluoxetine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Associated with low risk of cardiac malformations in some studies (controversial). Third trimester: PPHN and neonatal adaptation syndrome. Longest washout needed (5 weeks) before switching to MAOIs. Excreted in breast milk — less preferred than sertraline during breastfeeding due to long half-life.
quick_flags: LONGEST half-life of all SSRIs: fluoxetine 1-3 days, norfluoxetine 4-16 days
5-week washout needed before starting MAOI
Strong CYP2D6 inhibitor — many drug interactions
Most activating SSRI — give in the morning
Only SSRI approved for bulimia nervosa
Least discontinuation syndrome due to long half-life

amitriptyline.mechanism: Inhibits reuptake of both serotonin and norepinephrine. Also blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (anticholinergic), histamine H1 receptors (sedation, weight gain), alpha-1 adrenergic receptors (orthostatic hypotension), and sodium channels (cardiac conduction). Active metabolite: nortriptyline.
indications: Nocturnal enuresis in children (rarely used now)
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAOI use (within 14 days)
Acute myocardial infarction or recovery phase
Bundle branch block or cardiac conduction defects
Hypersensitivity to amitriptyline or other TCAs

dosing.adult: Depression: start 25 mg at bedtime, increase by 25 mg every 3-7 days, target 75-150 mg/day (max 300 mg/day for inpatients). Neuropathic pain: start 10-25 mg at bedtime, titrate by 10-25 mg weekly, max 75 mg. Migraine prophylaxis: 10-25 mg at bedtime, may increase to 50-75 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Depression: not recommended in children <12 years. Adolescents: start 10 mg TDS + 20 mg at bedtime (rarely used — SSRIs preferred). Enuresis (≥6 years): 10-25 mg at bedtime.
dosing.renal: Use with caution; metabolites may accumulate. No specific adjustment but start low.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment. Extensively hepatically metabolized.

adverse_effects: Anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, tachycardia)
Sedation (most sedating TCA — give at bedtime)
Cardiac: QT prolongation, arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities
LETHAL in overdose (cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, coma — as few as 10 days' supply can be fatal)
monitoring: ECG before starting (especially in patients >40 years, cardiac history)
TCA plasma levels if poor response or toxicity suspected (therapeutic range: combined amitriptyline + nortriptyline 100-250 ng/mL)
amitriptyline.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. TCAs cross placenta. Neonatal withdrawal symptoms reported. Limb reduction defects in some early studies (not confirmed). Excreted in breast milk. SSRIs generally preferred in pregnancy.
quick_flags: LETHAL in overdose — limit supply in suicidal patients (1 week at a time)
Most anticholinergic and sedating TCA — give entirely at bedtime
Low dose (10-25 mg) widely used in India for neuropathic pain and migraine prophylaxis
ECG mandatory before starting in cardiac-risk patients
Avoid in elderly (Beers list) — high anticholinergic burden
Tryptomer 10 mg at bedtime is one of the most prescribed drugs in India

venlafaxine.mechanism: Dose-dependent dual reuptake inhibition: at low doses (37.5-75 mg) primarily serotonin reuptake inhibition; at moderate-high doses (≥150 mg) adds norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. At very high doses (≥225 mg), weak dopamine reuptake inhibition. Active metabolite: O-desmethylvenlafaxine (desvenlafaxine).
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAOI use (within 14 days)

dosing.adult: Depression: start 37.5-75 mg once daily (XR) or in divided doses (IR), increase by 75 mg every 4-7 days, usual 75-225 mg/day, max 375 mg/day. GAD: 75-225 mg/day (XR). Panic: start 37.5 mg/day × 7 days, then 75 mg, max 225 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Not FDA-approved. Off-label in adolescents: start 37.5 mg/day, titrate to 75-150 mg/day. Limited data.
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-70 mL/min: reduce total daily dose by 25-50%. Hemodialysis: reduce by 50%, give after dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: reduce dose by 50%. Severe: reduce by >50%. Avoid in severe hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Dose-dependent hypertension (monitor BP — affects 3-13% at higher doses)
Discontinuation syndrome (SEVERE — among worst of any antidepressant)
Suicidality risk in young adults <25 (FDA black box)
monitoring: Blood pressure at baseline and regularly (dose-dependent hypertension)
venlafaxine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Third trimester: neonatal discontinuation syndrome and PPHN risk. Excreted in breast milk. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: Dose-dependent hypertension — MUST monitor BP
SEVERE discontinuation syndrome — NEVER stop abruptly, taper over 2-4 weeks minimum
Dose-dependent mechanism: serotonin at low dose, + norepinephrine at ≥150 mg
XR form preferred (better GI tolerability, once-daily dosing)
Nausea common initially — usually resolves in 1-2 weeks
Active metabolite is desvenlafaxine (available as separate drug)

duloxetine.mechanism: Potent and balanced inhibitor of both serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake. Unlike venlafaxine, achieves dual reuptake inhibition even at starting doses. Weak inhibitor of dopamine reuptake. Dual mechanism in both CNS and descending pain pathways makes it effective for pain conditions.
indications: Stress urinary incontinence (off-label, approved in some countries)
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAOI use (within 14 days)
Severe hepatic impairment or chronic liver disease
Concurrent use with potent CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin)

dosing.adult: Depression/GAD: 60 mg once daily (can start at 30 mg × 1 week for tolerability). Diabetic neuropathy: 60 mg once daily. Fibromyalgia: start 30 mg once daily × 1 week, then 60 mg once daily. Chronic pain: 60 mg once daily. Max: 120 mg/day (limited additional benefit above 60 mg).
dosing.pediatric: GAD (≥7 years): start 30 mg once daily × 2 weeks, then 30-60 mg once daily. Max 120 mg/day. Not recommended <7 years.
dosing.renal: CrCl <30 mL/min: AVOID (metabolites accumulate significantly). No adjustment for mild-moderate renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: AVOID in any hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh A, B, or C). Risk of hepatotoxicity.

adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity (rare but serious — avoid in liver disease)
Suicidality risk in young adults <25 (FDA black box)
monitoring: Blood pressure at baseline and periodically
Hepatic function (LFTs) at baseline and if symptoms arise
duloxetine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Third trimester: neonatal complications. Limited data. Excreted in breast milk. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: AVOID in severe renal (CrCl <30) and ANY hepatic impairment
Unlike venlafaxine, balanced SNRI even at starting dose of 60 mg
First-line for diabetic neuropathic pain and fibromyalgia
Nausea most common side effect — starting at 30 mg for 1 week helps
Less hypertension risk than venlafaxine
Used off-label for stress urinary incontinence (approved indication in EU/India)

mirtazapine.mechanism: Blocks presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors (increases norepinephrine and serotonin release). Blocks 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, and 5-HT3 receptors (reduces sexual dysfunction and nausea). Potent histamine H1 antagonist (sedation, appetite stimulation). Paradox: at 15 mg, histamine blockade predominates (more sedating); at 30-45 mg, noradrenergic activation partially counteracts sedation (less sedating at higher doses).
indications: Depression with insomnia and/or poor appetite (especially useful)
Depression in elderly and medically ill (well tolerated)
contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAOI use (within 14 days)

dosing.adult: Start 15 mg at bedtime, may increase every 1-2 weeks, usual 15-45 mg/day. Max: 45 mg/day. Give at bedtime due to sedation. Dose-response paradox: 15 mg is MORE sedating than 30 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Not FDA-approved for children. Off-label in adolescents: start 7.5-15 mg at bedtime, titrate cautiously.
dosing.renal: CrCl <40 mL/min: clearance decreased by 30% — use lower doses. CrCl <10: clearance decreased by 50%.
dosing.hepatic: Clearance decreased by ~30% in hepatic impairment. Start low, titrate slowly.

adverse_effects: Weight gain and increased appetite (significant — most among non-TCA antidepressants)
monitoring: Weight and BMI at each visit
CBC if unexplained fever, sore throat, or infection (rare agranulocytosis)
mirtazapine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Appears relatively safe in limited studies. Excreted in breast milk. Use if benefits outweigh risks.
quick_flags: PARADOX: MORE sedating at 15 mg than at 30-45 mg (histamine blockade predominates at low doses)
Significant weight gain and appetite stimulation — useful in underweight, elderly, cachexia
NO sexual dysfunction — unique advantage over SSRIs/SNRIs
NO nausea — 5-HT3 blockade (can be added to SSRI to counter nausea)
Give at bedtime due to sedation
Good choice in elderly/medically ill depressed patients with poor appetite and insomnia

bupropion.mechanism: Inhibits reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine (weak inhibitor). NO serotonergic activity — unique among antidepressants. Active metabolite hydroxybupropion also contributes to pharmacological effect.
