Exact mechanism in smoking cessation unclear but likely involves dopaminergic reward pathway modulation and nicotinic receptor antagonism.
indications: Sexual dysfunction caused by SSRIs (adjunct, off-label)
Weight loss (in combination with naltrexone — Contrave)
contraindications: Current or prior diagnosis of bulimia or anorexia nervosa (high seizure risk)
Abrupt discontinuation of alcohol or benzodiazepines
Concurrent use of other bupropion-containing products

dosing.adult: Depression (SR): start 150 mg once daily in the morning × 3-4 days, then 150 mg BD (at least 8 hours apart), max 400 mg/day. Depression (XL): 150 mg once daily in the morning, may increase to 300 mg once daily after 4 days, max 450 mg/day. Smoking cessation (SR): start 150 mg once daily × 3 days, then 150 mg BD × 7-12 weeks. Start 1-2 weeks before quit date.
dosing.pediatric: Not FDA-approved. Off-label ADHD in adolescents: 100-150 mg BD.
dosing.renal: CrCl <90 mL/min: consider reducing dose/frequency. Metabolites accumulate in renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh A): no adjustment or use with caution. Moderate-severe: reduce dose (SR: max 100 mg/day or 150 mg every other day; XL: max 150 mg every other day).

adverse_effects: Insomnia (most common — give last dose before 5 PM)
monitoring: Seizure risk assessment (history, alcohol use, concomitant medications)
bupropion.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. One registry did not show increased malformations. May be considered if SSRI alternatives are not suitable. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: NO sexual dysfunction — unique among antidepressants
CONTRAINDICATED in seizure disorders, eating disorders (bulimia/anorexia), acute alcohol/benzo withdrawal
Seizure risk dose-dependent — never exceed max dose
Weight neutral or causes weight LOSS
Also marketed as Zyban for smoking cessation
No serotonergic activity — can be combined with SSRIs safely
Give last dose before 5 PM (insomnia)

carbimazole.mechanism: Prodrug — rapidly converted to methimazole (active form) in the body. Methimazole inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), blocking iodine oxidation and organification, and coupling of iodotyrosines. This prevents synthesis of T3 and T4. Does NOT affect release of preformed thyroid hormones — takes 2-6 weeks for clinical effect as stored hormone is depleted.
indications: Toxic multinodular goiter / toxic adenoma (pretreatment before RAI or surgery)
contraindications: Pre-existing agranulocytosis or severe bone marrow suppression
First trimester of pregnancy if alternative (PTU) is available

dosing.adult: Graves disease: start 15-40 mg/day in 2-3 divided doses (severe: 40-60 mg). Maintenance: 5-15 mg once daily. Two strategies: (1) Block-replace: high dose carbimazole (40 mg) + levothyroxine; (2) Titration: gradually reduce based on TFTs. Duration: typically 12-18 months then trial off therapy.
dosing.pediatric: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day divided BD-TDS. Usual: 5-15 mg/day depending on age and severity. Titrate based on TFTs.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Rare cholestatic jaundice reported.

adverse_effects: Agranulocytosis (0.1-0.5%) — IDIOSYNCRATIC, usually in first 3 months. Instruct patients: immediate WBC if fever or sore throat
Aplasia cutis (scalp defect) in fetus — teratogenic
monitoring: TFTs (TSH, free T4, free T3): every 4-6 weeks during titration, then every 3 months
Urgent WBC and differential if ANY symptoms of infection (fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers)
LFTs at baseline and if symptomatic
TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) before stopping to predict relapse
carbimazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category D (teratogenic). Associated with aplasia cutis (scalp defect) and choanal atresia (methimazole embryopathy). SWITCH to propylthiouracil (PTU) in first trimester. Can resume carbimazole/methimazole in second trimester (lower hepatotoxicity risk than PTU). Compatible with breastfeeding at doses ≤20 mg/day.
quick_flags: Prodrug converted to methimazole (active form)
Agranulocytosis 0.1-0.5%: URGENT CBC if fever or sore throat
TERATOGENIC: switch to PTU in 1st trimester pregnancy
Takes 2-6 weeks for clinical effect (depletes stored thyroid hormone)
Relapse rate 40-50% after stopping 18-month course
Block-replace vs titration regimen — both valid approaches

propylthiouracil.mechanism: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO) — same as methimazole/carbimazole, blocking thyroid hormone synthesis. UNIQUE additional action: inhibits peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 by blocking type 1 deiodinase. This dual action makes PTU preferred in thyroid storm. Higher protein binding means less placental transfer than methimazole.
indications: Hyperthyroidism in first trimester of pregnancy (preferred over carbimazole)
Thyroid storm (preferred — also blocks T4→T3 conversion)

dosing.adult: Graves disease: start 100-200 mg TDS (total 300-600 mg/day). Maintenance: 50-100 mg BD-TDS. Thyroid storm: loading 500-1000 mg, then 250 mg every 4 hours. Duration: 12-18 months. Shorter half-life than methimazole — requires multiple daily doses (TDS).
dosing.pediatric: 5-10 mg/kg/day divided TDS (typically 50-100 mg TDS depending on age). Not first-line in children (hepatotoxicity risk).
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment.
dosing.hepatic: USE WITH EXTREME CAUTION — hepatotoxicity is major risk. Avoid if possible in pre-existing liver disease.

adverse_effects: HEPATOTOXICITY — FDA BLACK BOX WARNING. PTU hepatitis can be fulminant and fatal (liver failure requiring transplant). Risk: ~1 in 10,000
ANCA-positive vasculitis (more common than with methimazole)
monitoring: TFTs every 4-6 weeks during titration, then every 3 months
LFTs monthly for first 6 months, or immediately if symptoms (jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain, anorexia)
Urgent CBC if fever, sore throat, or mouth ulcers
Discontinue immediately if transaminases rise >3× ULN or jaundice develops
propylthiouracil.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Less teratogenic than carbimazole/methimazole in first trimester (no aplasia cutis). PREFERRED in first trimester. Switch to carbimazole in second trimester (PTU hepatotoxicity risk higher with prolonged use). Compatible with breastfeeding — minimal excretion (high protein binding).
quick_flags: FDA BLACK BOX: hepatotoxicity — can cause fatal liver failure
PREFERRED in: (1) First trimester pregnancy, (2) Thyroid storm
Unique: blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion (type 1 deiodinase)
Reserved for specific situations — carbimazole preferred otherwise due to safety
TDS dosing required (shorter half-life than methimazole)
Switch to carbimazole after 1st trimester in pregnancy
Cross-reactivity: ~50% with carbimazole allergy

rabeprazole.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) on gastric parietal cells, blocking the final step of acid secretion. Unlike omeprazole, rabeprazole undergoes predominantly non-enzymatic conversion to its active form — less dependent on CYP2C19 polymorphisms, providing more consistent acid suppression across genotypes.
indications: Helicobacter pylori eradication (part of triple/quadruple therapy)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other PPIs

dosing.adult: GERD: 20 mg once daily before breakfast × 4-8 weeks. Maintenance: 10-20 mg once daily. Duodenal ulcer: 20 mg once daily × 4 weeks. H. pylori eradication: 20 mg BD × 7-14 days (with antibiotics). Zollinger-Ellison: 60 mg once daily, adjust as needed (max 120 mg/day).
dosing.pediatric: ≥12 years: 20 mg once daily for GERD × 8 weeks. 1-11 years (≥15 kg): 5-10 mg once daily.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment. Severe: use with caution (limited data).

adverse_effects: Long-term risks: hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, iron and calcium malabsorption
Increased risk of Clostridioides difficile infection
Bone fracture risk with long-term use (hip, wrist, spine)
Potential increased risk of chronic kidney disease (long-term)
monitoring: Magnesium levels with long-term use (>1 year)
Vitamin B12 levels with long-term use
Bone density consideration in at-risk patients on long-term therapy
Review ongoing need — avoid indefinite use without indication
rabeprazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data. Animal studies show no harm. Use only if clearly needed. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Less CYP2C19 dependent than omeprazole — more predictable across genotypes
Give 30 minutes before breakfast for optimal efficacy
Long-term risks: hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, fractures, C. diff
Reassess need for ongoing PPI therapy regularly
One of the most prescribed PPIs in India

metoclopramide.mechanism: Central: blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (antiemetic). Peripheral: enhances acetylcholine release from myenteric neurons (prokinetic) — increases gastric motility, accelerates gastric emptying, increases lower esophageal sphincter tone, relaxes pylorus and duodenum. Also weak 5-HT4 agonist and 5-HT3 antagonist at higher doses.
indications: Prevention of chemotherapy-induced nausea (high dose — largely replaced by ondansetron)
contraindications: Concurrent use with drugs causing extrapyramidal reactions

dosing.adult: Nausea/gastroparesis: 10 mg TDS, 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime. Max duration: 5 days (EU recommendation) to 12 weeks (FDA). IV: 10 mg over 1-2 minutes (give slowly to reduce dystonia risk). Max: 30 mg/day or 0.5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.pediatric: 0.1-0.15 mg/kg per dose (max 10 mg), up to TDS. Avoid in children <1 year. Higher risk of EPS in children and young adults.
dosing.renal: CrCl <40 mL/min: reduce dose by 50%. Start with 5 mg TDS.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment (reduced clearance).

adverse_effects: Extrapyramidal symptoms — acute dystonia (especially in young women and children), akathisia, parkinsonism
Tardive dyskinesia (with chronic use — may be IRREVERSIBLE — FDA black box)
monitoring: Involuntary movement assessment (AIMS scale) with prolonged use
Duration of therapy — limit to shortest possible
metoclopramide.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. No evidence of teratogenicity. Used for hyperemesis gravidarum in some settings. Excreted in breast milk — may increase milk production (galactagogue effect) but also exposes infant.
quick_flags: Extrapyramidal side effects — especially dystonia in young women (give IV diphenhydramine/benztropine as rescue)
Limit duration: EU max 5 days; avoid chronic use
Tardive dyskinesia risk with chronic use — FDA black box
IV: give SLOWLY over 1-2 min (rapid injection increases dystonia risk)
Crosses BBB — central side effects unlike domperidone
Increases prolactin — galactorrhea, amenorrhea

sucralfate.mechanism: In acidic environment (pH <4), forms a viscous paste that binds to ulcer base proteins, creating a protective barrier against acid, pepsin, and bile salts. Also stimulates prostaglandin and bicarbonate secretion and promotes mucosal blood flow.
contraindications: Concurrent fluoroquinolone or tetracycline use (impairs absorption)

dosing.adult: Active ulcer: 1 g four times daily (1 hour before meals and at bedtime) for 4-8 weeks. Maintenance: 1 g twice daily.
dosing.pediatric: 40-80 mg/kg/day divided into 4 doses.
dosing.renal: Avoid in severe CKD/dialysis patients (aluminum toxicity risk).
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment required.

monitoring: Aluminum levels in renal impairment patients
sucralfate.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Minimal systemic absorption. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Must be taken on EMPTY stomach, 1 hour before meals — acid activates it
Separate from other medications by at least 2 hours (binds many drugs)
Contains aluminum — AVOID in dialysis patients (aluminum encephalopathy)
Shake suspension well before use

lactulose.mechanism: Synthetic disaccharide (galactose + fructose) not absorbed in small intestine. In colon, metabolized by bacteria to lactic acid, acetic acid, and other short-chain fatty acids. This: (1) Osmotic effect — draws water into colon (laxative). (2) Acidifies colonic contents (pH drops to ~5) — converts absorbable ammonia (NH3) to non-absorbable ammonium (NH4+), trapping it in colon for fecal excretion. This is the basis for hepatic encephalopathy treatment.
indications: Hepatic encephalopathy (acute and maintenance — mainstay of treatment)

dosing.adult: Constipation: 15-30 mL (10-20 g) once or twice daily, adjust to produce 2-3 soft stools per day. Hepatic encephalopathy (acute): 30-45 mL every 1-2 hours until initial bowel movement, then 30-45 mL TDS (target 2-3 soft stools/day). Rectal (enema): 300 mL in 700 mL water, retain for 30-60 minutes.
dosing.pediatric: Constipation: Infants: 5 mL/day. Children 1-6 years: 5-10 mL/day. Children 7-14 years: 10-15 mL/day. Hepatic encephalopathy: 40-90 mL/day divided TDS-QDS.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Primary use IS for hepatic impairment — no contraindication. Dose titrated to clinical response.

adverse_effects: Flatulence and bloating (most common — often limits compliance)
Electrolyte imbalance with excessive diarrhea (hyponatremia, hypokalemia)
Hyperglycemia (contains galactose and lactose — caution in diabetics)
monitoring: Stool frequency and consistency (target 2-3 soft stools/day for hepatic encephalopathy)
Serum electrolytes (sodium, potassium) with prolonged use or diarrhea
lactulose.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Minimal systemic absorption. Considered safe in pregnancy. Commonly used for pregnancy-related constipation. Not expected to be present in breast milk.
quick_flags: Hepatic encephalopathy mainstay: acidifies colon → traps ammonia as NH4+
Target 2-3 soft stools per day in hepatic encephalopathy
Flatulence is main compliance issue — usually decreases with time
Can be given as retention enema if oral route not possible
Safe in pregnancy (minimal absorption)
May take 24-48 hours for laxative effect (not for acute constipation relief)

loperamide.mechanism: Acts on mu-opioid receptors in gut wall to slow motility and increase water absorption

dosing.adult: 4 mg initially, then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day)
dosing.pediatric: 6-8 years: 2 mg twice daily; 9-12 years: 2 mg three times daily
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

loperamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Generally avoided in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Never use in dysentery/bloody diarrhea
Stop if no improvement in 48h

ursodeoxycholic_acid.mechanism: Hydrophilic bile acid that displaces toxic hydrophobic bile acids from the bile acid pool (reduces proportion of deoxycholic and chenodeoxycholic acid). Multiple mechanisms: (1) Choleresis — increases bile flow; (2) Cytoprotection — stabilizes hepatocyte membranes; (3) Immunomodulation — reduces MHC class I and II expression on hepatocytes; (4) Anti-apoptotic effects; (5) Reduces cholesterol saturation in bile (gallstone dissolution). Net effect: hepatoprotective, improves bile composition.
indications: Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) — first-line, only proven disease-modifying agent
Gallstone dissolution (radiolucent cholesterol stones <15 mm in functioning gallbladder)
Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) — widely used in India
Primary sclerosing cholangitis (controversial — no proven benefit on survival)
Prevention of gallstones during rapid weight loss
contraindications: Calcified or radiopaque gallstones (will not dissolve)

dosing.adult: Gallstone dissolution: 8-12 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses (with food). Duration: 6-24 months. PBC: 13-15 mg/kg/day in 2-4 divided doses with meals. ICP (cholestasis of pregnancy): 10-15 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses (usual 300 mg BD-TDS, up to 1500 mg/day). Gallstone prevention: 300 mg BD.
dosing.pediatric: 10-15 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses. Biliary atresia: 10-20 mg/kg/day (post-Kasai procedure). Cystic fibrosis liver disease: 20-30 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No dosage adjustment required (primarily biliary excretion).
dosing.hepatic: Used therapeutically for hepatic conditions. No specific adjustment needed.

adverse_effects: Very well tolerated overall — one of the safest hepatic drugs
Calcification of gallstones (rare — reducing dissolution potential)
monitoring: LFTs at baseline and every 1-3 months for PBC
Ultrasound every 6 months for gallstone dissolution (assess size reduction)
Bile acid levels in cholestasis of pregnancy
ursodeoxycholic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Widely used in intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy — considered safe and effective. Reduces pruritus and bile acid levels. Improves fetal outcomes (reduces risk of preterm delivery and fetal distress). Minimal data on breastfeeding — likely safe given minimal systemic exposure.
quick_flags: Widely used in India for ICP (intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy)
Only proven disease-modifying drug for PBC
Very safe drug — well tolerated even long-term
Gallstone dissolution: only works on radiolucent cholesterol stones <15 mm
Give with food to enhance absorption
One of the most commonly prescribed hepatoprotective drugs in India

mesalamine.mechanism: Acts topically on colonic mucosa. Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX) and lipoxygenase pathways locally, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis. Also inhibits NF-κB activation, scavenges reactive oxygen species, and inhibits TNF-α production. Different formulations deliver drug to different GI locations: Pentasa (small bowel + colon), Asacol/Mesacol (terminal ileum + colon), suppository (rectum), enema (left colon).
indications: Ulcerative colitis — induction and maintenance of remission (first-line for mild-moderate disease)
Crohn disease — mild ileal or ileocolonic (less effective than in UC)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to mesalamine, other salicylates, or any component

dosing.adult: Active UC: 2.4-4.8 g/day orally in divided doses or once daily (newer high-dose formulations). Maintenance: 1.2-2.4 g/day. Distal/left-sided colitis: add topical therapy — enema (1-4 g at bedtime) or suppository (500 mg-1 g BD) for proctitis. Combined oral + topical is more effective than either alone.
dosing.pediatric: 50-75 mg/kg/day divided BD-TDS (max 4.8 g/day). Weight-based dosing for children ≥5 years.
dosing.renal: Avoid in severe renal impairment. Use with caution in moderate impairment — monitor creatinine.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment. Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea (may mimic flare)
Interstitial nephritis (rare but serious — long-term use)
Acute intolerance syndrome (paradoxical worsening — cramping, bloody diarrhea — 3%)
monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine) at baseline, 3 months, then every 6-12 months long-term
CBC and LFTs at baseline and periodically
mesalamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Considered safe in pregnancy — should be continued to prevent flares (UC flares during pregnancy carry significant risk). Low concentrations in breast milk. Generally compatible with breastfeeding. Dibutyl phthalate (DBP) coating in some formulations is a theoretical concern — use DBP-free formulations in pregnancy if possible.
quick_flags: First-line for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis
Combined oral + topical (enema/suppository) more effective than oral alone for left-sided/distal UC
Monitor renal function long-term (interstitial nephritis)
Acute intolerance syndrome: paradoxical worsening — mimics flare (3%), stop drug
Different formulations target different GI segments — choose based on disease location
SAFE in pregnancy — continue to prevent flares (flares more harmful than medication)

cefixime_ofloxacin.mechanism: Cefixime inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs). Ofloxacin inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication and transcription. The combination provides broad-spectrum coverage against gram-positive, gram-negative, and atypical organisms.
indications: Acute gastroenteritis with suspected bacterial etiology
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to cephalosporins or fluoroquinolones
History of tendon disorders related to fluoroquinolone use
Epilepsy or seizure disorder (lowers seizure threshold)
Children under 6 months (cefixime) and under 18 years (ofloxacin — relative)
QT prolongation or concurrent QT-prolonging drugs

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (200+200 mg) twice daily for 5–7 days
dosing.pediatric: DT formulation (100+100 mg) for older children; ofloxacin generally avoided in children <18 years due to cartilage toxicity risk; use only when benefits outweigh risks
dosing.renal: Reduce dose if CrCl <20 mL/min; extend dosing interval for ofloxacin component
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment; monitor LFTs

adverse_effects: Allergic reactions including rash and anaphylaxis
monitoring: QTc interval if co-prescribed with other QT-prolonging agents
cefixime_ofloxacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Ofloxacin has shown arthropathy in juvenile animal studies. Avoid in pregnancy and lactation unless absolutely necessary. Both drugs are excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Controversial FDC — WHO Essential Medicines Committee questioned rationality
Ofloxacin adds QT prolongation risk
Very widely used in India despite debatable fixed-ratio logic
Reserve for documented mixed infections; avoid empiric overuse
FDA black box warning on fluoroquinolones applies to ofloxacin component

aceclofenac_paracetamol_serratiopeptidas.mechanism: Aceclofenac is an NSAID that inhibits COX-2 (preferentially over COX-1), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby decreasing inflammation, pain, and fever. Paracetamol acts centrally by inhibiting central COX and activating descending serotonergic pathways, providing additional analgesic and antipyretic effect. Serratiopeptidase is a proteolytic enzyme derived from Serratia marcescens that is proposed to reduce inflammation and edema by breaking down fibrin and other dead tissue at the site of inflammation (clinical evidence remains debatable).
contraindications: Active peptic ulcer disease or GI bleeding
Severe hepatic impairment or active liver disease
Concurrent anticoagulant therapy (relative — serratiopeptidase may enhance bleeding)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet twice daily after food; maximum duration 5–7 days for acute pain
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended for children; use individual components with age-appropriate dosing
dosing.renal: Avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min; use with caution and reduced duration if CrCl 30–60 mL/min
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; reduce paracetamol total daily dose; monitor LFTs

adverse_effects: Gastric irritation, dyspepsia, and epigastric pain
monitoring: GI symptoms and stool for occult blood
Renal function (BUN, creatinine) with prolonged use
Signs of bleeding (especially if on anticoagulants)
aceclofenac_paracetamol_serratiopeptidas.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (Category D in third trimester). Aceclofenac may cause premature closure of ductus arteriosus and inhibit labor. Avoid in third trimester. Paracetamol is generally safe. Serratiopeptidase lacks adequate safety data in pregnancy. Avoid the FDC during pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Extremely popular FDC in India for musculoskeletal complaints
Serratiopeptidase evidence for efficacy is debatable — marketed primarily in India and Japan
Always take after food to reduce GI side effects
Limit duration to shortest effective course
Account for paracetamol from all sources (max 4 g/day total)

metformin_glimepiride.mechanism: Metformin decreases hepatic glucose production, reduces intestinal absorption of glucose, and improves insulin sensitivity by increasing peripheral glucose uptake and utilization. Glimepiride is a second-generation sulfonylurea that stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells by binding to the SUR1 subunit of the ATP-sensitive potassium channel. Together they target both insulin resistance and insulin deficiency.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus when monotherapy with metformin is inadequate
Type 2 diabetes requiring dual oral therapy for glycemic control
contraindications: Type 1 diabetes mellitus or diabetic ketoacidosis
Known hypersensitivity to metformin, glimepiride, or other sulfonamide derivatives
Acute or chronic metabolic acidosis including lactic acidosis
Hepatic failure or severe hepatic impairment
Conditions predisposing to lactic acidosis (sepsis, dehydration, shock, acute MI)
Congestive heart failure requiring pharmacological management

dosing.adult: Start with 500+1 mg once or twice daily with meals; titrate gradually every 1–2 weeks based on glycemic response; maximum glimepiride 8 mg/day, metformin 2000 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution if eGFR 30–45 (do not initiate, may continue with monitoring); reduce metformin dose if eGFR 45–60
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; glimepiride clearance is reduced in liver disease increasing hypoglycemia risk

adverse_effects: GI disturbances — nausea, diarrhea, abdominal bloating, metallic taste (metformin)
Weight gain (glimepiride may offset metformin's weight-neutral effect)
Lactic acidosis (rare, metformin — risk increases with renal impairment)
monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then every 6 months
Renal function (serum creatinine, eGFR) at least annually
metformin_glimepiride.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Metformin crosses the placenta; glimepiride may cause neonatal hypoglycemia. Insulin is preferred for glycemic control in pregnancy. Both drugs are excreted in breast milk; avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Most prescribed diabetes FDC in India
Hypoglycemia risk primarily from glimepiride — educate patient on recognition and management
Always take with meals to reduce GI side effects and hypoglycemia risk
Withhold 48 hours before iodinated contrast procedures (metformin)
Elderly patients are at higher hypoglycemia risk — start with lowest strength

metformin_vildagliptin.mechanism: Metformin decreases hepatic glucose production and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity. Vildagliptin inhibits dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4), thereby increasing levels of active incretin hormones (GLP-1 and GIP). This enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppresses glucagon release, leading to improved glycemic control without significant hypoglycemia risk.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on metformin alone
Type 2 diabetes as initial combination therapy when HbA1c warrants dual therapy
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to metformin or vildagliptin
Hepatic impairment including ALT or AST >3× ULN
Acute conditions with potential for tissue hypoxia (sepsis, shock, acute heart failure)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (500+50 mg or 1000+50 mg) twice daily with meals; maximum vildagliptin 100 mg/day (50 mg BD)
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30; reduce metformin dose if eGFR 30–60; vildagliptin dose reduction to 50 mg OD if eGFR <50 (FDC may not be suitable — use individual components)
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated if ALT or AST >3× ULN; monitor LFTs before initiation and periodically

adverse_effects: Nasopharyngitis and upper respiratory tract infection
Elevated hepatic transaminases (vildagliptin — rare but important)
monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then every 6 months
Liver function tests before starting and periodically (quarterly in first year recommended for vildagliptin)
metformin_vildagliptin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data. Animal studies with vildagliptin have not shown teratogenicity. Metformin crosses the placenta. Insulin remains the preferred agent in pregnancy. Avoid during lactation due to insufficient data.
quick_flags: Better tolerated than metformin + sulfonylurea (less hypoglycemia)
Weight neutral combination
Must check LFTs before starting vildagliptin and periodically thereafter
Rare risk of pancreatitis — advise patients to report persistent severe abdominal pain
Take with meals to reduce GI side effects

metformin_sitagliptin.mechanism: Metformin reduces hepatic glucose output, decreases intestinal glucose absorption, and improves insulin sensitivity. Sitagliptin selectively inhibits DPP-4, prolonging the action of endogenous incretins (GLP-1 and GIP), which stimulate glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppress inappropriate glucagon release.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on metformin alone
Type 2 diabetes as initial dual therapy when appropriate
Adjunct to diet and exercise for improved glycemic control
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min — FDC contraindicated)
Known hypersensitivity to sitagliptin or metformin
History of serious hypersensitivity to DPP-4 inhibitors (anaphylaxis, angioedema, Stevens-Johnson syndrome)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (500+50 mg or 1000+50 mg) twice daily with meals; maximum sitagliptin 100 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years
dosing.renal: CrCl 30–45 mL/min: use 500+50 mg formulation only (sitagliptin needs dose reduction to 50 mg/day, which aligns with once-daily 50 mg but FDC is BD — consider individual components); CrCl <30 mL/min: contraindicated (FDC not suitable); adjust metformin per eGFR guidelines
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Upper respiratory tract infection and nasopharyngitis
Hypoglycemia (low risk as monotherapy; higher if combined with SU or insulin)
Pancreatitis (rare but serious — DPP-4 class effect)
Severe allergic reactions including anaphylaxis and angioedema (rare)
monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then every 6 months
Renal function at baseline and at least annually (more frequently if borderline)
Vitamin B12 levels with long-term metformin use
metformin_sitagliptin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Animal reproduction studies with sitagliptin did not show fetal harm. However, no adequate human studies exist. Metformin crosses the placenta. Insulin is preferred for gestational glycemic control. Unknown if sitagliptin is excreted in human breast milk; avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Renal dosing is critical — adjust or switch to individual components if CrCl 30–45 mL/min; avoid if CrCl <30
Low hypoglycemia risk compared to metformin + sulfonylurea
Weight neutral
Rare pancreatitis risk — counsel patients on warning signs
Widely prescribed globally and in India

telmisartan_amlodipine.mechanism: Telmisartan selectively blocks the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, inhibiting RAAS-mediated vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing peripheral vasodilation. The combination provides complementary antihypertensive mechanisms and the ARB component counteracts the reflex RAAS activation and pedal edema caused by the CCB.
indications: Essential hypertension not adequately controlled on monotherapy
Hypertension requiring combination therapy as initial treatment (stage 2 or high CV risk)
Hypertension with diabetic nephropathy (telmisartan provides renal protection)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to telmisartan or amlodipine
Severe hepatic impairment or biliary obstruction (telmisartan is hepatically eliminated)
Unstable angina or acute MI (amlodipine may worsen — controversial)
Co-administration with aliskiren in patients with diabetes or renal impairment

dosing.adult: 1 tablet once daily; may start with 40+5 mg and uptitrate to 80+5 mg based on response; can be taken regardless of food
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established in children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment; avoid in severe renal impairment or bilateral renal artery stenosis; monitor serum creatinine and potassium
dosing.hepatic: Telmisartan is primarily hepatically eliminated — maximum 40 mg in hepatic impairment; amlodipine clearance is also reduced; start low

adverse_effects: Peripheral edema (reduced compared to amlodipine alone due to telmisartan)
monitoring: Blood pressure regularly (target <130/80 for most patients)
Serum creatinine and potassium within 1–2 weeks of initiation and periodically
Signs of hypotension especially in volume-depleted patients
telmisartan_amlodipine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. ARBs are teratogenic — cause fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, and neonatal death when used in second and third trimesters. Discontinue immediately upon pregnancy detection. Amlodipine animal data shows no teratogenicity but human data is limited. Avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Very popular antihypertensive FDC in India
Complementary mechanisms — RAAS blockade + vasodilation
Telmisartan counteracts amlodipine-induced pedal edema
Category D — MUST discontinue if pregnancy detected
Longest acting ARB (24-hour half-life) — excellent for once-daily dosing and morning BP surge control

telmisartan_hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Telmisartan blocks AT1 receptors, reducing RAAS-mediated vasoconstriction and aldosterone release. Hydrochlorothiazide inhibits sodium-chloride cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, promoting natriuresis and diuresis. The combination is synergistic: HCTZ-induced volume depletion activates RAAS, which is then blocked by telmisartan, enhancing efficacy and reducing the risk of HCTZ-induced hypokalemia.
indications: Essential hypertension not adequately controlled on monotherapy
Hypertension in patients who require combination therapy
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to telmisartan, HCTZ, or sulfonamide-derived drugs
Co-administration with aliskiren in diabetes or renal impairment

dosing.adult: 1 tablet once daily; start with 40+12.5 mg; may increase to 80+12.5 mg if BP not controlled
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30 (thiazides ineffective); use with caution if eGFR 30–60; monitor electrolytes closely
dosing.hepatic: Telmisartan max 40 mg in hepatic impairment; HCTZ may precipitate hepatic encephalopathy in cirrhosis

adverse_effects: Hypokalemia (HCTZ) or hyperkalemia (telmisartan) — net effect depends on balance
monitoring: Serum electrolytes (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) within 2–4 weeks and periodically
Fasting blood glucose and lipid profile (long-term HCTZ)
Hydration status especially in elderly and hot climates
telmisartan_hydrochlorothiazide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. ARBs are fetotoxic and teratogenic in second and third trimesters. HCTZ may reduce placental perfusion. Discontinue immediately upon pregnancy detection. Both drugs are excreted in breast milk; avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Good for salt-sensitive hypertension
Monitor K+ — opposing effects on potassium (telmisartan retains, HCTZ wastes)
HCTZ can worsen gout, diabetes, and dyslipidemia
Category D — discontinue immediately if pregnancy detected
Avoid in patients with eGFR <30 (thiazide becomes ineffective)

atorvastatin_aspirin.mechanism: Atorvastatin competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, reducing LDL-C, triglycerides, and increasing HDL-C. It also has pleiotropic effects including plaque stabilization and anti-inflammatory activity. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, preventing thromboxane A2 synthesis and platelet aggregation for the lifetime of the platelet (~7–10 days).
indications: Secondary cardiovascular prevention (post-MI, post-stroke, established CAD/PAD)
Primary prevention in high-risk patients (diabetes with additional risk factors)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to atorvastatin or aspirin
Active liver disease or unexplained persistent elevation of transaminases
Active peptic ulcer disease or GI bleeding
Children with viral infections (aspirin — Reye syndrome risk)
Concurrent use of methotrexate at high doses

dosing.adult: 1 tablet once daily at bedtime or with dinner; dose selection based on LDL target (10 mg or 20 mg atorvastatin) and indication (75 mg aspirin for prevention, 150 mg for acute or higher-risk scenarios)
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment for atorvastatin; aspirin use caution in renal impairment (may reduce GFR); avoid in severe renal failure
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease; check LFTs before and periodically after starting

adverse_effects: GI disturbance — dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain
Rhabdomyolysis (rare, atorvastatin — especially with interacting drugs)
Easy bruising and prolonged bleeding time (aspirin)
monitoring: Lipid profile at baseline, 4–12 weeks after initiation, then annually
Liver function tests before starting and as clinically indicated
Signs of GI bleeding (dark stools, hematemesis)
atorvastatin_aspirin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X (atorvastatin component). Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy due to potential fetal harm — cholesterol is essential for fetal development. Aspirin at low doses may be used in specific pregnancy indications (pre-eclampsia prevention) but the FDC is contraindicated. Avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Category X — absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy
Take with food, preferably at dinner or bedtime for optimal statin efficacy
Secondary CV prevention pill — improves compliance by combining two essential medications
Monitor for muscle symptoms — discontinue if CK >10× ULN or rhabdomyolysis suspected
Avoid concomitant grapefruit juice (CYP3A4 interaction with atorvastatin)

atorvastatin_fenofibrate.mechanism: Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, primarily reducing LDL-C and triglycerides while modestly raising HDL-C. Fenofibrate activates peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-α), which enhances lipoprotein lipase activity, increases fatty acid oxidation, reduces VLDL production, and significantly lowers triglycerides while raising HDL-C. The combination addresses mixed dyslipidemia (elevated LDL + elevated TG + low HDL).
indications: Mixed dyslipidemia (elevated LDL-C, elevated triglycerides, and/or low HDL-C)
Patients not achieving lipid goals on statin monotherapy due to persistent hypertriglyceridemia
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to atorvastatin or fenofibrate
Active liver disease or unexplained persistent transaminase elevation
Pre-existing gallbladder disease (fenofibrate increases cholesterol excretion into bile)
Concurrent use of gemfibrozil (significantly increases myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet once daily with dinner; fenofibrate absorption is enhanced with food
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30; reduce fenofibrate dose if eGFR 30–60 (FDC may not be suitable — use individual components); monitor CK and creatinine
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease; monitor LFTs regularly

adverse_effects: Rhabdomyolysis (rare but serious — lower risk with fenofibrate than gemfibrozil)
GI disturbances — dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain
Elevated serum creatinine (fenofibrate — reversible, non-renal mechanism)
monitoring: Lipid profile at baseline, 4–12 weeks, then periodically
Liver function tests before starting, at 12 weeks, and periodically
CK at baseline and if muscle symptoms develop
Serum creatinine at baseline and periodically (fenofibrate can reversibly raise creatinine)
CBC periodically (rare blood dyscrasias with fenofibrate)
atorvastatin_fenofibrate.pregnancy_lactation: Category X (atorvastatin component). Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Cholesterol and its biosynthetic products are essential for fetal development. Fenofibrate also showed embryocidal effects in animal studies. Avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Myopathy risk is lower with fenofibrate + statin compared to gemfibrozil + statin — NEVER substitute gemfibrozil
Still monitor CK regularly and counsel patients on muscle pain
Fenofibrate reversibly raises serum creatinine (not true renal damage)
Avoid in severe renal impairment
Category X — contraindicated in pregnancy
Take with dinner for optimal absorption of both components

pantoprazole_domperidone.mechanism: Pantoprazole irreversibly inhibits the H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion. Domperidone is a peripheral dopamine D2 receptor antagonist that enhances upper GI motility (prokinetic), increases lower esophageal sphincter pressure, accelerates gastric emptying, and improves gastroduodenal coordination. It does not readily cross the blood-brain barrier, so extrapyramidal effects are rare.
indications: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with associated dysmotility symptoms
Functional dyspepsia with epigastric fullness, early satiety, and bloating
Peptic ulcer disease with nausea and bloating
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to pantoprazole or domperidone
Prolactinoma or conditions where prolactin elevation is undesirable (domperidone raises prolactin)
QT prolongation or concurrent use of QT-prolonging drugs
Hepatic impairment (moderate-severe — domperidone clearance reduced, QT risk increases)
GI hemorrhage, mechanical obstruction, or perforation (prokinetic contraindicated)
Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, erythromycin — increase domperidone levels and QT risk)

dosing.adult: 1 capsule once daily, 30–60 minutes before breakfast; swallow whole, do not crush or chew (SR formulation); duration typically 4–8 weeks for GERD
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended as FDC in children; use individual components with weight-based dosing
dosing.renal: No major dose adjustment for mild-moderate renal impairment; use with caution in severe renal impairment; domperidone dosing interval should be increased
dosing.hepatic: Pantoprazole: max 20 mg/day in severe hepatic impairment; Domperidone: contraindicated in moderate-severe hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias (domperidone — especially at doses >30 mg/day)
Increased risk of C. difficile infection (PPI-related)
monitoring: Magnesium levels if long-term PPI use (>1 year)
ECG if risk factors for QT prolongation exist
Reassess need for continued therapy periodically — avoid indefinite PPI use
Vitamin B12 levels with long-term use
pantoprazole_domperidone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Pantoprazole animal studies showed some fetotoxicity at high doses; no adequate human data. Domperidone is excreted in breast milk and may affect the nursing infant. Use in pregnancy only if clearly needed. Domperidone has been used off-label to enhance lactation, but this use is not recommended due to cardiac risk.
quick_flags: Very widely prescribed FDC in India
Domperidone QT prolongation risk — especially at high doses and in elderly
Take before meals (30–60 min before breakfast is ideal)
Avoid long-term PPI use without clear indication — reassess periodically
EMA restricted domperidone use to max 7 days at max 30 mg/day due to cardiac risk
Do not crush SR formulation

paracetamol_caffeine_phenylephrine.mechanism: Paracetamol inhibits central prostaglandin synthesis (COX pathway) and activates descending serotonergic pain pathways, providing analgesic and antipyretic effects. Caffeine is an adenosine receptor antagonist that enhances analgesic efficacy (adjuvant effect), reduces fatigue, and causes mild vasoconstriction. Phenylephrine is a selective alpha-1 adrenergic agonist that causes vasoconstriction of nasal mucosal blood vessels, relieving nasal congestion.
indications: Common cold with fever, headache, and nasal congestion
contraindications: Concurrent or recent (within 14 days) MAO inhibitor use (phenylephrine — hypertensive crisis risk)

dosing.adult: 1–2 tablets every 4–6 hours as needed; maximum 8 tablets per day (4 g paracetamol/day limit from all sources)
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended for children under 12 years in this formulation; use age-appropriate paracetamol formulations separately
dosing.renal: Extend dosing interval in severe renal impairment; reduce maximum daily dose
dosing.hepatic: Reduce maximum paracetamol dose to 2 g/day in hepatic impairment; avoid in severe liver disease

adverse_effects: Urinary retention (phenylephrine — especially in prostatic hypertrophy)
monitoring: Symptom duration — seek medical attention if symptoms persist >7 days
Total paracetamol intake from all sources (max 4 g/day)
Liver function if prolonged use or in at-risk patients
Caffeine intake from other sources (coffee, tea, energy drinks)
paracetamol_caffeine_phenylephrine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Paracetamol is generally considered safe in pregnancy at recommended doses. Caffeine in moderate amounts (<200 mg/day total) is acceptable. Phenylephrine has limited data; may reduce uteroplacental blood flow. Use the FDC only if clearly needed and prefer paracetamol alone when possible. All components pass into breast milk.
quick_flags: OTC cold and flu relief — widely available without prescription
CRITICAL: Max paracetamol 4 g/day across ALL sources — hepatotoxicity risk with overdose
Phenylephrine: avoid in uncontrolled hypertension and with MAOIs
Caffeine content may cause insomnia if taken late in the day
Not a cure — symptomatic relief only; antibiotics are not needed for viral URI

ofloxacin_ornidazole.mechanism: Ofloxacin inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA supercoiling, replication, and repair, leading to bactericidal activity against aerobic gram-positive and gram-negative organisms. Ornidazole is a 5-nitroimidazole that undergoes reduction in anaerobic organisms; the reduced intermediate disrupts DNA, causing strand breakage and cell death. Effective against anaerobic bacteria and protozoa.
indications: Gynecological infections (pelvic inflammatory disease, bacterial vaginosis)
GI infections (amoebic dysentery, giardiasis with secondary bacterial infection)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to fluoroquinolones or nitroimidazoles
Epilepsy or CNS disorders (both drugs lower seizure threshold)
History of tendon disorders related to fluoroquinolone use
QT prolongation or concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
Children under 18 years (relative — ofloxacin cartilage toxicity risk)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet twice daily for 5–7 days; take with or after food
dosing.pediatric: Generally avoided in children <18 years; if used, dose based on weight with individual components
dosing.renal: Reduce ofloxacin dose if CrCl <30 mL/min; ornidazole does not require significant dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment; ornidazole is hepatically metabolized — reduce dose in severe liver disease

adverse_effects: Tendinitis and tendon rupture (ofloxacin — risk increases with age and corticosteroid use)
CNS effects — insomnia, seizures, confusion (both drugs)
monitoring: QTc interval if concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
Signs of tendinopathy (pain, swelling, stiffness in tendons)
ofloxacin_ornidazole.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Ofloxacin has shown arthropathy in juvenile animals. Ornidazole showed no teratogenicity in animal studies but lacks adequate human data. Both drugs are excreted in breast milk. Avoid the FDC in pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Common for mixed aerobic + anaerobic infections in India
Ornidazole has better tolerability than metronidazole
No alcohol interaction with ornidazole (unlike metronidazole — disulfiram reaction)
FDA black box warning on fluoroquinolones (tendon rupture, neuropathy, CNS effects) applies to ofloxacin
Complete the full course to prevent antibiotic resistance

losartan_hydrochlorothiazide.mechanism: Losartan selectively blocks angiotensin II at the AT1 receptor, reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activity. Its active metabolite (EXP3174) is 10–40 times more potent. Losartan also has a unique uricosuric effect among ARBs. Hydrochlorothiazide inhibits the NCC transporter in the distal convoluted tubule, promoting natriuresis. The combination enhances antihypertensive efficacy — HCTZ activates RAAS, which losartan then blocks.
indications: Hypertension not adequately controlled on monotherapy
Hypertension with hyperuricemia (losartan's uricosuric effect is beneficial)
Reduction of stroke risk in hypertensive patients with left ventricular hypertrophy (LIFE study)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to losartan, HCTZ, or sulfonamide derivatives

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (50+12.5 mg) once daily; may take with or without food
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children for the combination product
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30 (thiazide is ineffective and losartan metabolite accumulates); use with caution if eGFR 30–60; monitor electrolytes and creatinine
dosing.hepatic: Losartan requires dose reduction in hepatic impairment (reduced conversion to active metabolite); start with lower doses; HCTZ may precipitate encephalopathy in cirrhosis

adverse_effects: Hyperuricemia and gout (HCTZ — partially offset by losartan's uricosuric effect)
Cough (less than ACE inhibitors, but possible)
monitoring: Serum electrolytes (K+, Na+, Ca2+) within 2–4 weeks and periodically
losartan_hydrochlorothiazide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. ARBs cause fetal injury and death when used in second and third trimesters — oligohydramnios, renal failure, hypotension. HCTZ may cause neonatal thrombocytopenia and jaundice. Discontinue immediately upon pregnancy detection. Excreted in breast milk; avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Category D — discontinue immediately if pregnancy detected
Losartan has unique uricosuric effect — beneficial in patients with hypertension and gout
LIFE study showed losartan superiority over atenolol for stroke reduction with LVH
Monitor potassium — opposing effects from components
Losartan is a prodrug — hepatic impairment reduces active metabolite formation

amlodipine_atenolol.mechanism: Amlodipine inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing arterial vasodilation and reducing peripheral resistance. Atenolol is a selective beta-1 adrenergic blocker that reduces heart rate, myocardial contractility, and cardiac output, and suppresses renin release. The combination addresses both afterload (amlodipine) and cardiac output (atenolol). Atenolol also counteracts the reflex tachycardia caused by amlodipine's vasodilatory effect.
indications: Hypertension not adequately controlled on monotherapy
Hypertension with concurrent tachycardia or angina
Hypertension in patients who develop reflex tachycardia on CCB alone
contraindications: Second- or third-degree heart block (without pacemaker)
Known hypersensitivity to amlodipine or atenolol

dosing.adult: 1 tablet once daily; start with 5+25 mg and titrate based on BP and heart rate response; may increase to 5+50 mg if needed
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended as FDC in children
dosing.renal: Atenolol is renally excreted — reduce dose if CrCl <35 mL/min (max 25 mg/day if CrCl 15–35; max 25 mg every other day if CrCl <15); amlodipine does not require renal adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Amlodipine clearance reduced in hepatic impairment — start with lower dose; atenolol undergoes minimal hepatic metabolism

adverse_effects: Cold extremities (atenolol — beta-blockade reduces peripheral perfusion)
Bronchospasm (atenolol — loss of beta-1 selectivity at high doses)
Masking of hypoglycemia symptoms in diabetics (atenolol)
Rebound hypertension and tachycardia if abruptly discontinued (atenolol)
monitoring: Blood pressure and heart rate at each visit
ECG at baseline and if symptomatic bradycardia
Signs of heart failure (weight gain, edema, dyspnea)
Blood glucose in diabetic patients (beta-blockers mask hypoglycemia symptoms)
Do not abruptly discontinue — taper atenolol over 1–2 weeks
amlodipine_atenolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category D (atenolol component). Atenolol crosses the placenta and has been associated with intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), neonatal bradycardia, and hypoglycemia. Amlodipine has limited human data but animal studies showed no teratogenicity. Atenolol is excreted in breast milk at concentrations higher than maternal plasma. Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Common combination for hypertension with tachycardia
Atenolol controls reflex tachycardia from amlodipine
NEVER abruptly stop — taper atenolol to avoid rebound hypertension and angina
Category D due to atenolol — not recommended in pregnancy
Atenolol masks hypoglycemic symptoms in diabetics — educate patients
Current guidelines (NICE, ESC) have moved away from atenolol as first-line — consider alternatives

glimepiride_metformin_pioglitazone.mechanism: Glimepiride stimulates pancreatic beta cell insulin secretion via SUR1 subunit binding. Metformin reduces hepatic glucose production, decreases intestinal glucose absorption, and enhances peripheral insulin sensitivity. Pioglitazone activates PPAR-gamma nuclear receptors, improving insulin sensitivity in adipose tissue, skeletal muscle, and liver, and redistributing fat from visceral to subcutaneous depots. The triple combination targets three complementary pathophysiological defects of type 2 diabetes.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus inadequately controlled on dual oral therapy (metformin + sulfonylurea or metformin + pioglitazone)
Uncontrolled T2DM requiring triple oral combination before insulin initiation
contraindications: Heart failure (NYHA Class III–IV) — pioglitazone causes fluid retention
Active bladder cancer or history of bladder cancer (pioglitazone — epidemiological concern)
Active liver disease or ALT >2.5× ULN
Osteoporosis or high fracture risk in postmenopausal women (pioglitazone reduces bone density)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (1+500+15 mg or 2+500+15 mg) twice daily with meals; titrate based on glycemic response; do not exceed glimepiride 8 mg/day or metformin 2000 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution and reduce metformin if eGFR 30–45; pioglitazone does not require renal adjustment but fluid retention risk increases with renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated if ALT >2.5× ULN; pioglitazone has rare hepatotoxicity risk; monitor LFTs before and periodically after starting

adverse_effects: Weight gain (pioglitazone + glimepiride — partially offset by metformin)
Bone fractures especially in postmenopausal women (pioglitazone — reduced BMD)
Bladder cancer risk (pioglitazone — controversial, small absolute risk)
monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then every 6 months
Liver function tests before initiation and periodically
Signs of heart failure (weight gain, edema, dyspnea)
Bone density in postmenopausal women on long-term pioglitazone
Hematuria or bladder symptoms (pioglitazone — bladder cancer screening)
glimepiride_metformin_pioglitazone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Glimepiride may cause neonatal hypoglycemia. Metformin crosses the placenta. Pioglitazone animal studies showed growth retardation. Insulin is the preferred agent for glycemic control in pregnancy. All three drugs are excreted in breast milk. Avoid the FDC during pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Triple combination FDC for uncontrolled T2DM — addresses three pathophysiological defects
Fluid retention risk from pioglitazone — monitor for heart failure (avoid in NYHA III–IV)
Hypoglycemia risk from glimepiride — educate on recognition and management
Pioglitazone reduces bone density — caution in postmenopausal women and osteoporosis
Consider switching pioglitazone to DPP-4 inhibitor or SGLT2 inhibitor if fluid retention or fracture risk is a concern
Weight gain is expected with this combination (glimepiride + pioglitazone)

celecoxib.mechanism: Selective inhibition of cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) enzyme, reducing prostaglandin synthesis with relatively less effect on COX-1 mediated gastric protection.

dosing.adult: OA: 200 mg OD or 100 mg BD; RA: 100-200 mg BD; Acute pain: 400 mg initially, then 200 mg BD
dosing.pediatric: JRA (>2 years): 10-25 kg: 50 mg BD; >25 kg: 100 mg BD
dosing.renal: Not recommended in advanced renal disease; use lowest dose if mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50% in moderate hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B); avoid in severe

celecoxib.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (first and second trimester), Category D (third trimester). Avoid in third trimester. Unknown if excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Lower GI risk than non-selective NSAIDs but cardiovascular risk persists
Contains a sulfonamide moiety — cross-reactivity with sulfa allergy is debated but caution advised
Not a substitute for low-dose aspirin for cardioprotection

etoricoxib.mechanism: Highly selective COX-2 inhibitor with approximately 106-fold selectivity over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin-mediated inflammation and pain.
contraindications: Active GI bleeding or peptic ulcer
Ischaemic heart disease, peripheral arterial disease, or cerebrovascular disease

dosing.adult: OA: 60 mg OD; RA/AS: 90 mg OD; Acute gout: 120 mg OD (max 8 days); Dental pain: 90 mg OD
dosing.pediatric: Not approved for children <16 years
dosing.renal: Avoid if CrCl <30 ml/min; no adjustment needed for mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh 5-6): no adjustment; Moderate (Child-Pugh 7-9): max 60 mg OD; Severe: contraindicated

etoricoxib.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in third trimester. Not recommended during breastfeeding due to lack of safety data.
quick_flags: Very popular in India for acute gout — 120 mg OD for up to 8 days
Must check and control BP before starting therapy
Longer half-life allows once-daily dosing
Not approved by FDA in the USA but widely used in India and Europe

colchicine.mechanism: Inhibits tubulin polymerization, disrupting microtubule-dependent functions in neutrophils including migration, chemotaxis, and inflammasome activation. Reduces urate crystal-induced inflammation.
indications: Gout prophylaxis during urate-lowering therapy initiation
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <10 ml/min) with concurrent P-gp or CYP3A4 inhibitors
Severe hepatic impairment with renal impairment
Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors in renal/hepatic impairment

dosing.adult: Acute gout: 1 mg initially, then 0.5 mg after 1 hour (max 1.5 mg per flare); Prophylaxis: 0.5 mg OD or BD; Pericarditis: 0.5 mg BD (if >70 kg) or 0.5 mg OD (if <70 kg) for 3 months
dosing.pediatric: FMF: children >4 years: 0.5-1 mg/day; adjust based on response
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: reduce dose, max 0.5 mg OD for prophylaxis; CrCl <30: 0.5 mg every 2-3 days; Dialysis: avoid or 0.5 mg per week
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: use with caution; Severe: dose reduction required; avoid with concurrent renal impairment

monitoring: GI symptoms as indicator of toxicity
colchicine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited human data suggests no major teratogenicity but use only if clearly needed. Excreted in breast milk — American Academy of Rheumatology considers it compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Diarrhoea is the earliest sign of toxicity — stop immediately if it occurs
Low-dose regimen (1.5 mg total) is as effective and much safer than old high-dose protocol
Excellent evidence for recurrent pericarditis (COPE/CORP trials)
Drug interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, ketoconazole) can cause fatal toxicity

hydroxychloroquine.mechanism: Accumulates in lysosomes raising pH, inhibiting antigen processing and MHC class II presentation. Inhibits toll-like receptor signaling (TLR-7, TLR-9). Anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, and photoprotective effects.

dosing.adult: RA: 200-400 mg/day (usually 200 mg BD); SLE: 200-400 mg/day; Maintenance: 200 mg OD. Max dose: <=5 mg/kg actual body weight/day
dosing.pediatric: JIA/SLE: 3-5 mg/kg/day (max 400 mg/day)
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment but use cautiously; drug accumulates in renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic disease; no formal guidelines

monitoring: Baseline and annual ophthalmological exam (OCT + visual fields) after 5 years or sooner if risk factors
hydroxychloroquine.pregnancy_lactation: Considered safe in pregnancy (Category N — benefits outweigh risk in SLE). Recommended to continue during pregnancy in SLE to prevent flares. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: One of the safest DMARDs in pregnancy — do NOT stop in pregnant SLE patients
Retinal toxicity risk increases significantly after 5 years of use; use real body weight for dosing
Max recommended dose revised to <=5 mg/kg/day actual body weight (AAO 2016 guidelines)
Has mild antithrombotic and lipid-lowering properties

budesonide.mechanism: Potent glucocorticoid that suppresses airway inflammation by inhibiting multiple inflammatory genes encoding cytokines, chemokines, adhesion molecules, and inflammatory enzymes. High topical potency with low systemic bioavailability.
contraindications: Active untreated pulmonary tuberculosis or fungal infections

dosing.adult: Asthma: Low dose: 200-400 mcg/day; Medium: 400-800 mcg/day; High: >800 mcg/day (via DPI/MDI); Nebulisation: 1-2 mg BD
dosing.pediatric: Asthma: Low dose: 100-200 mcg/day; Medium: 200-400 mcg/day; High: >400 mcg/day; Croup: 2 mg nebulised as single dose
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Systemic exposure may increase in severe hepatic impairment; monitor for systemic corticosteroid effects

adverse_effects: Growth retardation in children (minimal at low doses)
monitoring: Bone density with long-term high-dose use
Adrenal function if concerns for suppression
budesonide.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Preferred inhaled corticosteroid during pregnancy (most safety data available). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Rinse mouth and spit after each inhaled dose to prevent oral candidiasis
Preferred ICS in pregnancy due to most safety data
Single nebulised dose (2 mg) dramatically effective in croup
Do NOT use for acute bronchospasm relief — it is a controller, not a reliever

forms: Duolin: ipratropium 500 mcg + salbutamol 2.5 mg nebuliser respule
ipratropium.mechanism: Competitively blocks muscarinic (M3) receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, inhibiting acetylcholine-mediated bronchoconstriction and reducing mucus secretion.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to ipratropium or atropine derivatives
Soya or peanut allergy (some formulations contain soy lecithin)

dosing.adult: MDI: 2 puffs QDS; Nebulisation: 500 mcg every 4-6 hours; Acute asthma: 500 mcg nebulised every 20 min x3 with salbutamol
dosing.pediatric: Nebulisation: 250 mcg every 4-6 hours; Acute asthma: 250 mcg every 20 min x3 (with salbutamol)
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

ipratropium.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data; inhaled route minimizes systemic exposure. Use if benefits outweigh risks. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: In acute severe asthma, combine nebulised ipratropium with salbutamol — proven to reduce hospitalisation
Duolin (ipratropium + salbutamol) is one of the most commonly used nebulisation combinations in Indian emergency departments
Protect eyes during nebulisation — can precipitate acute angle-closure glaucoma
Onset of action slower than salbutamol (15-30 min vs 5 min)

theophylline.mechanism: Non-selective phosphodiesterase inhibitor increasing intracellular cAMP; also blocks adenosine receptors. Produces bronchodilation, mild anti-inflammatory effects, and respiratory muscle stimulation.

dosing.adult: Oral: 200-400 mg BD (SR formulations); Titrate based on serum levels. Loading dose (if theophylline-naive): 5 mg/kg IV aminophylline over 20-30 min
dosing.pediatric: 10-14 mg/kg/day in divided doses (max 300 mg/day initially); titrate with serum levels
dosing.renal: No major adjustment; monitor levels
dosing.hepatic: Clearance significantly reduced in hepatic impairment and heart failure — reduce dose by 50% and monitor levels closely

monitoring: Serum theophylline levels (therapeutic range: 5-15 mcg/ml; toxicity >20 mcg/ml)
theophylline.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta; may cause neonatal tachycardia and irritability. Excreted in breast milk — may cause irritability in infant.
quick_flags: Narrow therapeutic index — always monitor serum levels
Multiple drug interactions: levels increased by erythromycin, ciprofloxacin, cimetidine; decreased by rifampicin, phenytoin, smoking
Deriphyllin (theophylline + etofylline) is uniquely Indian and extremely widely prescribed
Smoking cessation increases theophylline levels — re-check after quitting

tiotropium.mechanism: Long-acting competitive antagonist at M1 and M3 muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, causing sustained bronchodilation. Very slow dissociation from M3 receptors allows once-daily dosing.
indications: Asthma (add-on in Step 4-5 for patients uncontrolled on ICS-LABA)
contraindications: Severe milk protein allergy (DPI capsule contains lactose)

dosing.adult: DPI: 18 mcg (1 capsule) inhaled OD; Respimat: 5 mcg (2 puffs of 2.5 mcg) OD; Asthma (Respimat): 2.5 mcg (2 puffs of 1.25 mcg) OD
dosing.pediatric: Asthma >=6 years (Respimat): 2.5 mcg OD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment; monitor for anticholinergic side effects in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required (minimal hepatic metabolism)

monitoring: COPD symptom assessment and exacerbation frequency
tiotropium.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. No adequate human studies. Use only if clearly needed. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Once-daily dosing improves compliance in COPD patients
Tiova Rotacaps are the most prescribed LAMA in India
UPLIFT trial: reduced exacerbations and improved quality of life in COPD
Capsules are for inhalation only — must NOT be swallowed

pioglitazone.mechanism: Selective agonist of PPARgamma (peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma) nuclear receptor, improving insulin sensitivity in adipose tissue, skeletal muscle, and liver by enhancing glucose transporter expression and reducing free fatty acids.
contraindications: Active bladder cancer or history of bladder cancer
Active liver disease or ALT >2.5 times ULN

dosing.adult: Start 15 mg OD; may increase to 30 mg OD; max 45 mg OD. Effect takes 2-4 weeks to manifest fully
dosing.pediatric: Not approved for children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required; not dialyzable
dosing.hepatic: Do not initiate if ALT >2.5x ULN; discontinue if ALT >3x ULN or jaundice develops

monitoring: LFTs before and periodically during treatment
Signs of heart failure (weight gain, oedema, dyspnoea)
pioglitazone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in pregnancy. May cause anovulatory women to ovulate — counsel about contraception. Not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Avoid in heart failure patients — causes fluid retention
May restore ovulation in PCOS patients — warn about unintended pregnancy
Emerging evidence supports use in NASH (PIVENS trial)
Full glycaemic effect takes 8-12 weeks — do not judge efficacy too early

vildagliptin.mechanism: Inhibits DPP-4 enzyme, prolonging activity of endogenous GLP-1 and GIP incretin hormones. This enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppresses inappropriate glucagon release.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (monotherapy or combination with metformin, sulfonylureas, pioglitazone, or insulin)

dosing.adult: 50 mg BD (morning and evening); or 50 mg OD when combined with sulfonylurea
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild impairment; GFR <50: 50 mg OD; ESRD/dialysis: 50 mg OD
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated if ALT or AST >3x ULN; do not use in moderate-severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: LFTs before starting and every 3 months during first year
vildagliptin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. No adequate human studies. Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Requires LFT monitoring — unique among DPP-4 inhibitors
Galvus Met (vildagliptin + metformin) is among the most prescribed diabetes combinations in India
Twice-daily dosing (vs OD for sitagliptin) may affect compliance
Not FDA-approved in the USA but widely used in India and Europe

timolol_eye_drops.mechanism: Non-selective beta-adrenergic blocker that reduces aqueous humor production by blocking beta-2 receptors in the ciliary epithelium, thereby lowering intraocular pressure (IOP).

dosing.adult: 1 drop of 0.25% or 0.5% in affected eye(s) BD; Gel formulation: 1 drop of 0.5% OD
dosing.pediatric: 0.25% solution: 1 drop BD in affected eye(s); use with extreme caution in neonates
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

adverse_effects: Masking of hypoglycaemia symptoms in diabetics
monitoring: Heart rate and blood pressure (especially at initiation)
Visual fields and optic disc assessment
timolol_eye_drops.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Beta-blockers may cause fetal bradycardia. Use only if clearly needed. Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Apply punctal occlusion (press inner corner of eye) for 2-3 minutes after instilling to reduce systemic absorption
Systemic side effects are real — always ask about asthma, COPD, and heart conditions before prescribing
First-line topical therapy for glaucoma in many Indian settings due to cost-effectiveness
Can mask hypoglycaemia in diabetic patients

latanoprost.mechanism: Synthetic prostaglandin F2-alpha analogue that lowers IOP by increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor. Also mildly increases trabecular outflow.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to latanoprost or benzalkonium chloride
Caution in aphakia, pseudophakia with torn posterior lens capsule (risk of macular oedema)

dosing.adult: 1 drop in affected eye(s) OD in the evening
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult dosing; safety established in children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

adverse_effects: Iris pigmentation change (irreversible darkening — more common in mixed-colour irides)
Eyelash growth (increased length, thickness, and darkness)
latanoprost.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May induce uterine contractions (prostaglandin analogue). Avoid in pregnancy. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Once-daily evening dosing makes it convenient — most effective IOP reduction when dosed at night
Warn patients about irreversible iris colour change (brown pigmentation) — important for medico-legal documentation
Eyelash growth is a cosmetic side effect — basis for bimatoprost (Latisse) cosmetic use
Store unopened bottles in refrigerator; opened bottle stable at room temperature for 4 weeks

brimonidine.mechanism: Selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that reduces IOP by decreasing aqueous humor production and enhancing uveoscleral outflow.
indications: Prevention of IOP spikes after laser procedures
contraindications: Neonates and infants (<2 years) — risk of CNS depression, apnoea

dosing.adult: 1 drop in affected eye(s) TDS (approximately 8 hours apart)
dosing.pediatric: >=2 years: 1 drop in affected eye(s) TDS; CONTRAINDICATED in children <2 years
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

brimonidine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data. Use only if clearly needed. Likely excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: High rate of allergic conjunctivitis (follicular reaction) — if redness develops after months of use, suspect allergy
NEVER use in infants or neonates — can cause life-threatening CNS and respiratory depression
Useful as a temporary measure to prevent IOP spikes post-laser trabeculoplasty or iridotomy
Lower concentration (0.1%) has fewer side effects with comparable efficacy

moxifloxacin_eye_drops.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, transcription, and repair. Broad-spectrum bactericidal activity against gram-positive and gram-negative ocular pathogens.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to moxifloxacin or other fluoroquinolones

dosing.adult: Conjunctivitis: 1 drop TDS for 7 days; Corneal ulcer: 1 drop every hour on day 1, then every 2 hours for days 2-3, then QDS until healed; Post-cataract: 1 drop QDS for 2-4 weeks starting 1 day pre-op
dosing.pediatric: >=1 year: same as adult dosing
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required (topical ophthalmic — minimal systemic absorption)
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required

monitoring: Culture and sensitivity if no improvement in 48-72 hours
moxifloxacin_eye_drops.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption with topical use. Use if benefits outweigh risks. Compatible with breastfeeding (topical ophthalmic use).
quick_flags: Preservative-free formulation (Vigamox) — preferred for corneal ulcers and post-surgical use
Most commonly used pre- and post-cataract surgery antibiotic drop in India
Self-preserved by moxifloxacin's inherent antimicrobial activity (Vigamox)
Do not use contact lenses during treatment for conjunctivitis

nepafenac.mechanism: Prodrug that is converted to amfenac by intraocular hydrolases. Amfenac inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 in the uveal and retinal tissues, reducing prostaglandin-mediated inflammation and pain.
indications: Pain and inflammation after cataract surgery
Prevention of cystoid macular oedema post-cataract surgery
Post-operative inflammation following other ocular surgeries
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to nepafenac or other NSAIDs
Active corneal epithelial defect (risk of corneal melting)

dosing.adult: 0.1%: 1 drop TDS starting 1 day before surgery, continue on day of surgery and for 2 weeks post-op; 0.3%: 1 drop OD with same schedule
dosing.pediatric: >=10 years: same as adult; limited data in younger children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment (minimal systemic absorption)
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (minimal systemic absorption)

nepafenac.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in late pregnancy (prostaglandin inhibition may affect fetal circulation). Minimal systemic absorption from topical use. Use with caution during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Prodrug design allows better corneal penetration and posterior segment delivery than other ophthalmic NSAIDs
Shake suspension well before use
Avoid in patients with complicated corneal disease — risk of corneal melting
0.3% (OD dosing) formulation improves compliance over 0.1% (TDS dosing)

isotretinoin.mechanism: Reduces sebaceous gland size and sebum production by up to 90%. Also normalizes follicular keratinization, has anti-inflammatory properties, and reduces Cutibacterium acnes colonization indirectly.

dosing.adult: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day in 1-2 divided doses with fatty food; usual course: 16-24 weeks; cumulative dose target: 120-150 mg/kg
dosing.pediatric: >=12 years: same as adult dosing; not recommended in younger children
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in severe renal impairment; start at lower end
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic disease; reduce dose in mild-moderate impairment

adverse_effects: Dry skin and lips (cheilitis — nearly universal)
monitoring: Pregnancy test before starting and monthly during treatment (mandatory)
Fasting lipid profile (baseline, 1 month, then every 2-3 months)
isotretinoin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X — ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED. Major teratogen causing craniofacial, cardiac, and CNS malformations. Two forms of contraception required 1 month before, during, and 1 month after treatment. Not compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Mandatory pregnancy prevention — two reliable contraception methods required for all women of childbearing potential
Must be taken with fatty food for adequate absorption
Cheilitis is so common that its absence suggests non-compliance
Avoid waxing and laser procedures during and 6 months after treatment (abnormal scarring)
Avoid blood donation during and for 1 month after treatment

mupirocin.mechanism: Reversibly binds to bacterial isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase, inhibiting protein and RNA synthesis. Bactericidal at high concentrations achieved topically. Highly active against Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Streptococcus pyogenes.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to mupirocin or polyethylene glycol (ointment base)
Do not apply to large open wounds or burns (polyethylene glycol absorption and nephrotoxicity risk)

dosing.adult: Topical: Apply thin layer TDS to affected area for 5-10 days; Nasal MRSA decolonization: Apply to each nostril BD for 5 days
dosing.pediatric: >=2 months: same as adult dosing for topical use
dosing.renal: Avoid application on large open areas (PEG absorption risk); standard topical use is safe
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required (minimal systemic absorption)

adverse_effects: Systemic toxicity unlikely with appropriate topical use
monitoring: Culture and sensitivity if no improvement in 3-5 days
Watch for development of resistance with prolonged use
mupirocin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Minimal systemic absorption. Generally considered safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding for topical use.
quick_flags: T-Bact is one of the most prescribed topical antibiotics in India
Effective against MRSA — important for nasal decolonization in recurrent staphylococcal infections
Do NOT use for more than 10 days — resistance develops
Ointment (PEG base) should not be used on large open wounds or burns
Not effective against gram-negative organisms

clobetasol.mechanism: Super-potent synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, inducing lipocortin synthesis which inhibits phospholipase A2. Reduces inflammation, immunosuppression, and cell proliferation.
indications: Severe eczema/dermatitis unresponsive to weaker steroids
contraindications: Application to face, axillae, or groin (relative — only under specialist supervision)

dosing.adult: Apply thin layer BD to affected areas; max 2 consecutive weeks; max 50 g/week; do not use under occlusion unless directed
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended <12 years; if used, limit to small areas for shortest duration possible
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment (topical use)
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (topical use)

monitoring: Skin for atrophy, striae, and telangiectasia
Area of application and quantity used
HPA axis function with prolonged or extensive use (morning cortisol)
clobetasol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid during pregnancy especially in first trimester; if needed, use on limited areas for shortest duration. Avoid application to breasts during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Most potent topical steroid — should NOT be used on face, axillae, or groin routinely
OTC availability in India has led to massive misuse causing topical steroid-damaged face (TSDF)
Max 2 weeks continuous use — taper to lower potency steroid for maintenance
Never stop abruptly after prolonged use — risk of rebound flare
Massive public health problem in India: steroid-antifungal-antibiotic combinations sold OTC for any skin rash

tacrolimus_topical.mechanism: Binds to FKBP-12, forming a complex that inhibits calcineurin phosphatase. This prevents dephosphorylation of NFAT and subsequent T-cell activation and cytokine release (IL-2, IL-4, IL-5, IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha). Provides anti-inflammatory effect without causing skin atrophy.
contraindications: Active cutaneous viral infections (herpes simplex, varicella)
