Netherton syndrome or other barrier-defect conditions (increased systemic absorption)

dosing.adult: 0.1% ointment: Apply thin layer BD to affected areas until clearing, then reduce to OD or twice weekly for maintenance (proactive therapy)
dosing.pediatric: 2-15 years: 0.03% ointment BD; >=16 years: 0.1% ointment BD
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment (minimal systemic absorption with intact skin)
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (minimal systemic absorption with intact skin)

adverse_effects: Burning and stinging at application site (common initially, resolves in days)
Theoretical lymphoma risk (FDA black box — debated)
monitoring: Signs of skin infection (viral, bacterial, fungal)
Avoid excessive sun exposure during use
tacrolimus_topical.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Very low systemic absorption with topical use. Use only if clearly needed during pregnancy. Avoid application to breasts during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Does NOT cause skin atrophy — preferred over steroids for face, eyelids, and intertriginous areas
Initial burning/stinging is common — warn patients and it typically resolves within a week
FDA black box warning for theoretical lymphoma risk — based on systemic tacrolimus data, clinical significance of topical use risk is debated
Excellent steroid-sparing agent for facial atopic dermatitis
Proactive twice-weekly maintenance therapy reduces flare frequency in atopic dermatitis

atropine.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M1-M5). Blocks parasympathetic effects: increases heart rate (vagolytic), reduces secretions, relaxes smooth muscle, and causes mydriasis.

dosing.adult: Bradycardia: 0.5-1 mg IV every 3-5 min (max 3 mg total); Organophosphate poisoning: 2-4 mg IV initially, then double dose every 5-10 min until atropinisation (dry secretions, HR >80); Pre-anaesthetic: 0.3-0.6 mg IV/IM
dosing.pediatric: Bradycardia: 0.02 mg/kg IV (min 0.1 mg, max 0.5 mg/dose); OP poisoning: 0.05 mg/kg IV, repeat as needed
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment in acute settings
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment in acute settings

monitoring: Secretions (in OP poisoning — endpoint is drying of secretions)
atropine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta; may cause fetal tachycardia. Use only if clearly needed (e.g., OP poisoning). Excreted in breast milk in small amounts.
quick_flags: In OP poisoning, there is NO maximum dose — titrate until secretions dry
Minimum dose 0.5 mg in adults — lower doses can cause paradoxical bradycardia
Atropinisation endpoints: dry secretions, heart rate >80/min, clear chest on auscultation
Organophosphate poisoning is extremely common in rural India (pesticide exposure)
Do NOT rely on pupil size as endpoint in OP poisoning — use secretions

dopamine.mechanism: Dose-dependent effects on dopaminergic (D1), beta-1 adrenergic, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors. Low dose (1-5 mcg/kg/min): dopaminergic — renal and mesenteric vasodilation. Medium dose (5-10 mcg/kg/min): beta-1 — increased cardiac output. High dose (>10 mcg/kg/min): alpha-1 — vasoconstriction.
indications: Low cardiac output states post-cardiac surgery

dosing.adult: Start 2-5 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate in 5-10 mcg/kg/min increments every 10-30 min; usual range 2-20 mcg/kg/min; max 50 mcg/kg/min
dosing.pediatric: 1-20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate to response
dosing.renal: No specific dose adjustment; low-dose dopamine for renal protection is NOT supported by evidence
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment; response may be variable

dopamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if life-threatening indication. May reduce uterine blood flow. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Low-dose (renal-dose) dopamine does NOT prevent AKI — debunked by multiple trials
Norepinephrine is preferred first-line vasopressor in septic shock (Surviving Sepsis Guidelines)
Must be given through central line if possible — extravasation causes severe tissue necrosis
If extravasation occurs, infiltrate area with phentolamine 5-10 mg in 10-15 ml NS
Always dilute before infusion — never give undiluted IV push

noradrenaline.mechanism: Potent alpha-1 adrenergic agonist causing peripheral vasoconstriction and increased systemic vascular resistance. Mild beta-1 agonism increases cardiac contractility without significant beta-2 mediated vasodilation or tachycardia.
contraindications: Mesenteric or peripheral vascular thrombosis (unless life-saving)

dosing.adult: Start 0.1-0.3 mcg/kg/min (or 5-15 mcg/min) IV infusion via central line; titrate in 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min increments every 5-15 min to target MAP >=65 mmHg; usual range 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min; no fixed maximum
dosing.pediatric: Start 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min; titrate to response (max 2 mcg/kg/min)
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment; ensure adequate volume resuscitation
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment; response may be variable in hepatic failure

noradrenaline.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May reduce uteroplacental blood flow. Use only in life-threatening maternal hypotension. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: First-line vasopressor for septic shock (Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)
Always administer via central venous catheter — peripheral extravasation causes severe necrosis
If central access unavailable, use large-bore peripheral IV temporarily with close monitoring
Phentolamine is the antidote for extravasation injury
Target MAP >=65 mmHg — titrate to this endpoint, not a fixed dose

adenosine.mechanism: Activates A1 adenosine receptors in the AV node, hyperpolarizing cells by increasing potassium conductance and decreasing calcium influx. This transiently blocks AV nodal conduction, interrupting re-entrant circuits involving the AV node.
indications: Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) — conversion to sinus rhythm
Diagnosis of wide complex tachycardia (helps differentiate SVT with aberrancy from VT)
contraindications: Second or third-degree AV block (without pacemaker)

dosing.adult: 6 mg rapid IV push over 1-2 seconds followed by 20 ml NS flush; if no effect in 1-2 min: 12 mg rapid IV push; may repeat 12 mg once more. Use proximal (antecubital) vein
dosing.pediatric: First dose: 0.1 mg/kg (max 6 mg) rapid IV push; Second dose: 0.2 mg/kg (max 12 mg) if needed
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment (rapidly metabolised by red blood cells and endothelium)

monitoring: Continuous ECG (12-lead preferred — capture rhythm during administration)
Respiratory status in those with reactive airways
adenosine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Very short half-life (<10 seconds) limits fetal exposure. Has been used in pregnancy for SVT without reported adverse fetal effects. Compatible with breastfeeding due to ultra-short half-life.
quick_flags: Must be given as RAPID IV push followed immediately by NS flush — half-life is <10 seconds
Warn patient they will feel brief chest tightness, flushing, and sense of doom — transient asystole is expected
Reduce dose by 50% if patient is on dipyridamole or carbamazepine (potentiate effect)
Increase dose if on theophylline or caffeine (antagonise effect)
Always record ECG rhythm strip during administration for diagnostic purposes

naloxone.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at mu, kappa, and delta opioid receptors. Rapidly displaces opioids from receptors, reversing respiratory depression, sedation, and hypotension caused by opioid overdose.
indications: Neonatal opioid depression (born to opioid-treated mothers)
contraindications: Use with extreme caution in opioid-dependent patients (precipitates severe withdrawal)

dosing.adult: Opioid overdose: 0.4-2 mg IV/IM/SC; repeat every 2-3 minutes if needed (max 10 mg); if IV not available: 2 mg IM or intranasal; Post-operative: 0.1-0.2 mg IV every 2-3 min (titrate carefully to avoid pain)
dosing.pediatric: 0.1 mg/kg IV/IM (if <20 kg); 2 mg (if >=20 kg); Neonatal: 0.01 mg/kg IV, may repeat every 2-3 min
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment

adverse_effects: Acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, diarrhoea, piloerection, tachycardia)
monitoring: Respiratory rate, depth, and oxygen saturation (primary endpoint)
Watch for re-sedation (naloxone wears off before most opioids — re-dosing often needed)
naloxone.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. May precipitate opioid withdrawal in opioid-dependent mother and fetus. Use only for life-threatening opioid overdose. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts.
quick_flags: Duration of action (30-90 min) is SHORTER than most opioids — patient must be monitored for re-sedation and may need repeat doses or infusion
Titrate carefully in opioid-dependent patients to restore breathing without precipitating full withdrawal
IM route if IV access unavailable — intranasal formulation gaining availability
In suspected opioid overdose: give naloxone, do NOT wait for confirmation
For continuous infusion: 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour in NS or D5W

oxytocin.mechanism: Synthetic analogue of endogenous posterior pituitary hormone. Binds oxytocin receptors on uterine myometrial cells, stimulating phospholipase C and increasing intracellular calcium, causing rhythmic uterine contractions. Also stimulates milk let-down reflex.
indications: Active management of third stage of labour (AMTSL)
Prevention and treatment of postpartum haemorrhage
contraindications: Prior classical uterine incision or significant uterine surgery

dosing.adult: Labour induction: Start 1-2 mU/min IV infusion; increase by 1-2 mU/min every 30-40 min (max 20-40 mU/min); AMTSL: 10 IU IM after delivery of anterior shoulder; PPH treatment: 20-40 IU in 1 L NS at 250 ml/hr
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment

adverse_effects: Water intoxication and hyponatraemia (prolonged infusion at high doses)
Postpartum haemorrhage (uterine atony from receptor desensitization)
monitoring: Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring during induction/augmentation
Uterine contraction frequency, duration, and tone
Maternal blood pressure and heart rate
oxytocin.pregnancy_lactation: Used specifically in pregnancy for labour induction. Category X if used improperly for elective termination. Safe during breastfeeding — endogenous oxytocin is part of the milk let-down reflex.
quick_flags: WHO recommendation: 10 IU IM oxytocin is the drug of choice for PPH prevention (AMTSL)
Must have continuous fetal monitoring during oxytocin infusion
Hyperstimulation: >5 contractions in 10 min or contractions lasting >2 min — stop infusion immediately
Water intoxication risk with prolonged high-dose infusion — use isotonic fluids
Titrate to 3 contractions every 10 minutes, each lasting 40-60 seconds

forms: 25 mcg tablet (vaginal insert in some formulations)
misoprostol.mechanism: Synthetic prostaglandin E1 analogue that binds EP2/EP3 receptors on myometrial cells, increasing intracellular calcium and causing uterine contractions. Also softens and dilates the cervix. Originally developed as a gastro-protective agent against NSAID-induced ulcers.
indications: Prevention and treatment of postpartum haemorrhage (when oxytocin unavailable)
contraindications: Prior caesarean section or major uterine surgery (for induction — risk of rupture)
Active inflammatory bowel disease (for GI indication)

dosing.adult: PPH prevention: 600 mcg oral (single dose, when oxytocin unavailable); PPH treatment: 800 mcg sublingual; Labour induction: 25 mcg vaginally every 4-6 hours (max 6 doses); Cervical ripening: 25 mcg vaginally every 4 hours; MTP (with mifepristone): 400-800 mcg vaginal/sublingual/buccal 24-48 hours after mifepristone; Gastric ulcer prevention: 200 mcg QDS with food
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable in obstetric indications
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment for obstetric use; dose reduction may be needed for GI indication in severe impairment
dosing.hepatic: No formal dose adjustment; use with caution

adverse_effects: Uterine rupture (rare, higher risk with prior uterine scar)
monitoring: Fetal heart rate (if used for induction in viable pregnancy)
Signs of uterine rupture in scarred uterus
misoprostol.pregnancy_lactation: Category X for gastric ulcer indication. Used therapeutically in pregnancy under supervision for induction and MTP. Excreted in breast milk — clinically insignificant amounts.
quick_flags: WHO Essential Medicine for PPH prevention where oxytocin is unavailable (does not require cold chain)
Keep sublingual for PPH treatment as it has fastest onset
NEVER use for labour induction in women with prior caesarean — high risk of uterine rupture
25 mcg is the safe dose for labour induction — higher doses cause hyperstimulation
Widely misused for illegal abortions in India — can cause life-threatening haemorrhage

forms: 50% w/v injection (500 mg/ml) in 2 ml, 10 ml, and 20 ml ampoules
magnesium_sulfate.mechanism: Blocks neuromuscular transmission by reducing acetylcholine release at the motor end plate. Acts as a NMDA receptor antagonist and calcium channel blocker. Reduces cerebral vasospasm and raises seizure threshold. In tocolysis, inhibits myometrial contractions by competing with calcium.
indications: Fetal neuroprotection (given before preterm delivery <32 weeks)

dosing.adult: Eclampsia (Pritchard regimen): Loading: 4 g IV over 15-20 min + 10 g IM (5 g each buttock); Maintenance: 5 g IM every 4 hours for 24 hours after last seizure. Zuspan regimen: 4 g IV loading then 1 g/hr IV infusion for 24 hours. Fetal neuroprotection: 4 g IV over 20-30 min. Torsades: 2 g IV over 5-10 min
dosing.pediatric: 25-50 mg/kg IV over 20 min (max 2 g); for hypomagnesaemia: 25-50 mg/kg/dose every 4-6 hours
dosing.renal: Reduce dose or avoid if CrCl <30 ml/min; accumulation risk — monitor serum magnesium levels closely
dosing.hepatic: No specific dose adjustment

monitoring: Deep tendon reflexes (patellar reflex) — check before EVERY maintenance dose
Urine output (must be >=25 ml/hr or >=100 ml in 4 hours)
Serum magnesium levels (therapeutic: 4-7 mEq/L; toxic: >7 mEq/L)
magnesium_sulfate.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Used specifically in pregnancy for eclampsia and pre-eclampsia. Crosses placenta — can cause neonatal depression if given close to delivery. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: The three pillars of monitoring before each dose: knee reflexes present + RR >=16 + urine >=100 ml in last 4 hours
ALWAYS keep calcium gluconate (1 g IV) at bedside as antidote for MgSO4 toxicity
Loss of patellar reflexes is the earliest sign of toxicity — occurs at 9-12 mEq/L
MAGPIE trial: MgSO4 reduces eclampsia risk by 58% — standard of care worldwide
Do NOT combine with nifedipine — risk of severe hypotension

methyldopa.mechanism: Prodrug converted to alpha-methylnorepinephrine in the CNS, which stimulates central alpha-2 adrenergic receptors in the brainstem vasomotor centre. This reduces sympathetic outflow, decreasing peripheral vascular resistance, heart rate, and blood pressure.
contraindications: Active liver disease or history of methyldopa-associated hepatitis

dosing.adult: Start 250 mg BD-TDS; increase every 2-3 days; usual maintenance 500 mg-2 g/day in 2-3 divided doses; max 3 g/day
dosing.pediatric: 10 mg/kg/day in 2-4 divided doses; max 65 mg/kg/day or 3 g/day (whichever is less)
dosing.renal: GFR 10-50: dose every 8-12 hours; GFR <10: dose every 12-24 hours
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease; monitor LFTs closely

adverse_effects: Positive direct Coombs test (10-20% of patients)
monitoring: LFTs (baseline, then periodically during first 6-12 weeks and whenever unexplained fever occurs)
CBC and Coombs test (at baseline and 6 months)
methyldopa.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Longest safety record of any antihypertensive in pregnancy. Recommended as first-line by WHO and Indian guidelines for chronic hypertension in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk — considered compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for chronic hypertension in pregnancy — decades of safety data
Sedation is common initially but usually improves within days — counsel patients
Positive Coombs test in up to 20% but clinically significant haemolysis is rare
Hepatotoxicity can occur at any time — check LFTs if fever or malaise develops
Do NOT stop abruptly — taper gradually to avoid rebound hypertension

nitroglycerin.mechanism: Releases nitric oxide (NO) which activates guanylate cyclase, increasing cGMP in vascular smooth muscle leading to venodilation (predominantly) and arteriolar dilation, reducing preload and afterload.

dosing.adult: Sublingual: 0.3-0.6 mg every 5 min, max 3 doses. IV: 5 mcg/min, titrate by 5-10 mcg/min every 3-5 min. Oral SR: 2.6-6.5 mg 2-3 times daily. Patch: 0.2-0.8 mg/hr, apply for 12-14 hours with 10-12 hour nitrate-free interval.
dosing.pediatric: IV: 0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min, titrate as needed.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.

monitoring: Methemoglobin levels with prolonged high-dose IV use
nitroglycerin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: MUST maintain a nitrate-free interval of 10-12 hours daily to prevent tolerance
Absolutely contraindicated with PDE-5 inhibitors — can cause fatal hypotension
Sublingual tablets lose potency once bottle is opened — replace every 6 months
If 3 sublingual doses fail to relieve chest pain, suspect MI and seek emergency care

isosorbide_mononitrate.mechanism: Active metabolite of isosorbide dinitrate. Releases nitric oxide causing venodilation predominantly, reducing preload and myocardial oxygen demand.
contraindications: Acute myocardial infarction with low filling pressures

dosing.adult: Immediate release: 20 mg twice daily (8 AM and 3 PM, asymmetric dosing to allow nitrate-free interval). Extended release: 30-60 mg once daily in the morning; may increase to 120 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established.
dosing.renal: No adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; start with lower doses.

monitoring: Headache severity (often resolves with continued use)
isosorbide_mononitrate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Asymmetric dosing (e.g., 8 AM and 3 PM) essential to maintain nitrate-free interval
Better oral bioavailability than isosorbide dinitrate (~100% vs ~25%)
Headache is very common initially but often diminishes with continued use

ivabradine.drug_class: Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated (HCN) Channel Blocker (If Current Inhibitor)
ivabradine.mechanism: Selectively and specifically inhibits the If (funny) current in the sinoatrial node, reducing heart rate without affecting myocardial contractility, blood pressure, or intracardiac conduction.
indications: Chronic stable angina (in patients intolerant to beta-blockers)
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) with resting HR >=70 bpm on maximally tolerated beta-blocker
contraindications: Resting heart rate <70 bpm before treatment
Sick sinus syndrome / sinoatrial block (without pacemaker)
Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, macrolides)

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg twice daily with meals. After 2 weeks, adjust based on HR. Target resting HR 50-60 bpm. Max: 7.5 mg twice daily. Reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily if HR <50 bpm.
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely recommended; limited data in pediatric dilated cardiomyopathy.
dosing.renal: No adjustment for CrCl >15 mL/min; limited data for CrCl <15.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe impairment. Use with caution in moderate impairment.

ivabradine.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic in animal studies). Contraindicated during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Unique MOA — reduces HR without affecting BP or contractility
Phosphenes (transient visual brightness) are a class-specific side effect, usually benign and reversible
Only works in sinus rhythm — not effective in atrial fibrillation
Take with food to improve absorption and reduce GI upset

sacubitril_valsartan.mechanism: Sacubitril (prodrug) is converted to sacubitrilat which inhibits neprilysin, increasing levels of natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP) that promote vasodilation, natriuresis, and reduced fibrosis. Valsartan blocks angiotensin II AT1 receptors, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion.
indications: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF, NYHA II-IV)
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF — emerging evidence)
contraindications: Concurrent ACE inhibitor use (36-hour washout required)
History of angioedema with ACE inhibitor or ARB

dosing.adult: Start 24/26 mg or 49/51 mg twice daily. Double dose every 2-4 weeks as tolerated. Target: 97/103 mg twice daily.
dosing.pediatric: Approved for age >=1 year with HFrEF; weight-based dosing per manufacturer guidelines.
dosing.renal: eGFR <30: Start at 24/26 mg twice daily. No data for dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment (Child-Pugh B): Start 24/26 mg twice daily. Contraindicated in Child-Pugh C.

sacubitril_valsartan.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (fetal toxicity — Category D equivalent). Discontinue as soon as pregnancy is detected. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: PARADIGM-HF trial: 20% reduction in cardiovascular death vs enalapril
MUST have 36-hour washout from any ACE inhibitor before starting — angioedema risk
Replace ACE inhibitor/ARB — do not use concomitantly
Superior to enalapril for HFrEF; now guideline-recommended first-line

ranolazine.mechanism: Inhibits the late phase of the inward sodium current (late INa) in cardiomyocytes, reducing intracellular sodium and calcium overload, thereby improving myocardial relaxation and reducing diastolic tension without affecting heart rate or blood pressure.
indications: Chronic stable angina (as add-on or alternative when other antianginals are inadequate)
contraindications: Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir)
QTc prolongation or concurrent QT-prolonging drugs

dosing.adult: Start 500 mg twice daily. May increase to 1000 mg twice daily based on symptoms. Swallow whole; do not crush or chew.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established.
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate impairment. Use with caution in severe renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in hepatic cirrhosis. Use with caution in mild-moderate impairment.

monitoring: ECG (QTc interval) at baseline and periodically
ranolazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Does NOT reduce heart rate or blood pressure — unique among antianginals
Particularly useful as add-on therapy when beta-blockers/CCBs are maxed out
Avoid grapefruit juice (CYP3A4 inhibition)
May lower HbA1c slightly in diabetic patients (secondary benefit)

trimetazidine.drug_class: Metabolic Anti-ischemic Agent (Partial Fatty Acid Oxidation Inhibitor)
trimetazidine.mechanism: Inhibits mitochondrial long-chain 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase, shifting cardiac energy metabolism from fatty acid oxidation to glucose oxidation, which is more oxygen-efficient, thus protecting the myocardium during ischemia.
contraindications: Parkinsonian symptoms including tremor, restless leg syndrome

dosing.adult: Immediate release: 20 mg three times daily with meals. Modified release: 35 mg twice daily with meals.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60 mL/min: 35 mg once daily or 20 mg twice daily. CrCl <30: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution.

monitoring: Neurological symptoms (parkinsonism — reassess if tremor develops)
trimetazidine.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy due to insufficient data. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Contraindicated in Parkinson disease — can worsen or induce parkinsonian symptoms
Does not affect hemodynamics (HR/BP unchanged) — purely metabolic protection
Widely used in India as add-on antianginal
Must be taken with food for optimal absorption

tranexamic_acid.mechanism: Competitively inhibits the activation of plasminogen to plasmin by binding to lysine-binding sites on plasminogen, thereby preventing fibrin degradation and stabilizing blood clots.
contraindications: Active thromboembolic disease (DVT, PE, arterial thrombosis)

dosing.adult: Menorrhagia: 1-1.5 g three times daily for up to 4 days during menses. Trauma: 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hours of injury, then 1 g IV over 8 hours (CRASH-2 protocol). Surgical: 10-15 mg/kg IV before incision.
dosing.pediatric: 10-25 mg/kg/dose, 2-3 times daily (oral). IV: 10 mg/kg.
dosing.renal: CrCl 20-50: Reduce to twice daily. CrCl <20: Reduce to once daily or avoid.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment needed.

adverse_effects: Visual disturbances (retinal toxicity with prolonged use)
monitoring: Visual acuity and color vision (if prolonged use >4 weeks)
tranexamic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Crosses placenta. Generally considered safe for short-term use in PPH. Present in breast milk in small amounts.
quick_flags: CRASH-2 trial: Give within 3 hours of trauma — after 3 hours, may increase mortality
Very effective for menorrhagia — reduces blood loss by 40-50%
Topical use for epistaxis (soak gauze in IV formulation)
Do NOT give with Factor IX complex concentrates — thrombosis risk

darbepoetin.mechanism: Hyperglycosylated analog of erythropoietin with longer half-life. Stimulates erythropoiesis by binding to erythropoietin receptors on erythroid progenitor cells in bone marrow, promoting red cell proliferation and differentiation.
contraindications: Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) due to prior ESA use

dosing.adult: CKD anemia: 0.45 mcg/kg IV/SC every 1-2 weeks. Chemo anemia: 2.25 mcg/kg SC weekly or 500 mcg SC every 3 weeks. Titrate to Hb 10-11.5 g/dL; do not exceed 12 g/dL.
dosing.pediatric: CKD: 0.45 mcg/kg IV/SC weekly; adjust per Hb response.
dosing.renal: Used specifically for CKD anemia. Dose based on Hb response, not renal function.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment.

monitoring: Hemoglobin every 1-2 weeks until stable, then monthly (target 10-11.5 g/dL)
Iron studies (ferritin, TSAT) — ensure iron replete before starting
darbepoetin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Longer half-life than epoetin — less frequent dosing (weekly or biweekly vs thrice weekly)
NEVER target Hb >12 g/dL — increases thromboembolic risk and mortality (CHOIR/TREAT trials)
Check and correct iron deficiency before starting — ESA will not work without adequate iron stores
Store refrigerated; do not shake

filgrastim.mechanism: Recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF). Stimulates proliferation, differentiation, and functional activation of neutrophil precursors and mature neutrophils. Also mobilizes hematopoietic stem cells into peripheral blood.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to filgrastim or E. coli-derived proteins
Use within 24 hours before or after cytotoxic chemotherapy

dosing.adult: Chemotherapy-induced neutropenia: 5 mcg/kg/day SC or IV, start 24-72 hours after chemo completion, continue until ANC recovery. Stem cell mobilization: 10 mcg/kg/day SC for 4-5 days before collection.
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult on per-kg basis: 5-10 mcg/kg/day SC.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: Complete blood count with differential (twice weekly during therapy)
Spleen size (if prolonged use or symptoms)
filgrastim.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Do NOT give within 24 hours of chemotherapy — can amplify myelotoxicity
Bone pain is very common — manage with paracetamol or NSAIDs
Monitor for splenic rupture — advise patients to report left upper quadrant pain
Store refrigerated; bring to room temperature before injection

hydroxyurea.mechanism: Inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, blocking the conversion of ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides, thereby arresting DNA synthesis in S phase. In sickle cell disease, it increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production, reducing sickling.
indications: Sickle cell disease (to reduce crises and ACS)

dosing.adult: Sickle cell: Start 15 mg/kg/day; titrate by 5 mg/kg every 12 weeks up to 35 mg/kg/day or MTD. CML/MPNs: 20-30 mg/kg/day. Round dose to nearest 250 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Sickle cell (>=2 years): Start 20 mg/kg/day; titrate to MTD based on counts.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: Reduce dose by 50%. CrCl <30 or dialysis: Reduce by 50-80%; administer post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No specific guidelines; monitor closely.

monitoring: CBC with differential every 2 weeks initially, then monthly
hydroxyurea.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (Category D — teratogenic). Both men and women should use effective contraception. Contraindicated in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Disease-modifying in sickle cell disease — increases HbF and reduces crises by 50%
CBC monitoring is essential — hold if ANC <2000 or platelets <80,000
Warn about skin hyperpigmentation and nail changes (melanonychia)
Macrocytosis (high MCV) is an expected pharmacologic effect, not a side effect

deferasirox.mechanism: Selectively binds trivalent (ferric, Fe3+) iron with high affinity in a 2:1 ratio, forming a complex that is excreted primarily in feces, thereby reducing total body iron burden.
indications: Chronic iron overload due to transfusion-dependent anemias (thalassemia major, sickle cell, MDS)
contraindications: CrCl <40 mL/min or serum creatinine >2x ULN
Poor performance status / advanced malignancy with low life expectancy

dosing.adult: Dispersible tablets: Start 20 mg/kg/day on empty stomach, 30 min before food. Adjust by 5-10 mg/kg every 3-6 months based on ferritin. Max 40 mg/kg/day. Film-coated tablets: Start 14 mg/kg/day with or without light meal; max 28 mg/kg/day.
dosing.pediatric: >=2 years: Same dosing as adults on per-kg basis.
dosing.renal: CrCl 40-60: Reduce starting dose by 50%. CrCl <40: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh A): No adjustment. Moderate-severe: Not recommended.

monitoring: Serum creatinine weekly for first month, then monthly
deferasirox.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (Category D — embryotoxic in animals). Effective contraception required. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Oral iron chelator — major advantage over IV deferoxamine for compliance in thalassemia
Dispersible tablets taken on EMPTY stomach; film-coated tablets with LIGHT meal — formulations NOT interchangeable mg-for-mg
Renal toxicity is the most important adverse effect — monitor creatinine closely
GI side effects are common initially but often improve with continued use

tamsulosin.mechanism: Selectively blocks alpha-1A adrenergic receptors in the prostate, bladder neck, and prostatic urethra, causing smooth muscle relaxation and improved urine flow without significant blood pressure reduction.
indications: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — lower urinary tract symptoms
contraindications: Planned cataract surgery (risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome — IFIS)

dosing.adult: BPH: 0.4 mg once daily, 30 minutes after the same meal each day. Stone expulsion: 0.4 mg once daily for 2-4 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable.
dosing.renal: CrCl >10 mL/min: No adjustment. CrCl <10: Not studied; use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: No adjustment. Severe: Not studied.

monitoring: Inform ophthalmologist of tamsulosin use before any eye surgery
tamsulosin.pregnancy_lactation: Not applicable (indicated for males). Category B.
quick_flags: MUST inform ophthalmologist before cataract surgery — IFIS risk persists even after stopping
Uroselective — less hypotension than prazosin/doxazosin
Retrograde ejaculation is common but reversible on discontinuation
Commonly used off-label for ureteric stone expulsion (4-10 mm stones)

silodosin.mechanism: Highly selective blockade of alpha-1A adrenergic receptors in the prostate and lower urinary tract smooth muscle, with minimal effect on alpha-1B receptors (vascular), providing targeted relief of BPH symptoms with minimal blood pressure effects.
indications: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — lower urinary tract symptoms
contraindications: Concurrent use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors

dosing.adult: 8 mg once daily with a meal. May reduce to 4 mg if not tolerated.
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: 4 mg once daily. CrCl <30: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: No adjustment. Severe: Contraindicated.

quick_flags: Most alpha-1A selective — lowest risk of orthostatic hypotension among alpha-blockers
Very high rate of retrograde ejaculation (~28%) — important to counsel patients
Must take with food to reduce GI side effects
Less BP effect than tamsulosin — advantageous in elderly patients

dutasteride.mechanism: Inhibits both type 1 and type 2 isoforms of 5-alpha reductase, blocking the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) more completely than finasteride, reducing DHT levels by >90% and decreasing prostate volume.
indications: Male pattern hair loss (androgenetic alopecia — off-label)

dosing.adult: BPH: 0.5 mg once daily. Combination therapy with tamsulosin (Duodart) is common. Effect on prostate volume takes 3-6 months.
dosing.pediatric: Contraindicated.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. No specific guidelines; metabolism is hepatic.

monitoring: PSA levels (reduces PSA by ~50% — double the measured value for accurate screening)
dutasteride.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in women. Women of childbearing potential should NOT handle crushed/broken capsules (teratogenic — risk of male fetus feminization). Men on dutasteride should use condoms if partner is pregnant or may become pregnant.
quick_flags: PSA will drop ~50% — MUST double the PSA value for accurate prostate cancer screening
Inhibits both type 1 and type 2 5-AR (finasteride only inhibits type 2)
Takes 3-6 months for symptom benefit; maximum at 12 months
Very long half-life (~5 weeks) — effects persist long after discontinuation
Women must NOT handle broken capsules — teratogenic to male fetus

solifenacin.mechanism: Selectively antagonizes M3 muscarinic receptors in the detrusor muscle of the bladder, reducing involuntary detrusor contractions and increasing bladder capacity.
indications: Overactive bladder (OAB) with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg once daily. May increase to 10 mg once daily if needed.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years.
dosing.renal: CrCl <30 mL/min: Max 5 mg/day.
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment (Child-Pugh B): Max 5 mg/day. Severe: Contraindicated.

monitoring: Post-void residual volume (if symptoms of retention)
Intraocular pressure in narrow-angle glaucoma risk
solifenacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: M3-selective — fewer CNS side effects than oxybutynin
Use with caution in elderly — anticholinergic cognitive burden
Avoid in patients with constipation or GI motility disorders
If dry mouth is significant, consider mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) as alternative

dapoxetine.mechanism: Short-acting SSRI that inhibits serotonin reuptake in the synaptic cleft, increasing serotonergic activity in ejaculatory pathways. Rapid onset and short half-life make it suitable for on-demand use.
indications: Premature ejaculation in men aged 18-64 years
contraindications: Significant cardiac disease (heart failure, conduction abnormalities)

dosing.adult: Start 30 mg as needed, 1-3 hours before sexual activity. May increase to 60 mg if inadequate response. Max: once per 24 hours.
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable in children.
dosing.renal: Mild-moderate: No adjustment. Severe: Not recommended.
dosing.hepatic: Mild: No adjustment. Moderate-severe: Contraindicated.

monitoring: Risk of syncope — test with first dose
dapoxetine.pregnancy_lactation: Not indicated for females. Category not assigned. Men should use condom if partner is pregnant.
quick_flags: Only SSRI approved for on-demand use (not daily dosing)
Very short half-life (~1.5 hours) — minimal accumulation
Counsel about syncope risk — avoid alcohol, stay hydrated
Do not combine with PDE-5 inhibitors without careful monitoring (additive hypotension)

itopride.mechanism: Dual mechanism: blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the upper GI tract and inhibits acetylcholinesterase, thereby enhancing acetylcholine-mediated gastric motility and promoting gastric emptying.
contraindications: GI hemorrhage, mechanical obstruction, or perforation
Prolactinoma or condition where prolactin stimulation is undesirable

dosing.adult: 50 mg three times daily before meals.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established in children.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution in severe impairment.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution.

itopride.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy due to insufficient safety data. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Dual MOA (D2 block + AChE inhibition) — may be more effective than metoclopramide for dyspepsia
Lower risk of extrapyramidal symptoms and QTc prolongation than metoclopramide
Does not cross blood-brain barrier significantly — fewer central side effects
Very commonly prescribed prokinetic in India

pinaverium.mechanism: Selectively blocks L-type calcium channels in gastrointestinal smooth muscle cells without systemic cardiovascular effects, reducing colonic smooth muscle spasm and visceral hypersensitivity.
indications: Functional bowel disorders with abdominal pain/spasm
contraindications: Galactose intolerance or glucose-galactose malabsorption (due to excipients)

dosing.adult: 50 mg three times daily with meals; may increase to 100 mg three times daily for severe symptoms.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children.
dosing.renal: No adjustment required (minimal systemic absorption).
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required.

pinaverium.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy due to limited data. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: GI-selective — no cardiovascular effects (unlike systemic calcium channel blockers)
Minimal systemic absorption (<10%) — very well tolerated
MUST be taken with food and a full glass of water to prevent esophageal irritation
Useful in IBS patients who cannot tolerate anticholinergic antispasmodics

pancreatin.mechanism: Contains exogenous lipase, protease, and amylase enzymes that substitute for deficient pancreatic secretions, enabling digestion and absorption of fats, proteins, and carbohydrates in the small intestine.
indications: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis)
contraindications: Acute pancreatitis (relative — may worsen acute inflammation)

dosing.adult: Start lipase 25,000-50,000 units per main meal and 10,000-25,000 units per snack. Adjust based on steatorrhea control. Max: 10,000 lipase units/kg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Infants: 2000-4000 lipase units per 120 mL formula. Children 1-4 years: 1000 lipase units/kg/meal. Children >4 years: 500 lipase units/kg/meal. Max: 10,000 units/kg/day.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment.

adverse_effects: Fibrosing colonopathy (very high doses in children)
monitoring: Fat-soluble vitamin levels (A, D, E, K)
pancreatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use if benefit outweighs risk. Enzymes are not systemically absorbed. Generally considered safe.
quick_flags: Must be taken WITH meals or snacks — enzymes need food substrate to work
Do not crush or chew enteric-coated capsules — acid destroys enzymes
Dose is titrated to LIPASE content, not total enzyme units
Do NOT exceed 10,000 lipase units/kg/day — risk of fibrosing colonopathy in children

testosterone.mechanism: Exogenous testosterone replaces endogenous testosterone deficiency. Binds to intracellular androgen receptors, modulating gene transcription for development and maintenance of male sexual characteristics, muscle mass, bone density, erythropoiesis, and libido.
contraindications: Pregnancy (Category X — virilization of female fetus)

dosing.adult: IM injection (enanthate/cypionate): 50-400 mg every 2-4 weeks. Undecanoate oral: 40-80 mg 2-3 times daily with fatty meals. Gel: 50 mg (5 g) daily applied to clean, dry skin. Sustanon: 250 mg IM every 3 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Delayed puberty: 50-100 mg IM monthly for 4-6 months.
dosing.renal: Use with caution — may cause fluid retention.
dosing.hepatic: Oral formulations: Use with caution. Avoid 17-alpha alkylated forms (hepatotoxic). Undecanoate is less hepatotoxic.

testosterone.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Causes virilization of female fetus.
quick_flags: Polycythemia is the most common dose-limiting adverse effect — monitor Hct regularly
Exogenous testosterone SUPPRESSES spermatogenesis — do NOT use for male infertility
Oral undecanoate (Cernos) must be taken with fatty food for absorption
Gel: warn about secondary transfer — wash hands, cover application site

cabergoline.mechanism: Potent, long-acting dopamine D2 receptor agonist that inhibits prolactin secretion from anterior pituitary lactotroph cells. Higher D2 affinity and longer duration of action than bromocriptine.
indications: Adjunct in Parkinson disease (higher doses — off-label)
contraindications: Hepatic impairment with concurrent use planned long-term

dosing.adult: Hyperprolactinemia: Start 0.25 mg twice weekly. Increase by 0.25 mg at monthly intervals. Usual dose: 0.25-2 mg weekly. Lactation inhibition: 1 mg single dose within 24 hours of delivery.
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; specialist use only.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe impairment; lower doses may be needed.

adverse_effects: Cardiac valvulopathy (rare, mainly at high Parkinson doses)
monitoring: Echocardiogram at baseline and every 1-2 years (if long-term/high-dose use)
cabergoline.pregnancy_lactation: Discontinue when pregnancy confirmed (unless macroadenoma requires treatment). Generally avoid in breastfeeding as it suppresses lactation.
quick_flags: Preferred over bromocriptine — more effective, better tolerated, twice-weekly dosing
Cardiac valve fibrosis risk mainly with high doses used in Parkinson disease
Very effective at shrinking prolactinomas — often first-line over surgery
Can restore fertility in hyperprolactinemic infertility — counsel about contraception if not desired

desmopressin.mechanism: Synthetic analogue of vasopressin (ADH) that increases plasma levels of Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor by releasing them from endothelial storage sites. Also increases renal water reabsorption via V2 receptors.
indications: Mild hemophilia A (to boost Factor VIII levels)
contraindications: Type IIB von Willebrand disease (may worsen thrombocytopenia)
Moderate to severe renal impairment (for enuresis indication)

dosing.adult: Hemophilia/vWD: 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 15-30 min; Diabetes insipidus: 1-4 mcg IV/SC BD or 10-40 mcg intranasal; Enuresis: 0.2-0.4 mg oral at bedtime
dosing.pediatric: Hemophilia/vWD: 0.3 mcg/kg IV; Diabetes insipidus: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/day; Enuresis (>5 years): 0.2 mg oral at bedtime
dosing.renal: Avoid or use with extreme caution in CKD (hyponatremia risk); contraindicated in enuresis with CrCl <50
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Factor VIII and vWF levels (for hemostatic use)
desmopressin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe in pregnancy for diabetes insipidus. Minimal excretion in breast milk.
quick_flags: Restrict fluid intake to 1-1.5 L around dosing to prevent hyponatremia
Tachyphylaxis develops with repeated use (>3 doses in 48 h) — reduced Factor VIII response
Always do a therapeutic trial before relying on DDAVP for surgery in mild hemophilia A

teriparatide.mechanism: Recombinant fragment of human parathyroid hormone (amino acids 1-34). Intermittent (pulsatile) administration stimulates osteoblast activity more than osteoclast activity, promoting new bone formation (anabolic effect) — unlike continuous PTH elevation in hyperparathyroidism which is catabolic.
indications: Severe osteoporosis with high fracture risk
contraindications: Metabolic bone diseases other than osteoporosis (e.g., Paget disease)
History of bone malignancy or skeletal metastases

dosing.adult: 20 mcg SC once daily in thigh or abdomen. Maximum treatment duration: 24 months (lifetime cumulative).
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended (open epiphyses — risk of osteosarcoma in animal studies).
dosing.renal: eGFR 30-60: Use with caution. eGFR <30: Not recommended.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment needed.

teriparatide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Not recommended in pregnancy. Avoid breastfeeding during treatment.
quick_flags: Only ANABOLIC osteoporosis drug — builds new bone (unlike bisphosphonates which only prevent resorption)
Limited to 24 months total lifetime use (osteosarcoma signal in rat studies — not confirmed in humans)
MUST follow with bisphosphonate or denosumab after stopping to prevent bone loss
First injection: sit or lie down (orthostatic hypotension risk)
Store refrigerated; pen is multi-dose for 28 days

alendronate.mechanism: Binds to hydroxyapatite in bone, is internalized by osteoclasts during resorption, and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase (mevalonate pathway), leading to osteoclast apoptosis and reduced bone resorption.
contraindications: Inability to stand/sit upright for 30 minutes

dosing.adult: Osteoporosis treatment: 70 mg once weekly OR 10 mg once daily. Prevention: 35 mg weekly OR 5 mg daily. Paget disease: 40 mg once daily for 6 months.
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely recommended; specialist use only (osteogenesis imperfecta).
dosing.renal: CrCl 35-60: No adjustment. CrCl <35: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required (not hepatically metabolized).

adverse_effects: Osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ — rare, mainly IV bisphosphonates)
monitoring: Serum calcium and vitamin D (correct deficiency before starting)
alendronate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Not recommended in pregnancy due to fetal skeletal effects. Long skeletal half-life means residual drug present even after stopping.
quick_flags: STRICT dosing rules: Take on empty stomach with FULL GLASS of PLAIN WATER, remain UPRIGHT for 30 minutes, no food/drink/other meds for 30 minutes
Correct calcium and vitamin D deficiency BEFORE starting
Consider drug holiday after 5 years (oral) — re-evaluate fracture risk
Dental exam before starting — ONJ risk with invasive dental procedures
Weekly dosing (70 mg) is preferred for better compliance and fewer GI side effects

fexofenadine.mechanism: Active metabolite of terfenadine. Selectively antagonizes peripheral H1 histamine receptors with negligible CNS penetration and no anticholinergic activity. Does not cross blood-brain barrier significantly — truly non-sedating.

dosing.adult: Allergic rhinitis: 120 mg once daily or 60 mg twice daily. Chronic urticaria: 180 mg once daily.
dosing.pediatric: 2-11 years: 30 mg twice daily. >=12 years: Adult dosing.
dosing.renal: CrCl <40: Start 60 mg once daily.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

fexofenadine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: Truly non-sedating — does NOT impair driving or cognitive performance
Do NOT take with grapefruit, orange, or apple juice — reduces absorption by ~30%
No QTc prolongation (unlike its parent drug terfenadine)
No dose adjustment needed for hepatic impairment

levocetirizine.mechanism: Active R-enantiomer of cetirizine with approximately twice the binding affinity for H1 receptors. Selectively blocks peripheral H1 histamine receptors with minimal CNS and anticholinergic effects.

dosing.adult: 5 mg once daily in the evening.
dosing.pediatric: 6 months-5 years: 1.25 mg once daily. 6-11 years: 2.5 mg once daily. >=12 years: 5 mg once daily.
dosing.renal: CrCl 50-80: 2.5 mg once daily. CrCl 30-50: 2.5 mg every other day. CrCl 10-30: 2.5 mg twice weekly. CrCl <10: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for hepatic impairment alone.

levocetirizine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe. Excreted in breast milk; use with caution.
quick_flags: R-enantiomer of cetirizine — same efficacy at half the dose (5 mg vs 10 mg)
Less sedating than cetirizine
Evening dosing preferred for allergic rhinitis (symptoms worse at night)
Widely used in Indian FDC combinations (especially with montelukast)

montelukast_levocetirizine.mechanism: Dual mechanism: Montelukast blocks cysteinyl leukotriene CysLT1 receptors, reducing bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion, and eosinophilic inflammation. Levocetirizine blocks peripheral H1 histamine receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms (rhinorrhea, sneezing, pruritus).
contraindications: Neuropsychiatric history (relative — montelukast black box warning)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet (10 mg + 5 mg) once daily in the evening.
dosing.pediatric: 6-14 years: Montelukast 5 mg + Levocetirizine 2.5 mg once daily. 2-5 years: Montelukast 4 mg + Levocetirizine 2.5 mg once daily.
dosing.renal: Reduce based on levocetirizine component. Avoid in CrCl <10.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for mild-moderate. Avoid in severe hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Neuropsychiatric effects (agitation, depression, suicidal ideation — montelukast FDA boxed warning)
montelukast_levocetirizine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B (both components individually). Use with caution. Benefits generally outweigh risks for asthma control.
quick_flags: FDA BLACK BOX WARNING on montelukast for neuropsychiatric events — counsel patients/parents
One of the most commonly prescribed FDCs in India for allergic rhinitis
Evening dosing essential — montelukast works best at night (leukotriene levels peak nocturnally)
Monitor children and adolescents for behavioral changes, sleep disturbances, suicidal ideation

chlorpheniramine.mechanism: Competitively antagonizes H1 histamine receptors. Crosses blood-brain barrier readily, causing sedation and anticholinergic effects. Also has mild anticholinergic, antiserotonergic, and local anesthetic properties.

dosing.adult: 4 mg every 4-6 hours. Max: 24 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 years: 1 mg twice daily. 2-5 years: 1-2 mg every 4-6 hours (max 6 mg/day). 6-12 years: 2 mg every 4-6 hours (max 12 mg/day).
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution in severe impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may accumulate.

chlorpheniramine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Generally considered safe in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk; may cause irritability or sedation in nursing infants.
quick_flags: Highly sedating — warn patients about driving and operating machinery
Avoid in elderly (Beers Criteria) — high anticholinergic burden, fall risk, confusion
Paradoxical excitation common in young children
Available OTC in India — frequently misused for sedation

methotrexate.mechanism: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), blocking conversion of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate, thereby inhibiting purine and thymidylate synthesis. At low doses (rheumatic), also promotes adenosine release (anti-inflammatory) and inhibits T-cell activation.
contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment / alcoholic liver disease

dosing.adult: RA/Psoriasis: Start 7.5-15 mg ONCE WEEKLY (oral or SC). Titrate by 2.5-5 mg every 4-8 weeks. Max: 25 mg/week. Always supplement with folic acid 5 mg weekly (not on MTX day).
dosing.pediatric: JIA: 10-15 mg/m2/week (oral or SC). Max: 20 mg/m2/week.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: Reduce dose by 50%. CrCl <30: Contraindicated.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment. Avoid with alcohol.

monitoring: CBC with differential every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 1-3 months
methotrexate.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Absolutely contraindicated. Causes neural tube defects, limb defects, and spontaneous abortion. Both men and women must use contraception during and 3-6 months after stopping.
quick_flags: ONCE WEEKLY dosing — accidental daily dosing causes fatal pancytopenia (common prescribing error)
Always co-prescribe folic acid 5 mg weekly (24-48 hours after MTX) to reduce toxicity
Methotrexate pneumonitis can mimic infection — high index of suspicion if new cough/dyspnea
Avoid NSAIDs, trimethoprim, and probenecid — increase MTX toxicity
Leucovorin rescue for high-dose MTX toxicity

azathioprine.mechanism: Prodrug converted to 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP), which is further metabolized to thioguanine nucleotides that incorporate into DNA, inhibiting purine synthesis and suppressing T-cell and B-cell proliferation.
indications: Organ transplant rejection prophylaxis (with other immunosuppressants)
Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis)
contraindications: Concurrent allopurinol (without dose reduction — fatal myelosuppression)
Pregnancy (relative — Category D but used cautiously in organ transplant)

dosing.adult: Transplant: 3-5 mg/kg/day initially, then 1-3 mg/kg/day maintenance. Autoimmune: Start 1 mg/kg/day; increase by 0.5 mg/kg every 4 weeks to max 2.5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Similar to adult on per-kg basis.
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-50: Reduce dose by 25%. CrCl <10: Reduce by 50%.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may need dose reduction.

adverse_effects: Increased malignancy risk (lymphoma, skin cancer with long-term use)
monitoring: CBC with differential weekly for first month, biweekly for 2 months, then monthly
azathioprine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic in animals but used in transplant patients when benefit outweighs risk. Active metabolites excreted in breast milk — avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: CRITICAL: If patient is on allopurinol, REDUCE azathioprine dose by 75% — allopurinol blocks xanthine oxidase, causing fatal 6-MP accumulation
Check TPMT genotype before starting if available — homozygous deficient patients get severe myelosuppression
Pancreatitis is idiosyncratic — discontinue permanently if occurs
Onset of action in autoimmune disease: 6-12 weeks

mycophenolate.mechanism: Prodrug hydrolyzed to mycophenolic acid (MPA), which selectively and reversibly inhibits inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH) type II, the rate-limiting enzyme in the de novo purine synthesis pathway in T and B lymphocytes, thereby suppressing lymphocyte proliferation.
indications: Organ transplant rejection prophylaxis (kidney, heart, liver)

dosing.adult: Transplant: 1 g twice daily (mycophenolate mofetil) or 720 mg twice daily (enteric-coated mycophenolic acid). Lupus nephritis: 1-1.5 g twice daily.
dosing.pediatric: Transplant: 600 mg/m2 twice daily (max 2 g/day).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment per renal function, but avoid doses >1 g twice daily if GFR <25.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution.

monitoring: CBC weekly for first month, biweekly for months 2-3, then monthly
Pregnancy testing (before starting in women of childbearing age)
mycophenolate.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. REMS program in place. Causes congenital malformations (ear, facial, cardiac, limb). Two forms of contraception required 4 weeks before, during, and 6 weeks after treatment. Contraindicated in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Diarrhea is the most common dose-limiting side effect — switch to enteric-coated formulation if GI intolerance
More selective immunosuppression than azathioprine (preferentially targets lymphocytes)
Do NOT use simultaneously with azathioprine
Must have negative pregnancy test and use two forms of contraception (REMS)

cyclosporine.mechanism: Binds to cyclophilin; the complex inhibits calcineurin, a phosphatase required for NFAT dephosphorylation and nuclear translocation, thereby blocking IL-2 transcription and T-cell activation/proliferation.

dosing.adult: Transplant: 10-15 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses initially; taper to 3-6 mg/kg/day by 3 months (guided by levels). RA/Psoriasis: 2.5-5 mg/kg/day. Nephrotic syndrome: 3-5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Similar to adult on per-kg basis; often need higher mg/kg doses.
dosing.renal: Monitor closely; reduce dose if creatinine rises >30% above baseline.
dosing.hepatic: May need dose adjustment; hepatic metabolism is significant.

monitoring: Trough levels (C0): Target varies by indication and time post-transplant
Serum creatinine (at least twice weekly initially, then monthly)
cyclosporine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Used in transplant patients when benefit outweighs risk. Lower birth weight reported. Excreted in breast milk — avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Narrow therapeutic index — MUST monitor trough levels regularly
Nephrotoxicity is the major dose-limiting toxicity — distinguish from transplant rejection
Modified (microemulsion) formulation (Neoral/Panimun Bioral) has better bioavailability than original Sandimmun
Avoid grapefruit juice, St. John's wort — major CYP3A4 interactions
Gingival hyperplasia + hirsutism are cosmetically distressing — common in Indian practice

allopurinol.mechanism: Structural analog of hypoxanthine. Inhibits xanthine oxidase, blocking the conversion of hypoxanthine to xanthine and xanthine to uric acid, thereby reducing uric acid synthesis and serum urate levels.
indications: Hyperuricemia secondary to chemotherapy (tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis)
Recurrent calcium oxalate kidney stones (with hyperuricosuria)
contraindications: Acute gout flare (do not initiate during — but do not stop if already on it)
Hypersensitivity (risk of severe hypersensitivity syndrome / DRESS)
HLA-B*5801 positive patients (screen in high-risk populations: Southeast Asian, Korean, African American)

dosing.adult: Start 100 mg once daily. Titrate by 100 mg every 2-4 weeks. Target serum urate <6 mg/dL. Usual maintenance: 200-300 mg/day. Max: 800 mg/day. Co-prescribe colchicine or NSAID for first 3-6 months (flare prophylaxis).
dosing.pediatric: Secondary hyperuricemia: <6 years: 150 mg/day. 6-10 years: 300 mg/day. >10 years: Adult dosing.
dosing.renal: CrCl 20-40: Max 200 mg/day. CrCl 10-20: Max 100 mg/day. CrCl <10: Max 100 mg every 2-3 days.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; monitor LFTs.

monitoring: Watch for rash — discontinue immediately if occurs
allopurinol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Screen HLA-B*5801 in at-risk populations (SE Asian, Korean, African American) before starting — predicts severe hypersensitivity
Start low, go slow — rapid urate lowering triggers gout flares
Always co-prescribe flare prophylaxis (low-dose colchicine 0.5 mg/day) for first 3-6 months
CRITICAL: If patient on azathioprine/6-MP, reduce their dose by 75% (xanthine oxidase inhibition blocks metabolism)

febuxostat.drug_class: Non-purine Selective Xanthine Oxidase Inhibitor (Urate-Lowering Agent)
febuxostat.mechanism: Non-purine, selective inhibitor of xanthine oxidase (both oxidized and reduced forms), blocking conversion of hypoxanthine to xanthine and xanthine to uric acid. More potent and selective than allopurinol.
indications: Alternative when allopurinol is contraindicated, not tolerated, or inadequate
contraindications: Known cardiovascular disease (relative — use allopurinol first per CARES trial)

dosing.adult: Start 40 mg once daily. If serum urate not <6 mg/dL after 2-4 weeks, increase to 80 mg once daily. Max: 120 mg/day. Co-prescribe flare prophylaxis for first 3-6 months.
dosing.pediatric: Safety and efficacy not established.
dosing.renal: Mild-moderate (CrCl 30-89): No adjustment needed. Severe (CrCl <30): Limited data; use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate (Child-Pugh A-B): No adjustment. Severe: Not recommended.

monitoring: LFTs at baseline, 2 and 4 months, then periodically
febuxostat.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Not recommended in pregnancy. Animal studies showed teratogenicity. Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: CARES trial: Higher cardiovascular death vs allopurinol in patients with pre-existing CV disease — use allopurinol first-line in CV patients
Advantage over allopurinol: No need for HLA-B*5801 screening, less renal dose adjustment
Like allopurinol, must co-prescribe flare prophylaxis when initiating
Do NOT combine with azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine

sildenafil.mechanism: Selectively inhibits phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE-5), preventing degradation of cGMP in corpus cavernosum smooth muscle and pulmonary vascular smooth muscle. Increased cGMP causes smooth muscle relaxation, enhanced erectile response (ED) and pulmonary vasodilation (PAH).
contraindications: Concurrent alpha-blockers (use with caution — orthostatic hypotension)

dosing.adult: ED: 50 mg as needed, 1 hour before sexual activity. Range: 25-100 mg. Max: once per 24 hours. PAH (Revatio): 20 mg three times daily (4-6 hours apart).
dosing.pediatric: PAH in children: 10 mg three times daily for <20 kg; 20 mg TID for >20 kg (specialist use only).
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: Start 25 mg.
dosing.hepatic: Start 25 mg.

adverse_effects: Non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION — rare)
sildenafil.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Not indicated in women (though studied for PAH in pregnancy with uncertain benefit). Excretion in breast milk unknown.
quick_flags: ABSOLUTE contraindication with nitrates — fatal hypotensive crisis; wait 24 hours after sildenafil before any nitrate
High-fat meal delays absorption — take on relatively empty stomach for ED
Blue vision (cyanopsia) is due to weak PDE-6 inhibition in retina — reversible
Separate ED and PAH indications use DIFFERENT brand names and doses

tadalafil.mechanism: Selectively inhibits PDE-5, increasing cGMP in corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature. Has longer half-life (~17.5 hours) than sildenafil, allowing daily low-dose use and longer window of efficacy.
indications: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — lower urinary tract symptoms

dosing.adult: ED (on-demand): 10 mg before activity; may increase to 20 mg. Max: once per 24 hours. ED/BPH (daily): 5 mg once daily at same time. PAH: 40 mg once daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable for ED/BPH. PAH in children: specialist use.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: Start 5 mg (on-demand). CrCl <30: Max 5 mg once daily; avoid on-demand 10-20 mg.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: Max 10 mg (on-demand). Severe: Avoid.

tadalafil.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Not indicated in women. No data on breast milk excretion.
quick_flags: Longest-acting PDE-5 inhibitor — 'weekend pill' with ~36-hour window
Daily 5 mg dosing can treat BOTH ED and BPH simultaneously
Back pain and myalgia are more common with tadalafil than other PDE-5 inhibitors (PDE-11 cross-reactivity)
NOT affected by food — unlike sildenafil
Same absolute contraindication with nitrates — wait 48 hours (longer than sildenafil due to longer half-life)

n_acetylcysteine.mechanism: Breaks disulfide bonds in mucus glycoproteins (mucolytic). Acts as a precursor to glutathione synthesis, replenishing hepatic glutathione stores (critical in paracetamol toxicity). Also has direct antioxidant properties via its free sulfhydryl group.

dosing.adult: Paracetamol overdose (IV protocol): 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 hours. Mucolytic: 200 mg three times daily or 600 mg once daily (oral). Nebulization: 3-5 mL of 20% solution every 6-8 hours.
dosing.pediatric: Paracetamol overdose: Same mg/kg protocol with adjusted fluid volumes. Mucolytic: 100-200 mg 2-3 times daily (age-dependent).
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment.
dosing.hepatic: Used specifically to TREAT hepatotoxicity. No contraindication in liver disease.

adverse_effects: Anaphylactoid reactions (IV — histamine-mediated, 10-20%: flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm)
monitoring: Anaphylactoid reactions during IV infusion (especially first bag)
n_acetylcysteine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe and recommended for paracetamol overdose in pregnancy (benefits clearly outweigh risks). Oral mucolytic use generally considered safe.
quick_flags: LIFE-SAVING antidote for paracetamol overdose — most effective within 8-10 hours but give at any time
Anaphylactoid reaction with IV (not true allergy) — slow infusion rate, give antihistamines, usually safe to resume
Tastes and smells terrible (sulfur) — mix oral formulation with cola or juice to improve compliance
600 mg effervescent OTC is a popular mucolytic in India

cholecalciferol.mechanism: Cholecalciferol (D3) is hydroxylated in the liver to 25-hydroxyvitamin D (calcidiol), then in the kidney to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol, active form). Calcitriol binds to vitamin D receptors (VDR), promoting intestinal calcium and phosphorus absorption, bone mineralization, and immune modulation.

dosing.adult: Deficiency treatment: 60,000 IU once weekly for 8-12 weeks (Indian protocol), then monthly maintenance. Maintenance: 1000-2000 IU daily or 60,000 IU monthly. Severe deficiency (<10 ng/mL): 60,000 IU weekly for 12 weeks, then monthly.
dosing.pediatric: Infants (0-1 year): 400 IU daily. 1-18 years: 600-1000 IU daily. Deficiency treatment: 1000-5000 IU daily or 60,000 IU weekly (age-adjusted, under supervision).
dosing.renal: CKD stages 1-3: Standard dosing for deficiency. CKD stages 4-5: May need active vitamin D (calcitriol) instead, as 1-alpha hydroxylation is impaired.
dosing.hepatic: Severe liver disease may impair 25-hydroxylation; may need calcifediol (25-OH-D3) directly.

cholecalciferol.pregnancy_lactation: Safe at recommended doses (up to 4000 IU/day). Excess doses can cause fetal hypercalcemia. Excreted in breast milk; supplementation recommended for breastfeeding mothers.
quick_flags: 60,000 IU weekly sachets/capsules are the most common treatment format in India (unique to Indian market)
Widespread deficiency in India despite abundant sunlight — screen liberally
Fat-soluble — take with a fatty meal for best absorption
Toxicity is rare at <10,000 IU/day but monitor calcium with high-dose regimens
Do NOT confuse cholecalciferol (D3) with ergocalciferol (D2) — D3 is more effective at raising 25-OH-D levels

chloroquine.mechanism: Concentrates in parasite food vacuole, inhibits heme polymerase, causing accumulation of toxic free heme that kills the Plasmodium parasite.
indications: P. ovale and P. malariae malaria

dosing.adult: Malaria treatment: 600 mg base Day 1, then 300 mg base at 6 h, 24 h, and 48 h (total 1500 mg base). Prophylaxis: 300 mg base once weekly starting 1 week before travel.
dosing.pediatric: Treatment: 10 mg base/kg Day 1, then 5 mg base/kg at 6 h, 24 h, and 48 h. Prophylaxis: 5 mg base/kg once weekly.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment for mild-moderate impairment; use with caution in severe renal failure.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment.

monitoring: Baseline and annual ophthalmologic exam if use >5 years
chloroquine.pregnancy_lactation: Generally considered safe in pregnancy for malaria treatment and prophylaxis. Compatible with breastfeeding. Avoid high cumulative doses.
quick_flags: NOT effective against P. falciparum in most of India due to widespread resistance
Always add primaquine for P. vivax radical cure
Cumulative retinal toxicity risk increases after 5 years of use

artemether_lumefantrine.mechanism: Artemether (artemisinin derivative) generates free radicals via endoperoxide bridge that damage parasite membranes; lumefantrine inhibits heme detoxification. Combination provides rapid parasite clearance and prevents recrudescence.

dosing.adult: 6-dose regimen: 4 tablets (80/480 mg) at 0, 8, 24, 36, 48, and 60 hours, taken with fatty food.
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based: 5-14 kg: 1 tab per dose; 15-24 kg: 2 tabs per dose; 25-34 kg: 3 tabs per dose; >35 kg: 4 tabs per dose. Same 6-dose schedule.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution in severe impairment.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution in severe impairment.

monitoring: Parasite count at 72 hours to confirm clearance
ECG if co-administered with QT-prolonging drugs
Repeat smear on Day 28 for cure assessment
artemether_lumefantrine.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in first trimester unless no alternative. Can be used in second and third trimesters. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First-line ACT for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in India (NVBDCP guidelines)
Must be taken with fatty food for adequate lumefantrine absorption
NOT for severe malaria — use IV artesunate

artesunate.mechanism: Converted to active metabolite dihydroartemisinin; endoperoxide bridge reacts with parasite heme iron generating free radicals that alkylate parasite proteins and damage membranes, achieving rapid parasite kill.
indications: Severe P. falciparum malaria (IV — drug of choice)
Uncomplicated P. falciparum (oral, as part of ACT)

dosing.adult: Severe malaria (IV): 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12, 24 hours, then every 24 h until oral ACT can be given. Oral: 4 mg/kg/day for 3 days as part of ACT.
dosing.pediatric: Same IV dosing as adults (2.4 mg/kg). Children <20 kg may need higher dose (3 mg/kg) per WHO.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: Haemoglobin on days 7, 14, and 28 post-treatment (PADH risk)
artesunate.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid first trimester; use in 2nd/3rd trimester when benefit outweighs risk. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for severe malaria — superior to quinine (AQUAMAT/SEAQUAMAT trials)
Watch for post-artesunate delayed haemolysis especially in non-immune travellers
Always follow IV artesunate with a full course of oral ACT

primaquine.mechanism: Generates reactive oxygen species that destroy hepatic schizonts (hypnozoites) and gametocytes of Plasmodium species. Only drug class that achieves radical cure of P. vivax/P. ovale.
indications: Radical cure of P. vivax and P. ovale (anti-hypnozoite)
Terminal prophylaxis after travel to endemic areas
contraindications: Breastfeeding infants with unknown G6PD status

dosing.adult: Radical cure (P. vivax): 0.25 mg/kg/day for 14 days. Gametocytocidal (P. falciparum): single dose 0.75 mg/kg. G6PD-intermediate: 0.75 mg/kg once weekly for 8 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Same weight-based dosing as adults. Must confirm G6PD status first.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment.

primaquine.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (risk of haemolysis in G6PD-deficient fetus). Avoid in breastfeeding unless infant G6PD status is known to be normal.
quick_flags: ALWAYS check G6PD status before prescribing — life-threatening haemolysis in deficiency
Only drug that kills P. vivax hypnozoites (prevents relapse)
Single low-dose used for P. falciparum gametocyte clearance per NVBDCP

mefloquine.mechanism: Accumulates in parasite food vacuole and forms toxic complexes with free heme, inhibiting haemozoin crystallization. Also disrupts parasite membrane transport.
indications: Chemoprophylaxis for chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum areas
contraindications: History of psychiatric disorders (depression, anxiety, psychosis)

dosing.adult: Prophylaxis: 250 mg once weekly starting 2 weeks before travel, during stay, and 4 weeks after. Treatment: 15-25 mg/kg split into 2-3 doses 6-8 hours apart.
dosing.pediatric: Prophylaxis: 5 mg/kg once weekly. Treatment: same as adults (mg/kg).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; drug is hepatically metabolized.

adverse_effects: Neuropsychiatric effects (vivid dreams, anxiety, depression, psychosis)
monitoring: Psychiatric symptom screening before and during use
mefloquine.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in first trimester. May be used in 2nd/3rd trimester if benefit outweighs risk. Small amounts excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Black box-equivalent warning for neuropsychiatric effects — screen carefully
Long half-life (~21 days) allows weekly dosing for prophylaxis
Not recommended if history of depression or anxiety

isoniazid.mechanism: Prodrug activated by mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase (KatG); active form inhibits InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase), blocking mycolic acid synthesis essential for mycobacterial cell wall.
indications: TB preventive therapy in HIV patients
contraindications: Active hepatic disease or INH-induced hepatitis history

dosing.adult: Active TB: 5 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) for intensive and continuation phase. Latent TB: 300 mg daily for 6-9 months or 900 mg twice weekly under DOTS.
dosing.pediatric: 10-15 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg daily).
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate. In severe CKD/dialysis: give post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active hepatitis. Monitor closely if pre-existing liver disease; discontinue if ALT >5x ULN.

monitoring: LFTs monthly for first 3 months then periodically
isoniazid.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy — benefits outweigh risk for active/latent TB. Always co-prescribe pyridoxine. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: ALWAYS co-prescribe pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) 10-25 mg/day to prevent neuropathy
Potent CYP inhibitor — watch for interactions with phenytoin, carbamazepine
Fast vs slow acetylators affect toxicity risk (slow acetylators more toxicity)

rifampicin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (beta subunit), blocking mRNA synthesis. Bactericidal against actively growing and semi-dormant bacilli.

dosing.adult: TB: 10 mg/kg/day (max 600 mg) given on empty stomach. Leprosy: 600 mg once monthly. Meningococcal prophylaxis: 600 mg q12h for 2 days.
dosing.pediatric: 10-20 mg/kg/day (max 600 mg daily).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid or reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment. Monitor LFTs closely.

monitoring: Warn patient about orange discoloration of urine, tears, sweat
rifampicin.pregnancy_lactation: Use in pregnancy if essential for TB treatment. Risk of neonatal haemorrhage — give Vitamin K to neonate. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: MOST potent CYP3A4 inducer in clinical use — reduces levels of OCP, warfarin, antiretrovirals, azoles, and many others
Orange-red body fluids are harmless — counsel patient to avoid alarm
Give on empty stomach for optimal absorption

pyrazinamide.mechanism: Prodrug converted to pyrazinoic acid by mycobacterial pyrazinamidase; disrupts membrane energetics and fatty acid synthase I (FAS-I) in acidic pH environment of phagolysosomes, killing semi-dormant bacilli.
indications: Active tuberculosis — intensive phase (first 2 months) of standard regimen

dosing.adult: 25-35 mg/kg/day (typically 1500 mg for 50-70 kg). Used only in intensive phase (first 2 months).
dosing.pediatric: 30-40 mg/kg/day (max 2000 mg).
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: 25-35 mg/kg three times per week. Give post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment. Monitor LFTs closely.

monitoring: LFTs at baseline and monthly during intensive phase
pyrazinamide.pregnancy_lactation: WHO recommends use in standard TB regimen during pregnancy. Benefits outweigh risks. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Only used in first 2 months (intensive phase) — key sterilising agent
Expect uric acid rise — often asymptomatic, treat only if gout symptoms occur
Hepatotoxicity risk is additive with isoniazid and rifampicin

ethambutol.mechanism: Inhibits arabinosyl transferase (EmbB), blocking arabinogalactan synthesis and mycobacterial cell wall assembly. Bacteriostatic.
indications: Active tuberculosis (part of RHZE regimen in intensive phase)
contraindications: Inability to report visual changes (young children, cognitively impaired)

dosing.adult: 15-20 mg/kg/day (typically 800-1200 mg). Used in intensive phase; may continue if drug resistance suspected.
dosing.pediatric: 20 mg/kg/day (max 1000 mg). Avoid in children too young for visual acuity testing unless essential.
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-50: 15-25 mg/kg every 24-36 h. CrCl <10: 15-25 mg/kg every 48 h. Give post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: Baseline visual acuity and colour vision (Ishihara chart)
Monthly visual acuity checks during treatment
ethambutol.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy for TB treatment. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Perform baseline eye exam — optic neuritis is the signature toxicity
Discontinue immediately if visual changes occur
Primary role is to prevent resistance emergence while awaiting DST results

forms: Tablet: Rifampicin 150 mg + Isoniazid 75 mg + Pyrazinamide 400 mg + Ethambutol 275 mg
rhze_fdc.mechanism: Combination of four first-line antitubercular agents with complementary mechanisms: cell wall synthesis inhibition (INH, EMB), RNA polymerase inhibition (RIF), and disruption of membrane energetics (PZA).
indications: Intensive phase treatment of drug-susceptible pulmonary and extrapulmonary tuberculosis

dosing.adult: Weight-based under NTEP: 30-39 kg: 2 tabs; 40-54 kg: 3 tabs; 55-69 kg: 4 tabs; ≥70 kg: 5 tabs. Daily for 2 months (56 doses).
dosing.pediatric: Use pediatric FDC formulations per NTEP weight bands.
dosing.renal: Adjust ethambutol and pyrazinamide components per CrCl. Consider individual drugs for severe CKD.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment. Monitor LFTs closely — three hepatotoxic drugs in combination.

adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity (combined risk from R, H, Z)
monitoring: LFTs at baseline, 2 weeks, then monthly
rhze_fdc.pregnancy_lactation: Standard TB treatment in pregnancy as per WHO and NTEP. Co-prescribe pyridoxine. Streptomycin is the only first-line agent contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: FDC improves adherence and reduces monotherapy risk — preferred by NTEP/RNTCP
Always co-prescribe pyridoxine 10 mg daily
If hepatotoxicity occurs, stop all drugs and re-introduce one at a time (R first, then H, then Z)

forms: 250 mg/500 mg vial for IV infusion
acyclovir.mechanism: Selectively phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase to acyclovir monophosphate, then by host kinases to active triphosphate form that inhibits viral DNA polymerase and terminates DNA chain elongation.

dosing.adult: HSV genital: 200 mg 5 times/day or 400 mg TID for 7-10 days. Herpes zoster: 800 mg 5 times/day for 7 days. HSV encephalitis: 10 mg/kg IV q8h for 14-21 days. VZV (immunocompromised): 10 mg/kg IV q8h.
dosing.pediatric: HSV neonatal: 20 mg/kg IV q8h for 14-21 days. Chickenpox: 20 mg/kg (max 800 mg) QID for 5 days.
dosing.renal: CrCl 25-50: full dose q12h. CrCl 10-25: full dose q24h. CrCl <10: 50% dose q24h. Supplement post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine) especially with IV use
acyclovir.pregnancy_lactation: Extensive safety data in pregnancy. Acyclovir registry shows no increased risk. Safe in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Ensure adequate hydration with IV dosing to prevent crystalline nephropathy
Start within 72 hours of zoster rash onset for best efficacy
Valacyclovir is the oral prodrug with better bioavailability for outpatient use

oseltamivir.mechanism: Inhibits influenza neuraminidase enzyme, preventing release of newly formed virions from infected cells and limiting viral spread.
indications: Treatment of influenza A and B (within 48 hours of symptom onset)
