contraindications: Severe renal impairment without dose adjustment

dosing.adult: Treatment: 75 mg BID for 5 days. Prophylaxis: 75 mg once daily for 10 days (or duration of exposure).
dosing.pediatric: Treatment: ≤15 kg: 30 mg BID; 15-23 kg: 45 mg BID; 23-40 kg: 60 mg BID; >40 kg: 75 mg BID. Duration 5 days.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: 30 mg BID (treatment). CrCl 10-30: 30 mg once daily. ESRD on dialysis: 30 mg after each session.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

adverse_effects: Neuropsychiatric events (rare, more in children — hallucinations, delirium)
oseltamivir.pregnancy_lactation: Can be used in pregnancy when benefit outweighs risk, especially during influenza pandemic. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Must start within 48 hours of symptom onset for therapeutic benefit
Stockpiled for pandemic preparedness
Neuropsychiatric events reported mainly in Japanese adolescents — monitor

remdesivir.drug_class: Nucleotide Analogue Antiviral (RNA-dependent RNA polymerase inhibitor)
forms: 100 mg lyophilized powder for IV infusion
remdesivir.mechanism: Adenosine nucleotide prodrug; active triphosphate metabolite (GS-443902) inhibits viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase causing delayed chain termination and stalling of viral RNA synthesis.
indications: COVID-19 in hospitalized patients requiring supplemental oxygen

dosing.adult: 200 mg IV on Day 1 (loading), then 100 mg IV daily for 4 days (total 5 days). Extend to 10 days if no clinical improvement.
dosing.pediatric: ≥40 kg: adult dosing. 3.5-40 kg: 5 mg/kg Day 1, then 2.5 mg/kg/day.
dosing.renal: Not recommended if eGFR <30 (cyclodextrin vehicle accumulation). No adjustment if eGFR ≥30.
dosing.hepatic: Discontinue if ALT ≥10x ULN. Discontinue if ALT elevation with signs of liver inflammation.

remdesivir.pregnancy_lactation: Limited data. Use only if potential benefit justifies potential risk. No data on breastfeeding.
quick_flags: IV-only drug — requires hospital or infusion centre setting
Avoid in eGFR <30 due to cyclodextrin accumulation
Most benefit when given early in moderate COVID-19 requiring oxygen

tenofovir.mechanism: Prodrug converted to tenofovir diphosphate intracellularly; competitively inhibits HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase, causing DNA chain termination.

dosing.adult: 300 mg once daily (with or without food). PrEP: 300 mg/day or event-driven dosing (2-1-1 regimen for MSM).
dosing.pediatric: ≥2 years and ≥17 kg: weight-based dosing per WHO bands. ≥35 kg: adult dose.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-49: 300 mg every 48 h. CrCl 10-29: 300 mg every 72-96 h. Haemodialysis: 300 mg every 7 days post-dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required. Caution: HBV flare risk on discontinuation.

monitoring: Serum creatinine and eGFR at baseline, 3 months, then every 6 months
tenofovir.pregnancy_lactation: Preferred NRTI backbone in pregnancy for HIV. Safe and recommended by WHO. Compatible with breastfeeding in settings where breastfeeding is recommended.
quick_flags: Screen for HBV before starting — severe flare can occur on withdrawal
Monitor renal function and phosphate — Fanconi syndrome is signature toxicity
TAF (tenofovir alafenamide) has better renal/bone safety but not available in all settings

sofosbuvir.mechanism: Nucleotide prodrug; active triphosphate (GS-461203) inhibits HCV NS5B RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, causing chain termination of viral RNA synthesis.
indications: Chronic hepatitis C (all genotypes, used in combination with other DAAs)
contraindications: Concurrent use with potent P-gp inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin)
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) without specialist guidance
Concurrent amiodarone (severe bradycardia risk with sofosbuvir-containing regimens)

dosing.adult: 400 mg once daily, always in combination with another DAA (daclatasvir, velpatasvir, or ledipasvir). Duration 12-24 weeks based on genotype and cirrhosis status.
dosing.pediatric: ≥6 years and ≥17 kg: weight-based dosing with approved combination. ≥35 kg: 400 mg daily.
dosing.renal: eGFR ≥30: no adjustment. eGFR <30: use under specialist guidance; limited data.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in Child-Pugh A/B. Avoid in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) without specialist input.

monitoring: HCV RNA at baseline, week 4, end of treatment, and 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12)
sofosbuvir.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy (insufficient data). If combined with ribavirin, pregnancy is absolutely contraindicated. Breastfeeding data limited.
quick_flags: India is a global leader in affordable generic sofosbuvir — transformed HCV treatment access
NEVER co-administer with amiodarone — fatal bradycardia reported
Screen for HBV co-infection — risk of HBV reactivation during DAA therapy

griseofulvin.mechanism: Binds to tubulin in fungal cells, disrupting mitotic spindle formation and arresting fungal cell division. Deposits in keratin precursor cells, making them resistant to fungal invasion.
indications: Tinea capitis (drug of choice in children)
Dermatophyte infections of skin, hair, and nails

dosing.adult: Microsize: 500-1000 mg/day in divided doses. Ultramicrosize: 375-750 mg/day. Duration: skin 4-6 weeks, scalp 6-8 weeks, nails 6-12 months.
dosing.pediatric: Microsize: 10-20 mg/kg/day. Ultramicrosize: 5-15 mg/kg/day. Tinea capitis: minimum 6-8 weeks.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic disease.

griseofulvin.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic). Men should avoid fathering children for 6 months after treatment. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with fatty food for optimal absorption
Drug of choice for tinea capitis in children
Being replaced by terbinafine and itraconazole for many indications but still relevant for paediatric tinea capitis

clotrimazole.mechanism: Inhibits ergosterol synthesis by blocking fungal CYP51 (14-alpha-demethylase), altering cell membrane permeability and causing leakage of cellular contents.

dosing.adult: Topical cream: Apply BID for 2-4 weeks. Vaginal tablet: 500 mg single dose or 100 mg daily for 6 days. Mouth paint: Apply 3-4 times daily for 7-14 days.
dosing.pediatric: Topical: same as adult. Oral troche: children >3 years: dissolve 1 troche 5 times daily for 14 days.
dosing.renal: Not applicable (minimal systemic absorption).
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable (minimal systemic absorption).

clotrimazole.pregnancy_lactation: Topical use generally safe in pregnancy. Vaginal use safe in 2nd/3rd trimester. Avoid oral troches in pregnancy.
quick_flags: OTC availability makes it first-line for superficial fungal infections
Candid mouth paint widely used for oral thrush in India
Single-dose 500 mg vaginal tablet convenient for vulvovaginal candidiasis

ivermectin.mechanism: Binds to glutamate-gated chloride channels in invertebrate nerve and muscle cells, increasing chloride permeability, causing hyperpolarization, paralysis, and death of parasites.

dosing.adult: Strongyloidiasis: 200 mcg/kg single dose, repeat in 2 weeks. Scabies: 200 mcg/kg on days 1 and 8. Filariasis: 200-400 mcg/kg single dose. Take on empty stomach with water.
dosing.pediatric: >15 kg: 200 mcg/kg. Not recommended <15 kg.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Mazzotti reaction (in onchocerciasis — fever, rash, hypotension)
monitoring: Clinical cure assessment for scabies at 4 weeks
Watch for Mazzotti reaction in onchocerciasis
ivermectin.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (limited safety data). Avoid in breastfeeding during first week of infant life.
quick_flags: For scabies: give TWO doses 1 week apart for reliable cure
Take on empty stomach — food increases absorption unpredictably
In immunosuppressed patients, check for Strongyloides hyperinfection before starting steroids

albendazole.mechanism: Binds to parasitic beta-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization, disrupting glucose uptake and depleting glycogen stores, leading to parasite immobilization and death.
contraindications: Retinal pathology (neurocysticercosis treatment — risk of retinal damage)

dosing.adult: Soil-transmitted helminths: 400 mg single dose. Neurocysticercosis: 15 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses for 8-30 days (with corticosteroids). Hydatid: 15 mg/kg/day (max 800 mg) in cycles.
dosing.pediatric: >2 years: same as adult for single-dose deworming. <2 years: 200 mg single dose.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Monitor LFTs closely. Discontinue if significant transaminase elevation.

monitoring: CBC and LFTs every 2 weeks during prolonged courses
Pregnancy test before starting in women of childbearing age
albendazole.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in first trimester (teratogenic). WHO deworming programs: safe in 2nd/3rd trimester. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Single 400 mg dose for deworming — India's National Deworming Day drug
For neurocysticercosis: ALWAYS co-administer corticosteroids to prevent inflammatory surge
Take with fatty food for improved absorption in systemic infections

mebendazole.mechanism: Inhibits beta-tubulin polymerization in helminths, disrupting microtubule-dependent glucose uptake and leading to glycogen depletion and parasite death.

dosing.adult: Pinworm: 100 mg single dose, repeat in 2 weeks. Mixed helminths: 100 mg BID for 3 days or 500 mg single dose.
dosing.pediatric: >2 years: same as adult dosing. <2 years: use with caution.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Monitor LFTs in prolonged courses.

monitoring: Stool examination 2-4 weeks post-treatment to confirm cure
mebendazole.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in first trimester. Can be used in 2nd/3rd trimester if benefit outweighs risk. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: For pinworm: treat entire household simultaneously and repeat in 2 weeks
Chewable tablet can be taken with or without food
Less effective for systemic helminth infections compared to albendazole

nitazoxanide.mechanism: Active metabolite tizoxanide inhibits pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR) enzyme-dependent electron transfer reaction essential for anaerobic energy metabolism in protozoa and anaerobic bacteria.

dosing.adult: 500 mg BID with food for 3 days (giardiasis/cryptosporidiosis). Amoebiasis: 500 mg BID for 3-10 days.
dosing.pediatric: 1-3 years: 100 mg BID for 3 days. 4-11 years: 200 mg BID for 3 days. ≥12 years: adult dose.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; caution in severe impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Highly protein-bound. Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.

nitazoxanide.pregnancy_lactation: Limited data. Use only if clearly needed. Breastfeeding safety unknown.
quick_flags: Broad spectrum — covers protozoa, bacteria, and some viruses
Take with food for optimal absorption
Urine may turn yellow-green — counsel patient

alprazolam.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity by binding to benzodiazepine site, increasing chloride channel opening frequency, leading to neuronal hyperpolarization and CNS depression.
contraindications: Concurrent use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole)

dosing.adult: Anxiety: 0.25-0.5 mg TID, titrate to max 4 mg/day. Panic disorder: 0.5 mg TID, titrate to 1-2 mg TID. Elderly: start 0.25 mg BID-TID.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years.
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use lower doses.
dosing.hepatic: Start 0.25 mg BID-TID. Avoid in severe hepatic failure.

monitoring: Monitor for respiratory depression if combined with opioids
alprazolam.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (risk of neonatal withdrawal and floppy infant syndrome). Category D. Excreted in breast milk — avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Schedule H1 drug in India — strict prescription control
Short half-life makes interdose rebound anxiety and withdrawal common
NEVER stop abruptly — taper by 0.25-0.5 mg every 3 days to prevent seizures

lorazepam.mechanism: Potentiates GABA-A receptor activity by increasing chloride channel opening frequency, producing anxiolytic, sedative, anticonvulsant, and muscle relaxant effects.

dosing.adult: Anxiety: 1-2 mg BID-TID (max 10 mg/day). Status epilepticus: 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) at 2 mg/min, may repeat once. Catatonia: 1-2 mg IV/IM challenge.
dosing.pediatric: Status epilepticus: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg). Anxiolysis: not routinely recommended.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment. Not removed by dialysis.
dosing.hepatic: Preferred benzodiazepine in liver disease — undergoes direct glucuronidation without CYP metabolism (LOT rule: Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam).

monitoring: Respiratory rate and SpO2 (especially IV use)
Duration of therapy (limit to 2-4 weeks for anxiety)
lorazepam.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy especially first trimester (Category D). Neonatal withdrawal syndrome reported. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: LOT benzodiazepine — safest in liver disease (direct glucuronidation, no active metabolites)
First-line IV benzodiazepine for status epilepticus in most protocols
Lorazepam challenge is diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia

zolpidem.mechanism: Selectively binds to alpha-1 subunit of GABA-A receptor (BZ1 receptor) in the brain, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission with more selective hypnotic effect and less anxiolytic/muscle relaxant activity compared to benzodiazepines.
contraindications: History of complex sleep behaviours on zolpidem

dosing.adult: Immediate-release: 5-10 mg at bedtime (start 5 mg in women and elderly). Extended-release: 6.25-12.5 mg. Max duration: 2-4 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children/adolescents.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: 5 mg IR or 6.25 mg ER. Avoid in severe hepatic impairment.

monitoring: Evaluate for complex sleep behaviours at each visit
Screen for depression and suicidal ideation
zolpidem.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (limited data, risk of neonatal sedation). Excreted in breast milk — avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: FDA boxed warning for complex sleep behaviours (sleep-driving, cooking, calling) — discontinue if occurs
Women metabolize zolpidem slower — start at 5 mg
Avoid alcohol and other CNS depressants

modafinil.mechanism: Exact mechanism unclear; inhibits dopamine reuptake transporter (DAT), increases hypothalamic histamine and orexin/hypocretin release, and modulates GABA/glutamate balance to promote wakefulness.
indications: Obstructive sleep apnoea (residual daytime sleepiness despite CPAP)

dosing.adult: Narcolepsy/OSA: 200 mg once daily in the morning. Shift-work disorder: 200 mg 1 hour before shift. Max: 400 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children (risk of serious skin reactions).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Reduce to 100 mg/day in severe hepatic impairment.

monitoring: Skin rash (discontinue immediately if suspected SJS)
modafinil.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (congenital malformations reported in registry). Use additional contraception as modafinil reduces OCP efficacy. Breastfeeding not recommended.
quick_flags: Schedule IV substance — lower abuse potential than amphetamines but still possible
Reduces efficacy of hormonal contraceptives — advise alternative methods
Discontinue immediately if rash develops — risk of SJS/TEN

atomoxetine.drug_class: Selective Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor (SNRI for ADHD)
atomoxetine.mechanism: Selectively inhibits presynaptic norepinephrine transporter (NET) in the prefrontal cortex, increasing norepinephrine and dopamine availability in prefrontal regions without significant striatal dopamine effects.
indications: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children, adolescents, and adults
contraindications: Concurrent or recent (14 days) MAOI use

dosing.adult: Start 40 mg/day, increase after 3 days to 80 mg/day (single or divided dose). Max: 100 mg/day.
dosing.pediatric: ≥6 years, <70 kg: start 0.5 mg/kg/day, increase to 1.2 mg/kg/day after 3 days. Max: 1.4 mg/kg/day or 100 mg.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment (Child-Pugh B): reduce to 50% of normal dose. Severe (Child-Pugh C): reduce to 25%.

adverse_effects: Increased heart rate and blood pressure
Suicidal ideation (black box warning in children)
monitoring: Height, weight, and growth in children
LFTs if symptoms of hepatic dysfunction
Symptom response (takes 4-6 weeks for full effect)
atomoxetine.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (insufficient human data). Excreted in breast milk in animal studies — caution advised.
quick_flags: Non-stimulant ADHD drug — no abuse potential (non-scheduled)
Takes 4-6 weeks for full therapeutic effect (unlike immediate stimulant response)
Black box warning for suicidal ideation in children and adolescents — counsel families

granisetron.mechanism: Selective 5-HT3 receptor antagonist that blocks serotonin binding in the CTZ and vagal afferent neurons, suppressing the vomiting reflex triggered by chemotherapy and radiation.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to granisetron or other 5-HT3 antagonists

dosing.adult: CINV: 1 mg IV before chemo or 2 mg PO once daily. Transdermal patch: apply 24-48 h before chemo, wear for up to 7 days. PONV: 1 mg IV.
dosing.pediatric: ≥2 years: 10-40 mcg/kg IV (max 1 mg) before chemotherapy.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

granisetron.pregnancy_lactation: Limited human data. Use only if clearly needed. Caution in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Transdermal patch provides extended coverage for multi-day chemo regimens
Longer half-life than ondansetron — once-daily dosing sufficient
Similar efficacy to ondansetron but may work when ondansetron fails (and vice versa)

aprepitant.mechanism: Selectively blocks substance P/neurokinin-1 (NK1) receptors in the CNS vomiting centre, preventing delayed-phase chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.
indications: Highly emetogenic chemotherapy (HEC) — part of triple antiemetic regimen
Prevention of postoperative nausea and vomiting

dosing.adult: HEC: 125 mg PO Day 1, then 80 mg PO Days 2-3 (with dexamethasone and 5-HT3 antagonist). Fosaprepitant: 150 mg IV Day 1 only. PONV: 40 mg PO within 3 hours of induction.
dosing.pediatric: ≥6 months: weight-based dosing available as oral suspension. ≥12 years: adult dosing.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate (Child-Pugh A/B): no adjustment. Severe (Child-Pugh C): limited data, use with caution.

monitoring: Drug interactions (moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor, CYP2C9 inducer)
Adjust dexamethasone dose (reduce by 50% when co-administered)
aprepitant.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (insufficient data). May reduce efficacy of hormonal contraceptives. Breastfeeding data lacking.
quick_flags: Triple antiemetic regimen for HEC: NK1 antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone
Reduce dexamethasone dose by 50% when co-prescribing (CYP3A4 inhibition)
May reduce hormonal contraceptive efficacy — use backup method

forms: 6 mg/0.6 mL prefilled syringe for subcutaneous injection
pegfilgrastim.mechanism: PEGylated recombinant G-CSF (filgrastim); binds to G-CSF receptor on myeloid progenitors, stimulating neutrophil proliferation, differentiation, and release from bone marrow. PEG moiety extends half-life for single-dose convenience.
indications: Reduction of neutropenia duration in myelosuppressive chemotherapy
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to pegfilgrastim, filgrastim, or E. coli-derived proteins
Do not administer between 14 days before and 24 hours after cytotoxic chemotherapy

dosing.adult: 6 mg SC once per chemotherapy cycle, given at least 24 hours after chemotherapy completion.
dosing.pediatric: >45 kg: 6 mg SC. <45 kg: 100 mcg/kg SC (max 6 mg) once per cycle.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: CBC with differential before and during treatment
Spleen size if left upper quadrant pain
pegfilgrastim.pregnancy_lactation: Limited human data. Use only if potential benefit justifies risk. Breastfeeding safety unknown.
quick_flags: Single dose per chemo cycle — major advantage over daily filgrastim
Do NOT give within 14 days before or 24 hours after chemo
Bone pain is very common — pre-medicate with loratadine or naproxen

donepezil.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits centrally-active acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine availability in synaptic clefts of the cerebral cortex, enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission in Alzheimer disease.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to donepezil or piperidine derivatives

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg once daily at bedtime. Increase to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks. 23 mg available for moderate-severe AD after stable 10 mg dose for ≥3 months.
dosing.pediatric: Not indicated in children.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment. Severe: not studied, use with caution.

monitoring: Cognitive assessment (MMSE/MoCA) at baseline and every 6 months
donepezil.pregnancy_lactation: Not applicable (patient population is elderly). No data available.
quick_flags: Give at bedtime to minimise daytime GI side effects
Symptomatic benefit only — does not modify disease progression
Watch for bradycardia, especially if on beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers

memantine.mechanism: Uncompetitive, low-affinity antagonist of NMDA glutamate receptors; blocks pathological excessive glutamate stimulation while preserving physiological signaling, reducing excitotoxic neuronal damage in Alzheimer disease.
indications: Often used in combination with donepezil
contraindications: Severe renal impairment without dose reduction

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg once daily; increase by 5 mg/week to target 10 mg BID (20 mg/day). Extended-release: start 7 mg, titrate to 28 mg once daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not indicated in children.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-49: no initial adjustment but monitor. CrCl 5-29: max 5 mg BID. Avoid in severe impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment. Severe: use with caution (limited data).

memantine.pregnancy_lactation: Not applicable (patient population is elderly). No data available.
quick_flags: Can be combined with donepezil (different mechanisms) for additive benefit in moderate-severe AD
Slow titration essential to minimise side effects
Alkaline urine reduces renal clearance — avoid concurrent sodium bicarbonate, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors

ropinirole.mechanism: Selective agonist at dopamine D2 and D3 receptors in the striatum, compensating for dopaminergic deficit in Parkinson disease. Also acts on spinal D3 receptors for restless legs syndrome.
indications: Parkinson disease (monotherapy or adjunct to levodopa)

dosing.adult: Parkinson: start 0.25 mg TID, titrate weekly by 0.25 mg TID increments. Usual: 3-9 mg/day. Max: 24 mg/day. RLS: start 0.25 mg at bedtime, titrate to max 4 mg.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children.
dosing.renal: No adjustment for CrCl >30. Not studied in severe renal impairment.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Titrate slowly. Avoid in severe impairment.

adverse_effects: Impulse control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality, binge eating)
monitoring: Daytime somnolence and sleep attacks (advise driving caution)
Impulse control behaviours at each visit
ropinirole.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (inhibits prolactin, may impair lactation). Not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: WARN about sudden sleep attacks — patient must not drive if affected
Screen for impulse control disorders (gambling, shopping, hypersexuality) at EVERY visit
Non-ergot dopamine agonist — no fibrosis risk unlike ergot-derived agents

pramipexole.mechanism: Selective non-ergot dopamine agonist with high affinity for D3 receptors and moderate affinity for D2 receptors. Stimulates dopaminergic transmission in the nigrostriatal pathway.
indications: Parkinson disease (early monotherapy or adjunct to levodopa)

dosing.adult: Parkinson IR: start 0.125 mg TID, titrate every 5-7 days. Usual effective: 0.5-1.5 mg TID. Max: 4.5 mg/day. RLS: 0.125 mg 2-3 h before bedtime, max 0.5 mg/day. ER: start 0.375 mg daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children.
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: start 0.125 mg BID, max 1.5 mg/day. CrCl 15-29: start 0.125 mg daily, max 1.5 mg/day.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required (primarily renally eliminated).

pramipexole.pregnancy_lactation: Avoid in pregnancy (inhibits prolactin secretion). Not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Primarily renally eliminated — dose adjustment critical in renal impairment
Same impulse control disorder warnings as all dopamine agonists
RLS augmentation (worsening of symptoms) can occur with long-term use — may need to switch agents

baclofen.mechanism: Agonist at presynaptic GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord and brain, inhibiting release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, aspartate) and reducing monosynaptic/polysynaptic spinal reflexes, decreasing spasticity.
indications: Spasticity (multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy)
Intrathecal: severe spasticity refractory to oral therapy

dosing.adult: Start 5 mg TID, increase by 5 mg per dose every 3 days. Usual: 40-80 mg/day in divided doses. Max: 80 mg/day oral. Intrathecal: test dose 50 mcg, then individualized infusion.
dosing.pediatric: Start 2.5 mg TID (2-7 years) or 5 mg TID (>8 years). Titrate cautiously. Max: 40-60 mg/day.
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment (primarily renally excreted). CrCl <15: start 5 mg daily.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; reduce dose in hepatic impairment.

adverse_effects: Withdrawal syndrome (seizures, hallucinations if abruptly stopped)
monitoring: Signs of withdrawal on dose changes
baclofen.pregnancy_lactation: Limited data. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts.
quick_flags: NEVER stop abruptly — baclofen withdrawal can cause seizures, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, and death
Intrathecal baclofen pump malfunction is a medical emergency
Renally excreted — dose reduction essential in CKD

leflunomide.drug_class: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drug (DMARD) — Pyrimidine Synthesis Inhibitor
leflunomide.mechanism: Active metabolite teriflunomide inhibits dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (DHODH), a key enzyme in de novo pyrimidine synthesis. Rapidly proliferating T and B lymphocytes (which depend on de novo synthesis) are selectively inhibited.

dosing.adult: Loading dose (optional): 100 mg daily for 3 days. Maintenance: 20 mg once daily. If intolerant: 10 mg daily.
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children (limited data).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment. Not dialyzable. Use cholestyramine washout if needed.
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in significant hepatic impairment. Monitor ALT closely.

monitoring: ALT at baseline, monthly for first 6 months, then every 6-8 weeks
Pregnancy test before starting in women of childbearing age
leflunomide.pregnancy_lactation: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic — FDA Category X). Women must use reliable contraception. If pregnancy desired, cholestyramine washout required (8 g TID for 11 days) until plasma teriflunomide <0.02 mg/L on two tests 14 days apart.
quick_flags: Ultra-long half-life (14-18 days for active metabolite) — requires cholestyramine washout for rapid elimination
Teratogenic — pregnancy test mandatory before starting and reliable contraception during treatment
Can be used as alternative to methotrexate in RA or as add-on

sulfasalazine.mechanism: Cleaved by colonic bacteria to 5-aminosalicylic acid (anti-inflammatory in gut) and sulfapyridine (immunomodulatory). In RA, suppresses T-cell proliferation, inhibits NF-kB, and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines.

dosing.adult: RA: start 500 mg daily, increase by 500 mg/week to 2-3 g/day in divided doses. UC: 1-2 g BID-QID (maintenance 2 g/day).
dosing.pediatric: UC: 40-60 mg/kg/day in 3-6 divided doses. JIA: 30-50 mg/kg/day (max 2 g/day).
dosing.renal: Avoid in severe renal impairment. Ensure adequate hydration.
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution. Monitor LFTs.

monitoring: CBC every 2-4 weeks for first 3 months, then every 3 months
sulfasalazine.pregnancy_lactation: Relatively safe in pregnancy (use folic acid supplementation). Compatible with breastfeeding (small amounts in milk). Sulfapyridine may cause kernicterus — use with caution in jaundiced neonates.
quick_flags: Enteric-coated formulation reduces GI side effects — preferred for RA
Reversible oligospermia — counsel male patients about fertility
Orange urine and tears are harmless — counsel patient

tofacitinib.mechanism: Preferentially inhibits JAK1 and JAK3 (with lesser activity on JAK2), blocking intracellular signaling from multiple cytokine receptors (IL-2, IL-4, IL-6, IL-7, IL-15, IL-21, IFN), suppressing lymphocyte activation and inflammatory cascades.
indications: Rheumatoid arthritis (after inadequate response to methotrexate)

dosing.adult: RA/PsA/AS: 5 mg BID or 11 mg ER once daily. UC: induction 10 mg BID for 8-16 weeks, then 5-10 mg BID maintenance.
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely recommended in children (limited data).
dosing.renal: Moderate-severe (CrCl <60): 5 mg once daily.
dosing.hepatic: Moderate (Child-Pugh B): 5 mg once daily. Severe: avoid.

adverse_effects: Malignancy risk (lymphoma, lung cancer — FDA boxed warning)
monitoring: Lipid profile at baseline and 4-8 weeks
CBC at baseline, 4-8 weeks, then every 3 months
tofacitinib.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic in animals). Use effective contraception during and 4-6 weeks after. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: FDA boxed warnings: increased risk of serious infections, malignancy, MACE, and VTE
Screen for latent TB, hepatitis B/C, and malignancy before starting
Reserve for patients who have failed or are intolerant to TNF inhibitors (per FDA post-marketing safety data)

forms: 5% solution for IV infusion (various volumes: 50 mL, 100 mL, 200 mL)
human_normal_immunoglobulin_ivig.mechanism: Provides broad-spectrum IgG antibodies from pooled human plasma. Modulates immune function through Fc receptor blockade (reducing platelet/RBC destruction), anti-idiotypic antibody neutralization, complement modulation, and cytokine regulation.
contraindications: IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies (anaphylaxis risk)

dosing.adult: ITP: 1 g/kg/day for 2 days or 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days. Kawasaki: 2 g/kg single infusion. GBS/CIDP: 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days. Immunodeficiency: 0.4-0.6 g/kg every 3-4 weeks.
dosing.pediatric: Same weight-based dosing as adults. Kawasaki: 2 g/kg single infusion with aspirin.
dosing.renal: Infuse slowly. Avoid sucrose-containing products (renal tubular necrosis risk). Ensure hydration.
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment.

monitoring: Vital signs during infusion (slow rate initially, escalate if tolerated)
Renal function before and after infusion
IgA levels if history of reactions
human_normal_immunoglobulin_ivig.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy when indicated (e.g., ITP, recurrent pregnancy loss). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Start infusion slowly and escalate — most reactions are rate-related
Check IgA level before first dose — anti-IgA antibodies cause anaphylaxis
Interferes with live vaccines — wait 3-11 months after IVIG before giving MMR/varicella

anti_d_immunoglobulin.mechanism: Anti-D IgG antibodies bind to Rh(D) antigen on fetal RBCs that have entered maternal circulation, facilitating their clearance by the reticuloendothelial system before maternal immune sensitization occurs, preventing haemolytic disease of the newborn.
indications: Prevention of Rh isoimmunization in Rh-negative mothers
Postpartum (within 72 hours of delivery of Rh-positive baby)
After miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, amniocentesis, CVS, abdominal trauma
contraindications: Already sensitized Rh-negative women (anti-D already present)

dosing.adult: Antenatal: 300 mcg IM at 28 weeks. Postpartum: 300 mcg IM within 72 hours. First-trimester event: 50-120 mcg IM. Large fetomaternal haemorrhage: additional doses based on Kleihauer-Betke test.
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

adverse_effects: Haemolysis (in ITP indication when given IV)
monitoring: Rh typing and antibody screen (indirect Coombs) at booking
Kleihauer-Betke test postpartum for large fetomaternal haemorrhage
Repeat antibody screen at 28 weeks before giving prophylaxis
anti_d_immunoglobulin.pregnancy_lactation: Specifically indicated in pregnancy for Rh prophylaxis. Safe in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Must be given within 72 hours of sensitizing event — efficacy drops rapidly after
Perform Kleihauer-Betke test after delivery to detect large fetomaternal haemorrhage requiring additional doses
300 mcg covers up to 30 mL of fetal whole blood or 15 mL of fetal RBCs

tetanus_immunoglobulin.mechanism: Provides pre-formed human anti-tetanus antibodies that neutralize circulating tetanospasmin toxin before it binds irreversibly to neural tissue, providing immediate passive immunity.
indications: Prophylaxis of tetanus in unimmunized or incompletely immunized patients with tetanus-prone wounds

dosing.adult: Prophylaxis: 250 IU IM (500 IU if wound >24 hours old, heavily contaminated, or in obese patients). Treatment: 3000-6000 IU IM (may give portion infiltrated around wound).
dosing.pediatric: Prophylaxis: 250 IU IM regardless of age/weight. Treatment: 500 IU/kg IM (some protocols use fixed 3000-6000 IU).
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

monitoring: Concurrent tetanus toxoid vaccination at different site
tetanus_immunoglobulin.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy. Indicated for tetanus-prone wounds in pregnant women with inadequate immunization. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Give TIG AND tetanus toxoid simultaneously at DIFFERENT sites in unimmunized patients
TIG provides immediate protection; toxoid provides long-term immunity — both are needed
Human TIG preferred over equine ATS (less hypersensitivity risk)

anti_rabies_immunoglobulin.mechanism: Provides pre-formed neutralizing antibodies against rabies virus, bridging the gap until active immunization (rabies vaccine) generates endogenous antibodies. Neutralizes virus at the wound site before it enters nerve tissue.
indications: Category III rabies exposure (transdermal bites, scratches with bleeding, mucous membrane contact with saliva)
All Category III exposures in previously unvaccinated individuals
contraindications: Previously completed rabies vaccination (no RIG needed)
Known hypersensitivity to equine serum (for ERIG — do skin test)
Administration >7 days after first rabies vaccine dose (antibodies already forming)

dosing.adult: HRIG: 20 IU/kg. ERIG: 40 IU/kg. Infiltrate as much as anatomically possible around and into the wound; inject remainder IM at a site distant from vaccine.
dosing.pediatric: Same weight-based dosing as adults.
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required.
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment required.

adverse_effects: Serum sickness (ERIG, 1-3% — fever, rash, arthralgia 7-14 days later)
Rare: anaphylaxis (ERIG — always do skin test)
monitoring: Observe for 30 minutes post-injection for anaphylaxis
Monitor for serum sickness (7-14 days post-ERIG)
Ensure rabies vaccine schedule is followed concurrently
anti_rabies_immunoglobulin.pregnancy_lactation: Rabies is almost universally fatal — administer RIG in pregnancy without hesitation. Safe in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Give RIG ONLY on Day 0 (with first vaccine dose) — never after Day 7
Infiltrate maximum dose into and around the wound — this is the most important step
HRIG preferred over ERIG (no serum sickness risk) but ERIG is more available in India

forms: Lyophilized powder for reconstitution (10 mL vial, each mL neutralizes defined units of Big Four venoms)
anti_snake_venom.mechanism: Contains equine-derived F(ab')2 immunoglobulin fragments that bind and neutralize venom toxins from India's Big Four snakes: Indian cobra (Naja naja), common krait (Bungarus caeruleus), Russell's viper (Daboia russelii), and saw-scaled viper (Echis carinatus).
indications: Envenomation by Indian cobra, krait, Russell's viper, or saw-scaled viper
Systemic envenomation (coagulopathy, neurotoxicity, renal failure, shock)
Significant local envenomation with progressive swelling
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in life-threatening envenomation
Known equine serum allergy (relative — pre-treat with adrenaline and antihistamines)

dosing.adult: Initial dose: 8-10 vials (80-100 mL) reconstituted in 200-500 mL NS, infused IV over 1 hour (start slow, increase if tolerated). Repeat same dose after 6 hours if coagulopathy persists (20-minute WBCT still abnormal). Same dose for children (venom amount is independent of body size).
dosing.pediatric: SAME dose as adults — snake injects same amount of venom regardless of victim's size.
dosing.renal: No adjustment. Monitor for venom-induced AKI.
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment.

monitoring: 20-minute whole blood clotting test (WBCT20) — gold standard for coagulopathy monitoring
Repeat WBCT20 at 6 hours post-ASV; if still non-clotting, repeat ASV
Vital signs every 15 minutes during infusion
Neurological status (ptosis, respiratory effort in neurotoxic bites)
anti_snake_venom.pregnancy_lactation: Administer without hesitation in pregnancy — snakebite envenomation is life-threatening. Benefits far outweigh any risk.
quick_flags: SAME dose for children and adults — snake venom dose is body-size independent
Pre-treat with subcutaneous adrenaline (0.25 mg) to reduce anaphylaxis risk — per WHO/Indian guidelines
20-minute WBCT is the bedside test to guide ASV re-dosing — if blood does not clot in 20 min, coagulopathy persists

propofol.mechanism: Potentiates GABA-A receptor activity, producing rapid-onset sedation and general anesthesia by enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the CNS.
indications: Induction and maintenance of general anesthesia
Sedation in mechanically ventilated ICU patients
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to propofol, soy, or egg lecithin
Not recommended for sedation in pediatric ICU patients

dosing.adult: Induction: 1.5-2.5 mg/kg IV; Maintenance: 100-200 mcg/kg/min infusion; Sedation: 25-75 mcg/kg/min
dosing.pediatric: Induction: 2.5-3.5 mg/kg IV (children >3 years); not for ICU sedation in children
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use lower doses; prolonged effect possible in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Blood pressure and heart rate continuously
Signs of propofol infusion syndrome (metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis)
propofol.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Crosses placenta; used for cesarean section induction. Compatible with breastfeeding (short half-life).
quick_flags: Always have airway management equipment ready
Lipid emulsion — count toward total caloric/lipid intake in ICU
Propofol infusion syndrome risk increases with doses >5 mg/kg/h for >48 h

ketamine.mechanism: Non-competitive antagonist of NMDA glutamate receptors producing dissociative anesthesia with analgesia and amnesia while preserving airway reflexes and spontaneous respiration.
contraindications: Conditions where elevated BP is hazardous (e.g., pre-eclampsia, aneurysm)
Psychosis or history of severe psychiatric illness

dosing.adult: IV induction: 1-2 mg/kg; IM: 4-5 mg/kg; Procedural sedation: 0.5-1 mg/kg IV; Analgesic sub-dissociative: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV
dosing.pediatric: IV: 1-2 mg/kg; IM: 4-5 mg/kg; Oral: 5-6 mg/kg
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment; consider dose reduction

ketamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use in pregnancy only if clearly needed. Avoid in early pregnancy if possible. May be used for emergency procedures.
quick_flags: Co-administer midazolam or glycopyrrolate to reduce emergence reactions and secretions
Preserves airway reflexes — useful in emergency/field settings
Bronchodilator properties — consider in status asthmaticus

midazolam.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor-mediated chloride ion influx by binding the benzodiazepine site, producing anxiolysis, sedation, amnesia, and anticonvulsant effects.

dosing.adult: Sedation: 0.5-2.5 mg IV titrated slowly; Premedication: 0.07-0.08 mg/kg IM; Status epilepticus: 10 mg buccal/IM
dosing.pediatric: Sedation: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV; Buccal for seizures: 0.2-0.5 mg/kg (max 10 mg); Oral premedication: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in severe renal impairment; active metabolite may accumulate
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose significantly; clearance markedly reduced in cirrhosis

monitoring: Continuous SpO2 and respiratory rate during IV use
midazolam.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Risk of neonatal benzodiazepine syndrome if used near delivery. Excreted in breast milk — avoid during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Flumazenil is the specific reversal agent
Onset IV: 2-3 min; IM: 5-15 min
Buccal midazolam is first-line prehospital treatment for prolonged seizures in children

fentanyl.mechanism: Potent synthetic opioid that binds mu-opioid receptors in the CNS, inhibiting ascending pain pathways and altering perception and response to pain. 80-100 times more potent than morphine.
contraindications: Acute or severe bronchial asthma without monitoring

dosing.adult: IV analgesia: 25-100 mcg; IV anesthesia adjunct: 2-20 mcg/kg; Transdermal: start 25 mcg/h patch (opioid-tolerant only); Infusion: 0.5-2 mcg/kg/h
dosing.pediatric: IV: 1-2 mcg/kg per dose; Infusion: 0.5-2 mcg/kg/h
dosing.renal: Reduce dose; metabolites accumulate in renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; decreased clearance in hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Muscle rigidity (especially chest wall at high doses)
fentanyl.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Neonatal respiratory depression if used near delivery. Transdermal use in labor is contraindicated. Small amounts in breast milk.
quick_flags: Chest wall rigidity with rapid high-dose IV — treat with naloxone or neuromuscular blocker
Transdermal patches take 12-24 h for onset — not for acute pain
Naloxone is the reversal agent; may need repeat doses as fentanyl outlasts naloxone

lignocaine.drug_class: Amide Local Anesthetic / Class IB Antiarrhythmic
lignocaine.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, inhibiting nerve impulse initiation and conduction. As an antiarrhythmic, suppresses ventricular automaticity and shortens action potential duration.
indications: Local and regional anesthesia (infiltration, nerve block, epidural, spinal)

dosing.adult: Local infiltration: max 4.5 mg/kg without adrenaline, 7 mg/kg with adrenaline; Antiarrhythmic: 1-1.5 mg/kg IV bolus then 1-4 mg/min infusion; Topical: apply to area as needed
dosing.pediatric: Local: max 4.5 mg/kg without adrenaline; Antiarrhythmic: 1 mg/kg IV bolus
dosing.renal: No significant adjustment for local use; reduce infusion rate for IV antiarrhythmic
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; extensively hepatically metabolized — risk of toxicity in liver disease

adverse_effects: Cardiovascular toxicity (bradycardia, hypotension, cardiac arrest at high levels)
Allergic reactions (very rare with amide type)
monitoring: Signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
Serum lidocaine levels for prolonged infusion (therapeutic: 1.5-5 mcg/mL)
lignocaine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Widely used for labor analgesia (epidural). Excreted in breast milk in small amounts — compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: 20% Intralipid is the rescue for local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
Never use adrenaline-containing preparations for ring blocks (fingers, toes, penis, ears)
IV lidocaine infusion increasingly used for perioperative pain management

amiodarone.mechanism: Blocks potassium, sodium, and calcium channels plus has anti-adrenergic properties. Prolongs action potential duration and effective refractory period across all cardiac tissues.
indications: Supraventricular tachycardia refractory to other agents
contraindications: Severe sinus node disease or AV block (without pacemaker)

dosing.adult: VF/pulseless VT (ACLS): 300 mg IV push then 150 mg; Loading (stable VT/AF): 150 mg IV over 10 min then 1 mg/min x 6 h then 0.5 mg/min x 18 h; Oral loading: 800-1600 mg/day x 1-3 weeks then 200-400 mg/day maintenance
dosing.pediatric: VF/pulseless VT: 5 mg/kg IV/IO bolus (max 300 mg); Oral: 10-15 mg/kg/day loading
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required; not dialyzable
dosing.hepatic: Use with extreme caution; hepatotoxicity risk — monitor LFTs

monitoring: Thyroid function tests (baseline and every 6 months)
Pulmonary function tests and chest X-ray
Serum amiodarone and desethylamiodarone levels if toxicity suspected
amiodarone.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Causes neonatal thyroid dysfunction and goiter. Contraindicated in breastfeeding — concentrated in breast milk.
quick_flags: Extremely long half-life (40-55 days) — effects persist weeks after stopping
Contains iodine — interferes with thyroid function and imaging
Use central line for IV infusion when possible to avoid phlebitis
Interacts with warfarin (increase INR), digoxin, and many drugs via CYP inhibition

flecainide.mechanism: Potent sodium channel blocker that markedly slows conduction velocity in atrial, ventricular, and accessory pathway tissues. Minimal effect on repolarization.
indications: Ventricular arrhythmias (life-threatening, in absence of structural heart disease)
contraindications: Structural heart disease (ischemic heart disease, heart failure)
Bundle branch block with bifascicular block

dosing.adult: AF: 50-150 mg BD; Pill-in-the-pocket: 200-300 mg single dose; SVT: 50-100 mg BD; Max: 400 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 3-6 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses
dosing.renal: Reduce dose if CrCl <35 mL/min; monitor levels
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in significant hepatic impairment

flecainide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data; use only if benefit outweighs risk. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: CAST trial: increased mortality in post-MI patients — never use with structural heart disease
Always co-prescribe AV nodal blocker (beta-blocker) when using for AF to prevent 1:1 flutter conduction
Correct hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia before initiating

propafenone.drug_class: Class IC Antiarrhythmic (with mild beta-blocking activity)
propafenone.mechanism: Blocks sodium channels (Class IC effect), slowing conduction. Also possesses weak beta-adrenergic blocking and calcium channel blocking properties.

dosing.adult: 150-300 mg TDS; Pill-in-the-pocket: 450-600 mg single dose; Max: 900 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 10-20 mg/kg/day in 3-4 divided doses (limited data)
dosing.renal: No significant adjustment in mild-moderate impairment; caution in severe CKD
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 20-30% in hepatic impairment; monitor levels

propafenone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited human data. Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: CYP2D6 poor metabolizers have higher drug levels — dose accordingly
Avoid in structural heart disease (same CAST trial concerns as flecainide)
Has intrinsic beta-blocking effect — may not need additional AV nodal blocker for AF

sotalol.mechanism: Combines non-selective beta-adrenergic blockade (Class II) with potassium channel blockade (Class III), prolonging action potential duration and refractory period.
indications: Atrial fibrillation/flutter maintenance of sinus rhythm

dosing.adult: 80 mg BD initially; titrate every 3 days to 120-160 mg BD; Max: 320 mg/day (AF), 640 mg/day (VT)
dosing.pediatric: 30 mg/m2 TDS; titrate based on response and QTc
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: dose q12-24h; CrCl 10-30: dose q24-48h; CrCl <10: contraindicated
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; primarily renally excreted

adverse_effects: QT prolongation and torsades de pointes
monitoring: ECG with QTc measurement before each dose increase
sotalol.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Beta-blocker effects on fetus possible (bradycardia, hypoglycemia). Excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Initiate in hospital with continuous ECG monitoring for first 3 days
Proarrhythmic risk highest in first 3 days and with dose increases
Renal dosing is critical — most common cause of toxicity is accumulation in renal impairment
Correct electrolytes before starting

mexiletine.mechanism: Blocks sodium channels with rapid kinetics, shortening action potential duration. Similar mechanism to lidocaine but orally bioavailable.
indications: Adjunct to amiodarone for refractory VT
contraindications: Second- or third-degree AV block (without pacemaker)

dosing.adult: 200 mg TDS initially; may increase to 300 mg TDS; Max: 1200 mg/day; Take with food to reduce GI side effects
dosing.pediatric: 5-10 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 25-30% in hepatic impairment; reduced clearance

mexiletine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Excreted in breast milk — caution advised.
quick_flags: Oral equivalent of lidocaine — useful for long-term VT suppression
GI side effects minimized by taking with food or antacids
Often combined with amiodarone to allow lower doses of both drugs

phytonadione.drug_class: Fat-Soluble Vitamin / Coagulation Factor Synthesis Cofactor
phytonadione.mechanism: Essential cofactor for hepatic synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Reverses coagulopathy caused by vitamin K antagonists (warfarin).
indications: Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn (prophylaxis and treatment)
Vitamin K deficiency from malabsorption, liver disease, or prolonged antibiotics
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to phytonadione or any component

dosing.adult: Warfarin reversal (elevated INR, no bleeding): 1-2.5 mg oral; Major bleeding: 10 mg slow IV + FFP/PCC; Deficiency: 10 mg oral or IM
dosing.pediatric: Neonatal prophylaxis: 1 mg IM at birth; Hemorrhagic disease: 1-2 mg IV/IM; Older children: 1-5 mg oral/IV
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: May be less effective in severe liver disease (impaired factor synthesis regardless of vitamin K)

adverse_effects: Anaphylactoid reactions (especially with rapid IV injection)
monitoring: INR/PT before and 6-8 hours after administration
phytonadione.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. No known teratogenicity. Compatible with breastfeeding — recommended for neonatal prophylaxis.
quick_flags: IV route: dilute and infuse slowly (max 1 mg/min) to reduce anaphylaxis risk
Oral vitamin K takes 24-48 h for full effect; IV takes 6-8 h
For life-threatening warfarin bleeding: give vitamin K + 4-factor PCC (not vitamin K alone)

protamine.mechanism: Strongly basic protein that forms a stable complex with strongly acidic heparin, neutralizing its anticoagulant activity. Partially reverses LMWH (enoxaparin).
contraindications: Caution in fish allergy (protamine derived from salmon sperm)
Previous exposure to protamine-containing insulin (risk of antibodies)

dosing.adult: 1 mg protamine per 100 units of heparin given in preceding 2-3 hours; Max single dose: 50 mg; For LMWH: 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (if within 8 h; 0.5 mg if 8-12 h); Infuse slowly over 10 min
dosing.pediatric: Same ratio as adults: 1 mg per 100 units heparin
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Blood pressure and heart rate during infusion
Watch for heparin rebound at 4-6 hours
protamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use in pregnancy only if clearly indicated. No data on breastfeeding excretion.
quick_flags: Infuse no faster than 5 mg/min to avoid hypotension
Excess protamine itself has anticoagulant effect — do not overdose
Only partially reverses LMWH (60-80%) — anti-Xa activity not fully neutralized
Higher allergy risk in patients with fish allergy or prior protamine insulin exposure

forms: Lyophilized powder for reconstitution (various IU strengths: 250, 500, 1000, 1500 IU)
factor_viii_concentrate.mechanism: Replaces deficient Factor VIII in hemophilia A, restoring the intrinsic coagulation pathway and enabling normal clot formation at sites of vascular injury.
indications: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) — prophylaxis and treatment of bleeding
Acute bleeding episodes in hemophilia A
contraindications: Known inhibitors to Factor VIII (consider bypassing agents)

dosing.adult: Minor bleed: 10-20 IU/kg (target 30-50% factor level); Moderate bleed: 15-30 IU/kg (target 50-80%); Major bleed/surgery: 40-50 IU/kg (target 80-100%); Prophylaxis: 25-40 IU/kg 3x/week
dosing.pediatric: Same IU/kg dosing; may require higher doses per kg due to larger volume of distribution
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

adverse_effects: Development of inhibitors (antibodies against Factor VIII)
factor_viii_concentrate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Hemophilia A is X-linked (rare in females). Use when indicated for carriers with low factor levels. No concerns with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: 1 IU/kg raises Factor VIII level by ~2% in adults
Development of inhibitors is the most serious complication — check Bethesda titers
Reconstitute with provided diluent; do not shake — swirl gently
Infuse within 3 hours of reconstitution

potassium_chloride.mechanism: Replaces potassium, the major intracellular cation essential for nerve impulse conduction, muscle contraction, acid-base balance, and cardiac electrical activity.
indications: Potassium replacement in patients on loop/thiazide diuretics
Diabetic ketoacidosis (as part of fluid/electrolyte protocol)

dosing.adult: Mild hypokalemia (3-3.5 mEq/L): 40-80 mEq/day oral in divided doses; Moderate-severe: IV 10-20 mEq/h (max 40 mEq/h via central line with cardiac monitoring); Max IV concentration: 40 mEq/L peripheral, 80 mEq/L central
dosing.pediatric: 0.5-1 mEq/kg/dose oral; IV: 0.5-1 mEq/kg over 1 hour (max 0.5 mEq/kg/h)
dosing.renal: Reduce dose and monitor closely; avoid in severe CKD/ESRD unless actively hypokalemic
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; monitor in cirrhosis

adverse_effects: Cardiac arrhythmias (if rapid IV or hyperkalemia)
monitoring: Serum potassium (before, during, and after replacement)
ECG monitoring for IV replacement >10 mEq/h
Magnesium level (correct hypomagnesemia for effective K repletion)
potassium_chloride.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Considered safe in physiological doses. Normal dietary potassium in breast milk.
quick_flags: NEVER give undiluted IV KCl push — lethal cardiac arrest risk
Always correct hypomagnesemia concurrently — refractory hypokalemia if Mg is low
Peripheral IV max rate: 10 mEq/h; central line max: 20-40 mEq/h with continuous ECG
1 g KCl = 13.4 mEq potassium

calcium_carbonate.mechanism: Provides supplemental elemental calcium for bone mineralization, nerve conduction, and muscle contraction. As an antacid, neutralizes gastric acid. As a phosphate binder, binds dietary phosphate in the GI tract.
indications: Phosphate binding in chronic kidney disease

dosing.adult: Supplementation: 500-1000 mg elemental calcium/day in divided doses with meals; Phosphate binder: 500-1500 mg with each meal; Antacid: 500-1000 mg as needed
dosing.pediatric: Supplementation: 45-65 mg/kg/day elemental calcium in divided doses
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in CKD; monitor calcium-phosphate product; avoid if Ca x P >55
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

adverse_effects: Milk-alkali syndrome (rare, with high doses + alkali)
calcium_carbonate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (supplemental doses considered safe). Recommended daily calcium intake in pregnancy: 1000-1300 mg. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with meals for best absorption (requires acid for dissolution)
Space 2 hours from levothyroxine, fluoroquinolones, and tetracyclines
Calcium carbonate provides 40% elemental calcium — highest of any calcium salt
Co-administer vitamin D for optimal absorption

magnesium_oxide.mechanism: Provides supplemental magnesium essential for enzymatic reactions, neuromuscular function, and cardiac rhythm. At higher doses, acts as an osmotic laxative by drawing water into the intestinal lumen.

dosing.adult: Supplementation: 400-800 mg/day (240-480 mg elemental Mg) in divided doses; Laxative: 2-4 g as single dose
dosing.pediatric: 6-10 mg/kg/day elemental magnesium in 3-4 divided doses
dosing.renal: Avoid or reduce dose significantly in CKD; monitor magnesium levels closely
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Deep tendon reflexes (clinical marker of magnesium status)
magnesium_oxide.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at recommended doses). Safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Low bioavailability compared to magnesium glycinate or citrate
Correct magnesium before potassium — hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia
Separate from tetracyclines, bisphosphonates, and fluoroquinolones by 2 hours

zinc_sulfate.mechanism: Provides zinc, an essential trace element serving as a cofactor for over 300 enzymes involved in immune function, wound healing, protein synthesis, DNA synthesis, and cellular growth.
indications: Adjunct therapy for acute diarrhea in children (WHO recommendation)
contraindications: Caution with concurrent copper-containing supplements (competitive absorption)

dosing.adult: Supplementation: 20-50 mg elemental zinc/day; Wilson disease: 50 mg TDS (between meals); Deficiency: 50 mg elemental zinc BD-TDS
dosing.pediatric: Diarrhea (<6 months): 10 mg/day x 10-14 days; Diarrhea (>6 months): 20 mg/day x 10-14 days; Supplementation: 5-10 mg/day
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution in severe hepatic disease

adverse_effects: Nausea and vomiting (especially on empty stomach)
monitoring: Serum zinc levels if treating deficiency
zinc_sulfate.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at recommended doses). Safe in pregnancy at RDA (11 mg/day). Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: WHO recommends zinc supplementation for 10-14 days for all acute diarrhea episodes in children
Take on empty stomach for Wilson disease; with food to reduce GI side effects for supplementation
High doses chronically can cause copper deficiency — monitor

forms: Powder sachet for reconstitution in 1 L water (WHO low-osmolarity formula)
oral_rehydration_salts.mechanism: Utilizes sodium-glucose co-transport mechanism in the small intestine to maximize water and electrolyte absorption, even in the presence of diarrhea, effectively replacing fluid and electrolyte losses.
indications: Mild to moderate dehydration from diarrhea
contraindications: Intractable vomiting (relative — try small frequent sips)

dosing.adult: Mild dehydration: 50-100 mL/kg over 4 hours; Maintenance: replace stool losses 1:1 plus ongoing needs
dosing.pediatric: WHO Plan A (no dehydration): <2 yr 50-100 mL after each stool; 2-10 yr 100-200 mL after each stool; Plan B (some dehydration): 75 mL/kg over 4 hours
dosing.renal: Use with caution in renal impairment — monitor electrolytes
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

adverse_effects: Hyponatremia (if excessive plain water given alongside)
monitoring: Hydration status (skin turgor, urine output, mucous membranes)
Serum electrolytes if prolonged use or renal impairment
oral_rehydration_salts.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Continue breastfeeding alongside ORS administration in infants.
quick_flags: WHO low-osmolarity ORS (245 mOsm/L) is now standard — reduced stool output and vomiting vs older formula
Dissolve one sachet in exactly 1 L of clean water — do not add sugar or salt
Zinc supplementation should accompany ORS in pediatric diarrhea

doxazosin.mechanism: Selectively blocks alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation and reduced peripheral vascular resistance. Also relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck.
contraindications: Caution with PDE5 inhibitors (risk of severe hypotension)

dosing.adult: HTN: Start 1 mg at bedtime; titrate to 2-8 mg/day; BPH: Start 1 mg at bedtime; titrate to 4-8 mg/day; Max: 16 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not commonly used; limited data
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; hepatically metabolized — lower doses may be needed

monitoring: Blood pressure (standing and supine — orthostatic check)
doxazosin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in pregnancy if possible. Not recommended in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Give first dose at bedtime to minimize first-dose syncope
Inform ophthalmologist before cataract surgery — IFIS risk
Not first-line for HTN monotherapy (ALLHAT trial showed higher HF rates)

minoxidil_oral.mechanism: Opens potassium channels in arteriolar smooth muscle, causing direct arteriolar vasodilation and reducing peripheral vascular resistance. No effect on venous capacitance vessels.
indications: Severe/resistant hypertension refractory to other agents
contraindications: Pulmonary hypertension secondary to mitral stenosis

dosing.adult: Start 2.5-5 mg/day; titrate every 3 days; Usual range: 10-40 mg/day; Max: 100 mg/day; Must co-prescribe beta-blocker + diuretic
dosing.pediatric: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day initially; Max: 50 mg/day
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment; may need supplemental dialysis
dosing.hepatic: Use lower doses; impaired metabolism possible

monitoring: Echocardiography if symptoms suggest pericardial effusion
minoxidil_oral.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Potential for adverse fetal effects. Avoid in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk — not recommended.
quick_flags: MUST co-prescribe a beta-blocker (reflex tachycardia) AND a loop diuretic (fluid retention)
Reserved for resistant HTN not controlled by 3+ drugs
Hypertrichosis is cosmetically troublesome but reversible on stopping
Pericardial effusion can occur — monitor with echo if chest pain or dyspnea

hydralazine.mechanism: Directly relaxes arteriolar smooth muscle through interference with calcium ion transport, reducing peripheral vascular resistance. Preferential dilation of arterioles over veins.
indications: Heart failure (in combination with isosorbide dinitrate — A-HeFT trial)
contraindications: SLE or positive ANA (drug-induced lupus risk)

dosing.adult: Oral: 25 mg BD-TDS initially; titrate to 50-100 mg BD-TDS; Max: 300 mg/day; IV/IM: 10-20 mg per dose; repeat q4-6h; Pre-eclampsia: 5-10 mg IV q20 min
dosing.pediatric: Oral: 0.75-1 mg/kg/day in 2-4 divided doses; IV: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/dose q4-6h
dosing.renal: Reduce dose interval in renal impairment; acetylator status affects elimination
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; hepatically metabolized via acetylation

monitoring: ANA titer (every 6 months if prolonged use)
Signs of lupus-like syndrome (arthralgia, rash, fever, serositis)
hydralazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Widely used in pre-eclampsia/eclampsia — long safety record. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Drug-induced lupus risk highest with doses >200 mg/day in slow acetylators
Combine with beta-blocker to prevent reflex tachycardia
Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (BiDil) is evidence-based for HF in African Americans
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 10-25 mg/day prevents peripheral neuropathy

forms: 50 mg powder for reconstitution for IV infusion
nitroprusside.mechanism: Releases nitric oxide which activates guanylyl cyclase, increasing cGMP and causing relaxation of both arterial and venous smooth muscle. Reduces both preload and afterload.
indications: Acute heart failure with severe afterload elevation
contraindications: Compensatory hypertension (coarctation of aorta, AV shunting)

dosing.adult: Start 0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate by 0.5 mcg/kg/min every 5 min; Usual range: 0.5-5 mcg/kg/min; Max: 10 mcg/kg/min (for <10 min); Duration should not exceed 48-72 h
dosing.pediatric: Same mcg/kg/min dosing as adults; start at 0.25 mcg/kg/min
dosing.renal: Increased risk of thiocyanate accumulation — monitor levels; reduce duration
dosing.hepatic: Increased risk of cyanide toxicity — avoid or use with extreme caution

adverse_effects: Cyanide toxicity (tachycardia, metabolic acidosis, confusion, seizures)
Thiocyanate toxicity (prolonged use — tinnitus, blurred vision, confusion)
monitoring: Cyanide/thiocyanate levels (if >48 h use or >4 mcg/kg/min)
nitroprusside.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Cyanide toxicity risk to fetus. Reserve for life-threatening situations only. No breastfeeding data.
quick_flags: Wrap infusion bag in foil — photodegradation occurs
Co-administer sodium thiosulfate to reduce cyanide toxicity risk
Rising lactate + resistance to dose escalation = suspect cyanide toxicity
Hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12a) is cyanide antidote if toxicity occurs

fenoldopam.mechanism: Selective agonist at dopamine D1 receptors in renal, mesenteric, and peripheral vasculature, causing vasodilation. Increases renal blood flow, sodium excretion, and urine output while lowering blood pressure.
indications: Short-term management of severe hypertension in hospital
contraindications: Concurrent use with beta-blockers (may cause unexpected hypotension)

dosing.adult: Start 0.1 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate every 15-20 min in increments of 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min; Max: 1.6 mcg/kg/min
dosing.pediatric: 0.2 mcg/kg/min initially; titrate to max 0.8 mcg/kg/min
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment; may actually be renal-protective
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Continuous blood pressure monitoring (arterial line preferred)
fenoldopam.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data. Use in pregnancy only if clearly needed. No breastfeeding data.
quick_flags: Advantage over nitroprusside: no cyanide toxicity and renal-protective
Contains sodium metabisulfite — caution in sulfite-sensitive patients
Avoid concurrent beta-blockers (loss of reflex tachycardia can cause severe hypotension)
Increases intraocular pressure — avoid in glaucoma

promethazine.mechanism: Blocks H1 histamine receptors and muscarinic cholinergic receptors centrally and peripherally. Also has weak dopamine receptor blocking activity in the chemoreceptor trigger zone.
indications: Adjunct to analgesics for pain management
contraindications: Lower respiratory tract symptoms including asthma

dosing.adult: Antiemetic: 12.5-25 mg PO/IM/IV q4-6h; Motion sickness: 25 mg 30 min before travel; Sedation: 25-50 mg at bedtime; Allergic: 25 mg at bedtime or 12.5 mg BD
dosing.pediatric: Children >2 years: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/dose q4-6h; Max: 25 mg/dose; NOT for use in children <2 years
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision)
Tissue necrosis (if IV extravasation or intra-arterial injection)
monitoring: Respiratory status (especially in children and postoperative patients)
promethazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Widely used for hyperemesis gravidarum with reasonable safety data. Compatible with breastfeeding in low doses.
quick_flags: BLACK BOX: Contraindicated in children <2 years — fatal respiratory depression
Never give intra-arterially — causes gangrene; check IV placement carefully
Dilute IV dose and administer slowly (max 25 mg/min) to reduce phlebitis risk
Strong sedative properties — useful as adjunct in pain management

prochlorperazine.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) and acts on the vomiting center in the medulla, suppressing nausea and vomiting. Also has antihistaminic and anticholinergic properties.
contraindications: Children <10 kg or <2 years (high dystonic reaction risk)

dosing.adult: Oral: 5-10 mg TDS-QDS; IM: 12.5 mg deep IM; Rectal: 25 mg BD; Migraine: 10 mg oral or 12.5 mg IM
dosing.pediatric: Children >10 kg: 0.1-0.15 mg/kg/dose IM; Oral: 0.4 mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses; Use with caution
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment

monitoring: Signs of dystonic reactions (especially in young patients)
prochlorperazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited safety data. Use only if benefit clearly outweighs risk. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Acute dystonic reactions treatable with IV diphenhydramine or benztropine
Higher incidence of EPS in children and young adults — use lowest effective dose
Effective for migraine-associated nausea — often combined with sumatriptan

cyclizine.mechanism: Blocks H1 histamine receptors and muscarinic receptors in the vomiting center and vestibular pathways, reducing the excitability of the labyrinth and inhibiting the CTZ.
contraindications: Caution in prostatic hypertrophy, glaucoma, and pyloric stenosis

dosing.adult: 50 mg TDS oral or IM/IV; Motion sickness: 50 mg 30 min before travel; Max: 150 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 6-12 years: 25 mg TDS; >12 years: 50 mg TDS
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment

cyclizine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Used in pregnancy for nausea — no clear evidence of teratogenicity. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Good choice for opioid-induced nausea and vomiting
Less sedating than promethazine
IV injection should be given slowly to avoid transient tachycardia

bisacodyl.mechanism: Stimulates sensory nerve endings in the colonic mucosa, increasing peristalsis and promoting electrolyte and fluid secretion into the colon. Also inhibits water absorption from the colon.
indications: Bowel preparation before surgery or diagnostic procedures

dosing.adult: Oral: 5-10 mg at bedtime (onset 6-12 h); Rectal: 10 mg suppository (onset 15-60 min); Bowel prep: as directed by protocol
dosing.pediatric: Children >4 years: 5 mg oral or rectal; 10-18 years: 5-10 mg
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

bisacodyl.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption; considered safe for occasional use in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Do not crush enteric-coated tablets — causes gastric irritation
Do not take within 1 hour of antacids or milk (premature coating dissolution)
Avoid long-term daily use — dependency and electrolyte depletion risk
Suppository provides fastest onset for acute constipation

forms: Powder for oral solution (17 g sachet)
polyethylene_glycol.mechanism: High molecular weight polymer that acts as an osmotic agent in the colon, retaining water in the stool and increasing stool volume, thereby stimulating bowel motility. Not absorbed or metabolized.

dosing.adult: Constipation: 17 g (1 sachet) in 240 mL water daily; Fecal impaction: 17 g TDS for up to 3 days; Bowel prep: as per colonoscopy protocol (e.g., 2-4 L PEG-electrolyte solution)
dosing.pediatric: 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day; Max: 17 g/day; Disimpaction: 1-1.5 g/kg/day for 3-6 days
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Electrolytes if used for bowel prep or prolonged use
polyethylene_glycol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Not systemically absorbed; considered safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First-line osmotic laxative for chronic constipation — better tolerated than lactulose
Can be mixed in any cold beverage (water, juice)
No electrolyte-free PEG for bowel prep — use electrolyte-containing formulations
Safe for long-term use in chronic constipation

senna.mechanism: Sennosides are prodrugs hydrolyzed by colonic bacteria to active rhein-anthrone, which stimulates myenteric plexus and increases colonic motility while inhibiting water and electrolyte absorption.

dosing.adult: 15-30 mg sennosides at bedtime; Max: 34 mg/day; Onset: 6-12 hours
dosing.pediatric: 2-6 years: 3.75-7.5 mg at bedtime; 6-12 years: 7.5-15 mg at bedtime
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

senna.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Considered safe for short-term use in pregnancy; not absorbed significantly. Compatible with breastfeeding (minimal excretion).
quick_flags: Best taken at bedtime — effect by morning
Often combined with a stool softener (docusate + senna) for opioid-induced constipation
Melanosis coli is benign and reversible on discontinuation
Avoid chronic daily use — risk of dependence and electrolyte depletion

lubiprostone.mechanism: Activates ClC-2 chloride channels on the apical surface of intestinal epithelial cells, increasing chloride-rich fluid secretion into the intestinal lumen, enhancing motility and transit.
indications: Irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C) in women
contraindications: Known or suspected mechanical GI obstruction

dosing.adult: Chronic constipation: 24 mcg BD with food; IBS-C: 8 mcg BD with food; Opioid-induced constipation: 24 mcg BD with food
dosing.pediatric: Not established in children <18 years
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment (Child-Pugh B): start 16 mcg BD for CIC, 8 mcg daily for IBS-C; Severe (Child-Pugh C): start 8 mcg BD for CIC, 8 mcg daily for IBS-C

adverse_effects: Dyspnea (within 1 hour of dosing, resolves spontaneously)
lubiprostone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic in animals). Women of childbearing age must have negative pregnancy test. Not recommended during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with food to reduce nausea
Nausea is dose-limiting side effect — may improve over time
IBS-C indication is only approved for women >=18 years
Dyspnea is a class effect — benign and self-resolving

sumatriptan.mechanism: Selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptors, causing cranial vasoconstriction, inhibition of trigeminal nociceptive transmission, and reduced release of vasoactive neuropeptides.
indications: Acute migraine with or without aura
contraindications: Ischemic heart disease or coronary vasospasm
Concurrent use of MAOIs or ergotamines

dosing.adult: Oral: 50-100 mg; may repeat after 2 h if needed; Max: 200 mg/day oral; SC: 6 mg; may repeat after 1 h; Max: 12 mg/day; Nasal: 20 mg; may repeat after 2 h; Max: 40 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Nasal spray 10-20 mg in adolescents; oral not well established for children <12
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: reduce oral dose (max 50 mg); Severe: contraindicated

monitoring: Cardiac risk assessment before first use
Frequency of use (>10 days/month suggests medication overuse)
sumatriptan.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data but no clear teratogenicity. Express and discard breast milk for 12 hours after dose (or consider acceptable with monitoring).
quick_flags: Perform cardiac evaluation in patients with risk factors before first triptan use
SC injection is fastest onset (10-15 min) — best for cluster headache
Limit to <10 days/month to prevent medication overuse headache
24-hour washout required between triptans and ergotamines

rizatriptan.mechanism: Selective serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonist causing intracranial vasoconstriction and inhibition of trigeminal nerve activation. Faster onset and higher oral bioavailability than sumatriptan.
indications: Acute migraine with or without aura
contraindications: Ischemic heart disease or coronary vasospasm

dosing.adult: 10 mg oral; may repeat after 2 h if needed; Max: 30 mg/day; If on propranolol: use 5 mg dose (max 15 mg/day)
dosing.pediatric: 6-17 years and <40 kg: 5 mg; >=40 kg: 10 mg (limited approval)
dosing.renal: No adjustment in mild-moderate; avoid in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in mild-moderate; avoid in severe impairment

rizatriptan.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Minimize use during pregnancy. Express and discard breast milk for 24 hours after dose.
quick_flags: Reduce dose to 5 mg when co-administered with propranolol (CYP interaction increases levels)
Orally disintegrating tablet (MLT) is convenient for patients with nausea — dissolves on tongue without water
Fastest onset among oral triptans (~30 min)

flunarizine.mechanism: Selectively blocks T-type and some L-type calcium channels in cerebral and vestibular vasculature. Has anti-histaminic and anti-serotonergic properties. Reduces cerebral vasospasm and neuronal hyperexcitability.

dosing.adult: Migraine prophylaxis: 10 mg at bedtime; Elderly: start 5 mg; Vertigo: 10 mg at bedtime; Duration: typically 2-6 months course
dosing.pediatric: Children >6 years: 5 mg at bedtime
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose or avoid in significant hepatic impairment

flunarizine.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy. Avoid in women of childbearing age without effective contraception. Excreted in breast milk — avoid during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Very long half-life (18 days) — steady state takes weeks; give at bedtime due to sedation
Stop if depression develops
Widely used for migraine prophylaxis in India — not available in US/UK
Weight gain is a frequent reason for discontinuation — counsel patients

propranolol.mechanism: Non-selectively blocks beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, myocardial contractility, and blood pressure. Migraine prophylaxis mechanism likely involves central beta-blockade and reduced adrenergic tone.
contraindications: Asthma and severe COPD (non-selective beta blockade)

dosing.adult: Migraine prophylaxis: 40-80 mg BD (up to 160-240 mg/day); HTN: 40 mg BD, titrate to 160-320 mg/day; Essential tremor: 40 mg BD, up to 320 mg/day; Portal HTN: 40 mg BD, titrate to reduce resting HR by 25%
dosing.pediatric: Migraine: 0.5-1 mg/kg/day in divided doses; HTN: 1-2 mg/kg/day in BD-TDS dosing; Infantile hemangioma: 1-3 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism — bioavailability increased in liver disease

propranolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. IUGR reported with chronic use. Monitor neonatal heart rate and glucose. Compatible with breastfeeding (low levels in milk).
quick_flags: First-line for migraine prophylaxis — takes 4-6 weeks for full benefit
Do not stop abruptly — rebound hypertension and angina risk; taper over 1-2 weeks
Non-selective: contraindicated in asthma (use cardioselective beta-blockers instead)
For portal hypertension: target 25% reduction in resting heart rate

tizanidine.drug_class: Central Alpha-2 Adrenergic Agonist / Muscle Relaxant
tizanidine.mechanism: Central alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that reduces spasticity by increasing presynaptic inhibition of motor neurons in the spinal cord, decreasing excitatory neurotransmitter release.
indications: Muscle spasticity (multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, stroke)
contraindications: Concurrent use with strong CYP1A2 inhibitors (fluvoxamine, ciprofloxacin)
Concurrent use with other alpha-2 agonists

dosing.adult: Start 2 mg at bedtime; titrate by 2-4 mg/dose every 6-8 h; Usual: 6-12 mg/day in 3 divided doses; Max: 36 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 0.05 mg/kg/dose; titrate as tolerated
dosing.renal: Start with lower dose; CrCl <25 mL/min: reduce dose by 50%
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment; use with extreme caution in mild-moderate

monitoring: Liver function tests (baseline, 1, 3, 6 months, then periodically)
tizanidine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited human data. Avoid if possible. Unknown if excreted in breast milk — caution advised.
quick_flags: Absolutely avoid with fluvoxamine or ciprofloxacin (CYP1A2 inhibitors — massive increase in levels)
Food affects bioavailability — be consistent (always with or without food)
