LFT monitoring is mandatory — hepatotoxicity can be fatal
Less sedating than baclofen at comparable antispasticity doses

thiocolchicoside.drug_class: Muscle Relaxant (GABA-A Receptor Agonist / Glycine Agonist)
thiocolchicoside.mechanism: Acts as an agonist at GABA-A and glycine receptors in the spinal cord, producing muscle relaxation. Also has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. Colchicine-like structure but different mechanism.
indications: Adjunct in painful vertebral column disorders
contraindications: Pregnancy and women of childbearing potential not using contraception

dosing.adult: Oral: 4-8 mg BD-TDS; Max: 7 days of treatment (EMA recommendation); IM: 4 mg BD; Max total duration: 5 days IM followed by oral
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <16 years
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment (limited data)
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment (limited data)

adverse_effects: Potential aneuploidogenic effects (prolonged high-dose use — EMA warning)
monitoring: Duration of use (limit to 7 days oral, 5 days IM as per EMA)
thiocolchicoside.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy (potential genotoxicity — aneuploidogenicity). Contraindicated in breastfeeding. Women of childbearing age must use effective contraception.
quick_flags: EMA restricted use to max 7 days oral / 5 days IM due to genotoxicity concerns
Very widely prescribed in India for acute back pain — ensure short course only
Not available in US or UK
Contraindicated in pregnancy — counsel women of childbearing age

chlorzoxazone.mechanism: Acts primarily at the spinal cord and subcortical levels to inhibit multisynaptic reflex arcs involved in muscle spasm. May also act at brainstem level. Exact mechanism not fully established.
indications: Muscle spasm associated with sprains, strains, and myalgia
Adjunct to rest and physical therapy
contraindications: Known hepatic impairment or history of chlorzoxazone-induced hepatotoxicity

dosing.adult: 250-500 mg TDS-QDS; May increase to 750 mg TDS-QDS for severe spasm; Usual course: 2-3 weeks
dosing.pediatric: 20 mg/kg/day in 3-4 divided doses (limited data)
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated; risk of severe hepatotoxicity

monitoring: Liver function tests (baseline and if symptoms develop)
Signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, anorexia, dark urine)
chlorzoxazone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Avoid in pregnancy unless clearly needed. Unknown excretion in breast milk.
quick_flags: Often combined with paracetamol in fixed-dose combination (Flexon)
Hepatotoxicity is rare but potentially fatal — discontinue immediately if signs of liver injury
Orange-red urine is expected and harmless — counsel patients
Short-term use only — not for chronic spasticity

dorzolamide.mechanism: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase II in the ciliary processes of the eye, reducing aqueous humor secretion and thereby lowering intraocular pressure.

dosing.adult: 1 drop in affected eye(s) TDS; If used with other drops, space by at least 10 minutes; Combination (dorzolamide-timolol): 1 drop BD
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult dosing in children >2 years
dosing.renal: Avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min (systemic accumulation risk)
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment for ophthalmic use

monitoring: Corneal health (endothelial cell count in long-term use)
dorzolamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Unknown if excreted in breast milk — use with caution.
quick_flags: Sulfonamide derivative — ask about sulfa allergy before prescribing
Combination with timolol (Cosopt) reduces drop burden for compliance
Avoid in patients with corneal endothelial compromise (e.g., Fuchs dystrophy)
Apply nasolacrimal occlusion after instillation to reduce systemic absorption

pilocarpine.mechanism: Direct muscarinic agonist that contracts the ciliary muscle (accommodation) and iris sphincter (miosis), opening the trabecular meshwork and increasing aqueous humor outflow. Orally stimulates salivary glands.
contraindications: Conditions where pupillary constriction is undesirable
Narrow-angle glaucoma with pupillary block that has not been broken

dosing.adult: Glaucoma: 1-2 drops of 1-4% solution BD-QDS; Acute angle closure: 1 drop of 2% q5 min x 3 doses then q15-30 min; Dry mouth: 5 mg TDS oral; Max: 30 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Glaucoma: 1 drop of 1% solution BD-TDS
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment for ophthalmic use
dosing.hepatic: Reduce oral dose in moderate hepatic impairment

monitoring: Retinal status (risk of detachment with miosis)
pilocarpine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Caution with systemic use during breastfeeding.
quick_flags: In acute angle closure: pilocarpine works best AFTER IOP is partially lowered (iris sphincter is ischemic at very high IOP)
Warn patients about decreased night vision due to miosis
Largely replaced by prostaglandin analogues for chronic glaucoma but still essential in acute angle closure

prednisolone_eye_drops.mechanism: Suppresses ocular inflammation by inhibiting phospholipase A2, reducing arachidonic acid-derived mediators (prostaglandins and leukotrienes). Stabilizes lysosomal membranes and reduces capillary permeability.

dosing.adult: Acute uveitis: 1 drop q1h during waking hours initially; taper over 2-4 weeks; Post-operative: 1 drop QDS for 2-4 weeks then taper; Mild inflammation: 1 drop BD-QDS
dosing.pediatric: Same as adults; use minimum effective dose and duration
dosing.renal: No systemic adjustment needed for ophthalmic use
dosing.hepatic: No systemic adjustment needed for ophthalmic use

adverse_effects: Raised intraocular pressure (steroid response — can be significant)
monitoring: Intraocular pressure (check at 2 weeks, then regularly)
prednisolone_eye_drops.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption with ophthalmic use. Likely compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: ALWAYS rule out herpes simplex keratitis before starting (slit lamp with fluorescein)
Shake suspension well before instilling — active drug settles
Steroid response (IOP rise) occurs in ~5% of general population, 30%+ of steroid responders — check IOP at 2 weeks
Taper gradually — do not stop abruptly to avoid rebound inflammation

flumazenil.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at the GABA-A receptor benzodiazepine binding site, reversing the sedative, anxiolytic, and respiratory depressant effects of benzodiazepines without affecting opioid or other sedative effects.
contraindications: Patients receiving benzodiazepines for seizure control

dosing.adult: Reversal of sedation: 0.2 mg IV over 15 sec; repeat 0.2 mg q1 min; Max: 1 mg total; Overdose: 0.2 mg IV then 0.3 mg then 0.5 mg at 1-min intervals; Max: 3 mg total; May re-dose if re-sedation occurs (0.5 mg q20 min, max 3 mg/h)
dosing.pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg per dose); repeat q1 min; Max total: 0.05 mg/kg or 1 mg
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Clearance reduced — initial dose unchanged but resedation more likely; repeat doses with caution

monitoring: Level of consciousness for at least 2 hours after last dose (resedation risk)
flumazenil.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use in pregnancy only for life-threatening situations. Unknown if excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Half-life (40-80 min) is shorter than most benzodiazepines — monitor for resedation
NEVER give to known benzodiazepine-dependent patients — precipitates life-threatening seizures
In mixed overdoses with TCA, avoid flumazenil — may unmask TCA-induced seizures
Does not reverse opioid, barbiturate, or alcohol effects

pralidoxime.mechanism: Reactivates acetylcholinesterase that has been inhibited by organophosphate compounds, by cleaving the phosphoryl-enzyme bond before irreversible aging occurs. Restores neuromuscular function.
indications: Adjunct to atropine in OP poisoning
contraindications: Carbamate poisoning (relative — may worsen toxicity with some carbamates)

dosing.adult: 1-2 g IV over 15-30 min; may repeat after 1 hour if muscle weakness persists; Infusion: 500 mg/h after loading; Continue until atropine no longer needed and symptoms resolve
dosing.pediatric: 20-50 mg/kg IV over 15-30 min; Max single dose: 2 g; May repeat in 1-2 hours
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment; renally excreted
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Respiratory function (look for improvement in muscle weakness)
pralidoxime.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. No human data but benefit outweighs risk in organophosphate poisoning. No breastfeeding data.
quick_flags: Must be given within 24-48 hours of OP exposure — before aging of OP-AChE bond becomes irreversible
ALWAYS give with atropine — pralidoxime alone is insufficient
Infuse slowly to avoid hypertension, tachycardia, and muscle rigidity
Aging time varies by agent (sarin: 5 h; parathion: 36-48 h; soman: 2 min — pralidoxime ineffective)

acetylcysteine_iv.mechanism: Replenishes depleted hepatic glutathione stores, providing sulfhydryl groups that conjugate with the toxic metabolite of paracetamol (NAPQI), preventing hepatocellular necrosis.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to acetylcysteine (anaphylactoid reactions can be managed; not an absolute contraindication in paracetamol OD)

dosing.adult: Standard 21-hour IV protocol: Loading: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% dextrose over 1 h; Then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 h; Then 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 h; Modified 2-bag protocol: 200 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 h then 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 h
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing; reduce diluent volumes to prevent fluid overload (<20 kg: use specific pediatric dilution protocols)
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment for paracetamol OD
dosing.hepatic: No dose adjustment — this IS the treatment for hepatotoxicity

adverse_effects: Anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm — especially with loading dose)
Coagulopathy (NAC prolongs INR independently of liver damage)
monitoring: Paracetamol level at 4 hours post-ingestion (plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram)
ALT, AST, INR, creatinine, blood glucose (at baseline and q12h)
Signs of anaphylactoid reaction during loading dose
acetylcysteine_iv.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy — treat paracetamol OD without hesitation. Both mother and fetus at risk from untreated overdose.
quick_flags: Most effective within 8 hours of paracetamol ingestion, but give even if >24 hours in severe cases
Anaphylactoid reactions common (10-20%) with loading dose — slow/pause infusion, give antihistamine, then resume
NAC itself raises INR slightly — do not confuse with liver damage
Use Rumack-Matthew nomogram only for acute single ingestions; staggered OD requires different approach

forms: 10% injection (10 mL ampoule = 1 g = 93 mg elemental calcium = 4.65 mEq Ca)
calcium_gluconate_iv.mechanism: Provides ionized calcium that directly antagonizes the membrane effects of hyperkalemia on cardiac myocytes, stabilizing the resting membrane potential and reducing the risk of fatal arrhythmias. Does not lower serum potassium.
indications: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes (cardiac membrane stabilization)
contraindications: Digoxin toxicity (relative — calcium may potentiate digoxin arrhythmias)

dosing.adult: Hyperkalemia: 10-20 mL of 10% solution (1-2 g) IV over 2-5 min; may repeat in 5-10 min if ECG changes persist; Hypocalcemia: 1-2 g IV over 10-20 min then infusion 0.5-2 mg/kg/h; CCB overdose: 3-6 g IV with repeat dosing
dosing.pediatric: Hyperkalemia: 0.5-1 mL/kg of 10% solution IV over 5-10 min; Max: 20 mL; Hypocalcemia: 200-500 mg/kg/day IV
dosing.renal: Monitor calcium closely; risk of hypercalcemia in CKD
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Serum potassium (calcium gluconate does NOT lower K — must use other measures)
calcium_gluconate_iv.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use when indicated (e.g., eclampsia with magnesium toxicity). Calcium is normal in breast milk.
quick_flags: Stabilizes cardiac membrane in hyperkalemia but does NOT lower potassium — must also give insulin+glucose, salbutamol, or kayexalate
Preferred over calcium chloride for peripheral IV (less tissue necrosis if extravasation)
In digoxin toxicity: avoid calcium unless life-threatening hyperkalemia (give slowly with close monitoring)
Do NOT mix with IV bicarbonate in same line — calcium carbonate precipitation

norfloxacin.mechanism: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV

dosing.adult: 400 mg twice daily for 3-14 days depending on indication
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: 400 mg once daily
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

norfloxacin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated in pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Very commonly prescribed UTI drug in India
SBP prophylaxis in cirrhosis

ofloxacin.mechanism: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV

dosing.adult: 200-400 mg twice daily
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended
dosing.renal: CrCl <20: 200 mg once daily
dosing.hepatic: Max 400 mg/day in hepatic impairment

quick_flags: Oflox-OZ (ofloxacin+ornidazole) very popular in India
Available as ear/eye drops

ornidazole.mechanism: Reduced by anaerobic organisms to form cytotoxic metabolites that damage DNA

dosing.adult: 500 mg twice daily for 5-10 days; Amoebiasis: 1.5g once daily for 3 days
dosing.pediatric: 25 mg/kg/day in divided doses
dosing.renal: No adjustment in mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution, reduce dose in severe disease

ornidazole.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in first trimester. Caution later.
quick_flags: Often combined with ofloxacin as O2/Oflox-OZ
Better tolerated than metronidazole for some patients

doxycycline_extra.mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit, inhibits bacterial protein synthesis

dosing.adult: 100 mg twice daily on day 1, then 100 mg once or twice daily
dosing.pediatric: >8 years: 2-4 mg/kg/day in 1-2 doses (max 200 mg/day)
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed (excreted via GI)
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic disease

quick_flags: Take with water sitting upright
No dose adjustment in renal failure unlike other tetracyclines
Photosensitivity — advise sun protection

ambroxol.mechanism: Stimulates surfactant production and mucociliary clearance

dosing.adult: 30 mg thrice daily or 75 mg SR once daily
dosing.pediatric: 2-5 years: 7.5 mg thrice daily; 6-12 years: 15 mg thrice daily
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment

quick_flags: Often combined with salbutamol/guaifenesin in India
Very commonly prescribed OTC

dextromethorphan.mechanism: Suppresses cough reflex via action on medullary cough center

dosing.adult: 10-20 mg every 4 hours or 30 mg every 6-8 hours (max 120 mg/day)
dosing.pediatric: 4-6 years: 5 mg q4h; 6-12 years: 10 mg q4h
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution

dextromethorphan.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Generally considered safe in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs
Abuse potential — monitor

guaifenesin.mechanism: Increases respiratory tract secretion volume, reduces viscosity

dosing.adult: 200-400 mg every 4 hours (max 2.4 g/day)
dosing.pediatric: 6-12 years: 100-200 mg every 4 hours
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

quick_flags: Often in combination cough syrups in India
Keep hydrated for best effect

fluorouracil_5fu.mechanism: Inhibits thymidylate synthase, blocking DNA and RNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells.

dosing.adult: 370-600 mg/m2 IV; varies by protocol (bolus or continuous infusion)
dosing.pediatric: Safety and dosing not well established
dosing.renal: Use with caution; dose reduction may be needed in severe impairment
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; dose reduce in moderate impairment

monitoring: CBC with differential before each cycle
fluorouracil_5fu.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Discontinue breastfeeding during treatment.
quick_flags: DPD deficiency testing recommended before initiation — fatal toxicity risk
Leucovorin potentiates activity in colorectal cancer protocols

mercaptopurine_6mp.mechanism: Converted to thioguanine nucleotides that incorporate into DNA, inhibiting purine synthesis and causing cell death.
contraindications: Concurrent use with allopurinol at full dose without dose reduction

dosing.adult: 1.5-2.5 mg/kg/day orally for ALL maintenance
dosing.pediatric: 1.5-2.5 mg/kg/day orally; adjust per protocol
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in significant renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; monitor LFTs closely

mercaptopurine_6mp.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Avoid in pregnancy. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Reduce dose by 67-75% if co-administered with allopurinol
TPMT/NUDT15 genotyping is essential before initiation

bleomycin.mechanism: Causes DNA strand breaks via formation of free radicals, leading to cell death. Cell cycle specific (G2/M phase).

dosing.adult: 10-20 IU/m2 IV/IM weekly or per protocol; max cumulative 400 IU
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: CrCl 40-50: give 70% dose; CrCl 30-40: give 60% dose; CrCl <30: consider alternative
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use caution

monitoring: Pulmonary function tests (DLCO) at baseline and periodically
bleomycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: NEVER exceed cumulative 400 IU — pulmonary fibrosis risk
Avoid high FiO2 during surgery in bleomycin-exposed patients
Test dose recommended in lymphoma patients

cisplatin.mechanism: Forms DNA cross-links and adducts, inhibiting DNA replication and transcription, triggering apoptosis.
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <60 ml/min unless protocol-specific)

dosing.adult: 50-100 mg/m2 IV every 3-4 weeks or 20 mg/m2/day x 5 days; protocol-dependent
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol, typically 60-100 mg/m2
dosing.renal: Contraindicated if CrCl <60; dose reduce if borderline
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

monitoring: Serum creatinine and CrCl before each cycle
cisplatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic and embryotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Aggressive pre- and post-hydration with normal saline is mandatory
Always use with antiemetic (5-HT3 + NK1 + dexamethasone)
Monitor magnesium — wasting is common and persistent

carboplatin.mechanism: Forms DNA cross-links similar to cisplatin, inhibiting DNA synthesis and repair.

dosing.adult: AUC 5-7 using Calvert formula: Dose = AUC x (GFR + 25)
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol; Calvert formula used
dosing.renal: Dose calculated by Calvert formula accounts for GFR; avoid if GFR <15
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment

monitoring: CBC with differential and platelets before each cycle
carboplatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic and teratogenic. Avoid in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Always dose using Calvert formula (AUC-based), not mg/m2
Less nephrotoxic and emetogenic than cisplatin
Hypersensitivity risk increases after 6+ cycles

cyclophosphamide.mechanism: Prodrug activated by hepatic CYP450 to phosphoramide mustard, which cross-links DNA strands.

dosing.adult: Oral: 1-5 mg/kg/day; IV: 500-1500 mg/m2 per cycle depending on protocol
dosing.pediatric: Per protocol; commonly 500-1000 mg/m2 IV
dosing.renal: GFR <10: reduce dose by 25%; supplement after hemodialysis
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (activation may be impaired)

cyclophosphamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Always co-administer mesna for high-dose IV to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis
Ensure adequate hydration
Counsel on fertility preservation before treatment

doxorubicin.mechanism: Intercalates DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals causing DNA damage and apoptosis.
contraindications: Cumulative dose >550 mg/m2 (or >400 mg/m2 with prior mediastinal radiation)

dosing.adult: 60-75 mg/m2 IV every 21 days; cumulative max 550 mg/m2
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol; typically 30-75 mg/m2
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment for mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Bilirubin 1.2-3: give 50% dose; Bilirubin 3.1-5: give 25% dose; Bilirubin >5: avoid

doxorubicin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
quick_flags: VESICANT — extravasation causes severe necrosis; dexrazoxane is the antidote
Track cumulative dose rigorously — irreversible cardiomyopathy
Consider dexrazoxane cardioprotection at cumulative dose >300 mg/m2

docetaxel.mechanism: Promotes microtubule assembly and inhibits depolymerization, arresting cells in mitosis.
contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment (bilirubin >ULN or AST/ALT >1.5x ULN with ALP >2.5x ULN)

dosing.adult: 60-100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe impairment; reduce dose 20-25% in moderate impairment

docetaxel.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic and fetotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Premedicate with dexamethasone for 3 days starting day before infusion to reduce fluid retention and hypersensitivity
Check LFTs before every cycle — hepatic impairment increases toxicity

paclitaxel.mechanism: Stabilizes microtubules, preventing depolymerization and blocking mitotic spindle function, causing cell cycle arrest at G2/M.
contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity to paclitaxel or Cremophor EL

dosing.adult: 135-175 mg/m2 IV over 3 hours every 3 weeks; or 80 mg/m2 weekly
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Moderate impairment: reduce dose; severe: avoid

paclitaxel.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Premedicate with dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, and ranitidine/famotidine to prevent Cremophor-related hypersensitivity
Administer paclitaxel BEFORE platinum agents to reduce myelosuppression

vincristine.mechanism: Binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, causing metaphase arrest.

dosing.adult: 1.4 mg/m2 IV weekly (max single dose 2 mg)
dosing.pediatric: 1.5-2 mg/m2 IV weekly (max 2 mg); <10 kg: 0.05 mg/kg
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: Bilirubin 1.5-3: give 50% dose; Bilirubin >3: give 25% dose

vincristine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: FATAL if given intrathecally — label syringe 'FOR IV USE ONLY'
Cap single dose at 2 mg regardless of BSA
Prescribe prophylactic laxatives for constipation

vinblastine.mechanism: Binds tubulin dimers, preventing microtubule polymerization and arresting mitosis at metaphase.
contraindications: Significant granulocytopenia not due to disease

dosing.adult: 6 mg/m2 IV weekly; increase by 1.8 mg/m2/week to max 18.5 mg/m2 based on WBC
dosing.pediatric: Initial 2.5 mg/m2 IV; increase to max 12.5 mg/m2 per protocol
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Bilirubin 1.5-3: give 50% dose; Bilirubin >3: give 25% dose

monitoring: CBC before each dose — do not administer if WBC <4000
vinblastine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: FATAL if given intrathecally — label 'FOR IV USE ONLY'
Dose-limiting toxicity is myelosuppression (unlike vincristine where it is neurotoxicity)
Vesicant — extravasation precautions required

etoposide.mechanism: Inhibits topoisomerase II, preventing re-ligation of DNA strand breaks, leading to apoptosis. Cell-cycle specific (S and G2 phases).

dosing.adult: 50-100 mg/m2/day IV on days 1-5 every 3-4 weeks; oral dose is 2x IV dose
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: CrCl 15-50: give 75% dose; CrCl <15: give 50% dose
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose with elevated bilirubin

etoposide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Infuse IV over at least 30-60 minutes to avoid hypotension
Risk of secondary AML (especially with alkylating agents)
Oral bioavailability ~50% — oral dose is double IV dose

gemcitabine.mechanism: Incorporated into DNA as a deoxycytidine analog, causing masked chain termination and inhibiting DNA synthesis.

dosing.adult: 1000-1250 mg/m2 IV over 30 min on days 1, 8, 15 of 28-day cycle
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: Use with caution; no formal adjustment for mild-moderate
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may worsen hepatotoxicity

gemcitabine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Infusion rate matters: 10 mg/m2/min (30-min infusion) — longer infusions increase toxicity
Hold if ANC <1000 or platelets <100,000 on day of treatment

capecitabine.mechanism: Oral prodrug converted to 5-FU preferentially in tumor tissue by thymidine phosphorylase. Inhibits thymidylate synthase.

dosing.adult: 1000-1250 mg/m2 twice daily for 14 days, followed by 7-day rest, in 21-day cycles
dosing.pediatric: Not established
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: reduce to 75% dose; CrCl <30: contraindicated
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; monitor closely in hepatic metastases

capecitabine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Take within 30 minutes after meals with water
DPD deficiency screening recommended before starting
Interrupt or dose-reduce promptly for Grade 2+ hand-foot syndrome

oxaliplatin.mechanism: Forms platinum-DNA adducts and interstrand cross-links, inhibiting DNA replication and transcription.

dosing.adult: 85 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (FOLFOX) or 130 mg/m2 every 3 weeks (XELOX)
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: avoid or reduce to 65 mg/m2
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

adverse_effects: Peripheral neuropathy (acute cold-triggered and chronic cumulative)
oxaliplatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Acute neuropathy is cold-triggered — advise patients to avoid cold drinks and objects for 3-5 days post-infusion
Cumulative neuropathy may be irreversible — consider stopping after 8-12 cycles
Do NOT use with chloride-containing solutions (use dextrose 5%)

imatinib.mechanism: Selectively inhibits BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, c-KIT, and PDGFR, blocking proliferation of malignant cells.

dosing.adult: CML chronic phase: 400 mg/day; CML accelerated/blast: 600 mg/day; GIST: 400 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 260-340 mg/m2/day (max 600 mg/day)
dosing.renal: CrCl 20-39: reduce max dose by 50%; CrCl <20: use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 25% in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: CBC weekly first month then monthly
imatinib.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic in animals. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take with food and a full glass of water to reduce GI effects
India was the first to produce affordable generic imatinib (Veenat)
Monitor molecular response milestones per NCCN/ELN guidelines

rituximab.mechanism: Binds CD20 on B lymphocytes, causing cell death via complement-dependent cytotoxicity, antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, and apoptosis.
contraindications: Hepatitis B reactivation risk without prophylaxis

dosing.adult: Lymphoma: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x 4-8 doses or per protocol; RA: 1000 mg IV on days 1 and 15
dosing.pediatric: Per protocol; similar mg/m2 dosing
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment

rituximab.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May cause B-cell depletion in fetus. Avoid in pregnancy. Use contraception for 12 months after last dose.
quick_flags: Screen for HBV before starting — risk of fatal reactivation
Premedicate with acetaminophen, antihistamine, and corticosteroid
First infusion: start slow (50 mg/hr) and escalate gradually

trastuzumab.mechanism: Binds HER2 receptor on tumor cells, inhibiting proliferation, mediating antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, and preventing HER2 extracellular domain cleavage.
contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity to trastuzumab or murine proteins

dosing.adult: Loading: 8 mg/kg IV; Maintenance: 6 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks; or weekly: 4 mg/kg then 2 mg/kg
dosing.pediatric: Not established for pediatric solid tumors
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment

monitoring: LVEF by echo/MUGA at baseline, every 3 months during treatment, and at completion
trastuzumab.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Can cause oligohydramnios and fetal renal damage. Avoid pregnancy during and for 7 months after treatment.
quick_flags: Confirm HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+ or FISH+) before starting
Hold if LVEF drops >16% from baseline or below normal
Cardiotoxicity is generally reversible (unlike anthracyclines) if caught early

temozolomide.mechanism: Prodrug converted to MTIC, which methylates DNA at O6-guanine and N7-guanine, causing cytotoxic lesions.

dosing.adult: Concurrent with RT: 75 mg/m2/day x 42 days; Adjuvant: 150-200 mg/m2/day x 5 days every 28 days
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol; similar dosing to adults
dosing.renal: Use with caution in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; monitor closely

monitoring: CBC weekly during concurrent RT, day 22 and weekly during adjuvant cycles
temozolomide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: MGMT promoter methylation predicts better response
PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) required during concurrent RT phase and until lymphocyte recovery
Take on empty stomach to reduce nausea

bortezomib.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits the 26S proteasome chymotrypsin-like activity, disrupting protein homeostasis and leading to apoptosis in myeloma cells.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to bortezomib, boron, or mannitol

dosing.adult: 1.3 mg/m2 IV or SC on days 1, 4, 8, 11 of 21-day cycle; or weekly dosing
dosing.pediatric: Not established
dosing.renal: No adjustment including dialysis patients
dosing.hepatic: Mild: no adjustment; Moderate-Severe (bilirubin >1.5x ULN): start at 0.7 mg/m2

bortezomib.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Use contraception during and for 2 months after treatment.
quick_flags: SC route has significantly less neuropathy than IV — prefer SC
Acyclovir/valacyclovir prophylaxis mandatory to prevent herpes zoster
Thrombocytopenia is cyclical — nadir day 11, recovers by day 21

actinomycin_d.mechanism: Intercalates into DNA between guanine-cytosine base pairs, inhibiting RNA polymerase and blocking transcription.

dosing.adult: 10-15 mcg/kg/day IV x 5 days every 3-6 weeks; or 2.5 mg/m2 as single dose in GTD
dosing.pediatric: 15 mcg/kg/day IV x 5 days (max 500 mcg/day); per specific protocol
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment; risk of hepatic veno-occlusive disease

actinomycin_d.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: VESICANT — severe extravasation injury; use central line preferred
Potentiates radiation toxicity (radiation recall)
Extremely low doses used (micrograms) — dosing errors can be fatal

daunorubicin.mechanism: Intercalates DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals, leading to DNA damage and apoptosis.

dosing.adult: AML induction: 45-90 mg/m2/day IV x 3 days; ALL: 25-45 mg/m2/day x 3 days
dosing.pediatric: 25-45 mg/m2 IV per protocol; BSA <0.5m2: use weight-based dosing
dosing.renal: SCr >3 mg/dL: give 50% dose
dosing.hepatic: Bilirubin 1.2-3: give 75% dose; Bilirubin >3: give 50% dose

monitoring: Cardiac function (LVEF) at baseline and periodically
daunorubicin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: VESICANT — extravasation causes severe necrosis
Cumulative dose limit 550 mg/m2 (400 mg/m2 with chest radiation)
Red urine is expected — counsel patients

melphalan.mechanism: Alkylates DNA via formation of interstrand cross-links, disrupting DNA function and causing cell death.
contraindications: Patients whose disease is resistant to melphalan

dosing.adult: Multiple myeloma: 6 mg/day orally x 2-3 weeks, then 2 mg/day maintenance; SCT conditioning: 140-200 mg/m2 IV
dosing.pediatric: Per transplant protocol
dosing.renal: BUN >30: reduce oral dose by 50%; SCT: consider reduced dose
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

melphalan.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Mutagenic and teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Delayed myelosuppression — nadir at 4-5 weeks, not 2 weeks
Oral absorption is variable — take on empty stomach
Reduce dose in renal impairment

chlorambucil.mechanism: Alkylates and cross-links DNA, interfering with DNA replication and transcription.

dosing.adult: CLL: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day for 3-6 weeks or pulse dosing 0.4 mg/kg every 2 weeks
dosing.pediatric: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day (rare pediatric use)
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment; use caution
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

monitoring: CBC weekly initially, then every 2-4 weeks
chlorambucil.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Well-tolerated oral alkylator — suitable for elderly/frail CLL patients
Being largely replaced by targeted therapies (ibrutinib, venetoclax)
Seizures can occur with overdose — dose carefully

ifosfamide.mechanism: Prodrug activated by hepatic CYP450; alkylates DNA causing interstrand cross-links and cell death.

dosing.adult: 1.2-2.4 g/m2/day IV x 3-5 days every 3-4 weeks with mesna
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol with mesna
dosing.renal: Avoid if CrCl <10; dose reduce in moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; metabolized hepatically

monitoring: Serum albumin (low albumin increases CNS toxicity risk)
ifosfamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: ALWAYS co-administer mesna — hemorrhagic cystitis is near-certain without it
CNS toxicity (ifosfamide encephalopathy) treated with methylene blue
Higher urotoxicity risk than cyclophosphamide

irinotecan.mechanism: Inhibits topoisomerase I, preventing re-ligation of single-strand DNA breaks during replication, leading to apoptosis.

dosing.adult: FOLFIRI: 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 min every 2 weeks; single-agent: 125 mg/m2 IV weekly x 4 every 6 weeks or 350 mg/m2 every 3 weeks
dosing.pediatric: Per oncology protocol
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment; use caution
dosing.hepatic: Bilirubin 1-2x ULN: reduce dose; Bilirubin >2x ULN: not recommended

irinotecan.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: TWO types of diarrhea: early (treat with atropine) and late (treat aggressively with loperamide)
UGT1A1*28 homozygotes (Gilbert syndrome) at high risk for toxicity — reduce dose
Late diarrhea can be life-threatening — patient education critical

bendamustine.mechanism: Contains both alkylating (nitrogen mustard) and antimetabolite (purine analog) properties; cross-links DNA and activates DNA damage checkpoints.

dosing.adult: CLL: 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1 and 2 of 28-day cycle; NHL: 120 mg/m2 IV on days 1 and 2 of 21-day cycle
dosing.pediatric: Not established
dosing.renal: CrCl <40: not recommended
dosing.hepatic: Mild: use with caution; Moderate-Severe: not recommended

bendamustine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Increasingly used as first-line with rituximab (BR regimen) for indolent NHL
Tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis needed in high tumor burden
Do not substitute for other alkylators — unique mechanism

lenalidomide.mechanism: Modulates the immune system by enhancing T-cell and NK-cell activity, inhibiting angiogenesis, and directly inducing apoptosis in tumor cells via cereblon-mediated degradation of IKZF1/3.
contraindications: Inability to comply with REMS/risk management program

dosing.adult: Multiple myeloma: 25 mg/day on days 1-21 of 28-day cycle; MDS: 10 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not established
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-60: 10 mg/day; CrCl <30 (non-dialysis): 15 mg every 48h; Dialysis: 5 mg/day
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

monitoring: CBC every 2 weeks for first 8 weeks, then monthly
Pregnancy test before each cycle in women of childbearing potential
lenalidomide.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated. Thalidomide analog — severe teratogenicity. Mandatory pregnancy prevention program. Two forms of contraception required.
quick_flags: Mandatory risk management program due to teratogenicity
VTE prophylaxis with aspirin or LMWH required
Renal dose adjustment is critical — accumulates in renal impairment

bicalutamide.mechanism: Competitively inhibits the androgen receptor, blocking the effects of testosterone and dihydrotestosterone on prostate cancer cells.
indications: Prostate cancer (advanced, in combination with LHRH agonist)
Prostate cancer (locally advanced, as monotherapy at 150 mg)
contraindications: Concurrent use with terfenadine, astemizole, or cisapride

dosing.adult: 50 mg once daily (with LHRH agonist) or 150 mg once daily (monotherapy)
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: Mild-Moderate: no adjustment; Severe: contraindicated

monitoring: LFTs at baseline, monthly for first 4 months, then periodically
bicalutamide.pregnancy_lactation: Not applicable (male-only indication). Category X if used in women. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnant women.
quick_flags: Start bicalutamide 1 week before LHRH agonist to prevent testosterone flare
Monitor LFTs — rare but serious hepatotoxicity reported
Gynecomastia occurs in up to 70% with 150 mg monotherapy

letrozole.mechanism: Selectively inhibits aromatase (CYP19), blocking conversion of androgens to estrogens, reducing circulating estrogen levels in postmenopausal women.
indications: Hormone receptor-positive breast cancer in postmenopausal women (adjuvant and metastatic)

dosing.adult: 2.5 mg once daily; adjuvant therapy for 5-10 years
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No adjustment if CrCl >10
dosing.hepatic: Mild-Moderate: no adjustment; Severe (Child-Pugh C): reduce dose to 2.5 mg every other day

monitoring: Bone mineral density (DEXA) at baseline and periodically
letrozole.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in pregnancy. May cause fetal harm.
quick_flags: Only for postmenopausal women — verify menopausal status
Bone health: supplement calcium and vitamin D; DEXA scan at baseline
Superior to tamoxifen in postmenopausal HR+ breast cancer

mesna.mechanism: Provides free thiol groups that bind and inactivate acrolein (toxic metabolite of cyclophosphamide and ifosfamide) in the urinary tract, preventing hemorrhagic cystitis.
indications: Prevention of hemorrhagic cystitis from ifosfamide
Prevention of hemorrhagic cystitis from high-dose cyclophosphamide
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to mesna or thiol compounds

dosing.adult: IV: 20% of ifosfamide dose at 0, 4, and 8 hours; or 100% of ifosfamide dose as continuous infusion; Oral/IV combo: 20% IV at 0h then 40% oral at 2h and 6h
dosing.pediatric: Same percentage-based dosing as adults
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

mesna.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Limited human data.
quick_flags: No antitumor activity — purely a uroprotectant
Can cause false-positive urine ketone tests
Timing is critical — must be given with and after the oxazaphosphorine

calcium_folinate_leucovorin.mechanism: Bypasses dihydrofolate reductase, providing reduced folate directly for nucleotide synthesis. Rescues normal cells from methotrexate toxicity and potentiates 5-FU cytotoxicity by stabilizing the 5-FU-thymidylate synthase complex.
indications: Megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency

dosing.adult: MTX rescue: 15 mg (approximately 10 mg/m2) IV/PO every 6 hours starting 24h after MTX until levels <0.05 µM; 5-FU potentiation: 200-500 mg/m2 IV before 5-FU
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult for MTX rescue
dosing.renal: No adjustment; however MTX clearance may be delayed in renal impairment requiring extended leucovorin
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

calcium_folinate_leucovorin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use if benefit outweighs risk. Generally considered safe for rescue therapy.
quick_flags: For MTX rescue: timing is critical — must start within 24 hours of MTX
Continue leucovorin until MTX level <0.05 µmol/L
Do NOT confuse with folic acid — leucovorin is the active reduced form

tamoxifen_nlem.mechanism: Competitively binds estrogen receptors, acting as an antagonist in breast tissue and agonist in bone and endometrium.
indications: Hormone receptor-positive breast cancer (adjuvant and metastatic)
Breast cancer risk reduction in high-risk women
contraindications: History of DVT/PE (if for risk reduction)

dosing.adult: 20 mg daily for 5-10 years (adjuvant breast cancer)
dosing.pediatric: McCune-Albright syndrome: 20 mg/day (off-label)
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment; use caution

tamoxifen_nlem.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Use barrier contraception during and for 2 months after.
quick_flags: Use in premenopausal women (aromatase inhibitors are for postmenopausal only)
CYP2D6 inhibitors (e.g., fluoxetine, paroxetine) reduce efficacy — avoid concurrent use
Annual endometrial monitoring for increased cancer risk

tretinoin_oral_atra.mechanism: Binds retinoic acid receptors, inducing differentiation of immature leukemic promyelocytes into mature granulocytes in APL with t(15;17).
indications: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) — induction and consolidation

dosing.adult: 45 mg/m2/day in 2 divided doses until complete remission or 90 days max
dosing.pediatric: 25-45 mg/m2/day in 2 divided doses
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may worsen hepatotoxicity

adverse_effects: Differentiation syndrome (formerly retinoic acid syndrome — fever, dyspnea, weight gain, pleural/pericardial effusions)
tretinoin_oral_atra.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Extremely teratogenic. Pregnancy must be excluded before starting. Two forms of contraception mandatory.
quick_flags: Differentiation syndrome is life-threatening — treat immediately with dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h
APL is a medical emergency — start ATRA immediately on suspicion, before genetic confirmation
Combined with arsenic trioxide has >90% cure rate

thalidomide.mechanism: Inhibits angiogenesis (VEGF, bFGF), modulates immune response (TNF-alpha inhibition, T-cell co-stimulation), and induces apoptosis via cereblon binding.
contraindications: Women of childbearing potential not on strict contraception

dosing.adult: Multiple myeloma: 200 mg/day at bedtime (with dexamethasone); ENL: 100-300 mg/day at bedtime
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended; if used, under strict protocol
dosing.renal: No formal adjustment; not significantly dialyzed
dosing.hepatic: No formal adjustment

monitoring: Pregnancy testing (weekly for first month, then monthly)
thalidomide.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Absolute contraindication. Causes severe birth defects (phocomelia, amelia). Mandatory risk management program (REMS equivalent).
quick_flags: Strictly controlled distribution — mandatory risk management program
VTE prophylaxis required when used with dexamethasone
Take at bedtime to minimize sedation
Peripheral neuropathy may be irreversible

abacavir.mechanism: Prodrug converted to carbovir triphosphate, which competes with dGTP for incorporation into viral DNA, causing chain termination.
indications: HIV-1 infection (in combination with other antiretrovirals)

dosing.adult: 300 mg twice daily or 600 mg once daily
dosing.pediatric: 8 mg/kg twice daily (max 300 mg per dose); weight-based dosing tables available
dosing.renal: No adjustment; not recommended in ESRD
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh A): 200 mg twice daily; Moderate-Severe: contraindicated

adverse_effects: Hypersensitivity reaction (HLA-B*5701 associated — multi-organ, potentially fatal)
monitoring: HIV viral load and CD4 count
Signs of hypersensitivity (fever, rash, GI, respiratory symptoms)
abacavir.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Can be used in pregnancy if indicated. Crosses placenta. Avoid breastfeeding (HIV transmission risk).
quick_flags: MANDATORY HLA-B*5701 testing before prescribing — do not rechallenge if positive or prior reaction
Hypersensitivity reaction worsens with each subsequent dose — STOP immediately and NEVER rechallenge
Part of preferred first-line combinations in national ART guidelines

zidovudine.mechanism: Thymidine analog phosphorylated to active triphosphate, which competes with deoxythymidine triphosphate for incorporation into viral DNA, causing chain termination.

dosing.adult: 300 mg twice daily or 200 mg three times daily
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based: 4-9 kg: 12 mg/kg twice daily; 9-30 kg: 9 mg/kg twice daily; >30 kg: adult dose
dosing.renal: CrCl <15 or hemodialysis: 100 mg every 6-8 hours
dosing.hepatic: May need dose reduction; limited data

monitoring: CBC every 2 weeks for first 3 months, then monthly
zidovudine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Recommended for PMTCT. IV zidovudine during labor reduces transmission. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: First antiretroviral ever approved — historical significance
Key drug for PMTCT (prevention of mother-to-child transmission)
Monitor Hb closely — switch to alternative if significant anemia develops

lamivudine.mechanism: Cytidine analog phosphorylated to active triphosphate, inhibiting HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase by chain termination.

dosing.adult: HIV: 150 mg twice daily or 300 mg once daily; HBV: 100 mg once daily
dosing.pediatric: HIV: 4 mg/kg twice daily (max 150 mg); various weight bands
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-49: 150 mg once daily; CrCl 15-29: 150 mg first dose then 100 mg daily; CrCl <15: 150 mg first dose then 25-50 mg daily
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment; monitor for HBV flare

lamivudine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Used in pregnancy for HIV. Part of PMTCT regimens. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: If patient has HBV co-infection, stopping lamivudine can cause severe HBV flare
Different dosing for HIV vs HBV — ensure correct dose
Backbone of many first-line ART regimens in India (TLE, TLD)

efavirenz.mechanism: Binds directly to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase at a site distinct from the active site, causing conformational change and enzyme inhibition.
contraindications: Concurrent use with terfenadine, astemizole, midazolam, triazolam, ergot alkaloids, St. John's wort

dosing.adult: 600 mg once daily at bedtime on empty stomach
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in mild; use with caution in moderate; avoid in severe

adverse_effects: CNS effects (vivid dreams, dizziness, insomnia, depression)
monitoring: Signs of rash in first 2-4 weeks
efavirenz.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Avoid in first trimester (neural tube defect risk). Can be continued if pregnancy discovered after first trimester. Avoid breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Take at bedtime on empty stomach to reduce CNS side effects — most resolve in 2-4 weeks
Being replaced by dolutegravir in most national guidelines due to better tolerability
False-positive cannabinoid urine screen can occur

nevirapine.mechanism: Binds directly to reverse transcriptase, disrupting the catalytic site and blocking RNA-dependent and DNA-dependent DNA polymerase activity.
contraindications: Women with CD4 >250 cells/mm3 initiating treatment (hepatotoxicity risk)
Men with CD4 >400 cells/mm3 initiating treatment

dosing.adult: 200 mg once daily for 14-day lead-in, then 200 mg twice daily (or 400 mg XR once daily)
dosing.pediatric: 150-200 mg/m2 once daily for 14 days, then twice daily (max 200 mg/dose)
dosing.renal: No adjustment; give supplemental dose after dialysis
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh A): use with caution; Moderate-Severe: contraindicated

adverse_effects: Hepatotoxicity (can be fatal — especially first 18 weeks)
monitoring: LFTs at baseline, 2 weeks, 4 weeks, then every 3 months for first year
nevirapine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Can be used in pregnancy. Single-dose nevirapine for PMTCT now largely replaced by better regimens.
quick_flags: 14-day lead-in period is MANDATORY — do not skip (reduces rash risk)
Higher hepatotoxicity risk in women with CD4 >250 — prefer efavirenz or dolutegravir
If rash occurs during lead-in, do NOT escalate dose until rash resolves

dolutegravir.mechanism: Inhibits HIV integrase by binding to the active site, preventing strand transfer of viral DNA into host chromosomal DNA.
indications: First-line and second-line ART per WHO/NACO guidelines

dosing.adult: Treatment-naive or INSTI-naive: 50 mg once daily; INSTI-resistant: 50 mg twice daily
dosing.pediatric: Weight >=20 kg: 50 mg once daily; lower weights: weight-band dosing per guidelines
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Mild-Moderate (Child-Pugh A/B): no adjustment; Severe: no data, avoid

adverse_effects: Elevated creatinine (inhibits OCT2, not true nephrotoxicity)
monitoring: Renal function (creatinine may rise 0.1-0.15 mg/dL — not nephrotoxic)
dolutegravir.pregnancy_lactation: Now considered safe in pregnancy. Initial concerns about neural tube defects at periconception not confirmed in larger studies. Part of preferred first-line regimens including in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Now the preferred INSTI in Indian national ART guidelines (TLD regimen: TDF + 3TC + DTG)
High barrier to resistance compared to other INSTIs
Raises serum creatinine by inhibiting tubular secretion — NOT true kidney damage

raltegravir.mechanism: Inhibits HIV-1 integrase, preventing integration of proviral DNA into host genome.
indications: HIV-1 infection (in combination with other antiretrovirals)

dosing.adult: 400 mg twice daily or 1200 mg (2 x 600 mg HD tablets) once daily
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based chewable tablets; >25 kg: adult dose; lower weights per guidelines
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in mild-moderate; limited data in severe

raltegravir.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Can be used in pregnancy. Preferred alternative INSTI when dolutegravir cannot be used.
quick_flags: Low drug interaction potential — useful in complex regimens
Low barrier to resistance compared to dolutegravir
Film-coated 400 mg and HD 600 mg tablets are NOT interchangeable

darunavir.mechanism: Inhibits HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins, resulting in immature non-infectious viral particles.
indications: HIV-1 infection (boosted with ritonavir or cobicistat, in combination with other ARVs)
contraindications: Concurrent use with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 (rifampin, certain statins, ergotamine)

dosing.adult: Treatment-naive: 800 mg + ritonavir 100 mg once daily; Treatment-experienced: 600 mg + ritonavir 100 mg twice daily
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based: 20-30 kg: 450 mg + rtv 100 mg once daily; 30-40 kg: 600 mg + rtv 100 mg once daily; >40 kg: adult dose
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Mild-Moderate: no adjustment; Severe: contraindicated

darunavir.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Can be used in pregnancy with ritonavir boosting. Use twice-daily dosing in pregnancy.
quick_flags: MUST be co-administered with ritonavir or cobicistat (pharmacokinetic booster)
Contains a sulfonamide moiety — use with caution in sulfa allergy
High barrier to resistance — preferred PI for second-line ART

ritonavir.mechanism: At therapeutic doses, inhibits HIV protease. At boosting doses (100 mg), potently inhibits CYP3A4, increasing plasma levels of co-administered PIs.
indications: HIV-1 infection (as pharmacokinetic booster for other PIs)
Rarely used at full therapeutic dose alone
contraindications: Concurrent use with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 for clearance (amiodarone, flecainide, ergot alkaloids, simvastatin, lovastatin, midazolam oral, triazolam)

dosing.adult: Boosting dose: 100-200 mg once or twice daily with another PI; Full dose (rare): 600 mg twice daily
dosing.pediatric: Boosting: varies by co-administered PI and weight
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Mild-Moderate: use with caution; Severe: avoid

monitoring: Drug interaction review at each visit
ritonavir.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Can be used as booster in pregnancy. Oral solution contains alcohol — avoid in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Primarily used as a BOOSTER at 100 mg, not as an antiretroviral by itself
Most potent CYP3A4 inhibitor — review ALL drug interactions
Store tablets at room temperature; oral solution requires refrigeration

entecavir.mechanism: Guanosine nucleoside analog phosphorylated to active triphosphate, which inhibits HBV polymerase at priming, reverse transcription, and DNA-dependent DNA synthesis steps.
contraindications: Not recommended as sole HBV treatment in HIV co-infection (risk of HIV resistance)

dosing.adult: Treatment-naive: 0.5 mg once daily on empty stomach; Lamivudine-refractory: 1 mg once daily
dosing.pediatric: Age >=2 years and >=10 kg: weight-based dosing (oral solution); >=30 kg: 0.5 mg
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-49: 0.25 mg daily or 0.5 mg every 48h; CrCl 10-29: 0.15 mg daily or 0.5 mg every 72h; CrCl <10/dialysis: 0.05 mg daily or 0.5 mg every 7 days
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment (treating the hepatic disease)

monitoring: Monitor for HBV flare for months after stopping
entecavir.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if benefit outweighs risk. Tenofovir preferred in pregnancy for HBV.
quick_flags: Take on empty stomach (2 hours before or after food)
High genetic barrier to resistance in treatment-naive patients
In HIV/HBV co-infection, must use full ART backbone — entecavir alone selects for HIV resistance (M184V)

ribavirin.mechanism: Multiple mechanisms: inhibits inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (depleting GTP pools), causes lethal mutagenesis of viral RNA, and enhances host immune response.
indications: Chronic hepatitis C (in combination with interferon or DAAs)
contraindications: Pregnancy and male partners of pregnant women
Decompensated cirrhosis when used with interferon

dosing.adult: HCV: weight-based 1000-1200 mg/day in 2 divided doses (with DAAs or interferon); duration per genotype/regimen
dosing.pediatric: 15 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-50: alternating 200/400 mg daily; CrCl <30: not recommended
dosing.hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B: use with caution; Child-Pugh C with interferon: contraindicated

monitoring: CBC at weeks 2, 4, then regularly (hemoglobin drops)
ribavirin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Highly teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy and in female partners of male patients. Two forms of contraception required during and for 6 months after treatment.
quick_flags: Hemolytic anemia is expected — reduce dose if Hb <10 g/dL, stop if <8.5 g/dL
Category X: both male and female patients must use contraception
Role diminishing as interferon-free DAA regimens become standard for HCV

valganciclovir.mechanism: Prodrug of ganciclovir. After conversion, ganciclovir triphosphate competitively inhibits viral DNA polymerase and incorporates into viral DNA, causing chain termination. Preferentially active in CMV-infected cells.
indications: CMV prophylaxis in solid organ transplant recipients
contraindications: ANC <500/mm3 or platelets <25,000/mm3 or Hb <8 g/dL

dosing.adult: CMV retinitis induction: 900 mg twice daily x 21 days; Maintenance: 900 mg once daily; CMV prophylaxis (transplant): 900 mg once daily
dosing.pediatric: Transplant prophylaxis: dose = 7 x BSA x CrCl (Schwartz), once daily; max 900 mg
dosing.renal: CrCl 40-59: induction 450 mg BID, maintenance 450 mg daily; CrCl 25-39: induction 450 mg daily, maintenance 450 mg every 2 days; CrCl 10-24: induction 450 mg every 2 days, maintenance 450 mg twice weekly
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: CBC twice weekly during induction, weekly during maintenance
valganciclovir.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (animal teratogen and carcinogen). Contraindicated in pregnancy unless life-threatening. Use contraception during and for 30 days (women) or 90 days (men) after.
quick_flags: Do NOT confuse valganciclovir with valacyclovir — dosing errors are dangerous
Renal dose adjustment is critical — accumulation causes severe myelosuppression
Take with food to improve bioavailability

clarithromycin.mechanism: Binds to 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting translocation of peptidyl-tRNA and blocking bacterial protein synthesis.
indications: MAC prophylaxis and treatment in HIV
contraindications: Concurrent cisapride, pimozide, ergotamine, colchicine (in renal/hepatic impairment)
History of QT prolongation or ventricular arrhythmia
Concurrent use with statins metabolized by CYP3A4 (lovastatin, simvastatin)

dosing.adult: 250-500 mg twice daily for 7-14 days; H. pylori: 500 mg twice daily x 14 days (with PPI and amoxicillin)
dosing.pediatric: 7.5 mg/kg twice daily (max 500 mg/dose)
dosing.renal: CrCl <30: reduce dose by 50% or double the interval
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in mild-moderate if renal function is normal; severe: use with caution

monitoring: ECG if risk factors for QT prolongation
clarithromycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid in pregnancy if possible (animal teratogenicity). Azithromycin preferred if macrolide needed.
quick_flags: Potent CYP3A4 inhibitor — check ALL drug interactions
Key component of H. pylori triple therapy
QT prolongation risk — avoid in patients on other QT-prolonging drugs

ampicillin.mechanism: Binds penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), inhibiting transpeptidation step of peptidoglycan cell wall synthesis, leading to bacterial lysis.

dosing.adult: Oral: 250-500 mg every 6 hours; IV/IM: 1-2 g every 4-6 hours; Meningitis: 2 g IV every 4 hours
dosing.pediatric: Oral: 50-100 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses; IV: 100-400 mg/kg/day depending on severity
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-30: extend interval to every 6-8 hours; CrCl <10: every 12 hours
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

ampicillin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy. Widely used for GBS intrapartum prophylaxis. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for Listeria meningitis and Enterococcal endocarditis
Near-universal rash in EBV/mono — not true allergy
Take oral form on empty stomach (food reduces absorption)

benzathine_benzylpenicillin.mechanism: Slowly releases penicillin G from the injection site, providing sustained low-level bactericidal activity by inhibiting PBPs and cell wall synthesis.
contraindications: IV administration (NEVER give IV — embolic and cardiac reactions)

dosing.adult: Syphilis (primary/secondary): 2.4 million IU IM single dose; Late latent/tertiary: 2.4 million IU IM weekly x 3 weeks; Rheumatic fever prophylaxis: 1.2 million IU IM every 3-4 weeks
dosing.pediatric: Pharyngitis: <27 kg: 600,000 IU IM single dose; >=27 kg: 1.2 million IU; RF prophylaxis: 600,000-1.2 million IU every 3-4 weeks
dosing.renal: CrCl <10: increase dosing interval
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

adverse_effects: Nicolau syndrome (rare, from inadvertent intra-arterial injection)
monitoring: Observe for 30 minutes post-injection for anaphylaxis
RPR/VDRL titers for syphilis treatment response
benzathine_benzylpenicillin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Drug of choice for syphilis in pregnancy. Safe in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: NEVER give IV — only deep IM injection (gluteal)
Drug of choice for syphilis in pregnancy — no alternative acceptable
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction expected in syphilis treatment — warn patient

clofazimine.mechanism: Binds to mycobacterial DNA, inhibiting template function. Also generates reactive oxygen species and has anti-inflammatory properties that help manage ENL reactions.
contraindications: Severe GI disease (risk of crystal deposition)

dosing.adult: Leprosy MDT: 300 mg monthly (supervised) + 50 mg daily (self-administered); ENL: 100-300 mg/day for up to 12 weeks, then taper
dosing.pediatric: 1-2 mg/kg/day (adjusted by age; 10-14 yr: 50 mg daily + 150 mg monthly supervised)
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Red-brown skin discoloration (reversible over months to years)
Crystal deposition in intestinal wall (rare, can cause obstruction)
Discoloration of body fluids (tears, sweat, urine)
monitoring: Signs of intestinal obstruction with high doses
clofazimine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Used in pregnancy for leprosy if clearly indicated. Infant skin discoloration reported via breast milk.
quick_flags: Skin discoloration is expected — counsel patients thoroughly before starting
Anti-inflammatory properties make it useful for ENL — can replace steroids
Part of WHO MDT for multibacillary leprosy

streptomycin.mechanism: Binds irreversibly to 30S ribosomal subunit, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis. Bactericidal.

dosing.adult: TB: 15 mg/kg/day IM (max 1 g) daily or 3 times weekly; age >59: max 750 mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 20-40 mg/kg/day IM (max 1 g)
dosing.renal: CrCl 50-80: every 24-72h; CrCl 10-50: every 72-96h; CrCl <10: monitor levels closely
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

monitoring: Renal function (BUN, creatinine) before and during treatment
streptomycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Contraindicated in pregnancy — causes fetal ototoxicity (eighth cranial nerve damage). Avoid in breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Vestibular toxicity more common than auditory — assess balance
Only aminoglycoside used for TB in India
Reduce dose and frequency in elderly and renal impairment

diltiazem.mechanism: Inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and smooth muscle, reducing heart rate, contractility, and vascular resistance.

dosing.adult: Oral: 30-60mg TID; SR: 120-360mg/day; IV: 0.25mg/kg bolus over 2 min
dosing.pediatric: Not well established; consult specialist
dosing.renal: No significant adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; increased bioavailability in hepatic impairment

diltiazem.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta; excreted in breast milk. Avoid unless benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: Do not crush SR/XR formulations
Avoid concurrent IV beta-blockers
CYP3A4 inhibitor — check drug interactions

verapamil.mechanism: Blocks L-type calcium channels predominantly in cardiac tissue, slowing AV conduction and reducing myocardial contractility.

dosing.adult: Oral: 40-120mg TID; SR: 120-480mg/day; IV: 2.5-5mg bolus over 2 min, may repeat 5-10mg after 15 min
dosing.pediatric: IV: 0.1-0.2mg/kg (age 1-15 yrs); Oral: 4-8mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses
dosing.renal: No significant adjustment; not removed by dialysis
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50-80%; increased bioavailability

verapamil.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta; excreted in breast milk. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Most potent negative inotrope among CCBs
Constipation is common — counsel patients
Never give IV verapamil with IV beta-blocker

esmolol.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and AV conduction. Ultra-short half-life (~9 min) due to rapid ester hydrolysis by RBC esterases.
contraindications: Heart block greater than first degree
IV administration of calcium channel blockers concurrently

dosing.adult: Loading: 500mcg/kg over 1 min; Maintenance: 50-200mcg/kg/min infusion; titrate every 4 min
dosing.pediatric: 50-200mcg/kg/min IV infusion; limited data
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed — metabolized by RBC esterases

monitoring: Blood pressure every 2-5 min during titration
esmolol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if potential benefit justifies risk. Monitor neonate for bradycardia.
quick_flags: Half-life ~9 minutes — effects wear off rapidly on stopping infusion
Ideal for acute rate control when you want easy on/off
Concentrated solution (250mg/mL) must be diluted before infusion

labetalol.drug_class: Combined Alpha-1 and Non-selective Beta Blocker
labetalol.mechanism: Blocks alpha-1 (vasodilation) and beta-1/beta-2 adrenergic receptors. Alpha:beta blocking ratio is 1:3 (oral) and 1:7 (IV). Reduces SVR without reflex tachycardia.
contraindications: Heart block greater than first degree

dosing.adult: Oral: 100mg BID, max 2400mg/day; IV bolus: 20mg, then 40-80mg q10min (max 300mg); IV infusion: 2mg/min
dosing.pediatric: Oral: 1-3mg/kg/day divided BID-TID; IV: 0.2-1mg/kg/dose
dosing.renal: No major adjustment; use with caution in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; extensively hepatically metabolized

labetalol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Preferred antihypertensive in pregnancy-induced hypertension. Small amounts in breast milk.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for hypertensive emergency in pregnancy
Keep patient supine during IV administration for 3 hours
Causes false-positive urinary catecholamines

dobutamine.mechanism: Predominantly stimulates beta-1 adrenergic receptors, increasing myocardial contractility (positive inotropy) and stroke volume with mild chronotropic effect. Mild beta-2 mediated vasodilation.
indications: Low cardiac output syndromes post cardiac surgery

dosing.adult: IV infusion: 2.5-20 mcg/kg/min; titrate to hemodynamic response
dosing.pediatric: IV infusion: 2-20 mcg/kg/min; same titration principles
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

dobutamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Use only if clearly needed. Insufficient human data.
quick_flags: Does NOT require alpha-blockade first unlike dopamine at high doses
Tolerance develops after 72 hours of continuous infusion
Incompatible with alkaline solutions — do not mix with sodium bicarbonate

bupivacaine.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing depolarization and nerve impulse conduction. High potency, long duration of action.

dosing.adult: Infiltration: up to 2mg/kg (without epi) or 3mg/kg (with epi); Spinal: 7.5-20mg heavy 0.5%; Epidural: 25-150mg
dosing.pediatric: Max 2mg/kg; caudal: 1-2.5mg/kg of 0.25%
dosing.renal: Caution in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in severe hepatic disease; impaired metabolism

monitoring: Vital signs during and after administration
Signs of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
bupivacaine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Widely used for labor epidural and spinal anesthesia. Minimal excretion in breast milk.
quick_flags: NEVER use for IV regional anesthesia (Bier block) — risk of fatal cardiac arrest
Most cardiotoxic local anesthetic
Intralipid 20% is antidote for LAST
0.5% heavy (hyperbaric) for spinal anesthesia

atracurium.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. Undergoes Hofmann elimination (spontaneous degradation at physiological pH/temperature) and ester hydrolysis.
contraindications: Situations where neuromuscular blockade is not appropriate

dosing.adult: Intubation: 0.4-0.5mg/kg IV; Maintenance: 0.08-0.1mg/kg q20-45 min or infusion 5-9 mcg/kg/min
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult on mg/kg basis; infusion may need higher rates in children
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed — organ-independent elimination via Hofmann degradation
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment needed — organ-independent elimination

adverse_effects: Laudanosine accumulation in prolonged ICU use
atracurium.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal placental transfer. Use in cesarean sections is acceptable.
quick_flags: Ideal for renal/hepatic failure — Hofmann elimination is organ-independent
Store refrigerated 2-8°C
Laudanosine metabolite can lower seizure threshold in prolonged use
Reversed with neostigmine + glycopyrrolate

vecuronium.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. Aminosteroid structure — no histamine release.
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to vecuronium or bromides

dosing.adult: Intubation: 0.08-0.1mg/kg IV; Maintenance: 0.01-0.015mg/kg q12-15 min or infusion 1-2 mcg/kg/min
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult on mg/kg basis; neonates may be more sensitive
dosing.renal: Prolonged duration in renal failure; reduce dose and monitor closely
dosing.hepatic: Prolonged duration in hepatic failure; reduce dose

adverse_effects: Myopathy with prolonged ICU use (with corticosteroids)
monitoring: Renal and hepatic function for dose adjustment
vecuronium.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Small amounts cross placenta. Use if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Does NOT cause histamine release — preferred in asthmatics and hemodynamically unstable patients
Reconstitute with sterile water
Reversed by neostigmine or sugammadex
ICU myopathy risk when combined with corticosteroids

succinylcholine.mechanism: Mimics acetylcholine at nicotinic receptors causing initial depolarization (fasciculations) followed by sustained depolarization block. Rapidly hydrolyzed by plasma cholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase).
indications: Short surgical procedures requiring muscle relaxation
contraindications: Hyperkalemia or risk of hyperkalemia (burns >24hrs, denervation, prolonged immobilization)

dosing.adult: IV: 1-1.5mg/kg; IM: 3-4mg/kg (max 150mg); onset 30-60 sec IV, duration 5-10 min
dosing.pediatric: IV: 1-2mg/kg; IM: 4-5mg/kg; infants may need higher doses
dosing.renal: Avoid in hyperkalemic patients; otherwise no adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Prolonged effect if pseudocholinesterase is reduced

succinylcholine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta in small amounts. Generally safe for RSI in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Only depolarizing agent in clinical use
Dantrolene is antidote for malignant hyperthermia
Contraindicated >24hrs after burns/denervation injury due to hyperkalemia risk
Store refrigerated

halothane.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity and inhibits NMDA receptors in the CNS. Produces dose-dependent CNS depression, unconsciousness, and skeletal muscle relaxation.
contraindications: Known or suspected susceptibility to malignant hyperthermia
Unexplained fever/jaundice after prior halothane exposure

dosing.adult: Induction: 2-4% in oxygen; Maintenance: 0.5-2%; MAC 0.75%
dosing.pediatric: Induction: 1.5-3%; Maintenance: 0.5-1.5%; MAC higher in infants
dosing.renal: Use with caution; may reduce renal blood flow
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in patients with prior halothane hepatitis or hepatic dysfunction

halothane.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Relaxes uterine smooth muscle — may increase bleeding. Use with caution.
quick_flags: Largely replaced by sevoflurane/isoflurane but still on NLEM
Halothane hepatitis: do not re-expose within 3 months
Sensitizes myocardium to catecholamines — avoid adrenaline infiltration >1:100,000
Still used in pediatric inhalational induction in resource-limited settings

isoflurane.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity and inhibits excitatory synaptic transmission. Produces unconsciousness, amnesia, and muscle relaxation in a dose-dependent manner.

dosing.adult: Induction: 1.5-3% (pungent, rarely used for induction); Maintenance: 1-2.5%; MAC 1.15%
dosing.pediatric: Maintenance: 1-2.5%; MAC varies by age (higher in infants)
dosing.renal: Minimal renal concerns; low fluoride production
dosing.hepatic: Preferred over halothane in hepatic disease; minimal hepatotoxicity

isoflurane.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Used for general anesthesia in cesarean section. Causes dose-dependent uterine relaxation.
quick_flags: Best coronary vasodilator among volatile agents — may cause coronary steal (controversial)
Pungent smell makes it unsuitable for inhalational induction
Less cardiac sensitization to catecholamines than halothane

sevoflurane.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity and inhibits excitatory neurotransmission. Non-pungent, allowing smooth inhalational induction. Rapid onset and recovery due to low blood-gas solubility coefficient (0.65).
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to sevoflurane or other halogenated agents

dosing.adult: Induction: up to 8% in oxygen; Maintenance: 0.5-3%; MAC 2.0%
dosing.pediatric: Induction: up to 7-8%; Maintenance: adjusted by age; MAC higher in infants (3.3%)
dosing.renal: Compound A formed with soda lime at low flows — maintain fresh gas flow >2 L/min to minimize risk; clinical significance debated
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic disease though safer than halothane

adverse_effects: Compound A nephrotoxicity (theoretical at low flows)
sevoflurane.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Used in obstetric anesthesia. Causes dose-dependent uterine relaxation.
quick_flags: Agent of choice for pediatric inhalational induction — non-pungent, sweet smell
Fastest onset/offset among potent volatile agents
Maintain fresh gas flow >2 L/min with soda lime to minimize Compound A formation
Emergence delirium common in children — consider prophylactic measures

thiopentone.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity by prolonging chloride channel opening, producing rapid CNS depression, unconsciousness, and reduced cerebral metabolic rate.

dosing.adult: Induction: 3-5mg/kg IV slowly; Status epilepticus: loading 2-4mg/kg then infusion 3-5mg/kg/hr
dosing.pediatric: Induction: 5-6mg/kg IV; Neonates: 3-4mg/kg
dosing.renal: Reduce dose; increased sensitivity due to altered protein binding
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; hepatically metabolized; prolonged effect

thiopentone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Crosses placenta rapidly. Neonatal depression if given close to delivery.
quick_flags: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION in porphyria
Reconstitute to 2.5% solution only
Intra-arterial injection is a surgical emergency — treat with papaverine and heparin
Highly alkaline (pH 10.5) — causes tissue necrosis on extravasation

dicyclomine.mechanism: Directly relaxes smooth muscle and has antimuscarinic activity, reducing GI tract spasm and motility.

dosing.adult: Oral: 10-20mg TID-QID; IM: 10-20mg QID (use IM for 1-2 days only)
dosing.pediatric: 6 months-2 years: 5mg TID-QID; >2 years: 10mg TID
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution

dicyclomine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Excreted in breast milk — may suppress lactation. Avoid in nursing mothers.
quick_flags: Very commonly used OTC combination in India (Cyclopam)
Avoid in elderly due to anticholinergic burden
IM injection only — never give IV

hyoscine_butylbromide.mechanism: Quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic that acts on muscarinic receptors in the GI and genitourinary smooth muscle. Does not cross BBB — no central side effects.

dosing.adult: Oral: 10-20mg TID-QID; IM/IV: 20mg, may repeat after 30 min; max 100mg/day
dosing.pediatric: >6 years: 10mg TID; <6 years: not recommended
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Urinary retention risk in BPH patients
hyoscine_butylbromide.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in first trimester. Limited data. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Does NOT cross blood-brain barrier — no central anticholinergic effects
Very commonly used for abdominal colic in Indian practice
IV injection should be given slowly

mefenamic_acid.mechanism: Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. Also has prostaglandin receptor antagonist activity.

dosing.adult: 500mg loading then 250mg QID with food; max 7 days for pain
dosing.pediatric: >6 months: 6.5mg/kg TID with food
dosing.renal: Avoid if CrCl <30; reduce dose in mild-moderate impairment
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

mefenamic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category C (first/second trimester), Category D (third trimester). Avoid in third trimester — risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure. Trace amounts in breast milk.
quick_flags: Diarrhea is more common with mefenamic acid than other NSAIDs
Limit use to 7 days for acute pain
Very popular in India for dysmenorrhea (Meftal-Spas combination)
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia with prolonged use

dinoprostone.mechanism: Synthetic prostaglandin E2 that causes cervical ripening (collagen degradation and softening), uterine smooth muscle contraction, and GI smooth muscle stimulation.

dosing.adult: Cervical gel: 0.5mg intracervically, may repeat in 6 hrs (max 1.5mg/24hrs); Vaginal insert: 10mg, remove after onset of labor or 12 hrs
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No specific data; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: No specific data; use with caution

dinoprostone.pregnancy_lactation: Used specifically in pregnancy for labor induction. Contraindicated in early pregnancy unless for termination.
quick_flags: Wait at least 6 hours after gel before starting oxytocin
Patient should remain supine for 15-30 min after gel insertion
Remove vaginal insert if hyperstimulation occurs
Keep patient near delivery suite during use

methylergometrine.mechanism: Acts on alpha-adrenergic and serotonin receptors in uterine smooth muscle, causing sustained tonic uterine contraction. Also causes vasoconstriction.

dosing.adult: IM/IV: 0.2mg; may repeat q2-4hrs; Oral: 0.125-0.25mg BID-QID for up to 7 days post-delivery
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: Avoid in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Blood pressure (before and after each dose)
methylergometrine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Contraindicated before delivery. Small amounts in breast milk — may reduce milk production.
quick_flags: ALWAYS check BP before administering — contraindicated in hypertension
IV administration should be slow to avoid acute hypertension/seizures
Avoid with CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides, protease inhibitors)
First-line for PPH when oxytocin fails (but not in hypertensive patients)

mifepristone.mechanism: Competitive progesterone receptor antagonist. Causes decidual necrosis, cervical softening, increased uterine prostaglandin sensitivity, and myometrial contractions leading to detachment and expulsion of products of conception.
indications: Medical termination of pregnancy (up to 63 days with misoprostol)

dosing.adult: MTP: 200mg oral (single dose), followed by misoprostol 800mcg vaginal/sublingual 24-48 hrs later
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No specific data; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Ultrasound to confirm complete abortion (follow-up at 14 days)
Rh status and anti-D if Rh-negative
mifepristone.pregnancy_lactation: Used specifically to terminate pregnancy. Contraindicated if continuation of pregnancy is desired. Not for use during lactation.
quick_flags: Must be used under medical supervision with access to emergency care
Always combine with misoprostol for medical abortion
Rule out ectopic pregnancy before administration
Regulated under MTP Act in India — requires registered facility

clomiphene_citrate.drug_class: Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM) — Ovulation Inducer
clomiphene_citrate.mechanism: Competitive estrogen receptor antagonist at hypothalamus, blocking negative feedback and increasing GnRH, FSH, and LH release, thereby stimulating ovarian follicular development and ovulation.
contraindications: Liver disease or history of hepatic dysfunction
Abnormal uterine bleeding of undetermined origin

dosing.adult: 50mg/day for 5 days starting day 2-5 of cycle; increase to 100mg/day if no ovulation; max 150mg/day; max 6 cycles
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
