dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in hepatic disease

clomiphene_citrate.pregnancy_lactation: Category X — contraindicated in pregnancy. Discontinue if pregnancy is confirmed. Not recommended during lactation.
quick_flags: First-line for ovulation induction in PCOS
If visual symptoms occur — discontinue immediately and refer for ophthalmologic evaluation
Limit treatment to 6 cycles maximum
Anti-estrogenic effect on endometrium — thin endometrium may limit success

human_chorionic_gonadotropin.mechanism: Mimics LH action. In females, triggers ovulation from mature follicle and supports corpus luteum progesterone production. In males, stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone.
indications: Diagnostic test for Leydig cell function
contraindications: Ovarian cyst or enlargement not due to PCOS

dosing.adult: Ovulation trigger: 5000-10000 IU IM/SC when lead follicle ≥18mm; Luteal support: 1500-5000 IU q3 days; Male hypogonadism: 1000-4000 IU IM 2-3 times/week
dosing.pediatric: Cryptorchidism: 500-1000 IU IM 2-3 times/week for 3-6 weeks
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Follicular size by ultrasound before trigger
human_chorionic_gonadotropin.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Not for use in established pregnancy (hCG is an endogenous pregnancy hormone but exogenous use is not indicated).
quick_flags: Administer trigger shot only when follicle ≥18mm on ultrasound
OHSS risk increases with multiple large follicles — consider withholding trigger
Can cause false-positive pregnancy test for up to 10 days after injection

levonorgestrel.mechanism: Inhibits or delays ovulation by suppressing LH surge. Alters cervical mucus viscosity and endometrial receptivity. Does NOT disrupt an established pregnancy.

dosing.adult: Emergency contraception: 1.5mg single dose OR 0.75mg x2 doses 12 hours apart (within 72 hrs of unprotected intercourse); IUS: insert within 7 days of cycle start
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult for post-menarche adolescents for EC
dosing.renal: No adjustment needed
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic disease

monitoring: Pregnancy test if period delayed >7 days after EC
levonorgestrel.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in known pregnancy but no evidence of teratogenicity. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Efficacy of EC decreases with time — most effective within 24 hours
NOT an abortifacient — does not affect implanted embryo
Efficacy reduced if BMI >25 — consider ulipristal or copper IUD
OTC availability in India

norethisterone.mechanism: Synthetic progestogen that transforms proliferative endometrium into secretory endometrium. Suppresses gonadotropin release and inhibits ovulation at higher doses.

dosing.adult: DUB: 5mg TID for 10 days; Period postponement: 5mg TID starting 3 days before expected period; Endometriosis: 10-25mg/day continuously
dosing.pediatric: Post-menarche: same as adult dosing where indicated
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe hepatic disease

norethisterone.pregnancy_lactation: Category X — contraindicated in pregnancy (risk of virilization of female fetus). Excreted in breast milk — not recommended.
quick_flags: Very commonly prescribed in India for period postponement
Withdrawal bleeding occurs 2-7 days after stopping
Has androgenic activity — may worsen acne/hirsutism
Not a contraceptive at standard doses

medroxyprogesterone_acetate.mechanism: Synthetic progestogen that inhibits gonadotropin secretion, prevents follicular maturation and ovulation, and transforms proliferative endometrium into secretory endometrium.

dosing.adult: Contraception: 150mg IM deep every 12 weeks; DUB: 5-10mg/day for 5-10 days; Amenorrhea: 5-10mg/day for 5-10 days; Endometrial cancer: 400-1000mg/week IM
dosing.pediatric: Post-menarche adolescents: same as adult for contraception/DUB
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease

adverse_effects: Bone mineral density loss (with prolonged depot use)
Delayed return to fertility (up to 18 months after depot)
medroxyprogesterone_acetate.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding — may be started 6 weeks postpartum.
quick_flags: Depot injection: delayed return to fertility (average 10 months after last injection)
FDA black box: loss of BMD with prolonged use >2 years — limit duration unless other methods inadequate
Non-androgenic — does not worsen acne/hirsutism unlike norethisterone
Breakthrough bleeding is most common reason for discontinuation

escitalopram.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) in presynaptic neurons, increasing serotonin availability in the synaptic cleft. S-enantiomer of citalopram with higher affinity.

dosing.adult: Start 10mg/day; may increase to 20mg/day after 1 week; max 20mg/day
dosing.pediatric: >12 years: Start 10mg/day; max 20mg/day; not recommended <12 years
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate; 10mg/day max in severe impairment
dosing.hepatic: Start 5mg/day; max 10mg/day

monitoring: Suicidality (especially first 4 weeks and in <25 years)
ECG if risk factors for QT prolongation
Sodium levels (risk of SIADH, especially in elderly)
escitalopram.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Risk of neonatal withdrawal and PPHN if used in third trimester. Excreted in breast milk — weigh risk vs benefit.
quick_flags: Most selective SSRI — fewest drug interactions
Therapeutic response takes 2-4 weeks
Taper gradually to avoid discontinuation syndrome
Monitor for suicidality in young patients — FDA black box warning
QT prolongation risk at doses >20mg

clomipramine.mechanism: Potent inhibitor of serotonin reuptake (most serotonergic TCA). Also inhibits norepinephrine reuptake. Blocks muscarinic, histamine H1, and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors.

dosing.adult: Start 25mg/day; increase gradually to 100-250mg/day in divided doses; OCD may need higher doses
dosing.pediatric: >10 years (OCD): Start 25mg/day; increase over 2 weeks to 3mg/kg/day or 200mg/day (whichever is less)
dosing.renal: Use with caution; no specific guidelines
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; extensively metabolized hepatically

clomipramine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Neonatal withdrawal reported. Excreted in breast milk. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: Gold standard TCA for OCD — most serotonergic TCA
Seizure risk increases above 250mg/day
Lethal in overdose — prescribe limited quantities in suicidal patients
Taper slowly to avoid withdrawal
Avoid in elderly due to anticholinergic burden

fluphenazine.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the mesolimbic and mesocortical pathways. High potency typical antipsychotic. Decanoate form provides sustained release over weeks.

dosing.adult: Oral: 1-5mg/day in divided doses; max 20mg/day; Decanoate: 12.5-50mg IM every 2-4 weeks
dosing.pediatric: >12 years: 1-5mg/day oral; depot not recommended in children
dosing.renal: Use with caution; reduce dose
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose or avoid; hepatically metabolized

monitoring: AIMS scale for tardive dyskinesia (every 6 months)
fluphenazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Third-trimester use may cause neonatal EPS. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Depot injection (decanoate) is key advantage for non-compliant patients
Highest EPS risk among phenothiazines
Decanoate injection: use a dry syringe, give deep IM in gluteal muscle
Tardive dyskinesia risk increases with duration of use — use lowest effective dose

trihexyphenidyl.mechanism: Centrally-acting antimuscarinic that blocks excess cholinergic activity in the striatum, restoring dopamine-acetylcholine balance. Reduces tremor and rigidity in parkinsonism.

dosing.adult: Start 1mg/day; increase by 2mg every 3-5 days; usual dose 6-10mg/day in 3-4 divided doses; max 15mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Not well established; use with extreme caution
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; use with caution

trihexyphenidyl.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Avoid unless clearly needed.
quick_flags: Very commonly co-prescribed with typical antipsychotics in India for EPS prophylaxis
Has abuse potential (euphoric effect) — monitor for misuse
Avoid in elderly — high anticholinergic burden, worsens cognitive impairment
Do NOT use for tardive dyskinesia — may worsen it

levodopa_carbidopa.mechanism: Levodopa crosses BBB and is converted to dopamine by DOPA decarboxylase, replenishing striatal dopamine. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral decarboxylase, reducing peripheral side effects and increasing CNS levodopa delivery.

dosing.adult: Start with carbidopa 25mg/levodopa 100mg TID; titrate gradually every 3-7 days; optimal carbidopa dose ≥75mg/day to minimize nausea
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended in children <18 years
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

levodopa_carbidopa.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May inhibit lactation. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Gold standard for Parkinson disease motor symptoms
Honeymoon period: best response in first 2-5 years
Wearing-off and dyskinesias develop with chronic use
Protein competes with absorption — take 30 min before meals
Never stop abruptly — risk of neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome

phenobarbitone.mechanism: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity by prolonging chloride channel opening duration. At higher concentrations, directly activates GABA-A receptors and blocks AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors.

dosing.adult: Oral: 60-180mg/day at bedtime; SE loading: 15-20mg/kg IV at max 60mg/min; Therapeutic level: 15-40 mcg/mL
dosing.pediatric: 3-5mg/kg/day in 1-2 doses; Neonatal seizures: load 20mg/kg IV; Febrile seizure prophylaxis: 3-5mg/kg/day
dosing.renal: Reduce dose; 20-50% is renally excreted unchanged
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose and monitor; hepatically metabolized

phenobarbitone.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Risk of congenital malformations and neonatal hemorrhage (vitamin K deficiency). Give vitamin K to mother in last month and neonate at birth. Excreted in breast milk — sedation in infant.
quick_flags: Most widely used antiepileptic in resource-limited settings due to low cost
Potent CYP450 inducer — numerous drug interactions
Supplement folic acid and vitamin D with chronic use
Never stop abruptly — risk of status epilepticus
Controlled substance — dependence and abuse potential

diethylcarbamazine.mechanism: Immobilizes microfilariae by altering their surface membrane, making them susceptible to host immune destruction. Also has some macrofilaricidal activity. Exact mechanism not fully elucidated.
indications: Mass drug administration for filariasis elimination
contraindications: Onchocerciasis (risk of Mazzotti reaction and blindness)
During acute inflammatory episodes of filariasis

dosing.adult: Filariasis: 6mg/kg/day in 3 divided doses for 12 days; MDA: single dose 6mg/kg annually (with albendazole ± ivermectin)
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing as adults; >2 years
dosing.renal: Reduce dose proportionally in renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Encephalopathy in heavy Loa loa infection
monitoring: Microfilarial count (night blood smear for W. bancrofti)
Watch for adverse reactions in first 24-48 hours
diethylcarbamazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Avoid in pregnancy unless essential. Safety in lactation not established.
quick_flags: Part of India's Mass Drug Administration (MDA) program for filariasis elimination
ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in onchocerciasis — can cause blindness
Give after food to reduce GI side effects
Reactions are often due to dying parasites, not drug toxicity

praziquantel.mechanism: Increases permeability of cell membrane of susceptible worms to calcium, causing intense muscular contraction, paralysis, and tegumental damage, leading to host immune attack.

dosing.adult: Schistosomiasis: 40-60mg/kg/day divided in 2-3 doses for 1 day; Neurocysticercosis: 50mg/kg/day in 3 doses for 14 days (with dexamethasone); Tapeworms: 5-25mg/kg single dose
dosing.pediatric: >4 years: same as adult dosing on mg/kg basis
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Caution in hepatosplenic schistosomiasis; higher drug levels expected

monitoring: Stool/urine examination for cure (4-6 weeks post-treatment)
Signs of inflammatory reaction (headache, seizures) in NCC
praziquantel.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. WHO allows use in pregnancy for schistosomiasis in endemic areas. Excreted in breast milk — withhold breastfeeding for 72 hours.
quick_flags: Drug of choice for all schistosome species
In neurocysticercosis: always co-administer corticosteroids to prevent inflammatory edema
Avoid grapefruit juice (increases levels)
Rifampicin dramatically reduces praziquantel levels — avoid combination

miltefosine.mechanism: Alkylphosphocholine analog that disrupts lipid-dependent cell signaling, inhibits phospholipid metabolism, and induces apoptosis-like cell death in Leishmania parasites.
contraindications: Women of childbearing potential without contraception

dosing.adult: 2.5mg/kg/day (max 150mg/day) for 28 days; take with food
dosing.pediatric: >2 years and >12 kg: 2.5mg/kg/day for 28 days
dosing.renal: Avoid in severe renal impairment; no specific adjustment data
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment

miltefosine.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy — teratogenic. Effective contraception mandatory during treatment and for 5 months after.
quick_flags: First effective oral drug for visceral leishmaniasis
MUST ensure contraception in women of childbearing age — teratogenic
GI side effects can be reduced by taking with fatty food
Part of India's Kala-azar Elimination Programme

paromomycin.mechanism: Binds to 30S ribosomal subunit of susceptible organisms, inhibiting protein synthesis. Poorly absorbed from GI tract — acts locally in intestinal lumen for intestinal parasites.
indications: Hepatic encephalopathy (oral, to reduce ammonia-producing bacteria)

dosing.adult: Visceral leishmaniasis: 11mg/kg/day IM for 21 days; Intestinal amebiasis: 25-35mg/kg/day oral in 3 divided doses for 7 days; Hepatic encephalopathy: 4g/day oral in divided doses
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing as adults
dosing.renal: Avoid IM form in severe renal impairment; nephrotoxic like other aminoglycosides
dosing.hepatic: Oral form used in hepatic encephalopathy; no dose adjustment needed

paromomycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Oral form poorly absorbed — low systemic risk. IM form: use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Oral form is NOT absorbed — acts only in GI lumen
IM form for visceral leishmaniasis is part of India's elimination program
Monitor renal function closely with IM use — aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity applies
Useful luminal amebicide after metronidazole treatment to eliminate cysts

dapsone.mechanism: Inhibits dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis in susceptible organisms. Also has anti-inflammatory properties by inhibiting neutrophil myeloperoxidase and chemotaxis.
indications: Leprosy (multidrug therapy with rifampicin and clofazimine)

dosing.adult: Leprosy (MB): 100mg/day for 12 months (WHO MDT); Dermatitis herpetiformis: 50-300mg/day; PCP prophylaxis: 100mg/day
dosing.pediatric: Leprosy: 1-2mg/kg/day; PCP prophylaxis: 2mg/kg/day (max 100mg)
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in hepatic impairment; hepatically metabolized

adverse_effects: Dapsone hypersensitivity syndrome (fever, rash, hepatitis — 5-6 weeks after starting)
monitoring: CBC with reticulocyte count (baseline, weekly x4, then monthly)
dapsone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Used in leprosy during pregnancy (benefits outweigh risks). Excreted in breast milk — monitor infant for hemolysis.
quick_flags: Always check G6PD status before starting — severe hemolysis in G6PD deficiency
Dapsone syndrome: fever + rash + hepatitis at 5-6 weeks — can be fatal
Part of WHO Multi-Drug Therapy (MDT) for leprosy
Cimetidine can reduce methemoglobinemia if needed

activated_charcoal.mechanism: Highly porous carbon with large surface area that adsorbs toxins and drugs in the GI tract, preventing systemic absorption. Does not get absorbed itself.
indications: Multi-dose activated charcoal for sustained-release preparations
Enhances elimination of theophylline, carbamazepine, dapsone, phenobarbital
contraindications: Substances not adsorbed: iron, lithium, potassium, alcohols, cyanide, hydrocarbons

dosing.adult: Single dose: 50-100g (or 1g/kg) orally or via NG tube; Multi-dose: 25-50g every 2-4 hours
dosing.pediatric: 1-2g/kg (children 1-12 years: 25-50g; infants: 10-25g)
dosing.renal: Not applicable — not absorbed systemically
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable — not absorbed systemically

monitoring: Airway protection (ensure gag reflex intact or intubated)
activated_charcoal.pregnancy_lactation: Safe in pregnancy for acute poisoning — not systemically absorbed.
quick_flags: Most effective within 1 hour of ingestion
Does NOT adsorb: iron, lithium, alcohols, potassium, cyanide, strong acids/alkalis
Protect airway before administration — aspiration is the main risk
Mix with water, not milk or ice cream

desferrioxamine.mechanism: Binds free iron (Fe3+) to form ferrioxamine complex which is water-soluble and excreted renally. Chelates iron from ferritin and hemosiderin but not from hemoglobin or cytochrome enzymes.
indications: Chronic iron overload (transfusion-dependent thalassemia, sickle cell disease)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (relative — ferrioxamine is renally excreted)

dosing.adult: Acute iron poisoning: 15mg/kg/hr IV infusion (max 80mg/kg/24hrs); Chronic overload: 20-60mg/kg/day SC infusion over 8-12 hours, 5-7 days/week
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing; SC infusion preferred for chronic use
dosing.renal: Use with caution; consider dose reduction; monitor closely
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Serum ferritin (target <1000 ng/mL in chronic overload)
Urine color (vin rosé = ongoing iron chelation)
desferrioxamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Teratogenic in animals. Use only if life-threatening iron poisoning.
quick_flags: Vin rosé (orange-red) colored urine indicates active iron chelation
IV infusion rate must not exceed 15mg/kg/hr — risk of hypotension
Increases susceptibility to Yersinia enterocolitica and Mucormycosis
Annual eye and hearing tests mandatory in chronic use

sodium_nitrite.mechanism: Converts hemoglobin to methemoglobin, which has high affinity for cyanide ions. Cyanide binds methemoglobin to form cyanmethemoglobin, freeing cytochrome oxidase to resume aerobic metabolism.
indications: Cyanide poisoning (part of cyanide antidote kit)
contraindications: Smoke inhalation with concurrent CO poisoning (relative — methemoglobin further reduces O2 carrying capacity)
G6PD deficiency (increased risk of severe methemoglobinemia)

dosing.adult: 300mg (10mL of 3%) IV over 2-4 minutes; followed by sodium thiosulphate
dosing.pediatric: 6-8mg/kg IV (max 300mg) over 2-4 minutes; based on hemoglobin level
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment in acute use
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment in acute use

sodium_nitrite.pregnancy_lactation: No category assigned. Use in life-threatening cyanide poisoning regardless of pregnancy status.
quick_flags: Part of classic cyanide antidote kit: amyl nitrite → sodium nitrite → sodium thiosulphate
Avoid or use cautiously in smoke inhalation (may have concurrent CO poisoning)
Pulse oximetry is UNRELIABLE with methemoglobinemia — use co-oximetry
Hydroxocobalamin is safer alternative if available

sodium_thiosulphate.mechanism: Acts as sulfur donor for rhodanese (thiosulfate sulfurtransferase) enzyme, which converts cyanide to relatively non-toxic thiocyanate that is renally excreted.
indications: Cyanide poisoning (with or after sodium nitrite)
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in cyanide poisoning

dosing.adult: 12.5g IV (50mL of 25%) over 10-20 minutes; may repeat at half dose if symptoms recur
dosing.pediatric: 400mg/kg IV (max 12.5g) over 10-20 minutes
dosing.renal: Thiocyanate accumulates in renal failure — monitor levels
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

adverse_effects: Thiocyanate toxicity with repeated doses (confusion, psychosis, tinnitus)
monitoring: Clinical response (resolution of lactic acidosis, improved mental status)
Thiocyanate levels if repeated dosing (toxic >60 mcg/mL)
sodium_thiosulphate.pregnancy_lactation: No category assigned. Use in life-threatening cyanide poisoning regardless of pregnancy status.
quick_flags: Safer than sodium nitrite — can be used alone if cyanide poisoning suspected but not confirmed
Give AFTER sodium nitrite in classic cyanide antidote protocol
Slow onset — rhodanese enzyme works gradually
Thiocyanate is dialyzable if toxicity develops

diphtheria_antitoxin.mechanism: Equine-derived antibodies that neutralize circulating (unbound) diphtheria exotoxin. Cannot neutralize toxin already bound to tissues. Effectiveness decreases with delay in administration.
indications: Suspected diphtheria (do not wait for culture confirmation)
contraindications: Known anaphylaxis to equine serum (relative — desensitization may be attempted)
No absolute contraindication when diphtheria is strongly suspected

dosing.adult: Pharyngeal/laryngeal (<48hrs): 20,000-40,000 IU IV/IM; Nasopharyngeal: 40,000-60,000 IU; Extensive/late (>3 days): 80,000-120,000 IU; IV preferred for severe cases
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult — dose is based on severity, not body weight
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Skin test/conjunctival test before administration (sensitivity to equine serum)
Watch for anaphylaxis for 1 hour post-administration
Monitor for serum sickness for 2 weeks
diphtheria_antitoxin.pregnancy_lactation: Use in life-threatening diphtheria regardless of pregnancy status. Benefits outweigh risks.
quick_flags: DO NOT wait for culture results — administer on clinical suspicion
Always do sensitivity test before administration
Dose depends on disease severity and duration, NOT body weight
Keep adrenaline and resuscitation equipment ready
Only neutralizes FREE circulating toxin — give as early as possible

forms: Injection lyophilized polyvalent (10mL reconstituted, covers Big 4 Indian snakes)
snake_venom_antiserum.mechanism: Equine F(ab')2 or whole IgG antibodies that neutralize venom toxins (hemotoxins, neurotoxins, myotoxins) of the four major Indian venomous snakes: Cobra, Krait, Russell Viper, and Saw-scaled Viper.
contraindications: No absolute contraindication in confirmed/suspected envenomation — benefits outweigh risks

dosing.adult: Initial: 8-10 vials (80-100mL) IV infusion over 1 hour; repeat 6 hourly based on 20-min WBCT and clinical response; no maximum dose
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult — venom dose is independent of body size
dosing.renal: No adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment

snake_venom_antiserum.pregnancy_lactation: Use without hesitation in envenomation during pregnancy. Untreated snakebite is far more dangerous.
quick_flags: Dose is same for children and adults — based on venom load, not body weight
Pre-medicate with low-dose adrenaline SC to reduce reactions (WHO recommendation)
20-min WBCT is the bedside test to guide repeat dosing
Indian polyvalent ASV covers only Big 4 snakes
Keep adrenaline ready for anaphylaxis management

cycloserine.mechanism: Structural analog of D-alanine that inhibits D-alanine racemase and D-alanine:D-alanine ligase, blocking mycobacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis.
indications: Urinary tract infections (rarely, when other agents fail)

dosing.adult: 250mg BID for first 2 weeks, then 250mg TID or 500mg BID; max 1g/day; target trough level 20-35 mcg/mL
dosing.pediatric: 10-20mg/kg/day (max 1g/day) in 2 divided doses
dosing.renal: Reduce dose; CrCl 10-50: 250mg q12-24h; CrCl <10: 250mg q24h; monitor levels
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; not hepatically metabolized

cycloserine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only for MDR-TB when no safer alternative. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: ALWAYS co-prescribe pyridoxine (B6) 50mg for every 250mg of cycloserine to prevent neurotoxicity
High incidence of CNS side effects — warn patients and families
Monitor for suicidal ideation
Therapeutic drug monitoring recommended

ethionamide.mechanism: Prodrug activated by EthA monooxygenase in mycobacteria. Active metabolite inhibits InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase), blocking mycolic acid synthesis in the mycobacterial cell wall. Structurally related to isoniazid.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to ethionamide or related compounds

dosing.adult: Start 250mg/day; increase gradually to 500-750mg/day in 1-3 divided doses; max 1g/day; take with food
dosing.pediatric: 15-20mg/kg/day (max 1g/day) in 2-3 divided doses
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment for mild-moderate; avoid in severe impairment if possible
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; hepatotoxic

adverse_effects: GI intolerance (nausea, vomiting, metallic taste — very common)
ethionamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Teratogenic in animals. Avoid in pregnancy if possible. Use only for life-threatening MDR-TB.
quick_flags: GI intolerance is the most common reason for discontinuation — start low, give with food, use antiemetics
Cross-resistance with isoniazid possible (shared InhA target)
Monitor thyroid function — hypothyroidism common especially when combined with PAS
Co-administer pyridoxine to prevent neuropathy

bedaquiline.mechanism: Inhibits mycobacterial ATP synthase (proton pump of ATP synthase subunit c), blocking energy production essential for both replicating and dormant M. tuberculosis.
contraindications: Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin)

dosing.adult: Weeks 1-2: 400mg once daily; Weeks 3-24: 200mg three times per week (at least 48 hours between doses); take with food
dosing.pediatric: Not approved for <5 years; 5-18 years: weight-based dosing under specialist guidance
dosing.renal: No adjustment for mild-moderate; limited data in severe impairment
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment; Severe: avoid

monitoring: ECG at baseline, 2 weeks, monthly, and as needed (QTc)
bedaquiline.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Limited human data. Use only if MDR-TB requires it and no safer alternative exists.
quick_flags: Game-changer for MDR-TB treatment — first new TB drug class in 40+ years
Very long half-life (~5.5 months) — effects persist long after stopping
QT monitoring essential — avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs (fluoroquinolones, clofazimine)
Part of India's PMDT (Programmatic Management of Drug-resistant TB) program
Take with food — increases absorption 2-fold

tropicamide.mechanism: Blocks muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle, causing pupillary dilation (mydriasis) and paralysis of accommodation (cycloplegia). Short duration of action.

dosing.adult: 1-2 drops of 0.5% or 1% in each eye; repeat in 5 min if needed; effect in 15-30 min; lasts 4-6 hours
dosing.pediatric: 0.5% preferred in infants; 1-2 drops; same frequency
dosing.renal: Not applicable — topical use
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable — topical use

adverse_effects: Systemic effects rare (tachycardia, dry mouth, flushing in children)
tropicamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption. Use if needed for eye examination.
quick_flags: Shortest-acting mydriatic — preferred for routine fundoscopy
Weak cycloplegic — atropine preferred for definitive cycloplegic refraction in children
Warn patients about temporary blurred vision and light sensitivity
Apply pressure on lacrimal sac after instillation to minimize systemic absorption in children

homatropine.mechanism: Antimuscarinic agent that blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary body, producing mydriasis and cycloplegia of intermediate duration.

dosing.adult: Refraction: 1-2 drops, repeat in 5-10 min; Uveitis: 1-2 drops BID-TID; Effect onset: 30-60 min; Duration: 24-72 hours
dosing.pediatric: 1 drop of 2%, repeat in 10 min if needed; recovery faster in children
dosing.renal: Not applicable — topical use
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable — topical use

monitoring: Anterior chamber depth assessment before use
homatropine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption with topical use. Use if needed.
quick_flags: Intermediate duration between tropicamide (4-6 hrs) and atropine (7-14 days)
Better cycloplegic than tropicamide — useful for refraction in older children/adults
Recovery in 24-72 hours vs 7-14 days for atropine
Useful in uveitis when long atropine effect is undesirable

proparacaine.mechanism: Ester-type local anesthetic that blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in corneal nerve endings, preventing pain signal transmission. Rapid onset, short duration.

dosing.adult: 1-2 drops in affected eye; onset 15-20 seconds; duration 15-20 minutes; repeat if needed for procedure
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult
dosing.renal: Not applicable — topical use
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable — topical use

adverse_effects: Transient stinging (less than most other topical anesthetics)
Corneal epithelial toxicity with repeated use
monitoring: Signs of corneal toxicity if repeated use
proparacaine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption. Safe for ophthalmic procedures in pregnancy.
quick_flags: Least stinging topical ocular anesthetic — best patient comfort
NEVER prescribe for patient self-use at home — risk of corneal melting with repeated use
Onset in 15-20 seconds — fastest ocular anesthetic
Store refrigerated for stability and added comfort on instillation

acetazolamide.mechanism: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase enzyme in renal tubular epithelium, ciliary body, and CNS. In kidney, reduces bicarbonate reabsorption causing alkaline diuresis and metabolic acidosis. In eye, reduces aqueous humor production.
contraindications: Severe hepatic cirrhosis (risk of hepatic encephalopathy)

dosing.adult: Glaucoma: 250mg QID or 500mg SR BID; Altitude sickness: 125-250mg BID starting 1 day before ascent; IIH: 250mg BID, titrate up to 1-2g/day; Epilepsy: 250-1000mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 5mg/kg/dose BID-TID; Altitude sickness: 2.5mg/kg BID
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-50: q12h; CrCl <10: avoid; ineffective in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in severe cirrhosis — can precipitate hepatic encephalopathy

acetazolamide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Teratogenic in animals (limb defects). Avoid in first trimester. Use only if clearly needed.
quick_flags: Paresthesias and taste alteration are common and expected — reassure patients
Sulfonamide derivative — ask about sulfa allergy
Makes carbonated drinks taste flat — useful counseling point
Tolerance develops in 2-3 days for diuretic effect but NOT for IOP-lowering or altitude effect

mannitol.mechanism: Non-reabsorbable sugar alcohol that creates an osmotic gradient, drawing water from intracellular and interstitial spaces into the intravascular compartment. In the kidney, it inhibits water reabsorption in the proximal tubule and loop of Henle.
indications: Prevention of acute renal failure (oliguric phase)
contraindications: Anuria due to severe renal disease

dosing.adult: Cerebral edema: 0.25-1g/kg IV over 15-30 min; may repeat q4-6h; max 2g/kg/day; Glaucoma: 1-2g/kg IV over 30-60 min; Renal protection: 12.5-25g IV test dose
dosing.pediatric: 0.25-1g/kg IV over 15-30 minutes
dosing.renal: Contraindicated in anuria; test dose recommended — if no diuresis in 2-3 hours, do not continue
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

adverse_effects: Acute kidney injury with high cumulative doses
mannitol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use if clearly needed. No human studies.
quick_flags: Use in-line filter — mannitol crystals can form, especially at low temperatures
Warm the solution if crystals are visible before use
Serum osmolality gap >55 mOsm/kg — discontinue mannitol (risk of renal toxicity)
Rebound rise in ICP can occur after effect wears off
Check for crystallization before infusing

d_penicillamine.mechanism: Chelates copper, lead, mercury, and other heavy metals, promoting urinary excretion. In rheumatoid arthritis, modulates immune response by reducing T-cell function and rheumatoid factor. In cystinuria, forms soluble disulfide with cysteine.
contraindications: Penicillin allergy (relative — not chemically related but cross-sensitivity reported)
History of penicillamine-induced aplastic anemia or agranulocytosis
Concurrent gold therapy or cytotoxic agents

dosing.adult: Wilson disease: Start 250mg/day, increase gradually to 1-2g/day in 4 divided doses on empty stomach; RA: Start 125-250mg/day, increase by 125mg every 1-3 months; max 1.5g/day; Cystinuria: 1-4g/day
dosing.pediatric: Wilson disease: 20mg/kg/day in 2-4 divided doses; max 1g/day
dosing.renal: Reduce dose; monitor closely; avoid if CrCl very low
dosing.hepatic: Initial worsening of hepatic function possible in Wilson disease — continue under specialist supervision

adverse_effects: Bone marrow suppression (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, aplastic anemia)
monitoring: CBC with differential and platelets every 2 weeks for 6 months, then monthly
Urinalysis for proteinuria every 2 weeks initially, then monthly
d_penicillamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic — causes cutis laxa and connective tissue defects. Contraindicated in pregnancy for RA/cystinuria. In Wilson disease pregnancy, reduce dose to minimum (250-500mg/day).
quick_flags: Take on empty stomach (1 hour before or 2 hours after food)
Supplement pyridoxine 25mg/day — penicillamine is a B6 antagonist
Monitor closely for proteinuria — stop if nephrotic syndrome develops
Worsening of Wilson disease hepatic symptoms may occur initially — do not stop
Numerous autoimmune complications — a challenging drug to manage

ormeloxifene.drug_class: Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM) — Non-steroidal Oral Contraceptive
ormeloxifene.mechanism: Non-steroidal SERM that has anti-estrogenic effect on the uterus (prevents endometrial proliferation and implantation) while having weak estrogenic effects on other tissues. Accelerates tubal transport of ovum.

dosing.adult: Contraception: 30mg twice weekly for first 12 weeks, then 30mg once weekly thereafter; start on day 1-2 of menstrual cycle; DUB: same schedule
dosing.pediatric: Not applicable
dosing.renal: No specific data; use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic disease

monitoring: Pregnancy test if amenorrhea >2 weeks beyond expected
ormeloxifene.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy. Not recommended during lactation. Exclude pregnancy before starting.
quick_flags: Only non-steroidal, non-hormonal oral contraceptive in the world
Developed in India by CDRI, Lucknow
Once-weekly dosing after initial loading — excellent compliance
No hormonal side effects (no thromboembolic risk, no metabolic effects)
Available free under India's National Family Planning Programme

alprostadil.mechanism: Prostaglandin E1 analog. Relaxes smooth muscle of ductus arteriosus (maintaining patency in neonates). Relaxes trabecular and arterial smooth muscle in corpus cavernosum (erectile function). Also inhibits platelet aggregation and vasodilates.
indications: Patent ductus arteriosus maintenance in neonates (duct-dependent congenital heart disease)
contraindications: Conditions predisposing to priapism (sickle cell, myeloma, leukemia)
Men for whom sexual activity is inadvisable

dosing.adult: Erectile dysfunction: Start 2.5mcg intracavernosal, titrate by 2.5-5mcg increments; max 60mcg; Peripheral arterial disease: 40-60mcg IV over 2 hours daily
dosing.pediatric: Ductus arteriosus: Start 0.05-0.1mcg/kg/min IV infusion; reduce to 0.01-0.05mcg/kg/min once response achieved
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment for intracavernosal use; caution with IV use
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

adverse_effects: Neonates: apnea (10-12%), fever, flushing, hypotension, bradycardia, seizures
Erectile use: penile pain, priapism, fibrosis at injection site, hematoma
monitoring: Neonates: continuous respiratory and cardiac monitoring, temperature, blood gases, blood pressure
Erectile use: duration of erection (seek help if >4 hours — priapism)
Peripheral use: limb perfusion, blood pressure
alprostadil.pregnancy_lactation: Category X for female use. Used in neonates (not a pregnancy concern per se).
quick_flags: In neonates: apnea occurs in 10-12% — have ventilatory support ready
For erectile dysfunction: first intracavernosal dose must be given in clinic under supervision
PRIAPISM: if erection >4 hours, aspirate blood and inject phenylephrine intracavernosally
For ductus arteriosus: use lowest effective dose and taper once intervention is planned

cefadroxil.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), disrupting peptidoglycan cross-linking.

dosing.adult: 500mg to 1g PO every 12 hours
dosing.pediatric: 30mg/kg/day in divided doses every 12 hours
dosing.renal: Increase interval to every 24-36 hours if CrCl <25 mL/min
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

cefadroxil.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Excreted in breast milk in small amounts; use with caution during lactation.
quick_flags: Longer half-life allows twice-daily dosing — advantage over cephalexin
Good oral bioavailability regardless of food intake

cefazolin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to PBPs; bactericidal against susceptible gram-positive organisms and some gram-negatives.

dosing.adult: 1-2g IV/IM every 8 hours; surgical prophylaxis: 1-2g IV 30-60 min before incision
dosing.pediatric: 25-100mg/kg/day IV/IM divided every 6-8 hours
dosing.renal: CrCl 10-30: every 12 hours; CrCl <10: every 24 hours
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

cefazolin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Excreted in low concentrations in breast milk; generally considered compatible.
quick_flags: Gold standard for surgical prophylaxis — most procedures
Preferred parenteral 1st-gen cephalosporin for MSSA bacteremia step-down is NOT cefazolin (it's oral agents); cefazolin IS first-line parenteral for MSSA

cefotaxime.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. Broad-spectrum with enhanced gram-negative coverage and good CNS penetration.

dosing.adult: 1-2g IV/IM every 6-8 hours; meningitis: 2g IV every 4-6 hours
dosing.pediatric: 50-200mg/kg/day IV divided every 6-8 hours; neonatal meningitis: 50mg/kg every 6-8 hours
dosing.renal: CrCl <20: reduce dose by 50%
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment in mild-moderate impairment

cefotaxime.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Excreted in breast milk in low concentrations; use with caution.
quick_flags: Preferred over ceftriaxone in neonates — no bilirubin displacement risk
Active metabolite desacetylcefotaxime has synergistic antibacterial activity

ceftazidime.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. Distinguished by potent anti-pseudomonal activity among third-generation cephalosporins.

dosing.adult: 1-2g IV/IM every 8 hours; severe infections/Pseudomonas: 2g IV every 8 hours
dosing.pediatric: 30-50mg/kg IV every 8 hours (max 6g/day)
dosing.renal: CrCl 16-30: 1g every 24h; CrCl 6-15: 500mg every 24h; CrCl <5: 500mg every 48h
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

ceftazidime.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Excreted in breast milk in low concentrations; use with caution.
quick_flags: The only 3rd-gen cephalosporin with reliable Pseudomonas coverage
Weak gram-positive activity compared to cefotaxime/ceftriaxone

phenoxymethylpenicillin.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to PBPs. Acid-stable oral penicillin with narrow spectrum targeting streptococci and oral anaerobes.

dosing.adult: 250-500mg PO every 6 hours; rheumatic fever prophylaxis: 250mg PO twice daily
dosing.pediatric: 25-50mg/kg/day PO divided every 6-8 hours
dosing.renal: CrCl <10: reduce dose or extend interval
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

quick_flags: Oral alternative to benzathine penicillin for rheumatic fever prophylaxis
Best absorbed on empty stomach — 1 hour before or 2 hours after food

procaine_benzylpenicillin.mechanism: Depot intramuscular formulation of penicillin G that provides sustained low-level bactericidal concentrations by slow release from the procaine salt.

dosing.adult: 600,000-1.2 million units IM once daily
dosing.pediatric: 25,000-50,000 units/kg IM once daily
dosing.renal: Reduce dose with severe renal impairment (CrCl <10)
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

procaine_benzylpenicillin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Safe in pregnancy; used for congenital syphilis prevention.
quick_flags: NEVER give IV — IM only; can cause fatal emboli if given IV
Hoigne syndrome is a procaine toxicity reaction, not true allergy

fusidic_acid.mechanism: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by blocking elongation factor G (EF-G), preventing translocation on the ribosome. Bacteriostatic.
indications: Bone and joint infections (systemic, in combination)

dosing.adult: Topical: apply thin layer 2-3 times daily; Oral: 500mg every 8 hours
dosing.pediatric: Topical: same as adult; Oral: 20mg/kg/day divided every 8 hours
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; monitor LFTs with systemic use

fusidic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Not formally categorized. Topical use considered low risk. Systemic use — limited data; use only if benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: Topical monotherapy limit to 2 weeks to reduce resistance
Never use as monotherapy systemically — rapid resistance emerges; combine with rifampicin for MRSA

natamycin.mechanism: Binds to ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, causing membrane disruption and cell death. Fungicidal.

dosing.adult: 1 drop in affected eye every 1-2 hours initially, then taper to every 3-4 hours; continue for 14-21 days
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult dosing
dosing.renal: Not applicable (topical ophthalmic)
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable (topical ophthalmic)

natamycin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption from ophthalmic use; likely safe.
quick_flags: First-line for fungal keratitis — especially Fusarium species
Shake suspension well before use; poor corneal stromal penetration — may need intrastromal injection for deep keratitis

nystatin.mechanism: Binds to ergosterol in fungal cell membranes causing pore formation, leakage of intracellular contents, and cell death.

dosing.adult: Oral suspension: 400,000-600,000 units swish-and-swallow QID; Vaginal: 1 tablet daily for 14 days
dosing.pediatric: Neonates: 100,000 units to each side of mouth QID; Children: 250,000-500,000 units QID
dosing.renal: No adjustment (not systemically absorbed)
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment (not systemically absorbed)

nystatin.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (oral), Category C (vaginal). Not absorbed systemically; safe in pregnancy and lactation for oral/topical use.
quick_flags: Not absorbed from GI tract — effective only for local (oral/GI/topical) candidiasis
Swish-and-swallow technique: hold in mouth for several minutes before swallowing

betamethasone.mechanism: Synthetic glucocorticoid that suppresses inflammation and immune responses by inhibiting phospholipase A2, NF-kB pathway, and multiple inflammatory cytokines.
indications: Antenatal corticosteroid for fetal lung maturity

dosing.adult: 0.5-9mg/day PO depending on condition; Antenatal: 12mg IM x 2 doses, 24 hours apart
dosing.pediatric: 0.0175-0.25mg/kg/day PO divided every 6-8 hours
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; may require dose adjustment in severe hepatic impairment

betamethasone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Used specifically at 24-34 weeks gestation for fetal lung maturity (well-established benefit). Crosses placenta. Excreted in breast milk.
quick_flags: Antenatal steroids: 2 doses of 12mg IM 24h apart between 24-34 weeks — reduces RDS, IVH, and neonatal mortality
25x more potent than hydrocortisone with minimal mineralocorticoid activity

betamethasone_valerate.mechanism: Topical glucocorticoid that suppresses cutaneous inflammation by inhibiting phospholipase A2 and inflammatory mediator release; reduces edema, erythema, and pruritus.
contraindications: Use on face/flexures for prolonged periods

dosing.adult: Apply thin layer to affected area 1-2 times daily; max 2-4 weeks continuous use
dosing.pediatric: Use sparingly for shortest duration; avoid prolonged use especially under occlusion
dosing.renal: Not applicable (topical)
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable (topical)

monitoring: Signs of skin atrophy or striae
Adrenal function if large areas or prolonged use
betamethasone_valerate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Avoid prolonged use on large areas. Do not apply to breasts before breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Potent steroid — avoid on face, groin, axillae for more than a few days
Fingertip unit (FTU) rule: 1 FTU covers an area roughly equal to 2 adult palms

fludrocortisone.mechanism: Synthetic corticosteroid with potent mineralocorticoid activity; promotes sodium retention and potassium excretion in renal distal tubules, expanding plasma volume.
indications: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) — mineralocorticoid replacement

dosing.adult: 0.05-0.2mg PO once daily; orthostatic hypotension: 0.1-0.3mg/day
dosing.pediatric: 0.05-0.1mg PO once daily
dosing.renal: Use with caution; monitor electrolytes closely
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; monitor clinically

fludrocortisone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed. Monitor neonatal adrenal function.
quick_flags: Plasma renin activity is the best guide for dose titration in adrenal insufficiency
Even 0.1mg has significant mineralocorticoid effect — 125x the mineralocorticoid potency of hydrocortisone

benzoyl_peroxide.mechanism: Generates free oxygen radicals that oxidize bacterial proteins in Cutibacterium acnes; also provides mild keratolytic and comedolytic effects.
indications: Acne — adjunct to retinoids or antibiotics to prevent resistance

dosing.adult: Apply thin layer to affected area once or twice daily; start with 2.5% to assess tolerance
dosing.pediatric: Children >12 years: same as adult
dosing.renal: Not applicable (topical)
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable (topical)

benzoyl_peroxide.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Minimal systemic absorption; generally considered safe topically. Avoid applying to breast area during lactation.
quick_flags: Key advantage: bacteria do NOT develop resistance to benzoyl peroxide — always combine with topical antibiotics (clindamycin) to prevent antibiotic resistance
Warn patients it bleaches fabrics and hair

silver_sulphadiazine.mechanism: Silver ions disrupt bacterial DNA and cell membranes; sulphadiazine inhibits dihydropteroate synthase in folate synthesis pathway. Broad-spectrum bactericidal against gram-positive, gram-negative, and yeast.

dosing.adult: Apply 1-2mm thick layer to burn/wound 1-2 times daily after wound cleaning
dosing.pediatric: Same as adult; contraindicated in neonates <2 months
dosing.renal: Use with caution in renal impairment; monitor sulfonamide levels with large surface area application
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Interstitial nephritis (rare with large area use)
monitoring: Serum sulfonamide levels with large surface area
silver_sulphadiazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B but contraindicated near term — risk of kernicterus in neonate from sulfonamide component.
quick_flags: Transient leukopenia is common (up to 20%) and self-resolving — do not discontinue unless sustained
Remove old cream and reapply fresh layer with each dressing change

permethrin.mechanism: Disrupts sodium channel function in parasite nerve cells, causing paralysis and death of lice and scabies mites.

dosing.adult: Scabies: apply 5% cream from neck down to all skin, wash off after 8-14 hours; repeat in 7-14 days. Head lice: apply 1% lotion to damp hair, wash off after 10 minutes
dosing.pediatric: Children >2 months: same as adult for scabies (include scalp, face, ears in infants). Head lice: same as adult
dosing.renal: Not applicable (topical)
dosing.hepatic: Not applicable (topical)

monitoring: Wash all bedding/clothing in hot water
permethrin.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Considered safe in pregnancy. Compatible with breastfeeding (avoid application to breast area).
quick_flags: Post-treatment itching can persist for up to 4 weeks — does not indicate treatment failure
Treat ALL close contacts simultaneously even if asymptomatic to prevent re-infestation

ascorbic_acid.mechanism: Essential cofactor for collagen synthesis, carnitine biosynthesis, and catecholamine synthesis. Potent antioxidant and enhances non-heme iron absorption.
indications: Iron deficiency anemia (adjunct to enhance iron absorption)
contraindications: History of calcium oxalate kidney stones (high doses)

dosing.adult: Scurvy: 100-250mg PO 1-2 times daily; Supplementation: 40-80mg/day; Iron absorption: 200mg with iron dose
dosing.pediatric: Scurvy: 100-300mg/day; RDA varies by age
dosing.renal: Avoid high doses — increased oxalate stone risk
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

ascorbic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at RDA doses). Safe during pregnancy and lactation at recommended doses. High doses may cause rebound scurvy in neonate.
quick_flags: Co-administer with oral iron to significantly enhance non-heme iron absorption
High doses (>1g/day) can interfere with glucose monitoring (false elevations)

folic_acid.mechanism: Converted to tetrahydrofolate, an essential coenzyme in one-carbon transfer reactions for DNA synthesis, amino acid metabolism, and erythropoiesis.
indications: Megaloblastic anemia due to folate deficiency
contraindications: Pernicious anemia (untreated B12 deficiency — folic acid corrects anemia but masks neurological damage)

dosing.adult: Deficiency: 5mg PO daily for 4 months; NTD prevention: 0.4mg daily periconceptionally; High-risk NTD prevention: 5mg daily
dosing.pediatric: Infants: 50mcg/day; Children: 100-400mcg/day based on age
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; supplementation often needed in dialysis patients
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

adverse_effects: Masking of B12 deficiency is the main concern
monitoring: Serum folate and RBC folate levels
ALWAYS check B12 levels before starting folic acid
folic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category A. Essential periconceptional supplementation. Start at least 1 month before conception and continue through first trimester. Safe during lactation.
quick_flags: CRITICAL: Always rule out B12 deficiency before giving folic acid — corrects anemia but worsens subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord
5mg/day for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy or on antiepileptics

pyridoxine.mechanism: Converted to pyridoxal phosphate (PLP), the active coenzyme form involved in amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis (serotonin, dopamine, GABA), heme synthesis, and glycogenolysis.

dosing.adult: INH neuropathy prophylaxis: 10-25mg/day; INH overdose: gram-for-gram dose matching INH ingested (usually 5g IV); Deficiency: 10-50mg/day
dosing.pediatric: INH prophylaxis: 1-2mg/kg/day; INH-induced seizures: 70mg/kg IV (max 5g)
dosing.renal: Supplementation recommended in dialysis patients
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

adverse_effects: Peripheral sensory neuropathy at chronic high doses (>200mg/day)
monitoring: Neuropathy symptoms in TB patients on INH
pyridoxine.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at RDA doses). First-line for pregnancy nausea (with doxylamine). Avoid high chronic doses.
quick_flags: Antidote for isoniazid overdose-induced seizures: give pyridoxine gram-for-gram IV
Always co-prescribe with isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy

thiamine.mechanism: Converted to thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP), essential coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and transketolase in carbohydrate metabolism.

dosing.adult: Wernicke's: 500mg IV TID for 3 days, then 250mg IV/IM daily for 5 days; Prophylaxis in alcoholism: 100mg PO daily; Beriberi: 100mg IM/IV daily
dosing.pediatric: 10-25mg/day PO or 10-25mg IV/IM daily
dosing.renal: No adjustment; water-soluble, removed by dialysis — supplementation needed
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

monitoring: Clinical improvement (ataxia, confusion, ophthalmoplegia in Wernicke's)
thiamine.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at RDA doses). Safe during pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: ALWAYS give thiamine BEFORE glucose in suspected Wernicke's — glucose without thiamine can precipitate or worsen encephalopathy
Wernicke triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia — but complete triad present in only ~10% of cases

riboflavin.mechanism: Precursor of flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), essential coenzymes in oxidation-reduction reactions, electron transport chain, and metabolism of other B vitamins.
indications: Neonatal jaundice (adjunct — phototherapy enhances riboflavin degradation)

dosing.adult: Deficiency: 5-10mg/day PO; Migraine prophylaxis: 400mg/day PO
dosing.pediatric: 3-10mg/day PO depending on age
dosing.renal: No adjustment (excess excreted renally)
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

monitoring: Clinical resolution of deficiency signs (angular stomatitis, glossitis, seborrheic dermatitis)
riboflavin.pregnancy_lactation: Category A (at RDA doses). Safe during pregnancy and lactation.
quick_flags: Ariboflavinosis signs: angular stomatitis, cheilosis, magenta tongue, corneal vascularization
High-dose riboflavin (400mg/day) has evidence for migraine prophylaxis with minimal side effects

vitamin_a.mechanism: Essential for retinal function (rhodopsin formation), epithelial cell differentiation, immune function, bone growth, and reproduction. Acts through nuclear retinoic acid receptors.

dosing.adult: Severe deficiency: 200,000 IU PO on day 1, day 2, and 2 weeks later
dosing.pediatric: Measles/deficiency: <6mo: 50,000 IU; 6-12mo: 100,000 IU; >12mo: 200,000 IU PO; same schedule as adult
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose or avoid in hepatic impairment — hepatotoxic in excess

adverse_effects: Acute toxicity: headache, nausea, papilledema (pseudotumor cerebri)
Chronic toxicity: hepatotoxicity, bone pain, alopecia, dry skin
monitoring: Pregnancy test before therapeutic doses in women of childbearing age
vitamin_a.pregnancy_lactation: Category X at therapeutic doses. Highly teratogenic — never exceed 10,000 IU/day in pregnancy. Low doses compatible with lactation.
quick_flags: WHO measles protocol: 2 doses given on consecutive days; reduces measles mortality by up to 50%
Bitot spots + night blindness = pathognomonic of Vitamin A deficiency

dextran_40.mechanism: High-molecular-weight polysaccharide that expands intravascular volume by exerting oncotic pressure. Also reduces blood viscosity and inhibits platelet aggregation, improving microcirculation.
indications: Improvement of microcirculation in vascular surgery

dosing.adult: Shock: 500-1000mL IV rapidly (first 500mL may be infused over 15-30 min); max 20mL/kg in first 24 hours; Thromboprophylaxis: 500-1000mL on day of surgery, then 500mL/day for 2-3 days
dosing.pediatric: 10-20mL/kg; total dose should not exceed 20mL/kg/day
dosing.renal: Contraindicated in oliguria/anuria; may cause osmotic nephrosis
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution

monitoring: Watch for anaphylaxis especially in first few minutes
dextran_40.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use only if clearly needed; limited data.
quick_flags: Dextran-1 (Promit) can be given as a hapten before dextran-40 to reduce anaphylaxis risk
Can interfere with blood typing and cross-matching — draw blood samples BEFORE infusion

ferrous_sulfate.mechanism: Provides elemental iron for incorporation into hemoglobin, myoglobin, and iron-containing enzymes. Absorbed primarily in duodenum and upper jejunum.

dosing.adult: Treatment: 100-200mg elemental iron/day in 2-3 divided doses; Prophylaxis: 60mg elemental iron/day
dosing.pediatric: 3-6mg/kg/day of elemental iron in 2-3 divided doses
dosing.renal: Oral iron poorly effective in CKD; consider IV iron
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in liver disease

monitoring: Continue treatment 3-6 months after Hb normalization
ferrous_sulfate.pregnancy_lactation: Category A. Recommended supplementation in pregnancy by WHO. Safe during lactation.
quick_flags: Take on empty stomach with vitamin C for best absorption; avoid with tea, coffee, milk, antacids
Black stools are expected — distinguish from melena by clinical context

ferrous_salts_folic_acid.mechanism: Combines iron for hemoglobin synthesis with folic acid for DNA synthesis and erythropoiesis. Addresses the two most common nutritional deficiencies in pregnancy simultaneously.
indications: Prevention of neural tube defects combined with iron therapy

dosing.adult: 1 tablet PO daily; in established deficiency: 1 tablet PO twice daily
dosing.pediatric: Based on elemental iron content: 3-6mg/kg/day
dosing.renal: Oral iron poorly absorbed in CKD; consider IV iron
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution

ferrous_salts_folic_acid.pregnancy_lactation: Category A. WHO-recommended IFA supplementation throughout pregnancy and 3 months postpartum in endemic areas.
quick_flags: India's national IFA program provides 60mg iron + 0.5mg folic acid for all pregnant women
Always check B12 status — folic acid can mask B12 deficiency anemia while neurological damage progresses

daclatasvir.mechanism: Inhibits the HCV NS5A protein, which is essential for viral RNA replication and virion assembly. Pan-genotypic activity against Hepatitis C virus.
indications: Chronic Hepatitis C infection (all genotypes, in combination regimens)
contraindications: Co-administration with strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin)
Concomitant use with amiodarone (symptomatic bradycardia risk with sofosbuvir combination)

dosing.adult: 60mg PO once daily (with sofosbuvir 400mg) for 12-24 weeks; reduce to 30mg with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
dosing.pediatric: Not established for children <18 years in most guidelines
dosing.renal: No adjustment required including severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for Child-Pugh A/B; not recommended in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) without expert guidance

monitoring: HCV RNA at baseline, week 4, end of treatment, and 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12)
ECG if concomitant amiodarone cannot be avoided
daclatasvir.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy — insufficient data. Effective contraception required during treatment.
quick_flags: Daclatasvir + Sofosbuvir is a key pan-genotypic HCV regimen in India's national program
Dose-adjust with CYP3A4 inhibitors (30mg) and inducers (90mg if mild inducer)

hydroxocobalamin.mechanism: Converted to methylcobalamin and adenosylcobalamin, essential coenzymes for methionine synthase (homocysteine remethylation) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (fatty acid metabolism). Critical for DNA synthesis and myelination.
indications: Subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord

dosing.adult: B12 deficiency: 1000mcg IM every other day for 2 weeks, then 1000mcg IM every 3 months; Cyanide poisoning: 5g IV over 15 min
dosing.pediatric: B12 deficiency: 250-1000mcg IM every other day for 2 weeks, then monthly
dosing.renal: No adjustment; supplementation often needed in CKD
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment required

adverse_effects: Red discoloration of urine (high dose/cyanide kit)
monitoring: Serum potassium (hypokalemia risk during initial treatment)
hydroxocobalamin.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Required during pregnancy for fetal development. Deficiency during pregnancy can cause neural tube defects. Safe during lactation.
quick_flags: Hypokalemia can occur during initial B12 replacement as potassium is consumed for new RBC production — monitor closely
Hydroxocobalamin preferred over cyanocobalamin: longer retention, cyanide antidote capability, and better tissue delivery

somatostatin.mechanism: Native peptide hormone that inhibits secretion of growth hormone, insulin, glucagon, gastrin, secretin, and vasoactive intestinal peptide. Reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure.

dosing.adult: Variceal bleeding: 250mcg IV bolus then 250-500mcg/hr continuous infusion for 2-5 days; Pancreatic fistula: 250mcg/hr continuous infusion
dosing.pediatric: Limited data; 3.5mcg/kg/hr continuous infusion has been used
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; frequently used in hepatic disease (portal hypertension)

somatostatin.pregnancy_lactation: Contraindicated in pregnancy. No adequate human data. Avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Very short half-life (1-3 minutes) — requires CONTINUOUS infusion; octreotide (synthetic analogue) has longer half-life and is often preferred
In variceal bleeding: give alongside endoscopic therapy, not as substitute

isosorbide_dinitrate.mechanism: Prodrug converted to nitric oxide, which activates guanylyl cyclase increasing cGMP in vascular smooth muscle, causing venodilation (predominant) and arteriolar dilation. Reduces preload and afterload.
indications: Chronic heart failure (with hydralazine — A-HeFT regimen)

dosing.adult: Angina: 5-40mg PO 2-3 times daily; Sublingual: 2.5-5mg as needed; Heart failure: 20-40mg PO TID (with hydralazine); Ensure nitrate-free interval of 10-14 hours
dosing.pediatric: Not routinely used
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution in hypotension
dosing.hepatic: Increased bioavailability in hepatic impairment; start with lower doses

isosorbide_dinitrate.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Limited data. Use only if benefit outweighs risk.
quick_flags: MUST maintain a 10-14 hour nitrate-free interval to prevent tolerance — typically omit the nighttime dose
Isosorbide dinitrate + hydralazine is the preferred vasodilator regimen for HFrEF in African American patients (A-HeFT trial)

quinine.mechanism: Accumulates in Plasmodium food vacuole, inhibiting heme polymerization. Toxic heme accumulates and kills the parasite. Also has muscle relaxant and antipyretic properties.
indications: Nocturnal leg cramps (limited evidence, not recommended by FDA)

dosing.adult: Severe malaria: 20mg/kg loading dose IV over 4 hours (if no quinine/mefloquine in last 24h), then 10mg/kg IV every 8 hours; switch to oral when possible for 7 days total; Oral: 600mg every 8 hours for 7 days
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing as adult; 10mg/kg IV every 8 hours
dosing.renal: Reduce to every 12 hours after 48 hours if severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 30-50% in severe hepatic impairment

monitoring: Blood glucose every 4-6 hours (hypoglycemia is common and dangerous)
quinine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C but used for severe malaria in pregnancy (benefit outweighs risk). Quinine is the drug of choice for severe malaria in first trimester.
quick_flags: Hypoglycemia is the most dangerous common side effect — monitor glucose frequently, especially in pregnancy and children
NEVER give IV bolus — must infuse slowly over 4 hours to prevent fatal cardiac arrhythmias

l_asparaginase.mechanism: Hydrolyzes asparagine to aspartic acid and ammonia, depleting circulating asparagine. Leukemic lymphoblasts lack asparagine synthetase and cannot synthesize their own asparagine, leading to cell death.
indications: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) — key component of induction and consolidation
contraindications: History of serious anaphylaxis to L-asparaginase
History of serious thrombotic events with L-asparaginase

dosing.adult: 6,000-25,000 IU/m2 IM/IV per protocol; varies significantly by regimen (BFM, UKALL, etc.)
dosing.pediatric: 6,000-25,000 IU/m2 per protocol; pediatric ALL protocols define specific scheduling
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment in guidelines
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; can itself cause hepatotoxicity

monitoring: Fibrinogen and coagulation parameters (thrombosis and bleeding risk)
Allergic reactions during and after each dose
l_asparaginase.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Coagulopathy is unique: reduces antithrombin III, protein C, protein S (thrombosis risk) AND fibrinogen (bleeding risk) simultaneously
If hypersensitivity to E. coli asparaginase, switch to Erwinia asparaginase or PEG-asparaginase

leuprolide_acetate.mechanism: GnRH super-agonist that initially stimulates then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, causing profound suppression of LH, FSH, and consequently gonadal sex hormones (chemical castration).

dosing.adult: Prostate cancer: 7.5mg IM monthly or 22.5mg IM every 3 months; Endometriosis: 3.75mg IM monthly for up to 6 months; Fibroids: 3.75mg IM monthly for 3-6 months
dosing.pediatric: Central precocious puberty: 7.5-15mg IM monthly based on weight
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment; limited data

adverse_effects: Initial testosterone flare (first 2 weeks — can worsen symptoms)
monitoring: Signs of tumor flare in first 2 weeks
leuprolide_acetate.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Testosterone FLARE in first 1-2 weeks can cause spinal cord compression or bladder outlet obstruction in prostate cancer — co-administer antiandrogen (bicalutamide) for flare protection
Add-back therapy with low-dose HRT reduces bone loss when treating endometriosis

dacarbazine.mechanism: Prodrug activated by hepatic CYP450 to methylating species that add methyl groups to DNA bases (O6-guanine), causing DNA cross-links, strand breaks, and cell death.
indications: Hodgkin lymphoma (ABVD regimen — A: adriamycin, B: bleomycin, V: vinblastine, D: dacarbazine)

dosing.adult: Hodgkin (ABVD): 375mg/m2 IV on days 1 and 15 of each 28-day cycle; Melanoma: 200-250mg/m2 IV daily for 5 days every 3 weeks
dosing.pediatric: Protocol-dependent; similar mg/m2 dosing
dosing.renal: Dose reduction may be needed; no specific guidelines — use with caution
dosing.hepatic: Avoid in severe hepatic impairment; hepatic metabolism required for activation

monitoring: CBC with differential before each cycle
dacarbazine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic and mutagenic. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: Highly emetogenic — requires triple antiemetic prophylaxis (5-HT3 + steroid + NK1 antagonist)
The 'D' in ABVD regimen for Hodgkin lymphoma — one of the most curable cancers with chemotherapy

gefitinib.mechanism: Selectively inhibits EGFR (ErbB1/HER1) tyrosine kinase by binding to ATP-binding site, blocking downstream RAS-RAF-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling cascades that drive cancer cell proliferation and survival.
indications: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with activating EGFR mutations (exon 19 deletion, L858R)
Locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC (first-line in EGFR-mutant)

dosing.adult: 250mg PO once daily, with or without food; continue until disease progression or intolerable toxicity
dosing.pediatric: Not established
dosing.renal: No adjustment for CrCl >20; limited data for CrCl <20
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: use with caution and monitor LFTs; Severe: not recommended

adverse_effects: Interstitial lung disease (ILD — rare but potentially fatal)
monitoring: LFTs monthly for first 3 months then periodically
Chest imaging for ILD symptoms (new dyspnea, cough, fever)
gefitinib.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Embryotoxic in animal studies. Effective contraception required. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: EGFR mutation testing is MANDATORY before prescribing — no benefit in EGFR wild-type
Acneiform rash paradoxically predicts better response — manage but do not stop drug; ILD requires immediate discontinuation

buprenorphine.mechanism: Partial agonist at mu-opioid receptors providing analgesia with ceiling effect on respiratory depression, and antagonist at kappa receptors. High receptor affinity and slow dissociation contribute to long duration.
contraindications: Concurrent full mu-agonist use (may precipitate withdrawal in dependent patients)

dosing.adult: Pain: 0.3-0.6mg IM/IV every 6-8 hours; Opioid dependence: 2-8mg SL on day 1, titrate to 8-24mg SL daily; Transdermal: 5-20mcg/hr patch every 7 days
dosing.pediatric: 2-6mcg/kg IM/IV every 6-8 hours (age >2 years)
dosing.renal: No adjustment required; not removed by dialysis
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in moderate-severe hepatic impairment; avoid SL tablets in severe hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Precipitated withdrawal (if given too early in opioid-dependent patients)
monitoring: Signs of withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients
buprenorphine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Preferred over methadone by some guidelines for opioid use disorder in pregnancy (less neonatal withdrawal). Excreted in breast milk in low amounts.
quick_flags: Ceiling effect on respiratory depression is a major safety advantage over full agonists
For opioid dependence: patient must be in early withdrawal (COWS score ≥12) before first dose to avoid precipitated withdrawal

caffeine_citrate.mechanism: Competitive adenosine receptor antagonist that stimulates the medullary respiratory center, increases minute ventilation, CO2 sensitivity, and diaphragmatic contractility. Also has mild bronchodilatory and diuretic effects.
indications: Prevention of bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) in VLBW infants
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to caffeine or other xanthines
Neonatal seizures (relative — may lower seizure threshold)

dosing.adult: Not applicable — neonatal drug
dosing.pediatric: Loading dose: 20mg/kg IV/PO (caffeine citrate); Maintenance: 5-10mg/kg IV/PO once daily, starting 24 hours after loading dose. Note: caffeine citrate dose is 2x caffeine base dose.
dosing.renal: Use with caution in renal impairment; reduced clearance in neonates
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; caffeine is hepatically metabolized. Neonates have immature metabolism — monitor levels

caffeine_citrate.pregnancy_lactation: Not applicable — used exclusively in neonates. Crosses into breast milk when consumed by mothers but neonatal drug use is direct.
quick_flags: CAP trial landmark: early caffeine in VLBW infants reduces BPD and improves neurodevelopmental outcomes at 18 months
Caffeine CITRATE dose is double the caffeine BASE dose — always clarify which form is being prescribed to avoid dosing errors

neostigmine.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine concentration at cholinergic synapses. Enhances neuromuscular transmission and stimulates muscarinic receptors (GI motility, secretions).

dosing.adult: NMB reversal: 0.03-0.07mg/kg IV (with glycopyrrolate 0.2mg per 1mg neostigmine); Myasthenia gravis: 15-30mg PO every 3-4 hours; Ogilvie syndrome: 2mg IV over 3-5 minutes
dosing.pediatric: NMB reversal: 0.04-0.08mg/kg IV; Myasthenia: 2mg/kg/day PO divided every 3-4 hours
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment; accumulation risk
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Signs of cholinergic crisis (SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis)
neostigmine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Use with caution; transient neonatal weakness possible. Poorly excreted in breast milk; compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: ALWAYS co-administer with glycopyrrolate (or atropine) to prevent muscarinic side effects (bradycardia, secretions)
Cholinergic crisis vs myasthenic crisis: edrophonium (Tensilon) test — but neostigmine overdose worsens cholinergic crisis

insulin_nph.mechanism: Recombinant human insulin complexed with protamine to delay absorption. Provides intermediate-duration basal insulin coverage. Binds insulin receptors, activating glucose uptake and glycogen/lipid/protein synthesis.
indications: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (basal component in basal-bolus regimen)

dosing.adult: T2DM: Start 0.1-0.2 units/kg/day SC at bedtime or twice daily; T1DM: 40-50% of total daily dose as NPH; titrate by 2 units every 3 days to fasting glucose target
dosing.pediatric: T1DM: Total daily dose 0.5-1 unit/kg/day; NPH as 40-60% of total dose
dosing.renal: Reduce dose by 25% if GFR 10-50; reduce by 50% if GFR <10; insulin clearance reduced
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; gluconeogenesis impaired and insulin clearance reduced

insulin_nph.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Preferred insulin in pregnancy (long safety record). NPH is considered safe as basal insulin in GDM and pre-existing diabetes. Safe during lactation.
quick_flags: Prominent peak at 4-8 hours creates higher nocturnal hypoglycemia risk vs insulin glargine — monitor 3am glucose
MUST resuspend (roll/invert gently 10 times) before injection; cloudy appearance is normal

insulin_premix_30_70.mechanism: Fixed combination of 30% regular (soluble) insulin for prandial coverage and 70% NPH (isophane) insulin for basal coverage. Provides both rapid-onset and intermediate-duration glycemic control.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (when basal-bolus not feasible)
Resource-limited settings where premix regimen is preferred

dosing.adult: Start 0.3-0.5 units/kg/day SC divided as 2/3 pre-breakfast + 1/3 pre-dinner; Titrate by 2-4 units every 3-5 days based on glucose patterns
dosing.pediatric: 0.5-1 unit/kg/day divided twice daily; adjust based on glucose monitoring
dosing.renal: Reduce dose as for individual insulins; CKD reduces insulin clearance
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose; monitor closely

adverse_effects: Hypoglycemia (especially between meals if meal is delayed)
insulin_premix_30_70.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Human insulin premix has long safety record in pregnancy. However, separate basal-bolus regimen generally preferred in pregnancy for tighter control.
quick_flags: Fixed ratio means you CANNOT adjust basal and bolus independently — switch to basal-bolus if frequent hypoglycemia or poor control
Patient must eat meals on time — the 30% regular insulin peaks at 2-4 hours and will cause hypoglycemia if meal is skipped

ethinylestradiol_levonorgestrel.mechanism: Estrogen (ethinylestradiol) and progestin (levonorgestrel) synergistically suppress ovulation by inhibiting GnRH/LH/FSH axis. Also thickens cervical mucus, thins endometrium, and reduces tubal motility.

dosing.adult: 1 tablet PO daily for 21 days, then 7 pill-free days (or placebo pills); start on Day 1 of menses or quick-start method
dosing.pediatric: Not recommended before menarche; suitable for post-menarchal adolescents
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment; use with caution in nephrotic syndrome (thrombotic risk)
dosing.hepatic: Contraindicated in active liver disease, liver tumors

monitoring: Blood pressure before starting and every 6-12 months
ethinylestradiol_levonorgestrel.pregnancy_lactation: Category X. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Estrogen reduces breast milk production — avoid during first 6 weeks postpartum; progestin-only methods preferred during lactation.
quick_flags: UKMEC/WHO Category 4 (absolute contraindication): migraine with aura, current breast cancer, smoking >15 cigs/day over age 35
Missed pill rule: if >24 hours late, take missed pill + today's pill + use backup contraception for 7 days

budesonide_formoterol.mechanism: Budesonide: topical corticosteroid that reduces airway inflammation, edema, and mucus production. Formoterol: long-acting beta-2 agonist with rapid onset that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle. Synergistic: ICS upregulates beta-2 receptors; LABA enhances nuclear translocation of glucocorticoid receptor.
indications: Asthma (maintenance and reliever therapy — MART/SMART approach)
contraindications: Primary treatment of acute bronchospasm (as maintenance-only without rescue plan)

dosing.adult: Asthma MART: 200/6mcg 1 puff BID maintenance + 1 puff PRN for relief (max 8 puffs/day); COPD: 200/6 or 400/12mcg 1 puff BID
dosing.pediatric: Children >6 years: 100/6mcg 1-2 puffs BID (GINA step 3+)
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution in severe hepatic impairment; systemic budesonide exposure may increase

monitoring: Adrenal function with high-dose prolonged use
budesonide_formoterol.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Budesonide is the preferred ICS in pregnancy (most safety data). Formoterol has less human data but LABA use considered acceptable when needed for asthma control. Compatible with breastfeeding.
quick_flags: GINA 2023: Budesonide-formoterol MART is preferred strategy for Steps 3-5 — same inhaler for maintenance AND relief
Formoterol has rapid onset (1-3 minutes), unlike salmeterol — this is what makes MART possible

buprenorphine_naloxone.drug_class: Opioid Partial Agonist + Opioid Antagonist (FDC for opioid use disorder)
buprenorphine_naloxone.mechanism: Buprenorphine: partial mu-agonist provides anti-craving and anti-withdrawal effects with ceiling on euphoria and respiratory depression. Naloxone: opioid antagonist that is poorly absorbed sublingually but precipitates withdrawal if injected (deters IV misuse).
contraindications: Not in withdrawal yet (risk of precipitated withdrawal)

dosing.adult: Induction: Day 1: 2-4mg SL when COWS ≥12; may repeat every 2 hours (max 8mg on day 1); Day 2: up to 16mg; Maintenance: 8-24mg SL daily
dosing.pediatric: Not established for children; adolescents >16 years per adult protocols
dosing.renal: No adjustment required
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment; Severe: contraindicated (naloxone hepatotoxicity risk)

buprenorphine_naloxone.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Buprenorphine monotherapy (without naloxone) preferred in pregnancy to avoid any risk of naloxone exposure to fetus. Compatible with breastfeeding if mother is stable.
quick_flags: Naloxone component is the diversion deterrent — poorly absorbed SL but precipitates withdrawal if crushed and injected
In pregnancy: use buprenorphine MONOTHERAPY (without naloxone) — switch from combo product

tenofovir_lamivudine.mechanism: Both are nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors. Tenofovir (nucleotide analogue) and lamivudine (cytidine analogue) inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase by competing with natural substrates and causing chain termination.
indications: HIV infection (as NRTI backbone with a third agent)
Hepatitis B co-infection with HIV (both drugs active against HBV)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet PO once daily
dosing.pediatric: Weight-based dosing; FDC suitable for adolescents ≥35kg
dosing.renal: CrCl 30-49: increase interval to every 48 hours; CrCl <30: not recommended as FDC (individual dose adjustment needed)
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment; monitor for HBV flare if discontinued

monitoring: Renal function (creatinine, phosphate) at baseline and every 6 months
HIV viral load and CD4 count
tenofovir_lamivudine.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Both components used safely in pregnancy. WHO-preferred backbone for pregnant women with HIV. Compatible with breastfeeding in settings where breastfeeding is recommended.
quick_flags: CRITICAL: Never discontinue abruptly in HBV co-infection — risk of severe hepatitis flare
Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF) has more renal/bone toxicity than tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) — but TDF is in NLEM

forms: Tablet (300mg tenofovir disoproxil fumarate + 300mg lamivudine + 50mg dolutegravir)
