tenofovir_lamivudine_dolutegravir.mechanism: Tenofovir and lamivudine inhibit reverse transcriptase (chain termination). Dolutegravir inhibits HIV integrase, preventing viral DNA integration into host chromosome. Triple combination provides high barrier to resistance.
indications: Treatment-naive and treatment-experienced HIV patients transitioning to simplified regimen
contraindications: Co-administration with polyvalent cations within 2 hours (calcium, iron, antacids)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet PO once daily, with or without food
dosing.pediatric: Adolescents ≥35kg and Tanner stage 4+: adult dose; younger children: individual components by weight
dosing.renal: CrCl <50: not recommended as FDC; use individual components with dose adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No adjustment for mild-moderate; not recommended in severe hepatic impairment

adverse_effects: Elevated creatinine (dolutegravir inhibits tubular secretion — not true nephrotoxicity)
monitoring: Renal function at baseline and every 6 months
HIV viral load and CD4 count
tenofovir_lamivudine_dolutegravir.pregnancy_lactation: Category B (individual components). WHO recommends TLD as preferred first-line including in pregnant women (updated 2019 after Tsepamo study data matured). Neural tube defect risk with periconceptional dolutegravir is ~0.2% (similar to background rate).
quick_flags: WHO-preferred first-line ART globally — high efficacy, high resistance barrier, once-daily, well-tolerated
Dolutegravir raises serum creatinine by 10-15% by inhibiting OCT2 — this is NOT nephrotoxicity; do not adjust eGFR for this

forms: Tablet (300mg tenofovir disoproxil fumarate + 300mg lamivudine + 600mg efavirenz)
tenofovir_lamivudine_efavirenz.mechanism: Tenofovir and lamivudine inhibit reverse transcriptase (chain termination). Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor that binds directly to reverse transcriptase, causing conformational change and enzyme inhibition.
indications: Previously standard first-line before TLD transition
contraindications: Pregnancy in first trimester (efavirenz — though recent data more reassuring)

dosing.adult: 1 tablet PO once daily at bedtime (to reduce CNS side effects)
dosing.pediatric: Adolescents ≥35kg: adult dose; not recommended for younger children as FDC
dosing.renal: CrCl <50: not recommended as FDC
dosing.hepatic: Mild (Child-Pugh A): use with caution; Moderate-Severe: contraindicated

adverse_effects: CNS effects (vivid dreams, dizziness, insomnia, depression — common in first 2-4 weeks)
monitoring: HIV viral load and CD4 count
tenofovir_lamivudine_efavirenz.pregnancy_lactation: Efavirenz was Category D (historical teratogenicity concern) but updated data shows risk similar to background. WHO no longer restricts efavirenz in pregnancy, but TLD is now preferred. Caution in first trimester.
quick_flags: Being phased out as first-line in favor of TLD (dolutegravir-based) due to lower resistance barrier and more side effects
Take at BEDTIME to minimize CNS side effects; warn patients that vivid dreams and dizziness usually resolve within 2-4 weeks

atazanavir_ritonavir.mechanism: Atazanavir inhibits HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and production of mature, infectious virions. Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor that 'boosts' atazanavir levels by slowing its metabolism (pharmacokinetic enhancer).
indications: PI-based regimen when INSTI-based regimens are not suitable
contraindications: Co-administration with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 (rifampicin, simvastatin, ergotamine, midazolam)
Co-administration with proton pump inhibitors (significantly reduces atazanavir absorption)

dosing.adult: 300mg/100mg PO once daily with food
dosing.pediatric: Children ≥25kg: weight-based dosing per WHO guidelines; not as FDC in young children
dosing.renal: No adjustment for treatment-experienced; avoid in treatment-naive with CrCl <50 if on tenofovir backbone
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate (Child-Pugh A/B): use with caution; Severe: contraindicated

adverse_effects: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice/scleral icterus — cosmetic, not hepatotoxic)
monitoring: HIV viral load and CD4 count
atazanavir_ritonavir.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Can be used in pregnancy but ritonavir-boosted atazanavir levels may be lower in 2nd/3rd trimester — some guidelines suggest increased dosing. Not first-choice in pregnancy (TLD or LPV/r preferred).
quick_flags: Cosmetic jaundice from indirect hyperbilirubinemia is the signature side effect — mechanism similar to Gilbert syndrome; benign but may affect adherence
PPIs are CONTRAINDICATED with atazanavir — H2 blockers can be used with timing restrictions (12 hours apart)

teneligliptin.mechanism: Inhibits dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) enzyme, preventing degradation of incretin hormones (GLP-1 and GIP). This increases insulin secretion and decreases glucagon secretion in a glucose-dependent manner.
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (as monotherapy or add-on to metformin, sulfonylurea, or insulin)

dosing.adult: 20mg PO once daily, may increase to 40mg once daily if needed
dosing.pediatric: Not established for children <18 years
dosing.renal: No dose adjustment required at any level of renal impairment (unique advantage)
dosing.hepatic: Mild-moderate: no adjustment; Severe: avoid

adverse_effects: Hypoglycemia (mainly when combined with SU or insulin)
teneligliptin.pregnancy_lactation: Not recommended in pregnancy — insufficient data. Switch to insulin. Avoid during lactation.
quick_flags: Key advantage: NO dose adjustment needed even in severe renal impairment or dialysis — unique among DPP-4 inhibitors available in India
Widely prescribed in India; included in NLEM 2022; very affordable compared to sitagliptin

phenylephrine.mechanism: Selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor agonist causing vascular smooth muscle contraction, increasing systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure. No significant beta-adrenergic activity. Mydriatic effect via iris dilator muscle contraction.

dosing.adult: Hypotension: 100-500mcg IV bolus every 10-15 min; Infusion: 10-35mcg/min IV titrated to MAP; Nasal: 2-3 drops of 0.25-0.5% every 4 hours; Ophthalmic: 1 drop of 2.5% or 10%
dosing.pediatric: Hypotension: 5-20mcg/kg IV bolus; Infusion: 0.1-0.5mcg/kg/min; Nasal: 0.125-0.25% solution
dosing.renal: No specific adjustment
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

monitoring: Blood pressure (invasive arterial line for infusion)
phenylephrine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. May reduce uterine blood flow due to vasoconstriction. Use only if clearly needed. Limited data on lactation.
quick_flags: Pure alpha-1 agonist — causes reflex bradycardia (unlike norepinephrine which has beta-1 component)
Nasal decongestant use should be limited to 3-5 days to avoid rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion)

glycopyrrolate.mechanism: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M1-M5). Quaternary ammonium structure prevents BBB crossing, minimizing central side effects. Reduces secretions, heart rate vagal effects, and GI motility.
indications: Reversal of NMB (paired with neostigmine to block muscarinic effects)
contraindications: Myasthenia gravis (except when used with neostigmine)

dosing.adult: Pre-anesthetic: 0.2mg IM/IV; NMB reversal: 0.2mg IV per 1mg neostigmine; Peptic ulcer: 1-2mg PO 2-3 times daily; Sialorrhea: 1mg PO TID
dosing.pediatric: Pre-anesthetic: 4-10mcg/kg IV/IM; NMB reversal: 0.2mg per 1mg neostigmine
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment; primarily renally excreted
dosing.hepatic: No specific adjustment

glycopyrrolate.pregnancy_lactation: Category B. Does not cross placenta significantly due to quaternary structure. Limited data on breast milk excretion.
quick_flags: Preferred over atropine in many perioperative settings — does NOT cross BBB (no central anticholinergic syndrome) and has less tachycardia
Standard pairing: glycopyrrolate 0.2mg + neostigmine 1mg for NMB reversal

morphine.mechanism: Full agonist at mu-opioid receptors in CNS and peripheral tissues. Activates descending inhibitory pain pathways, alters pain perception in limbic system, and inhibits substance P release at spinal cord level.
contraindications: Head injury with raised ICP (masks pupillary signs, raises ICP)

dosing.adult: Opioid-naive: 5-15mg PO IR every 4 hours PRN; IV: 2.5-5mg every 3-4 hours titrated; SR: calculate 24h IR requirement then convert; Cancer pain: titrate to effect with no ceiling dose
dosing.pediatric: 0.1-0.2mg/kg PO/IV every 3-4 hours (start low); Neonates: 0.05-0.1mg/kg
dosing.renal: Reduce dose and increase interval; active metabolite M6G accumulates — avoid in severe renal failure if possible
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose by 50% in severe hepatic impairment; prolonged half-life

adverse_effects: Constipation (most persistent — tolerance does NOT develop)
morphine.pregnancy_lactation: Category C. Neonatal abstinence syndrome with prolonged use near term. Small amounts in breast milk — single doses compatible, chronic use requires monitoring of infant for sedation.
quick_flags: ALWAYS start a laxative when starting morphine — constipation is the one side effect to which tolerance does NOT develop
M6G (active metabolite) accumulates in renal failure causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression — prefer fentanyl or hydromorphone in ESRD

arsenic_trioxide.mechanism: Induces degradation of PML-RARα oncoprotein (pathognomonic of APL), triggers apoptosis, and promotes differentiation of leukemic promyelocytes. Also generates reactive oxygen species and inhibits angiogenesis.
indications: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) — induction and consolidation
First-line APL with ATRA (low-risk APL, chemotherapy-free regimen)

dosing.adult: Induction: 0.15mg/kg/day IV over 1-2 hours until bone marrow remission (max 60 doses); Consolidation: 0.15mg/kg/day IV for 25 doses over 5 weeks, starting 3-4 weeks after induction
dosing.pediatric: Same mg/kg dosing as adults for APL
dosing.renal: Use with caution; limited data in severe renal impairment
dosing.hepatic: Use with caution; hepatotoxicity is a known adverse effect

adverse_effects: Differentiation syndrome (formerly ATRA syndrome — fever, dyspnea, weight gain, pulmonary infiltrates)
QTc prolongation and torsades de pointes
monitoring: ECG and QTc before and weekly during treatment
Electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) — correct before starting
CBC with differential daily during induction
arsenic_trioxide.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic and embryotoxic. Contraindicated in pregnancy. Effective contraception mandatory. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: ATRA + Arsenic trioxide is now STANDARD first-line for low/intermediate-risk APL — chemotherapy-free regimen with >95% cure rate
Differentiation syndrome: treat immediately with dexamethasone 10mg IV BID at first sign — can be fatal if untreated

cytarabine.mechanism: Converted intracellularly to ara-CTP, which inhibits DNA polymerase and incorporates into DNA causing chain termination. S-phase specific. Most effective in rapidly dividing cells.
indications: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML — backbone of induction '7+3' regimen)

dosing.adult: Standard: 100-200mg/m2/day continuous IV infusion for 7 days (AML induction); High-dose: 1-3g/m2 IV every 12 hours for 4-6 doses (consolidation); Intrathecal: 30-50mg in preservative-free saline
dosing.pediatric: Protocol-dependent; standard dose similar mg/m2
dosing.renal: Reduce dose in renal impairment for high-dose regimens; standard dose usually tolerated
dosing.hepatic: Reduce dose in significant hepatic dysfunction; drug is hepatically cleared

monitoring: CBC with differential daily during induction
Cerebellar function assessment before each high-dose cycle (finger-nose, heel-shin)
Prophylactic steroid eye drops for high-dose regimens
cytarabine.pregnancy_lactation: Category D. Teratogenic — especially in first trimester. Contraindicated in pregnancy if possible. Discontinue breastfeeding.
quick_flags: The '7+3' AML induction: cytarabine 100-200mg/m2 CI x7 days + daunorubicin x3 days — cornerstone of AML treatment for decades
MUST assess cerebellar function before EACH high-dose cycle — irreversible cerebellar toxicity if continued despite signs; age >60 and renal impairment increase risk
